> The following derivation is a **high-yield KTU 2024 exam topic**. Students must memorize the final transfer function and the assumption used (negligible valve dynamics compared to flow process).

**System Setup:**
- Wild flow $q_A$ enters as a disturbance to the ratio relay.
- Ratio relay multiplies $q_A$ by $K$ to produce the set-point $SP_B = K \cdot q_A$.
- Slave controller $G_c(s)$ acts on error $e = SP_B - q_B$.
- Control valve $K_v$ manipulates the controlled stream.
- Flow process dynamics: $G_p(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}$.

**Step 1: Form the controller output signal.**

$$m(s) = G_c(s) \cdot e(s) = G_c(s) \cdot \left[K \cdot q_A(s) - q_B(s)\right]$$

**Step 2: The control valve modulates the flow.**

$$q_B^{\text{valve}}(s) = K_v \cdot m(s)$$

**Step 3: The process dynamics filter the valve output to produce the measured $q_B$.**

$$q_B(s) = G_p(s) \cdot K_v \cdot m(s)$$

**Step 4: Substitute Step 1 into Step 3.**

$$q_B(s) = G_p(s) \cdot K_v \cdot G_c(s) \cdot \left[K \cdot q_A(s) - q_B(s)\right]$$

**Step 5: Expand and group the $q_B(s)$ terms on one side.**

$$q_B(s) = G_p(s) \cdot K_v \cdot G_c(s) \cdot K \cdot q_A(s) - G_p(s) \cdot K_v \cdot G_c(s) \cdot q_B(s)$$

**Step 6: Move the $q_B(s)$ term from the right-hand side to the left.**

$$q_B(s) + G_p(s) \cdot K_v \cdot G_c(s) \cdot q_B(s) = K \cdot K_v \cdot G_p(s) \cdot G_c(s) \cdot q_A(s)$$

**Step 7: Factor out $q_B(s)$ on the left.**

$$q_B(s) \cdot \left[1 + K_v \cdot G_p(s) \cdot G_c(s)\right] = K \cdot K_v \cdot G_p(s) \cdot G_c(s) \cdot q_A(s)$$

**Step 8: Solve for the closed-loop transfer function $\dfrac{q_B(s)}{q_A(s)}$.**

$$\dfrac{q_B(s)}{q_A(s)} = \dfrac{K \cdot K_v \cdot G_p(s) \cdot G_c(s)}{1 + K_v \cdot G_p(s) \cdot G_c(s)}$$

**Step 9: Substitute the process transfer function explicitly.**

$$\dfrac{q_B(s)}{q_A(s)} = \dfrac{K \cdot K_v \cdot G_c(s) \cdot \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}}{1 + K_v \cdot G_c(s) \cdot \dfrac{K_p}{\tau_p s + 1}}$$

**Step 10: Multiply numerator and denominator by $(\tau_p s + 1)$ to simplify.**

$$\dfrac{q_B(s)}{q_A(s)} = \dfrac{K \cdot K_v \cdot K_p \cdot G_c(s)}{(\tau_p s + 1) + K_v \cdot K_p \cdot G_c(s)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Closed-Loop Transfer Function**:
> $$\dfrac{q_B(s)}{q_A(s)} = \dfrac{K \cdot K_v \cdot K_p \cdot G_c(s)}{\tau_p s + 1 + K_v \cdot K_p \cdot G_c(s)}$$

**Step 11: Analyze steady-state behavior for a step change $q_A(s) = \dfrac{Q_A}{s}$ with PI controller $G_c(s) = K_c \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$.**

As $s \to 0$, $G_c(s) \to \infty$, hence:

$$\lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{q_B(s)}{q_A(s)} = \dfrac{K \cdot K_v \cdot K_p \cdot \infty}{1 + 0 + K_v \cdot K_p \cdot \infty} = K$$

This proves that the **steady-state ratio is exactly the desired $K$** — confirming zero steady-state error with integral action.

### 3.2 Derivation: Ratio Error Bound for a P-Only Controller

If $G_c(s) = K_c$ (proportional only), then the steady-state ratio becomes:

$$R_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{K \cdot K_v \cdot K_p \cdot K_c}{1 + K_v \cdot K_p \cdot K_c} = \dfrac{K \cdot L}{1 + L}$$

where $L = K_v \cdot K_p \cdot K_c$ is the **open-loop gain**. The **steady-state ratio error** is:

$$e_{ss} = K - R_{ss} = K - \dfrac{K \cdot L}{1 + L} = \dfrac{K}{1 + L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This shows that **proportional control alone cannot achieve perfect ratio tracking** — a finite steady-state offset of magnitude $\dfrac{K}{1+L}$ persists. Hence the **mandatory inclusion of integral action** in any high-precision ratio loop.

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Aspect | Scheme I (Linear) | Scheme II (Measured Ratio) | Scheme III (Cascade) |
|--------|-------------------|----------------------------|----------------------|
| Accuracy | Moderate | High | Highest |
| Complexity | Lowest | Medium | Highest |
| Set-point for $q_B$ loop | Fixed $K \cdot I_A$ | Varies with $R_{meas}$ | Output of master controller |
| Rejects disturbances in $q_B$ | Yes | Yes | **Yes (faster)** |
| Handles nonlinearity | No | Partial | Yes (master can re-tune) |
| Preferred in | Simple blending | Quality-critical blending | **Refineries, reactors** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Linear Ratio Control (Scheme I)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1[Wild Flow qA] --> node2[Flow Transmitter FT-A]
    node2 --> node3[Ratio Relay K]
    node3 --> node4[Set-Point SP for qB Loop]
    node4 --> node5[Subtract e = SP - qB]
    node6[Flow Transmitter FT-B qB] --> node5
    node5 --> node7[Slave Controller Gc]
    node7 --> node8[Control Valve Kv]
    node8 --> node9[Controlled Flow qB]
    node9 --> node6
    classDef sig fill:#fff3b0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef ctrl fill:#b0d4ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef proc fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000,stroke-width:1px;
    class node1,node9 proc
    class node2,node3,node6,node4,node5,node7 sig
    class node8 ctrl
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Cascade Ratio Control (Scheme III)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA1[Wild Flow qA] --> nodeA2[FT-A Transmitter]
    nodeA2 --> nodeA3[Ratio Set-Point K]
    nodeA3 --> nodeA4[Master Controller Gc-master]
    nodeA4 --> nodeA5[Set-Point for Slave FC-B]
    nodeA5 --> nodeA6[Subtraction e_slave]
    nodeA7[FT-B Transmitter qB] --> nodeA6
    nodeA6 --> nodeA8[Slave Controller Gc-slave]
    nodeA8 --> nodeA9[Control Valve on Stream B]
    nodeA9 --> nodeA10[Process qB output]
    nodeA10 --> nodeA7
    classDef wild fill:#ffd6a5,stroke:#663300,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef sig fill:#caffbf,stroke:#003300,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef ctrl fill:#bdb2ff,stroke:#1a0033,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef proc fill:#ffadad,stroke:#330000,stroke-width:1px;
    class nodeA1,nodeA10 proc
    class nodeA2,nodeA3,nodeA5,nodeA6,nodeA7 sig
    class nodeA4,nodeA8,nodeA9 ctrl
```

### 4.3 Process Flow Schematic — Typical Blending Application

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeB1[Tank A Wild Stream] --> nodeB2[Pump A]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3[Orifice + DPT for qA]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4[Mixing Manifold]
    nodeB1a[Tank B Controlled Stream] --> nodeB5[Pump B]
    nodeB5 --> nodeB6[Control Valve CV-B]
    nodeB6 --> nodeB7[Orifice + DPT for qB]
    nodeB7 --> nodeB4
    nodeB4 --> nodeB8[Blended Product Outlet]
    classDef tank fill:#fdffb6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef inst fill:#a0c4ff,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef valve fill:#ff6f6f,stroke:#800000,stroke-width:1px;
    class nodeB1,nodeB1a,nodeB4,nodeB8 tank
    class nodeB2,nodeB3,nodeB5,nodeB7 inst
    class nodeB6 valve
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow Representation of the Ratio Control Math

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sA[qA input] --> mult1[Multiplier K]
    mult1 --> sum1[+]
    sum1 --> ctrl[Gc controller]
    ctrl --> valv[Kv control valve]
    valv --> proc[Gp = Kp over tau_p s + 1]
    proc --> qBout[qB output]
    qBout --> sum1
    qBout --> divide[Divider qB over qA]
    divide --> ratioDisp[R actual display]
    classDef math fill:#e0e0ff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px;
    classDef plant fill:#ffe0e0,stroke:#660000,stroke-width:1px;
    class mult1,sum1,divide math
    class valv,proc plant
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1]**

**Q1.** Define **Ratio Control**. State any **two industrial applications** where ratio control is essential. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> Ratio control is a control strategy in which one flow variable is automatically maintained in a fixed proportion $K$ to another freely varying (wild) flow. The relationship is $q_B = K \cdot q_A$.
> 
> **Two industrial applications:**
> 1. **Air-fuel ratio control in boiler combustion** — to maintain stoichiometric balance and maximize efficiency.
> 2. **Reflux-to-distillate ratio control in distillation columns** — to keep product purity stable despite feed disturbances.

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 1]**

**Q2.** What is the difference between a **wild flow** and a **controlled flow** in a ratio control loop? **(3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> - **Wild flow ($q_A$):** A process stream whose flow rate changes independently and is **not manipulated** by the ratio controller. It acts as the reference or master variable.
> - **Controlled flow ($q_B$):** A process stream whose flow rate is **actively manipulated** by the controller to maintain the desired ratio with the wild flow. It is the dependent variable.
> 
> Example: In an air-fuel ratio loop, the fuel flow is the **wild flow** (driven by steam demand) and the air flow is the **controlled flow** (manipulated to maintain the desired ratio).

---

### 5.2 Part B: Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1 | 14 Marks]**

### **Question A (Option 1)**

**(a)** With a neat **block diagram**, explain the working of a **linear ratio control system** for blending two liquids A and B in the ratio 1:3. Clearly identify the **wild flow**, **controlled flow**, **ratio relay**, and **slave controller**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A linear ratio control system for blending streams A and B in ratio $K = 1/3$ (i.e., $q_B = 3 \cdot q_A$) works as follows:

1. **Stream A (wild flow)** flows freely into the mixing tank. A flow transmitter **FT-A** measures $q_A$ and sends a proportional signal $I_A$ to the **ratio relay**.
2. The **ratio relay** multiplies $I_A$ by $K = 3$ to produce the set-point $SP_B = 3 \cdot I_A$ for stream B.
3. **Stream B (controlled flow)** has a control valve **CV-B** actuated by a **slave flow controller FC-B**. The controller compares $SP_B$ with the measured $q_B$ from **FT-B**.
4. The error $e = SP_B - q_B$ drives the valve, adjusting $q_B$ until the ratio is achieved.
5. If $q_A$ increases (e.g., a larger order arrives), the set-point $SP_B$ automatically rises, and more of stream B is admitted — **maintaining the 1:3 proportion at all times**.

**Block Diagram (Textual Representation):**

$$q_A \to \text{FT-A} \to \text{Ratio Relay } (K=3) \to SP_B \to (-) \to \text{FC-B} \to \text{CV-B} \to q_B \to \text{FT-B} \to (-)$$

**(b)** Derive the **closed-loop transfer function** $\dfrac{q_B(s)}{q_A(s)}$ for the linear ratio control scheme. Show that the **steady-state error is zero** when a **PI controller** is used. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Refer to the derivation in **Section 3.1 (Steps 1–10)** of these notes. The final result is:

$$\dfrac{q_B(s)}{q_A(s)} = \dfrac{K \cdot K_v \cdot K_p \cdot G_c(s)}{\tau_p s + 1 + K_v \cdot K_p \cdot G_c(s)}$$

Substituting a PI controller $G_c(s) = K_c \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)$ and applying the final value theorem for a step change $q_A(s) = \dfrac{Q_A}{s}$:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} q_B(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \dfrac{K \cdot K_v \cdot K_p \cdot K_c \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)}{\tau_p s + 1 + K_v \cdot K_p \cdot K_c \left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\tau_I s}\right)} \cdot \dfrac{Q_A}{s}$$

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $\tau_I s$ to clear the integrator:

$$\lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{K \cdot K_v \cdot K_p \cdot K_c \cdot (\tau_I s + 1) \cdot Q_A}{\tau_I s(\tau_p s + 1) + K_v \cdot K_p \cdot K_c \cdot (\tau_I s + 1)}$$

As $s \to 0$, the terms $(\tau_I s + 1) \to 1$ and $\tau_I s(\tau_p s + 1) \to 0$. Hence:

$$q_B(\infty) = \dfrac{K \cdot K_v \cdot K_p \cdot K_c \cdot Q_A}{K_v \cdot K_p \cdot K_c} = K \cdot Q_A$$

Since the desired steady-state value is also $K \cdot Q_A$, the **steady-state error $e_{ss} = 0$**. $\blacksquare$

---

### **Question B (Option 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)**

**(a)** Explain the working of a **cascade ratio control system** with a block diagram. How is it superior to a simple linear ratio control scheme? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

In cascade ratio control, the wild flow loop is closed by a **primary (master) controller** whose output becomes the **set-point of the secondary (slave) controller** on the controlled stream.

- **Master loop:** Controls the wild flow $q_A$ using transmitter FT-A and a controller whose set-point is the desired ratio $K \cdot q_A^{desired}$ (or simply a fixed set-point in true cascade).
- **Slave loop:** The output of the master controller dynamically becomes the set-point of the slave flow controller on $q_B$.
- When a disturbance hits $q_B$ (e.g., a pressure change downstream), the **slave loop reacts first** (fast inner loop), correcting $q_B$ before the master loop even senses the disturbance propagating to $q_A$.

**Superiority over Linear Scheme:**
1. **Faster disturbance rejection** — inner slave loop eliminates $q_B$ disturbances before they affect the ratio.
2. **Better set-point tracking** — the master loop ensures $q_A$ is also at the right value.
3. **Handles nonlinearities** in either stream more robustly.
4. **Tuning is hierarchical** — tune the slave loop first (faster), then the master loop (slower).

**(b)** A boiler is to be operated with an **air-fuel ratio of 15:1** by mass. The fuel flow varies as a wild stream between 100 and 500 kg/hr following load demand. If a **P-only controller** with $K_c = 2$ is used in a linear ratio control scheme and the loop gain $K_v K_p = 0.5$, calculate the **maximum steady-state ratio error**. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Desired ratio $K = 15$
- Loop gain $L = K_v K_p K_c = 0.5 \times 2 = 1.0$

**Formula for steady-state ratio error with P-only controller** (derived in Section 3.2):

$$e_{ss} = \dfrac{K}{1 + L} = \dfrac{15}{1 + 1.0} = \dfrac{15}{2} = 7.5$$

**Interpretation:**
- The actual achieved ratio at steady state is $R_{ss} = K - e_{ss} = 15 - 7.5 = 7.5$
- The error is **50% of the desired ratio** — unacceptably large!
- This demonstrates why **P-only control is unsuitable** for precise ratio control; **PI control is mandatory** to drive the error to zero. $\blacksquare$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
> 
> 1. **Mixing up wild and controlled flow definitions** in part (a) — examiners deduct 2 marks immediately. The wild flow is **never manipulated** by the ratio controller.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply the final value theorem** when computing steady-state error — students often simply plug $s = 0$ without justifying the step.
> 3. **Confusing steady-state ratio error $e_{ss}$ with the deviation $K - R_{ss}$** — they are identical in magnitude but the sign convention matters.
> 4. **Not drawing the feedback path** in the block diagram — the FT-B transmitter and subtraction junction must be shown explicitly; missing them loses 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the units of $K$** — if $q_A$ and $q_B$ have different units, $K$ carries units; failing to mention this loses 1 mark in numerical problems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- **Definition:** Ratio control maintains $q_B = K \cdot q_A$ where $K$ is the ratio set-point.
- **Wild flow ($q_A$):** Free, uncontrolled, acts as reference.
- **Controlled flow ($q_B$):** Manipulated to track the ratio.
- **Three schemes:** Linear, Measured-Ratio, and Cascade — **Cascade is industry-preferred**.
- **Master-slave relationship:** In cascade ratio control, the wild flow controller is the master; the controlled flow controller is the slave.
- **Computing element:** The ratio relay multiplies the wild flow signal by $K$ to generate the set-point.
- **Closed-loop TF** (Linear Scheme I): $\dfrac{q_B(s)}{q_A(s)} = \dfrac{K \cdot K_v \cdot K_p \cdot G_c(s)}{\tau_p s + 1 + K_v \cdot K_p \cdot G_c(s)}$
- **Steady-state error:**
    - PI/PID controller → $e_{ss} = 0$ (perfect tracking)
    - P-only controller → $e_{ss} = \dfrac{K}{1 + L}$ where $L = K_v K_p K_c$
- **Industrial Applications:** Combustion (air-fuel), blending (refineries), neutralization (pH), reflux/distillate (distillation columns).
- **Tuning Order:** Always tune the **slave (inner) loop first** in cascade, then the master.
- **Common Mistake:** Forgetting dimensional scaling when flows have different units.
- **Exam Favorites:** Derivation of closed-loop TF, computation of $e_{ss}$ for P-only vs. PI, drawing the block diagram, identifying components.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Split Range Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT633 - SUPERVISORY CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Split Range Control ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Split Range Control (SRC)** is an advanced regulatory control strategy in which a **single process controller output signal** (typically the standard pneumatic **3–15 psi** or electronic **4–20 mA** / **0–100%** range) is mathematically **partitioned into two or more sub-ranges**, with each sub-range driving a **distinct final control element** (usually a control valve or variable speed drive). The partitioning is achieved through dedicated **signal splitter relays**, **characterizing relays**, or modern **DCS function blocks** that re-map the controller's output to multiple actuator setpoints. The technique is most frequently deployed where a single manipulated variable must be **physically split** into two opposing or complementary actions (e.g., heating *and* cooling, charging *and* discharging, pressurizing *and* venting). > [!NOTE] > **Key Terminology (KTU Syllabus Aligned):** > - **Final Control Element (FCE):** The physical device (valve, damper, VFD) that directly alters the manipulated flow. > - **Splitting Point (Toggle Point):** The exact value of controller output at which one valve starts to open as the other starts to close. > - **Signal Characterizer:** A function generator that linearises or reshapes the split output to compensate for nonlinear valve/process gain. --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Car's Air-Conditioning Thermostat" Imagine driving a car on a hot day with automatic climate control: 1. You set the cabin temperature to **22 °C**. 2. The thermostat measures the actual cabin temperature and computes a single command between **0% (cold/cool) and 100% (hot/heat)**. 3. This **single command** is then *split*: - When the cabin is **too hot**, the controller output rises above 50%, the **A/C compressor (cooling)** is **fully off**, and the **heater valve (hot coolant)** opens proportionally to push hot water into the heater core. - When the cabin is **too cold**, the output drops below 50%, the heater valve **shuts**, and the **A/C compressor** engages to chill the air. - At exactly **50% output**, both the heater and the A/C are at their minimum (or fully closed) — this is the *splitting point* or *toggle point*. This single thermostat output controlling two completely opposite actuators is **exactly** the principle of split range control. The "magic" is in how the **3–15 psi** or **4–20 mA** signal is interpreted differently by each valve. > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for Exams:** "**One Brain, Two Hands**" — A single PID controller (the brain) commands two final control elements (the hands) to push the process variable in opposite or complementary directions, depending on which side of the setpoint the process has drifted to. --- ### 1.3 Engineering Metrics & Standards (Bold for Emphasis) - **Standard pneumatic signal range:** **3–15 psi** (legacy ISA S7.4) - **Standard electronic signal range:** **4–20 mA DC** (current-loop, ISA S50.1) - **Modern smart signal range:** **0–100%** normalized DCS internal representation - **Typical splitting point:** **50%** of the output span (12 mA or 9 psi), but **field-tunable** - **Standard split configuration:** **2-way split** (most common), **3-way split** (rare, e.g., economizer + burner + vent) - **Valve failure positions (commonly paired with SRC):** Air-to-Open (AO) and Air-to-Close (AC) used in combination for **fail-safe** redundancy > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Alert:** When describing split range, you MUST specify whether each valve is **air-to-open (AO)** or **air-to-close (AC)**. Marks are frequently lost for omitting this detail in block diagrams. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Operational Philosophy — The "Why" Behind SRC In many industrial processes, a **single physical actuator cannot provide the full range of dynamic response** required. Consider a jacketed chemical reactor: - To **raise** temperature → admit **steam** into the jacket. - To **lower** temperature → admit **cooling water** into the jacket. - A single valve cannot simultaneously pass steam and water without cross-contamination and thermal shock. Therefore, two physically separate valves (one on the steam line, one on the cooling water line) must be installed. A single temperature controller, however, can only emit **one** output. The solution is **Split Range Control** — the controller's single output is **mapped** so that: - A **low output** (e.g., 4–12 mA) drives the cooling water valve. - A **high output** (e.g., 12–20 mA) drives the steam valve. The opposite action (heating on low signal, cooling on high signal) is also used depending on the **fail-safe** requirement. --- ### 2.2 Standard Split Configurations | Configuration | Valve A Range | Valve B Range | Toggle Point | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Cooling on Low, Heating on High** | 0–50% (CO) | 50–100% (CO) | **50% (12 mA / 9 psi)** | Jacketed reactor temperature | | **Heating on Low, Cooling on High** | 0–50% (CO) | 50–100% (CO) | **50% (12 mA / 9 psi)** | Reverse-acting furnace | | **Sequential (Both open together)** | 0–100% (CO) | 0–100% (CO) | None (parallel) | Redundant pump staging | | **Three-way split (Acid/Base/Wash)** | 0–33%, 33–66%, 66–100% | — | **33% and 66%** | pH neutralization | | **Vent + Feed split** | 0–60% (vent) | 40–100% (feed) | **40–60% (overlap zone)** | Pressure safety control | > [!NOTE] > **Overlap Zone:** In practical installations, a small overlap (typically 2–5%) is intentionally built in around the toggle point to ensure that **at least one valve is always passing some flow**, preventing a "dead band" where neither valve moves and the loop loses authority. --- ### 2.3 Linear Split-Range Mathematical Model Let the controller output be represented as a normalized fraction $m$ where: - $m = 0$ corresponds to 4 mA or 3 psi - $m = 1$ corresponds to 20 mA or 15 psi **Case 1: Cooling water valve $V_c$ (acts on lower half)** $$V_c = \begin{cases} 1 - 2m & \text{if } 0 \leq m \leq 0.5 \\ 0 & \text{if } 0.5 < m \leq 1 \end{cases}$$ **Case 2: Steam valve $V_s$ (acts on upper half)** $$V_s = \begin{cases} 0 & \text{if } 0 \leq m \leq 0.5 \\ 2m - 1 & \text{if } 0.5 < m \leq 1 \end{cases}$$ These two piecewise linear functions ensure that: - $V_c + V_s$ does not exceed 1 at any point - The total flow capacity is preserved - There is no simultaneous full opening of both valves --- ### 2.4 Signal Conversion Equations (Real Engineering Units) The student must often convert between controller output in **mA / psi** and the corresponding **valve stem position in %**. The general linear interpolation formula is: $$V(\%) = \frac{C - C_{\min}}{C_{\max} - C_{\min}} \times 100$$ where $C$ is the actual controller output signal and $C_{\min}$, $C_{\max}$ are the **effective minimum and maximum signals for that particular valve** (not the full 4–20 mA span). For the **cooling water valve** with effective range 4–12 mA: $$V_c(\%) = \frac{C - 4}{12 - 4} \times 100 = \frac{C - 4}{8} \times 100$$ For the **steam valve** with effective range 12–20 mA: $$V_s(\%) = \frac{C - 12}{20 - 12} \times 100 = \frac{C - 12}{8} \times 100$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Realization:** The two valves share a common input signal $C$ but have **different denominators** in their scaling equations. This is the *mathematical essence* of split range — the same signal is interpreted on **two different scales simultaneously**. --- ### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Boundary Conditions | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Normalized Controller Output | $m = \dfrac{C - 4}{20 - 4} = \dfrac{C - 4}{16}$ | dimensionless | $0 \leq m \leq 1$ | | 2 | Pneumatic Normalized Output | $m_p = \dfrac{P - 3}{15 - 3} = \dfrac{P - 3}{12}$ | dimensionless | $0 \leq m_p \leq 1$ | | 3 | Valve A (Lower Half) Position | $V_A = \max\!\left(0,\; \dfrac{S - C_A^{low}}{C_A^{high} - C_A^{low}}\right)$ | fraction | $0 \leq V_A \leq 1$ | | 4 | Valve B (Upper Half) Position | $V_B = \max\!\left(0,\; \dfrac{S - C_B^{low}}{C_B^{high} - C_B^{low}}\right)$ | fraction | $0 \leq V_B \leq 1$ | | 5 | Sum of Stem Positions (non-overlap) | $V_A + V_B \leq 1$ | dimensionless | Conservation check | | 6 | Sum of Stem Positions (overlap) | $V_A + V_B \geq 1$ at toggle | dimensionless | Overlap verification | | 7 | Linear Characterizer Slope | $K = \dfrac{\Delta V}{\Delta C}$ | fraction / mA | Constant for linear split | | 8 | Effective Process Gain | $K_p^{eff} = K_p \cdot K_v \cdot K_c$ | varies | Used in stability analysis | *(In the table, $S$ denotes the controller output signal, $C_A^{low}$ and $C_A^{high}$ are the signal limits of valve A, and likewise for valve B.)* > [!TIP] > **Exam Shortcut:** If a problem states "controller output is 12 mA" and the toggle point is 12 mA, **both valves are at 0% opening** simultaneously. This is the **toggle point equilibrium** — the controller is satisfied and the process is at setpoint. --- ### 2.6 Real-World Industrial Utility 1. **Petrochemical Reactors:** Jacketed CSTRs (Continuous Stirred Tank Reactors) use SRC to control exothermic/endothermic reactions, swinging between chilled glycol and hot oil services. 2. **Power Plant Boiler Drum Level:** Feedwater valve and steam dump valve on a common level signal for surge protection. 3. **HVAC Chilled Water Systems:** Chiller staging with cooling tower bypass. 4. **pH Neutralization:** Acid pump and caustic pump on a single pH controller output for wastewater treatment. 5. **Compressor Anti-Surge:** Recycle valve (open on low flow) and discharge valve (open on high pressure) share a single surge controller. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Problem Statement (Worked Example #1) A temperature controller has an output of **4–20 mA** that is split to operate two valves on a jacketed reactor: - **Cooling Water Valve (V_cw):** Air-to-Open (AO), fully closed at 20 mA, fully open at 4 mA. - **Steam Valve (V_st):** Air-to-Close (AC), fully closed at 4 mA, fully open at 20 mA. The toggle point is set at **12 mA** (the 50% mark). Determine the opening percentage of each valve when the controller output is: - (i) $C = 8$ mA - (ii) $C = 12$ mA - (iii) $C = 16$ mA --- ### 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Solution **Step 1: Identify the valid range for each valve.** Cooling water valve (AO, fully open at 4 mA): $$V_{cw} \in [0, 100\%] \quad \text{for} \quad C \in [20 \text{ mA}, 4 \text{ mA}]$$ Steam valve (AC, fully open at 20 mA): $$V_{st} \in [0, 100\%] \quad \text{for} \quad C \in [4 \text{ mA}, 20 \text{ mA}]$$ **Step 2: Identify the active region for each output value.** Since the toggle point is 12 mA: - $C = 8$ mA $\rightarrow$ Below 12 mA $\rightarrow$ **Cooling region** - $C = 12$ mA $\rightarrow$ At toggle point $\rightarrow$ **Transition** - $C = 16$ mA $\rightarrow$ Above 12 mA $\rightarrow$ **Heating region** **Step 3: Derive the linear relationship for the cooling water valve (AO, inverse action).** For the AO cooling valve, the stem position decreases as $C$ increases from 4 mA to 20 mA. The general linear form is: $$V_{cw}(\%) = 100 - \left[\frac{C - 4}{20 - 4}\right] \times 100$$ Simplify: $$V_{cw}(\%) = 100 - \frac{C - 4}{16} \times 100$$ $$V_{cw}(\%) = 100 - 6.25(C - 4)$$ $$V_{cw}(\%) = 100 - 6.25C + 25$$ $$\boxed{V_{cw}(\%) = 125 - 6.25C}$$ **Validity Check:** At $C = 4$ mA, $V_{cw} = 125 - 25 = 100\%$ ✓ (fully open). At $C = 20$ mA, $V_{cw} = 125 - 125 = 0\%$ ✓ (fully closed). **Step 4: Derive the linear relationship for the steam valve (AC, direct action).** For the AC steam valve, the stem position increases as $C$ increases from 4 mA to 20 mA: $$V_{st}(\%) = \left[\frac{C - 4}{20 - 4}\right] \times 100$$ $$V_{st}(\%) = \frac{C - 4}{16} \times 100$$ $$\boxed{V_{st}(\%) = 6.25(C - 4)}$$ **Validity Check:** At $C = 4$ mA, $V_{st} = 0\%$ ✓ (fully closed). At $C = 20$ mA, $V_{st} = 100\%$ ✓ (fully open). **Step 5: Apply the toggle-point constraint.** However, both valves cannot operate over the **full** 4–20 mA range simultaneously — this is the essence of split range. The standard interpretation is that: - $V_{cw}$ is active only when $C \leq 12$ mA (uses subrange 4–12 mA, span = 8 mA) - $V_{st}$ is active only when $C \geq 12$ mA (uses subrange 12–20 mA, span = 8 mA) **Recalibrate the equations for the split sub-ranges.** For the **cooling water valve** (active range: 4 to 12 mA): $$V_{cw}(\%) = \frac{12 - C}{12 - 4} \times 100 = \frac{12 - C}{8} \times 100$$ $$V_{cw}(\%) = 12.5(12 - C)$$ $$\boxed{V_{cw}(\%) = 150 - 12.5C}$$ For the **steam valve** (active range: 12 to 20 mA): $$V_{st}(\%) = \frac{C - 12}{20 - 12} \times 100 = \frac{C - 12}{8} \times 100$$ $$V_{st}(\%) = 12.5(C - 12)$$ $$\boxed{V_{st}(\%) = 12.5C - 150}$$ **Step 6: Compute the three required values.** **(i) At $C = 8$ mA:** $$V_{cw}(\%) = 150 - 12.5 \times 8 = 150 - 100 = \mathbf{50\%}$$ $$V_{st}(\%) = 12.5 \times 8 - 150 = 100 - 150 = -50\% \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Clamped to } \mathbf{0\%}$$ *Interpretation: The controller is calling for cooling. The cooling water valve is half open; the steam valve is fully closed.* **(ii) At $C = 12$ mA (Toggle Point):** $$V_{cw}(\%) = 150 - 12.5 \times 12 = 150 - 150 = \mathbf{0\%}$$ $$V_{st}(\%) = 12.5 \times 12 - 150 = 150 - 150 = \mathbf{0\%}$$ *Interpretation: Both valves are fully closed. The controller is satisfied and the process is at setpoint.* **(iii) At $C = 16$ mA:** $$V_{cw}(\%) = 12.5 \times 16 - 150 = 200 - 150 = 50\% \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Clamped to } \mathbf{0\%}$$ $$V_{st}(\%) = 12.5 \times 16 - 150 = 200 - 150 = \mathbf{50\%}$$ *Interpretation: The controller is calling for heating. The steam valve is half open; the cooling water valve is fully closed.* --- ### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix | Controller Output | Active Region | $V_{cw}$ (Cooling) | $V_{st}$ (Steam) | Process Action | |---|---|---|---|---| | 4 mA | Cooling (max) | **100%** | 0% | Maximum cooling | | 8 mA | Cooling (mid) | **50%** | 0% | Moderate cooling | | 12 mA | Toggle point | **0%** | **0%** | Equilibrium (no flow) | | 16 mA | Heating (mid) | 0% | **50%** | Moderate heating | | 20 mA | Heating (max) | 0% | **100%** | Maximum heating | --- ### 3.4 Worked Example #2 — Pneumatic (3–15 psi) Signal Split A pressure controller output of **3–15 psi** is split such that: - Valve A (Vent to atmosphere, AC) is fully open at 6 psi and fully closed at 12 psi. - Valve B (Feed from compressor, AO) is fully open at 12 psi and fully closed at 6 psi. Find the opening of each valve when the controller output is **9 psi**. **Step 1: Identify Valve A (AC) range.** The valve is **fully open at 6 psi** and **fully closed at 12 psi**. Since it is AC, the stem position decreases as pressure increases. The linear relationship is: $$V_A(\%) = 100 - \frac{P - 6}{12 - 6} \times 100$$ $$V_A(\%) = 100 - \frac{P - 6}{6} \times 100 = 100 - 16.67(P - 6)$$ $$V_A(\%) = 100 - 16.67P + 100 = 200 - 16.67P$$ **Step 2: Identify Valve B (AO) range.** The valve is **fully open at 12 psi** and **fully closed at 6 psi**. Since it is AO, the stem position increases as pressure increases. The linear relationship is: $$V_B(\%) = \frac{P - 6}{12 - 6} \times 100 = 16.67(P - 6) = 16.67P - 100$$ **Step 3: Evaluate at $P = 9$ psi.** $$V_A(\%) = 200 - 16.67 \times 9 = 200 - 150.03 = \mathbf{49.97\%} \approx 50\%$$ $$V_B(\%) = 16.67 \times 9 - 100 = 150.03 - 100 = \mathbf{50\%}$$ **Step 4: Interpretation.** At 9 psi (the midpoint), **both valves are 50% open simultaneously**. This is a **5 psi overlap region (6–12 psi)** where both valves are partially open. In practice, this overlap is desirable to prevent process oscillations in pressure control loops where the dynamics are slow and a "dead zone" would cause hunting. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Observation:** In the previous Example #1, the toggle point was a **hard switch** (both valves at 0% at the toggle). In this Example #2, the toggle is **gradual** (overlap region where both valves are partially open). KTU problems frequently test the student's ability to recognize which configuration is being asked. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Split Range Control The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the signal flow from the process variable sensor through the PID controller, the signal splitter/characterizer block, and the two final control elements with their respective process inlets. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[PV Sensor
Transmitter 4 to 20 mA] --> B[PID Controller
Single Output] B --> C[Signal Splitter and Characterizer
Function Block] C --> D1[Valve 1
Cooling Water Line
Air to Open AO] C --> D2[Valve 2
Steam Line
Air to Close AC] D1 --> E1[Jacket Cooling
Process Side] D2 --> E2[Jacket Heating
Process Side] E1 --> F[Reactor Vessel
Process Variable PV] E2 --> F F --> A ``` ### 4.2 Signal Mapping Chart (Graphical Representation) The following Mermaid graph visualizes how the **single 4–20 mA controller output** is divided between the two valves. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Controller Output mA] I1[4 mA] I2[8 mA] I3[12 mA] I4[16 mA] I5[20 mA] end subgraph VALVEA[Valve A Cooling Water] A1[Open 100 percent] A2[Open 50 percent] A3[Closed 0 percent] A4[Closed 0 percent] A5[Closed 0 percent] end subgraph VALVEB[Valve B Steam] B1[Closed 0 percent] B2[Closed 0 percent] B3[Closed 0 percent] B4[Open 50 percent] B5[Open 100 percent] end I1 --> A1 I2 --> A2 I3 --> A3 I4 --> A4 I5 --> A5 I1 --> B1 I2 --> B2 I3 --> B3 I4 --> B4 I5 --> B5 ``` ### 4.3 P&ID-Style Architecture (Logical Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart TD TT[TT Temperature Transmitter] --> PID[TRC Temperature Recording Controller] PID --> SP[SPLIT Signal Splitter Relay] SP --> TY1[TY 1 Cooling Water Valve Positioner] SP --> TY2[TY 2 Steam Valve Positioner] TY1 --> FCV1[FCV 1 Cooling Water Control Valve] TY2 --> FCV2[FCV 2 Steam Control Valve] FCV1 --> JK[Jacket of Reactor] FCV2 --> JK JK --> PR[Process Reactor Body] PR --> TT ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the P&ID Tags:** The prefix **T** denotes *Temperature*, **C** denotes *Controller*, **Y** denotes *Relay/Positioner*, **FCV** denotes *Flow Control Valve*, **SP** denotes *Set Point*, and **SPLIT** is a non-standard but widely understood tag for the splitter block. In ISA-5.1 compliant drawings, this block may also be labeled **FY** (Function Relay) or **SH** (Signal Handler). ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam — Model Question Paper Style]** **Q1. Define split range control and list any two industrial applications. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** Split range control is an advanced control strategy in which a single controller output signal (e.g., 4–20 mA) is divided into two or more sub-ranges, with each sub-range used to operate a separate final control element. The toggle point is the controller output value at which one valve begins to open as the other begins to close. **Applications:** 1. Temperature control of a jacketed chemical reactor (steam + cooling water). 2. pH control in a neutralization tank (acid pump + caustic pump). 3. *(any other valid example — pressure control, level control, anti-surge)* --- **Q2. State the significance of the toggle point in split range control. (3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The toggle point is the specific value of the controller output (commonly 50% or 12 mA / 9 psi) at which one final control element reaches its minimum (fully closed) position as the other final control element reaches its minimum (fully closed) position. At this exact value, the process is at equilibrium with respect to the controller, and **neither valve is passing flow** (in a non-overlap configuration). The toggle point is field-tunable and can be shifted left or right to bias the controller toward heating or cooling dominance, allowing the engineer to compensate for asymmetric process dynamics (e.g., faster heating than cooling). --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / June 2024 Model Pattern]** --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** **(a)** Explain the concept of split range control with the help of a neat block diagram. Discuss its application in the temperature control of a jacketed CSTR. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** Split range control (SRC) is a control configuration in which a single process controller output is mapped to operate two or more final control elements, each active over a different segment of the output range. This is essential when the process requires **two opposing corrective actions** that cannot be achieved by a single actuator. **Block Diagram Description:** 1. The **temperature transmitter (TT)** senses the reactor temperature and sends a **4–20 mA** signal proportional to the PV. 2. The **temperature controller (TRC)** compares the PV to the setpoint (SP) and generates an output in the range **4–20 mA** (or 0–100% normalized). 3. The **signal splitter block (SPLIT)** mathematically divides this single output into two independent signals routed to two control valves. 4. **Valve 1 (Cooling Water)** receives the lower half of the controller output (e.g., 4–12 mA) and is **fully open at 4 mA, fully closed at 12 mA**. 5. **Valve 2 (Steam)** receives the upper half (e.g., 12–20 mA) and is **fully closed at 4 mA, fully open at 20 mA**. **Application — Jacketed CSTR:** A jacketed Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor is used for exothermic or endothermic reactions. The jacket can be fed with either **steam** (for heating) or **cooling water** (for cooling) depending on the reaction heat duty. A single temperature controller handles both — the same signal is split so that when the reactor temperature rises above the setpoint, the controller output increases, **closing the cooling water valve and opening the steam valve** (or vice versa, depending on fail-safe design). This eliminates the need for two separate controllers and prevents interaction between heating and cooling loops. **Key Benefits:** - Single controller reduces hardware cost. - Smooth transition between heating and cooling modes. - Avoids process upset during mode switching. --- **(b)** A controller output of **9–15 psi** is split between two valves as follows: Valve A is fully closed at 9 psi and fully open at 12 psi; Valve B is fully closed at 12 psi and fully open at 15 psi. Calculate the opening of both valves when the controller output is **10.5 psi** and **13.5 psi**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Identify the active sub-ranges.** - Valve A active range: 9 psi (closed) to 12 psi (open) → span = 3 psi - Valve B active range: 12 psi (closed) to 15 psi (open) → span = 3 psi - Toggle point: 12 psi **Step 2: Derive the linear equations.** For Valve A (direct action, opening as $P$ increases from 9 to 12): $$V_A(\%) = \frac{P - 9}{12 - 9} \times 100 = \frac{P - 9}{3} \times 100$$ $$V_A(\%) = \frac{100}{3}(P - 9) = 33.33(P - 9)$$ For Valve B (direct action, opening as $P$ increases from 12 to 15): $$V_B(\%) = \frac{P - 12}{15 - 12} \times 100 = \frac{P - 12}{3} \times 100 = 33.33(P - 12)$$ **Step 3: Evaluate at $P = 10.5$ psi.** $$V_A(\%) = 33.33 \times (10.5 - 9) = 33.33 \times 1.5 = \mathbf{50\%}$$ $$V_B(\%) = 33.33 \times (10.5 - 12) = 33.33 \times (-1.5) = -50\% \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Clamped to } \mathbf{0\%}$$ **Result at 10.5 psi:** $V_A = 50\%$, $V_B = 0\%$. The controller is in the **cooling/heating transitional region** (depending on valve A's function). **Step 4: Evaluate at $P = 13.5$ psi.** $$V_A(\%) = 33.33 \times (13.5 - 9) = 33.33 \times 4.5 = 150\% \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Clamped to } \mathbf{100\%}$$ $$V_B(\%) = 33.33 \times (13.5 - 12) = 33.33 \times 1.5 = \mathbf{50\%}$$ **Result at 13.5 psi:** $V_A = 100\%$, $V_B = 50\%$. The controller is in the **upper region**. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative to Question A)** **(a)** With the aid of a suitable block diagram, explain split range control as applied to a **compressor anti-surge system**. How does SRC improve surge protection? **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Compressor Surge Phenomenon:** Surge is a destructive low-flow instability in dynamic compressors (centrifugal, axial) where the compressor cannot sustain the discharge pressure, causing backflow, vibration, and mechanical damage. Surge protection requires **recycling** excess discharge gas back to the suction side via an **anti-surge valve**. **SRC Application:** A surge controller monitors the compressor's differential pressure ($\Delta P$) and flow ($Q$). The controller output is split between: - **Anti-surge recycle valve (ASV):** Air-to-Open (AO), opens as flow decreases (low output region). - **Discharge vent valve (DVV):** Air-to-Open (AO), opens as pressure increases (high output region). The split configuration ensures that: - When the compressor operates at **high flow, normal pressure** → controller output is around 50% → **both valves are at minimum** (no recycle, no venting). - When **flow drops toward surge** → controller output drops below 50% → **ASV opens progressively to recycle gas**, preventing surge. - When **discharge pressure rises dangerously** → controller output rises above 50% → **DVV opens to vent excess gas**, protecting downstream equipment. **Advantages over Single-Valve Control:** - The single controller handles both surge (low flow) and overpressure (high pressure) scenarios. - Faster response because the controller output is always active in one direction. - Avoids the "no action" dead zone inherent in single-valve control. --- **(b)** A controller output of **4–20 mA** drives a cooling water valve (fully open at 4 mA, fully closed at 12 mA) and a brine valve (fully closed at 12 mA, fully open at 20 mA). Determine the stem position of each valve when the output is **6 mA**, **12 mA**, and **18 mA**. Also state the physical significance of the 12 mA value. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Identify ranges and derive equations.** Cooling water valve (AO, fully open at 4 mA, fully closed at 12 mA): $$V_{cw}(\%) = \frac{12 - C}{12 - 4} \times 100 = \frac{12 - C}{8} \times 100 = 12.5(12 - C)$$ Brine valve (AO, fully closed at 12 mA, fully open at 20 mA): $$V_{br}(\%) = \frac{C - 12}{20 - 12} \times 100 = \frac{C - 12}{8} \times 100 = 12.5(C - 12)$$ **Step 2: Evaluate at the three output values.** **At $C = 6$ mA:** $$V_{cw}(\%) = 12.5 \times (12 - 6) = 12.5 \times 6 = \mathbf{75\%}$$ $$V_{br}(\%) = 12.5 \times (6 - 12) = 12.5 \times (-6) = -75\% \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Clamped to } \mathbf{0\%}$$ **At $C = 12$ mA (Toggle Point):** $$V_{cw}(\%) = 12.5 \times (12 - 12) = \mathbf{0\%}$$ $$V_{br}(\%) = 12.5 \times (12 - 12) = \mathbf{0\%}$$ **At $C = 18$ mA:** $$V_{cw}(\%) = 12.5 \times (12 - 18) = -75\% \;\Rightarrow\; \text{Clamped to } \mathbf{0\%}$$ $$V_{br}(\%) = 12.5 \times (18 - 12) = 12.5 \times 6 = \mathbf{75\%}$$ **Step 3: Tabulate the results.** | Controller Output | Cooling Water Valve $V_{cw}$ | Brine Valve $V_{br}$ | Process State | |---|---|---|---| | 6 mA | 75% | 0% | Active cooling | | 12 mA | 0% | 0% | Equilibrium (setpoint) | | 18 mA | 0% | 75% | Active heating (brine) | **Physical Significance of 12 mA:** The 12 mA value is the **toggle point** (or splitting point) — it is the controller output at which both valves are fully closed simultaneously. Physically, this represents the **equilibrium state** of the process, where the process variable is exactly at the setpoint and no corrective action (heating or cooling) is required. Any deviation from 12 mA indicates that the process has drifted and one of the two valves must open to restore the setpoint. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting to clamp negative values to 0%** — A common mistake is reporting a negative valve opening. The valve cannot be "negatively open"; it must be clamped to 0%. > 2. **Using the wrong span in the interpolation formula** — Students often use the full 4–20 mA span (= 16 mA) instead of the **split sub-range span** (= 8 mA). This leads to a 50% error in computed valve positions. > 3. **Not specifying AO/AC nature** — In a 7-mark question, the AO/AC specification is worth at least 1 mark. Always declare it. > 4. **Confusing 4–20 mA with 3–15 psi** — These are two different standards. Read the question carefully. > 5. **Drawing a block diagram without arrows** — The signal flow arrows (sensor → controller → splitter → valves) carry 2–3 marks in the diagram. > 6. **Forgetting units in the final answer** — Always state "75%" not just "75". --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **One-Page Last-Minute Revision for Split Range Control** - [ ] **Definition:** Single controller output split to operate **two or more** final control elements over **different sub-ranges**. - [ ] **Standard Signals:** **4–20 mA** (electronic), **3–15 psi** (pneumatic), **0-100%** (DCS normalized). - [ ] **Toggle Point:** The controller output value at which the action **transfers** from one valve to the other. **Default = 50%** (12 mA / 9 psi), but field-tunable. - [ ] **Sub-Range Span:** Each valve operates over only **part** of the full signal range. The split is typically **50/50** but can be asymmetric (e.g., 30/70) to compensate for unequal process gains. - [ ] **Overlap Zone:** A small region (typically 2–5%) around the toggle point where **both valves are partially open** to prevent hunting and ensure smooth transition. - [ ] **Hard Toggle:** A configuration with **no overlap** — both valves are at 0% exactly at the toggle point. - [ ] **AO vs AC:** **Air-to-Open (fail-closed)** and **Air-to-Close (fail-open)** valves are commonly mixed in SRC to provide **fail-safe** protection for both heating and cooling modes. - [ ] **Linear Interpolation Formula:** $V(\%) = \dfrac{C - C_{\min}^{valve}}{C_{\max}^{valve} - C_{\min}^{valve}} \times 100$ — use the **valve-specific** $C_{\min}$ and $C_{\max}$, **not** the full signal range. - [ ] **Conservation Check:** In non-overlap SRC, $V_A + V_B \leq 100\%$ at all times. In overlap SRC, $V_A + V_B \geq 100\%$ in the overlap zone. - [ ] **Key Applications:** Jacketed reactor temperature, pH neutralization, boiler drum level, compressor anti-surge, HVAC staging, furnace pressure control. - [ ] **Signal Characterizer:** A nonlinear function generator (square root, equal percentage) may be inserted in one or both branches to compensate for valve nonlinearity. - [ ] **Impossible Scenario:** A single controller output **cannot** simultaneously command two **opposing** actuators (e.g., heating **and** cooling at full power) — the total manipulated energy is always conserved. - [ ] **Real-World Tip:** In DCS (Distributed Control Systems), split range is implemented using **SPLIT** or **CHAR** function blocks; in legacy pneumatic systems, it uses **relays** with adjustable set and reset springs. - [ ] **Exam Trap:** A controller output of exactly **12 mA** does NOT always mean both valves are at 0% — only in the **hard toggle** (50/50, no overlap) configuration. In an overlap or asymmetric split, the toggle point may be at a different output value. - [ ] **Stability Consideration:** SRC introduces a **gain discontinuity** at the toggle point. If the process gain on the heating side differs significantly from the cooling side, the loop may oscillate as it crosses the toggle. The remedy is **gain-scheduled** tuning or an **equal-percentage characterizer**. > [!TIP] > **Final Mnemonic:** "**S-P-L-I-T**" — **S**ingle controller, **P**artitioned output, **L**inked to **I**ndependent valves, **T**oggled at a field-settable point.

topics.title: Computerized Control: Basic Building Blocks of Computer Controlled Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT633 - SUPERVISORY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Computerized Control: Basic Building Blocks of Computer Controlled Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Computer-Controlled System (CCS)**, also known as a **Computerized Process Control System**, is an integrated hardware-software architecture in which a digital computer (microprocessor, PLC, DCS controller, or industrial PC) is employed as the central decision-making and computational element to monitor, regulate, and optimize the operation of a physical process or plant in real time. According to the KTU 2024 scheme, the "basic building blocks" constitute the irreducible functional stages through which real-world analog signals are acquired, conditioned, discretized, processed digitally, and converted back into analog actuating signals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** A computer-controlled system is one in which a digital computer replaces the conventional analog controller (PID, lead-lag, etc.) and implements the control algorithm through software, performing sequential operations of *sampling → quantization → encoding → computation → control action output*.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Smart Cruise Control" View

Imagine you are driving a car on a highway. The *traditional analog controller* is like your **right foot** modulating the accelerator purely by feel — it has a continuous, instinctive response. A **computerized control system** is like a **modern adaptive cruise control**:
- The **radar/ultrasonic sensor** (input transducer) "sees" the distance to the car ahead.
- The **signal conditioner** cleans the noisy radar echo.
- The **A/D converter** turns the analog distance into a digital number (e.g., 27.4 m).
- The **onboard computer (ECU)** runs an algorithm: "if distance < 30 m, reduce throttle by 12%."
- The **D/A converter and throttle actuator** apply the corrective action.
- The **display/HMI** supervises the whole process.

In short, the computer is the **"digital brain"** sitting between the **"nervous system" (sensors)** and the **"muscles" (actuators)** of the plant.

### 1.3 Classification of Computer-Controlled Systems

| Class | Abbreviation | Description | KTU Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Direct Digital Control | **DDC** | Computer directly computes the control signal for each loop. | HVAC, Boiler control |
| Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition | **SCADA** | Computer supervises multiple local controllers (PLCs/RTUs). | Power grids, Water treatment |
| Distributed Control System | **DCS** | Geographically distributed controllers with centralized HMI. | Refineries, Chemical plants |
| Set-Point Control (SPC) | — | Computer only generates setpoints; analog/PID controllers do the regulating. | Legacy process plants |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** In Module 1, the emphasis is on identifying the *building blocks* — they are *universal* and appear in **all** the above classes. The only thing that changes is *where* the computer sits in the hierarchy.

### 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Board Favourite)

The following constants and metrics are frequently tested:

- **Sampling period $T_s$** — typically **0.1 s to 1 s** in process control.
- **Nyquist–Shannon sampling frequency:** $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ where $f_{max}$ is the highest signal frequency in **Hz**.
- **Quantization step (LSB):** $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n}$ where $n$ = number of bits, $V_{FS}$ = full-scale voltage (usually **$\pm 10$ V** or **4–20 mA** industrial standard).
- **Computer word length:** typically **8, 12, 16, or 32 bits**.
- **Standard analog transmission signals:** **4–20 mA current loop**, **0–10 V**, **1–5 V** (KTU frequently asks these values).
- **Throughput / Scan time:** Time taken by the computer to service one complete input–output cycle.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Functional Stages of a Computer-Controlled System

A computer-controlled system performs the following sequence of operations, regardless of the application:

1. **Acquisition (Input Stage)**
   - **Sensors / Transducers** convert physical variables (temperature, pressure, flow, level) into electrical signals (mV, mA, V).
   - Examples: RTD/thermocouple (temperature), strain gauge (pressure), orifice plate + dPT (flow), ultrasonic/conductivity (level).

2. **Conditioning (Signal Conditioning Stage)**
   - **Amplification** — raises low-level signals (e.g., thermocouple ~mV) to standard ranges.
   - **Filtering** — removes high-frequency noise (anti-aliasing filter is *critical*).
   - **Linearization** — corrects non-linear sensor characteristics.
   - **Cold-junction compensation** (for thermocouples).
   - **Isolation** — protects the computer from ground loops and transients.

3. **Multiplexing and Conversion (I/O Interface Stage)**
   - **Multiplexer (MUX)** — shares one A/D converter across many channels (time-division).
   - **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** — freezes the analog value during A/D conversion.
   - **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** — discretizes amplitude.
   - **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** — reconstructs analog control signal.
   - **Digital I/O ports** — handle ON/OFF signals, relays, contactors.

4. **Computation (Digital Computer / CPU Stage)**
   - **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** — executes the control algorithm.
   - **Memory** — RAM (volatile, for data), ROM/EPROM (non-volatile, for program), bulk storage.
   - **I/O bus / Address decoder** — routes data to peripherals.
   - **Real-time clock** — synchronizes sampling.
   - **Software** — algorithm in C, ladder logic, function blocks, etc.

5. **Actuation and Feedback (Output Stage)**
   - **Output transducer / Actuator** — converts the computer's decision into a physical action.
   - Examples: Control valve (pneumatic/hydraulic/electric), variable-frequency drive (VFD), SCR/thyristor stack, relay coil, stepper/servo motor.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Block

- **Why Multiplex?** A single ADC is expensive; sharing it across N channels reduces hardware cost and complexity.
- **Why Sample-and-Hold?** A/D conversion takes finite time; S/H prevents the signal from changing during the conversion window — guarantees amplitude accuracy.
- **Why Anti-Aliasing Filter?** Any analog signal must be **band-limited to $f_c < f_s / 2$** before sampling, otherwise *aliasing* corrupts the data. The filter is a *low-pass* with cut-off $f_c \approx f_s / 2.5$ (slight safety margin).
- **Why Real-Time OS?** Control decisions must be completed *before* the next sample arrives. A delay longer than $T_s$ causes **sampling jitter** or **missed deadlines**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **Use $\vert$ (LaTeX) instead of $\|$ inside table cells to avoid markdown breakage.**

| # | Quantity | Formula / Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sampling period | $T_s$ | **seconds (s)** | Sets the loop cycle time. |
| 2 | Sampling frequency | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T_s}$ | **Hz** | Determines the dynamic range of signals handled. |
| 3 | Nyquist criterion | $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ | **Hz** | Minimum rate to avoid aliasing. |
| 4 | Quantization step (LSB) | $\Delta = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}$ | **Volts/bit** | Resolution of the A/D converter. |
| 5 | Quantization error (max) | $e_q \le \dfrac{\Delta}{2}$ | **Volts** | Worst-case digitisation noise. |
| 6 | Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio | $\text{SQNR} \approx 6.02\,n + 1.76$ | **dB** | Quality measure of an $n$-bit ADC. |
| 7 | Anti-aliasing filter cut-off | $f_c \le \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | **Hz** | Prevent spectral overlap. |
| 8 | Throughput / scan time | $t_{scan} = N \cdot T_{conv}$ | **s** | Total time to scan $N$ channels. |
| 9 | Control signal | $u(k) = K_p e(k) + K_i \sum e(j) + K_d [e(k) - e(k-1)]$ | **% / Engineering units** | Discrete PID algorithm used in computer control. |
| 10 | Error | $e(k) = r(k) - y(k)$ | **EU** | Difference between setpoint and measured output. |
| 11 | Industrial current loop | $4 \text{ mA} \Leftrightarrow 0\%$, $20 \text{ mA} \Leftrightarrow 100\%$ | **mA** | Noise-immune, 2-wire transmission. |
| 12 | Industrial voltage standard | $0$–$10$ V / $1$–$5$ V | **Volts** | Panel-level signal. |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas Refineries (DCS):** Thousands of loops controlled simultaneously; identical building blocks repeated for each loop.
- **Power Generation (SCADA + DCS):** Boiler drum level, steam temperature, turbine speed — all rely on these blocks.
- **Automotive ECU:** Mass-production application of computerized control — 50–100 sensors per car, 1 ms loop time.
- **Smart Grids & Renewable Energy:** Wind turbine pitch control, solar MPPT — pure computer-controlled feedback systems.
- **Pharmaceutical Batch Reactors:** KTU-relevant application; computerized recipe control with strict regulatory compliance.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Sampling Frequency from the Nyquist Criterion

The Shannon–Nyquist sampling theorem states that a band-limited analog signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_{max}$ can be **perfectly reconstructed** from its samples $x(kT_s)$ if and only if the sampling frequency is at least twice the highest frequency present.

**Step 1 — Define the highest signal frequency component.**
Let the analog measurement signal $y(t)$ have a spectral content bounded by $f_{max}$ in Hz.

**Step 2 — Define the sampling period.**
The digital computer reads the input once every $T_s$ seconds, giving samples $y(kT_s)$ for $k = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, N$.

**Step 3 — Define the sampling frequency.**
By definition, the sampling frequency is the reciprocal of the period:

$$f_s = \frac{1}{T_s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the Nyquist inequality.**

$$f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the period.** Inverting the inequality:

$$T_s \le \frac{1}{2 f_{max}}$$

**Step 6 — Practical Engineering Rule (with safety margin).** To allow for imperfect anti-aliasing filters, industrial practice uses:

$$T_s = \frac{1}{(4 \text{ to } 10)\, f_{max}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Tip:** A common 3-mark question is *"Why is the sampling frequency chosen to be much higher than $2 f_{max}$?"* — the answer is the **practical anti-aliasing filter roll-off** and **loop stability margin**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Quantization Step (LSB) of an A/D Converter

**Step 1 — Define full-scale voltage range.**
The A/D converter can represent analog signals between $-V_{FS}/2$ and $+V_{FS}/2$. Total span is $V_{FS}$.

**Step 2 — Define the bit depth.**
An $n$-bit A/D produces $2^n$ discrete levels.

**Step 3 — Compute the size of one level (LSB).**
The smallest distinguishable voltage step is:

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute maximum quantization error.**
The actual analog value lies somewhere inside a single LSB-sized interval centred on the digital output, so the maximum error is half an LSB:

$$\vert e_q \vert_{max} = \frac{\Delta}{2} = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n+1}}$$

**Step 5 — Derive the SQNR.**
For a full-scale sinusoidal input, the signal-to-quantization-noise ratio in dB is the celebrated textbook result:

$$\text{SQNR} \approx 6.02\,n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$$

*Every additional bit of resolution improves SQNR by ~6 dB — this is the "6 dB per bit" rule.*

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Discrete-Time PID Control Law (Algorithmic Core of a Computer Controller)

The continuous-time PID controller is:

$$u(t) = K_p\, e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\, dt + K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$$

Discretise each term with the **forward-rectangle, backward-rectangle, and backward-difference** approximations respectively:

$$u(k) = K_p\, e(k) + K_i\, T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + K_d \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T_s}$$

**Positional (absolute) form** — computed at every sample $k$:

$$u(k) = K_p\, e(k) + K_i\, T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\,[e(k) - e(k-1)]$$

**Incremental (velocity) form** — preferred in KTU for computer implementation because it avoids integral windup and large accumulators:

$$\Delta u(k) = u(k) - u(k-1)$$

$$\Delta u(k) = K_p [e(k) - e(k-1)] + K_i\, T_s\, e(k) + \frac{K_d}{T_s}[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)]$$

This is the **discrete control law** computed by the CPU at every sampling instant — the actual "computation" step of the computer-controlled system.

> [!NOTE]
> The complete worked derivation of $\Delta u(k)$ from $u(k)$ is given below.

---

### 3.4 Worked Step-by-Step Derivation: From $u(k)$ to $\Delta u(k)$

**Step 1 — Write the positional form at sample $k$:**

$$u(k) = K_p\, e(k) + K_i T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{K_d}{T_s}[e(k) - e(k-1)]$$

**Step 2 — Write the positional form at sample $k-1$:**

$$u(k-1) = K_p\, e(k-1) + K_i T_s \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} e(j) + \frac{K_d}{T_s}[e(k-1) - e(k-2)]$$

**Step 3 — Subtract: $\Delta u(k) = u(k) - u(k-1)$.**
The summation telescopes: $\sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) - \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} e(j) = e(k)$.

**Step 4 — Collect terms:**

$$\Delta u(k) = K_p [e(k) - e(k-1)] + K_i T_s\, e(k) + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\,[e(k) - e(k-1) - e(k-1) + e(k-2)]$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the derivative term:**

$$\Delta u(k) = K_p [e(k) - e(k-1)] + K_i T_s\, e(k) + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\,[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)]$$

This is the **velocity-form discrete PID** — the canonical output of the digital computer block.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — The Building Blocks vs. Their Functions

| Building Block | Functional Role | Failure Mode if Removed | KTU Board-Mentioned Industrial Part |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor / Transducer | Converts process variable → electrical signal | Computer receives zero / no control possible | RTD (Pt-100), Thermocouple (K-type), dPT |
| Signal Conditioner | Amplify, filter, linearize, isolate | Noisy / low-amplitude signal → garbage in / garbage out | Universal input transmitter |
| Multiplexer (MUX) | Shares one ADC across $N$ channels | Need $N$ ADCs (cost ↑↑ 5×) | CD4051, 74HC4051 |
| Sample-and-Hold (S/H) | Freezes analog value during conversion | Conversion error, ringing artefacts | LF398, AD585 |
| A/D Converter | Analog → digital conversion | Computer cannot "see" the process | ADS1115 (16-bit), AD7606 (16-bit, 8-ch) |
| Digital Computer (CPU + Memory) | Runs the control algorithm | No decision-making possible; plant uncontrolled | PLC, DCS controller, Industrial PC |
| D/A Converter | Digital → analog control signal | Computer decision never reaches actuator | DAC0808, AD5752 |
| Output Actuator | Final control element | Computed signal remains unexecuted | Pneumatic valve, VFD, Contactor |
| HMI / Display | Operator supervision | Operator is blind; troubleshooting impossible | SCADA screen, Touch panel |
| Communication Bus | Multi-controller data exchange | No distributed control | Modbus, Profibus, Foundation Fieldbus, Ethernet/IP |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Block Diagram of a Computer-Controlled System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Plant] --> B[Sensor and Transducer]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioner - Amplify Filter Isolate]
    C --> D[Analog Multiplexer MUX]
    D --> E[Sample and Hold S and H]
    E --> F[Analog to Digital Converter ADC]
    F --> G[Digital Computer CPU Memory Software]
    G --> H[Digital to Analog Converter DAC]
    H --> I[Output Transducer and Actuator]
    I --> A
    G <--> J[HMI Operator Display and Keyboard]
    G <--> K[Communication Network SCADA Bus]
    K --> L[Other Controllers and Remote IOs]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Information flows **clockwise** around the loop. The digital computer is the *only* intelligent block. The dashed return from `Process Plant` to `A` and back through `B` represents the **closed-loop feedback path**. The bus `K` extends the system into a SCADA / DCS hierarchy.

### 4.2 Internal Architecture of the Digital Computer Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU[Central Processing Unit CPU] --> REG[Registers and ALU]
    CPU --> CTRL[Control Unit]
    CPU --> CLK[Real Time Clock and Timer]
    MEM[Memory Unit] --> ROM[ROM EPROM - Program]
    MEM --> RAM[RAM - Data and Variables]
    MEM --> MASS[Bulk Storage SSD HDD]
    IO[I O Interface] --> ADC_IF[ADC Interface]
    IO --> DAC_IF[DAC Interface]
    IO --> DIO[Digital I O Lines]
    IO --> COMM[Serial and Ethernet Ports]
    BUS[Address and Data Bus] --> CPU
    BUS --> MEM
    BUS --> IO
```

### 4.3 Signal Path — Analog World vs. Digital World

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ANALOG[Analog Domain]
        PA[Process Variable e.g. Temperature in degC]
    end
    subgraph CONV1[Input Boundary]
        TR[Transducer]
        SC[Signal Conditioner]
        AH[Anti Aliasing Filter]
    end
    subgraph DIGITAL[Digital Domain]
        SHS[Sample and Hold]
        AD[ADC]
        COMP[CPU - Algorithm]
        DA[DAC]
        ZOH[Zero Order Hold]
    end
    subgraph ACT[Output Boundary]
        ACT2[Actuator]
    end
    PA --> TR --> SC --> AH --> SHS --> AD --> COMP --> DA --> ZOH --> ACT2
    ACT2 -. feedback .- PA
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Note:** The **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** is the standard model for the DAC's behaviour — it *holds* the last digital value constant over the full sampling period $T_s$ until the next update arrives. This is what the examiners expect when asked "what does the DAC do between samples?".

### 4.4 Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop Computer Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OPEN[Open Loop Computer Control]
        R1[Setpoint r] --> C1[Computer] --> A1[Actuator] --> P1[Plant]
        P1 -. no feedback .- C1
    end
    subgraph CLOSED[Closed Loop Computer Control]
        R2[Setpoint r] --> C2[Computer] --> A2[Actuator] --> P2[Plant]
        S2[Sensor] --> C2
        P2 --> S2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical insight:** Most industrial computer-controlled systems are **closed-loop**. The computer subtracts the measured value from the setpoint to compute the error $e(k) = r(k) - y(k)$, then applies the PID law derived in §3.3.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**List the basic building blocks of a computer-controlled system. State the function of any four of them in two lines each.**

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**
The basic building blocks are: (i) Sensor / Transducer, (ii) Signal Conditioner, (iii) Multiplexer, (iv) Sample-and-Hold, (v) A/D Converter, (vi) Digital Computer (CPU + Memory), (vii) D/A Converter, (viii) Actuator, (ix) HMI, (x) Communication Network.

- **Sensor / Transducer:** Converts physical variables (temperature, pressure, flow) into proportional electrical signals (mV, mA).
- **Signal Conditioner:** Amplifies, filters, linearises and isolates the raw transducer signal to a standard range.
- **A/D Converter:** Samples and quantises the analog signal into a binary number that the computer can process.
- **Actuator:** Converts the computer's digital output into a physical action (valve position, motor speed).

#### Question 2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Explain the role of an anti-aliasing filter in a computer-controlled system.**

**Model Answer (≈3 marks):**
An anti-aliasing filter is a **low-pass filter** placed immediately before the A/D converter. Its function is to band-limit the analog measurement signal so that no frequency component above $f_s/2$ (the Nyquist frequency) is presented to the sampler. If this filter is omitted, **aliasing** occurs — high-frequency noise folds back into the lower spectrum and corrupts the digital readings, leading to incorrect control decisions. The cut-off frequency is typically chosen as $f_c \le f_s/2$, with a practical margin of $f_s/2.5$.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

#### Question A. [14 Marks] [KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** Draw the block diagram of a computer-controlled process and explain the function of each block.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat sketch, explain the internal architecture of the digital computer block. Discuss the role of RAM, ROM and the real-time clock.  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Block Diagram and Function of Each Block (7 marks):**

Refer to the master block diagram in §4.1 of these notes. The functions are:

1. **Sensor/Transducer** (1 mark): Converts the process variable (PV) — temperature, pressure, flow, level, etc. — into a proportional electrical signal. Example: Pt-100 RTD for temperature, differential pressure transmitter for flow.

2. **Signal Conditioner** (1 mark): Performs amplification, filtering, linearisation, cold-junction compensation and isolation. Brings the low-level (mV) transducer output to industrial-standard levels (4–20 mA or 0–10 V).

3. **Multiplexer + Sample-and-Hold + ADC** (1 mark): Time-shares the single A/D converter across multiple input channels, freezes each signal during conversion, and quantises the analog amplitude into binary code ($n$ bits, $2^n$ levels).

4. **Digital Computer (CPU + Memory + I/O Bus)** (1 mark): Executes the control algorithm — typically a discrete PID — at every sampling instant $T_s$. Memory stores the program (ROM) and live data (RAM). The CPU computes the error $e(k) = r(k) - y(k)$ and the new control output $u(k)$.

5. **D/A Converter + Zero-Order Hold** (1 mark): Converts the digital control word back into an analog voltage/current. The ZOH holds the value constant between samples.

6. **Actuator** (1 mark): Final control element — pneumatic control valve, VFD, thyristor power controller, etc. — modulates the plant input to drive the process toward the setpoint.

7. **HMI / Communication Bus** (1 mark): Provides operator visibility (trend, alarm, setpoint entry) and inter-controller data exchange in SCADA/DCS architectures.

**(b) Internal Architecture of the Digital Computer (7 marks):**

Refer to the architecture diagram in §4.2. Explanation:

- **CPU (1.5 marks):** The arithmetic-logic unit (ALU) executes arithmetic, the control unit decodes instructions, and registers provide fast scratch-pad memory. The CPU runs the *control algorithm* in real time.
- **RAM (1.5 marks):** *Random Access Memory* is volatile (data lost on power-off). It holds live process variables, current setpoints, alarm flags, intermediate PID sums ($\sum e(j)$) and stack/heap for the running program.
- **ROM / EPROM / Flash (1.5 marks):** *Read-Only Memory* is non-volatile. It permanently stores the compiled control program, the boot loader and fixed calibration constants. Power failure does NOT erase it.
- **Real-Time Clock / Timer (1 mark):** Generates precise interrupts at interval $T_s$ to trigger sampling. Critical because all control computations must complete *within* one period.
- **Address and Data Bus (1 mark):** Parallel highways that carry memory addresses (read/write selection) and data (values). Decoders route bus traffic to ADC, DAC, DIO and communication ports.
- **Communication Ports (0.5 mark):** RS-485 / Modbus / Ethernet / Profibus for SCADA-level data exchange.

---

#### Question B. [14 Marks — Alternative Choice] [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain the concept of quantization in A/D conversion. Derive the expression for the LSB (quantization step) of an $n$-bit converter. If $V_{FS} = 10$ V and $n = 12$, compute the LSB.  **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the velocity-form discrete PID control law used in a computer-controlled system. List two advantages of the velocity form over the positional form.  **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Quantization and LSB Derivation (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** Quantization is the process of mapping a continuous-amplitude analog value into one of $2^n$ discrete binary codes, where $n$ is the number of bits of the A/D converter. Each code corresponds to a fixed voltage interval of width $\Delta$, called the **Least Significant Bit (LSB)** or **quantization step**.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

$$\Delta = \frac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}$$

This comes directly from the definition: an $n$-bit A/D produces $2^n$ levels spanning the full-scale range $V_{FS}$ (unipolar) or $\pm V_{FS}/2$ (bipolar).

**Numerical Computation (2 marks):**
Given $V_{FS} = 10$ V, $n = 12$:

$$\Delta = \frac{10 \text{ V}}{2^{12}} = \frac{10}{4096} = 2.4414 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 2.44 \text{ mV}$$

**Maximum quantization error:** $\vert e_q \vert_{max} = \Delta/2 = 1.22$ mV.

---

**(b) Velocity-Form Discrete PID (7 marks):**

**Derivation (5 marks):** The complete derivation is given in §3.4 of these notes. Final form:

$$\Delta u(k) = K_p [e(k) - e(k-1)] + K_i T_s\, e(k) + \frac{K_d}{T_s}\,[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)]$$

The actual valve command is then updated by:

$$u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u(k)$$

**Advantages of Velocity Form (2 marks — any two):**

1. **Bumpless transfer:** When the computer is switched from MANUAL to AUTO mode (or the algorithm is restarted), the velocity form only outputs *changes*, so there is no sudden jump in the valve position.
2. **No integral wind-up accumulation:** The summation $\sum e(j)$ is eliminated — the integral action is implicit in the running sum, which is naturally bounded by the actuator limits.
3. **Safer failure mode:** If the computer fails, the last computed $\Delta u$ becomes zero and the valve stays at its previous position, rather than slamming to 0% or 100% (as can happen with positional form).
4. **Easier implementation on PLCs and DCS:** Only differences need to be stored; less memory and no large accumulator.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Before You Write!**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the "Zero-Order Hold"** when describing the D/A converter block — this is the most common 1-mark loss in the block-diagram question.
> 2. **Confusing the multiplexer with the sample-and-hold** — they are *separate* blocks. MUX selects the channel; S/H freezes the value. Listing only "MUX/S&H" as a single block costs a mark.
> 3. **Wrong LSB formula** — students often write $\Delta = V_{FS}/n$ (linear) instead of $V_{FS}/2^n$ (exponential/bit-depth). This is an instant zero for the derivation sub-question.
> 4. **No safety margin on $f_s$** — stating $f_s = 2 f_{max}$ is *theoretically* correct but **practically wrong**. Always mention the factor 4–10 safety margin and the anti-aliasing filter roll-off.
> 5. **Mixing up RAM and ROM** — RAM is *volatile* (data), ROM is *non-volatile* (program). The examiner checks this distinction.
> 6. **Positional vs. Velocity PID** — confusing the two forms in the derivation is the single most common 14-mark disaster. Memorise the *final* form of $\Delta u(k)$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **computer-controlled system** replaces the analog controller with a **digital computer** that implements the control algorithm in software.
- The **five universal stages** are: *Acquisition → Conditioning → Multiplexing & Conversion → Computation → Actuation* (plus HMI and Communication).
- The **sampling theorem**: $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$ (Nyquist–Shannon); industrial practice uses $f_s = (4 \text{ to } 10) f_{max}$ with an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** at $f_c \approx f_s/2.5$.
- The **LSB / quantization step** of an $n$-bit ADC over $V_{FS}$ is $\Delta = V_{FS}/2^n$; the maximum quantization error is $\Delta/2$.
- The **6 dB per bit rule** quantifies ADC quality: $\text{SQNR} \approx 6.02\,n + 1.76$ dB.
- The **D/A converter is modelled as a Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** — it holds the last value constant over $T_s$.
- The **discrete PID** comes in two forms: **positional** $u(k) = \ldots$ and **velocity** $\Delta u(k) = \ldots$ — the velocity form is preferred for computer implementation (no wind-up, bumpless transfer).
- **Industrial signal standards to memorise:** 4–20 mA (current loop), 0–10 V / 1–5 V (voltage), Pt-100 / K-type thermocouple (sensors), Modbus / Profibus / Foundation Fieldbus (communication).
- **RAM = volatile data, ROM = non-volatile program** — examiners love this distinction.
- A computer-controlled system may be **DDC** (direct), **Supervisory SCADA**, or **Distributed DCS** — the *building blocks* are the same in all three.
- **Real-time constraint:** Control computation must finish *before* the next sample arrives — use a real-time OS or hardware timer interrupt.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advantages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT633 - SUPERVISORY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Advantages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Supervisory Control** refers to a class of computer-based industrial automation systems that monitor, gather, and process real-time data from geographically distributed or localized field devices, enabling centralized operators to issue high-level supervisory commands (set-points, start/stop, mode changes) to remote or local control loops. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PERAT633**, Supervisory Control encompasses **SCADA (Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition)**, **DCS (Distributed Control Systems)**, and modern **HMI/SCADA hybrid architectures** that sit one level above direct digital control (DDC) in the automation hierarchy (ISA-95 / Purdue Reference Model Levels 2 & 3).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The term "Supervisory" implies the system does *not* perform low-level closed-loop control directly. Instead, it *supervises* field-level controllers (PLCs, RTUs, DCS controllers) and provides a unified human-machine interface (HMI) for plant-wide visibility and command.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the cockpit of a modern commercial airliner (Boeing 787 or Airbus A350). The pilots do not physically push the rudder or turn the engine fuel valves by hand. Instead, they:

- **Monitor** dozens of digital displays (altitude, speed, engine health).
- **Issue high-level commands** like "climb to 35,000 feet" or "engage autopilot."
- Lower-level systems (Flight Management Computer, autopilot, engine FADEC) execute the commands.

The pilots are performing **supervisory control**. A SCADA/DCS system is the "cockpit" of an industrial plant — the operator becomes the "pilot," and PLCs/RTUs become the "autopilot and actuators."

### 1.3 Standard Industrial Metrics & Thresholds

| Metric | Typical Industrial Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **MTBF** (Mean Time Between Failures) | **> 50,000 hours** | Indicates reliability of SCADA servers |
| **MTTR** (Mean Time To Repair) | **< 30 minutes** | Hot-standby redundancy target |
| **Response Time** (Operator command → Field action) | **0.5 – 2 seconds** | Real-time supervisory loop latency |
| **Data Refresh Rate** | **1 – 10 seconds** | HMI tag update interval |
| **Tag Capacity** | **10,000 – 250,000 tags** | Scalability benchmark |

> [!NOTE]
