> - Digital Logic Levels: **24 V DC** (industrial), **120/230 V AC** (discrete AC I/O)
> - Resolution: typically **12-bit, 14-bit, or 16-bit** for industrial ADCs
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of I/O Types

The **Types of Inputs and Outputs** in a supervisory control system can be broken down into a strict hierarchy:

#### A. Discrete (Digital) Inputs — DI
A **Discrete Input** reads a binary state. It answers the question: *"Is the switch ON or OFF?"*
- **Voltage-based DI:** The controller measures whether a voltage is present above a threshold (e.g., > 10 V DC = ON).
- **Current-based DI:** The controller detects current flow above a threshold.
- **AC DI:** Often uses an optocoupler to sense 120/230 V AC presence (e.g., motor contactor auxiliary contacts).
- **Contact-type DI:** Dry contact from a mechanical switch.

#### B. Discrete (Digital) Outputs — DO
A **Discrete Output** drives a binary actuator.
- **Relay Output (Form A/B/C contacts):** Electromechanical, handles high inrush currents, but slow (~10 ms).
- **Transistor Output (Sinking vs. Sourcing):** Solid-state, fast, DC only.
  - **Sinking (NPN):** Controller *sinks* current to ground.
  - **Sourcing (PNP):** Controller *sources* current from positive rail.
- **TRIAC Output:** For AC loads like solenoids and small motors.

#### C. Analog Inputs — AI
A **Continuous, time-varying signal** representing a process variable.
- **Voltage Inputs:** ±10 V, 0–10 V, 1–5 V.
- **Current Inputs:** **0–20 mA** or **4–20 mA** (the dominant industrial standard).
- **Thermocouple Inputs:** Low-voltage mV signals (e.g., Type K = ~41 µV/°C).
- **RTD Inputs:** Resistance change (e.g., Pt100 = 0.385 Ω/°C).

#### D. Analog Outputs — AO
Used for proportional control (e.g., modulating a control valve 0–100%).
- Standard ranges: **4–20 mA** (most common), **0–10 V**.

#### E. Pulse / Frequency Inputs
High-speed counters reading flow meters, tachometers, or encoders.
- Measured in **Hertz (Hz)** or **Pulses Per Revolution (PPR)**.

#### F. Smart / Networked I/O
- **HART (Highway Addressable Remote Transducer):** Digital signal superimposed on 4–20 mA.
- **Fieldbus, Profibus, Modbus, EtherNet/IP:** Fully digital, multi-variable communication.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values to prevent table syntax breakage.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Unit / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{loop} = 4 + 16 \cdot \left(\dfrac{PV - P_{min}}{P_{max} - P_{min}}\right)$ | Live-zero current loop conversion (4–20 mA) | mA |
| 2 | $V_{out} = 0 + 10 \cdot \left(\dfrac{PV - P_{min}}{P_{max} - P_{min}}\right)$ | Voltage output conversion (0–10 V) | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $Resolution = \dfrac{FS}{2^n}$ | ADC Least Significant Bit (LSB) value | Engineering Units / bit |
| 4 | $Quantization\_Error = \pm \dfrac{LSB}{2}$ | Theoretical max ADC error | Engineering Units |
| 5 | $f = \dfrac{N}{t}$ | Frequency = Pulses / Time | Hertz (Hz) |
| 6 | $\omega = 2 \pi f$ | Angular velocity from frequency | rad/s |
| 7 | $R_{Pt100} = R_0 \left( 1 + A T + B T^2 \right)$ | Callendar-Van Dusen RTD equation | Ohms (Ω) |
| 8 | $V_{out} = V_{ref} \cdot \left(\dfrac{D}{2^n - 1}\right)$ | DAC output for binary input D | Volts (V) |
| 9 | $SNR = 6.02n + 1.76$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio of an n-bit ADC | dB |
| 10 | $V_{TC} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Thermocouple Seebeck voltage | µV |

Where:
- $PV$ = Process Variable
- $FS$ = Full Scale of measurement
- $n$ = Number of ADC bits
- $D$ = Digital input word to DAC
- $V_{ref}$ = DAC reference voltage

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mastery of I/O types is the foundation of:
- **Power Plant DCS (Distributed Control Systems)** — Reading steam pressure, controlling feedwater valves.
- **Oil & Gas SCADA** — Monitoring pipeline flow, opening/closing remote block valves.
- **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0)** — Using smart sensors with HART/IO-Link for predictive maintenance.
- **Water Treatment Plants** — Level transmitters, pH sensors, chemical dosing pumps.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why "Live-Zero" (4 mA)?** A 0–20 mA loop cannot distinguish between a *true zero reading* and a *broken wire* (both produce 0 mA). The 4 mA offset means "zero process value = 4 mA," and any reading below 4 mA (e.g., 3.8 mA) is instantly flagged as a **fault condition** by the controller.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: The 4–20 mA to Engineering Units Conversion

**Problem Scenario:** A pressure transmitter with a range of **0 to 10 bar** outputs **12 mA**. What is the measured pressure?

**Step 1 — Identify the boundaries:**
- $P_{min} = 0 \text{ bar}$ corresponds to $I_{min} = 4 \text{ mA}$
- $P_{max} = 10 \text{ bar}$ corresponds to $I_{max} = 20 \text{ mA}$
- $I_{measured} = 12 \text{ mA}$

**Step 2 — Use the linear mapping formula:**

$$
PV = P_{min} + \left( \dfrac{P_{max} - P_{min}}{I_{max} - I_{min}} \right) \cdot (I_{measured} - I_{min})
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values:**

$$
PV = 0 + \left( \dfrac{10 - 0}{20 - 4} \right) \cdot (12 - 4)
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the fraction:**

$$
PV = \left( \dfrac{10}{16} \right) \cdot 8
$$

$$
PV = 0.625 \cdot 8 = 5.0 \text{ bar}
$$

**Conclusion:** The transmitter is reading **5.0 bar** of pressure (which is 50% of full scale — exactly matching the 12 mA midpoint between 4 mA and 20 mA).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: ADC Resolution and Quantization Error

**Problem Scenario:** A 14-bit ADC is used to digitize a **0–10 V** analog input. Calculate the resolution per bit and the maximum quantization error.

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**
- $n = 14 \text{ bits}$
- $V_{FS} = 10 \text{ V}$ (Full Scale Voltage)

**Step 2 — Calculate the number of discrete steps:**

$$
N_{steps} = 2^n = 2^{14} = 16{,}384
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the resolution (size of 1 LSB):**

$$
Resolution = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1} = \dfrac{10}{16383} = 6.1035 \times 10^{-4} \text{ V}
$$

$$
Resolution \approx 0.61035 \text{ mV/bit}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the maximum quantization error (±½ LSB):**

$$
Q_{error} = \pm \dfrac{Resolution}{2} = \pm 0.305 \text{ mV}
$$

**Conclusion:** Each bit change in the ADC's digital output represents a voltage change of **0.61 mV**, and the worst-case conversion error is **±0.305 mV**. This precision is why 14-bit ADCs are standard for industrial analog inputs.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sinking vs. Sourcing I/O

| Parameter | **Sourcing (PNP)** | **Sinking (NPN)** | **Relay** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Current flow direction | Source → Load | Load → Sink | Bidirectional |
| Common in | Europe, Asia | Japan, older US systems | Universal |
| Load connection | To **0 V** (Ground) | To **+V** (Positive) | Either polarity |
| Voltage on signal wire when ON | High (+V) | Low (0 V) | Contact closed |
| Leakage current tolerance | Low (better for sensors) | Moderate | Highest (galvanic isolation) |
| KTU Exam Tip | "P**N**P = **N**egative switching" (pulls to 0V) | "**N**PN = **P**ositive switching" | "Form C = SPDT" |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 I/O Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    IO[Supervisory Control I/O] --> DIO[Discrete I/O]
    IO --> AIO[Analog I/O]
    IO --> PIO[Pulse / Frequency I/O]
    IO --> SIO[Smart / Networked I/O]
    DIO --> DI[Discrete Inputs]
    DIO --> DO[Discrete Outputs]
    DI --> DIV[Voltage DI 24V DC]
    DI --> DIA[AC DI 230V AC]
    DI --> DIC[Contact / Dry DI]
    DO --> DOR[Relay Output Form A B C]
    DO --> DOT[Transistor Output]
    DOT --> DOP[Sourcing PNP]
    DOT --> DON[Sinking NPN]
    DO --> DOT2[TRIAC Output AC]
    AIO --> AI[Analog Inputs]
    AIO --> AO[Analog Outputs]
    AI --> AIC[Current 4 to 20 mA]
    AI --> AIV[Voltage 0 to 10 V]
    AI --> AIT[Thermocouple mV]
    AI --> AIR[RTD Resistance]
    AO --> AOC[Current 4 to 20 mA]
    AO --> AOV[Voltage 0 to 10 V]
    PIO --> PI1[High Speed Counter]
    PIO --> PI2[Frequency Input]
    PIO --> PI3[Encoder Quadrature]
    SIO --> SH[HART Protocol]
    SIO --> SF[Foundation Fieldbus]
    SIO --> SM[Modbus RTU TCP]
    SIO --> SE[EtherNet IP]
```

### 4.2 Signal Conditioning Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    FS[Field Sensor] --> SC[Signal Conditioner]
    SC --> AAF[Anti-Alias Filter]
    AAF --> MUX[Multiplexer]
    MUX --> ADC[Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> PLC[PLC RTU CPU]
    PLC --> DAC[Digital to Analog Converter]
    DAC --> AMP[Output Amplifier]
    AMP --> ISO[Galvanic Isolation]
    ISO --> FA[Field Actuator]
```

### 4.3 Sinking vs. Sourcing Load Wiring Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph SP[Sourcing PNP Configuration]
        SP_V[Plus V Supply] --> SP_LD[Load Indicator Lamp]
        SP_LD --> SP_OUT[Controller Output Terminal]
        SP_OUT -.Closes to Plus V.-> SP_V
    end
    subgraph SN[Sinking NPN Configuration]
        SN_V[Plus V Supply] --> SN_OUT[Controller Output Terminal]
        SN_OUT -.Closes to 0V.-> SN_LD[Load Indicator Lamp]
        SN_LD --> SN_GND[0V Ground]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid for the Diagrams:** Each block in the Signal Conditioning diagram represents one physical stage inside an industrial controller's I/O module. The *Anti-Alias Filter* is critical — without it, high-frequency noise above the Nyquist frequency can fold back into the measurement and corrupt the digital value.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
*Explain the difference between a 0–20 mA and a 4–20 mA current loop. Why is the 4–20 mA standard preferred in industrial supervisory control?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Both are standard analog current signals used to transmit process variables (pressure, temperature, flow) from a field transmitter to a controller.

- **0–20 mA loop:** A reading of 0 mA represents the lowest process value. A reading of 20 mA represents the highest.
- **4–20 mA loop:** A reading of 4 mA represents the lowest process value. 20 mA represents the highest. The 4 mA offset is called a **"live zero."**

**Why 4–20 mA is preferred (2 key reasons):**
1. **Fault Detection:** In a 0–20 mA loop, a 0 mA signal is *ambiguous* — it could mean either "true zero process value" OR "broken wire / open circuit." In a 4–20 mA loop, any reading *below 4 mA* is unambiguously a **fault condition** (e.g., 0 mA = broken wire, 2 mA = sensor failure), enabling instant diagnostics.
2. **Power to the Transmitter:** The 4 mA provides a constant minimum current that can power a 2-wire (loop-powered) transmitter from the controller itself, eliminating the need for separate power wiring.

*(Award 1 mark for definitions, 2 marks for the live-zero fault-detection explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
*Differentiate between **Sinking** and **Sourcing** discrete output configurations. Give one example load for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | **Sinking Output (NPN)** | **Sourcing Output (PNP)** |
|---|---|---|
| Switching action | Switches the *negative* (0 V) line | Switches the *positive* (+V) line |
| Current direction | Current flows *from* load *into* controller | Current flows *out of* controller *into* load |
| Load connected to | +V supply | 0 V (ground) |
| Example load | A relay coil whose other end is tied to +24 V | A PNP-style indicator LED with cathode to ground |

*(1 mark for clear definition of each, 1 mark for the difference, 1 mark for valid examples.)*

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question is split into **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Answer **either** Option A **or** Option B in full.

---

### ✅ OPTION A (Question A — 14 Marks)

**Q.A. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the complete signal path of a 4–20 mA analog input signal — from the field transmitter through signal conditioning, ADC conversion, and into the controller CPU. List any **two advantages** of current signals over voltage signals for long-distance transmission. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A level transmitter in a water tank is calibrated for a range of **0 to 5 meters**. The current output reads **8 mA**. Calculate the actual water level in meters. If the ADC has **12-bit resolution**, what is the digital count corresponding to 8 mA? *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A:

**Part (a) — Signal Path Explanation (7 marks):**

The complete signal chain for a 4–20 mA analog input consists of the following blocks in sequence:

1. **Field Transmitter (Sensor + Transducer):** Measures the physical quantity (e.g., hydrostatic pressure of water column) and converts it linearly into a 4–20 mA DC current signal.
2. **Wiring & Surge Protection:** The 2-wire loop carries the current; TVS diodes and fuses protect against transients.
3. **Current-Sensing Resistor (Shunt):** A precision resistor (typically **250 Ω**) in the controller converts 4–20 mA into **1–5 V DC** ($V = I \times R = 0.020 \times 250 = 5\text{V}$).
4. **Anti-Alias Filter (Low-Pass RC Filter):** Removes high-frequency noise above the Nyquist rate.
5. **Multiplexer (MUX):** Allows multiple analog channels to share one ADC.
6. **Sample-and-Hold Amplifier (SHA):** Freezes the voltage for stable conversion.
7. **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC):** Converts the voltage to a binary number (e.g., 12-bit or 16-bit).
8. **Controller CPU (PLC/RTU):** Processes the digital value, applies scaling, and uses it in control logic.

**Two advantages of current signals over voltage signals:**
1. **Immunity to Voltage Drop:** Current is identical throughout a series loop, regardless of wire length or resistance. Voltage drops across long cables, distorting the reading.
2. **Superior Noise Rejection:** The twisted-pair current loop rejects electromagnetically induced (EMI) noise far better than a high-impedance voltage line.

*(Award 1 mark per block in the diagram, 2 marks for full signal path description, 2 marks each for the two advantages — but adapt as needed for the 7-mark split.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the ranges:**

$$
L_{min} = 0 \text{ m}, \quad L_{max} = 5 \text{ m}, \quad I_{min} = 4 \text{ mA}, \quad I_{max} = 20 \text{ mA}, \quad I_{measured} = 8 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the linear conversion formula:**

$$
L = L_{min} + (L_{max} - L_{min}) \cdot \dfrac{I_{measured} - I_{min}}{I_{max} - I_{min}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute and solve:**

$$
L = 0 + (5 - 0) \cdot \dfrac{8 - 4}{20 - 4} = 5 \cdot \dfrac{4}{16} = 5 \cdot 0.25 = 1.25 \text{ m}
$$

**The water level is 1.25 meters.**

**Step 4 — Convert 8 mA to voltage across 250 Ω shunt:**

$$
V = 8 \text{ mA} \times 250 \text{ Ω} = 2000 \text{ mV} = 2.0 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate 12-bit ADC digital count:**

For a 1–5 V input range on a 12-bit ADC:

$$
Count = \dfrac{V_{measured} - V_{min}}{V_{max} - V_{min}} \cdot (2^n - 1)
$$

$$
Count = \dfrac{2.0 - 1.0}{5.0 - 1.0} \cdot (4095) = \dfrac{1.0}{4.0} \cdot 4095 = 0.25 \cdot 4095 = 1023.75
$$

**The digital count is approximately 1024** (rounded to nearest integer).

---

### ✅ OPTION B (Question B — 14 Marks — *Alternative to Option A*)

**Q.B. [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Model]**
**(a)** Classify the different **types of inputs and outputs** used in a SCADA/PLC-based supervisory control system. For each type, give **one real-world field device** example. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A **Type K thermocouple** is used to measure the temperature of a furnace. The measured Seebeck voltage is **20.524 mV**. The Seebeck coefficient for Type K is approximately **40 µV/°C** in the operating range. Calculate the furnace temperature in °C. If the signal must be transmitted via 4–20 mA to the controller, and the transmitter is calibrated for **0–1000 °C**, find the current output for this temperature. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B:

**Part (a) — Classification Table (7 marks):**

A complete classification of supervisory control I/O types with examples:

| # | I/O Type | Sub-Type | Real-World Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Discrete Input | Voltage DI (24 V DC) | Push button, limit switch |
| 2 | Discrete Input | AC DI (230 V) | Motor contactor aux contact |
| 3 | Discrete Input | Contact/Dry | Pressure switch, float switch |
| 4 | Discrete Output | Relay | Motor starter coil, alarm horn |
| 5 | Discrete Output | Transistor (Sourcing/Sinking) | LED indicator, small solenoid |
| 6 | Discrete Output | TRIAC | AC heater, lamp dimmer |
| 7 | Analog Input | Current (4–20 mA) | Pressure transmitter, flow meter |
| 8 | Analog Input | Voltage (0–10 V) | Variable speed drive feedback |
| 9 | Analog Input | Thermocouple (mV) | Furnace temperature sensor |
| 10 | Analog Input | RTD (Resistance) | Bearing temperature monitoring |
| 11 | Analog Output | Current (4–20 mA) | Control valve positioner |
| 12 | Analog Output | Voltage (0–10 V) | VFD speed reference |
| 13 | Pulse Input | Frequency/Counter | Flow turbine meter, tachometer |
| 14 | Pulse Input | Encoder (Quadrature) | Servo motor position feedback |
| 15 | Smart/Networked | HART | Smart pressure transmitter (multi-var) |
| 16 | Smart/Networked | Modbus/Ethernet | VFD parameter access, energy meter |

*(Award 1 mark per 2 correctly described rows; full marks for complete coverage with examples.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters:**

$$
V_{TC} = 20.524 \text{ mV}, \quad \alpha_{K} = 40 \text{ µV/°C} = 0.040 \text{ mV/°C}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the furnace temperature from the thermocouple voltage:**

$$
T = \dfrac{V_{TC}}{\alpha_{K}} = \dfrac{20.524 \text{ mV}}{0.040 \text{ mV/°C}}
$$

$$
T = 513.1 \text{ °C}
$$

**The furnace temperature is approximately 513 °C.**

**Step 3 — Identify the transmitter scaling:**

$$
T_{min} = 0 \text{ °C} \rightarrow I_{min} = 4 \text{ mA}
$$
$$
T_{max} = 1000 \text{ °C} \rightarrow I_{max} = 20 \text{ mA}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the linear scaling formula:**

$$
I = 4 + (20 - 4) \cdot \dfrac{T - T_{min}}{T_{max} - T_{min}}
$$

$$
I = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{513.1 - 0}{1000 - 0}
$$

$$
I = 4 + 16 \cdot 0.5131 = 4 + 8.2096 = 12.21 \text{ mA}
$$

**The 4–20 mA output current for 513.1 °C is approximately 12.21 mA.**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting the "Live-Zero" offset (4 mA):** The most common error in numericals is using a 0–20 mA formula when the problem states 4–20 mA. Always re-read the problem and use $I_{min} = 4$ mA unless stated otherwise. **Loss: up to 2 marks.**
> 2. **ADC Count Boundary:** A 12-bit ADC has digital counts from **0 to 4095** (i.e., $2^{12} - 1$). Students often use $2^{12} = 4096$ as the divisor *and* the max count, which is wrong. The max count is always $2^n - 1$. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Sinking vs. Sourcing confusion:** When asked for a wiring diagram, students mix up *where the load connects*. Memorize: **Sourcing → Load to 0V; Sinking → Load to +V.** **Loss: up to 2 marks on diagram questions.**
> 4. **Thermocouple Linearization:** Using a constant Seebeck coefficient ($\alpha$) is an approximation valid only for narrow ranges. Real Type K thermocouples are non-linear — mention this in viva/exam to earn the "extra insight" marks.
> 5. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing µV and mV is a fatal error. Always convert: $1 \text{ mV} = 1000 \text{ µV}$.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Two master categories of I/O:** **Discrete (Binary ON/OFF)** and **Continuous/Analog (variable magnitude)**.
- **The Industrial Current Standard is 4–20 mA**, not 0–20 mA, because of **live-zero fault detection**.
- **Voltage Standard for Analog I/O:** 0–10 V DC or 1–5 V DC.
- **Digital I/O Voltages:** 24 V DC (most common in PLCs); 120/230 V AC for high-power discrete signals.
- **Sinking (NPN) vs. Sourcing (PNP):** Sourcing switches the **+V line**; Sinking switches the **0V line**.
- **ADC Resolution Formula:** $\text{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n - 1}$. Max digital count is $2^n - 1$, not $2^n$.
- **Quantization Error** of an ideal ADC is $\pm \dfrac{1}{2}$ LSB.
- **Special I/O Types:** Pulse counters (for flow/tachometry), HART (digital over 4–20 mA), and Fieldbus (fully digital multi-drop).
- **Thermocouple** = mV signal, low-level, requires cold-junction compensation.
- **RTD (Pt100)** = Resistance signal (100 Ω at 0°C), more stable and linear than thermocouples.
- **Signal Conditioning Chain:** Sensor → Shunt/Filter → Multiplexer → Sample-and-Hold → ADC → CPU.
- **Always state units** in your final numerical answer; KTU examiners deduct marks for unit-less answers.
- **Current signals beat voltage signals** for long-distance transmission due to **noise immunity** and **no voltage drop loss**.
- **Form A** relay = SPST-NO; **Form B** = SPST-NC; **Form C** = SPDT (changeover).
- **Smart Sensors (HART/Fieldbus)** can transmit **multiple process variables** (e.g., pressure + temperature) over a single pair of wires.
- **Examiners love** comparison tables, block diagrams, and well-labeled sketches — use them liberally to score above 80%.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analog and Discrete I/O Modules
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT633 - SUPERVISORY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Analog and Discrete I/O Modules

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1 — Analog and Discrete I/O Modules

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In a **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA)** system, an **I/O Module** is the engineered interface layer that physically connects real-world field devices (sensors, actuators, valves, motors, transmitters) to the central controller (PLC/RTU). These modules perform the critical function of signal conditioning, isolation, and translation between the **physical process domain** and the **digital computational domain**.

The two principal categories of I/O modules covered in the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> **Analog I/O Modules** — Subsystems that interface with continuous, time-varying process signals (e.g., 4–20 mA current loops, 0–10 V DC voltage signals, RTD/thermocouple inputs). They rely on an internal **Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC)** for inputs and a **Digital-to-Analog Converter (DAC)** for outputs.
>
> **Discrete I/O Modules** — Subsystems that interface with binary, two-state process signals (ON/OFF, OPEN/CLOSED, TRUE/FALSE). They handle signals such as 24 V DC, 120 V AC, or potential-free relay contacts. They require no conversion algorithm — only voltage-level detection and optical/galvanic isolation.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **hotel front desk interpreter**:

- A **Discrete I/O module** is like a translator who only understands two languages: "YES" and "NO." A guest says "I want to check in" → translator signals "ON" to the manager. Simple, fast, unambiguous.
- An **Analog I/O module** is like a translator who understands a *spectrum* of languages. When a guest whispers "I am slightly hungry" versus shouts "I am starving," the translator must measure the *volume, pitch, and tone* precisely and convert this continuous emotion into a numerical value on a 0–100 scale. This requires a sophisticated codec (the ADC) and works on **Resolution**, **Range**, and **Quantization**.

### Standard Signal Metrics (KTU Board Standards)

| Metric | Discrete I/O | Analog I/O |
|---|---|---|
| Signal States | 2 (Binary) | $\infty$ (Continuous, quantized) |
| Standard Ranges | **24 V DC**, **120 V AC**, Relay Dry Contact | **4–20 mA**, **0–10 V**, **1–5 V**, **±10 V** |
| Conversion Required | None (Threshold detection) | ADC / DAC |
| Resolution | 1 bit (Boolean) | **8-bit, 12-bit, 16-bit** typical |
| Isolation | Optical / Relay | Optical + Transformer |
| Typical Update Time | < 1 ms | 1 ms – 100 ms |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The **4–20 mA current loop** is the industrial gold standard because a live zero (**4 mA ≠ 0 mA**) allows the controller to distinguish between a genuine "zero reading" and a **broken wire (0 mA = fault condition)**. This is a frequently tested concept in university exams.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Discrete I/O Modules — Operational Logic

A discrete I/O module consists of four fundamental sub-blocks:

1. **Input Conditioning Circuit** — A resistor divider network scales high-voltage field signals (e.g., 120 V AC) down to logic-level voltages (e.g., 5 V DC) safe for the processor.
2. **Galvanic / Optical Isolation** — An **opto-isolator** (LED + phototransistor) or a **relay coil** provides a physical air-gap separation between the field side and the controller side. This protects the expensive CPU from field-side voltage surges, transients, and ground loops. The isolation voltage is typically **1500 V RMS** or **2500 V RMS**.
3. **Threshold Detector (Schmitt Trigger)** — Compares the conditioned signal against a reference threshold ($V_{TH}$) to produce a clean logic-level binary bit.
4. **Status LED & Buffer Register** — Each channel has a status LED for field diagnostics, and the bits are aggregated into a **byte/word buffer** that the CPU reads.

### Discrete Output — Sinking vs. Sourcing

- **Sourcing Output (PNP):** The module *supplies* positive voltage to the load.
- **Sinking Output (NPN):** The module *provides a ground path* for the load.

This is a classic KTU Part A question — students frequently confuse the two.

## 2.2 Analog I/O Modules — Operational Logic

An analog input module performs the following sequential chain:

**Step 1 — Signal Conditioning:** The raw field signal (e.g., a thermocouple's microvolt-level output, or a 4–20 mA loop signal across a 250 $\Omega$ shunt producing 1–5 V) is amplified, filtered (anti-aliasing low-pass filter with cutoff $f_c$), and level-shifted to match the ADC's input range (typically 0–5 V or $\pm$10 V).

**Step 2 — Multiplexing:** Multiple analog input channels share a **single high-precision ADC** via an analog multiplexer (e.g., 8:1 or 16:1 MUX). This is cost-efficient.

**Step 3 — ADC Conversion:** The successive-approximation ADC samples the input voltage and produces a digital word of $n$ bits.

**Step 4 — Scaling & Engineering Units Conversion:** The raw digital count is converted to engineering units (°C, bar, L/min) using a linear transfer function.

**Step 5 — Isolation:** Digital isolation (typically using **transformer-based or capacitive isolators** like Analog Devices iCoupler®) separates field and logic grounds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the 4–20 mA Standard Dominates:**
> 1. **Live-zero fault detection** — 4 mA represents 0% process variable; 0 mA = wire break.
> 2. **Immunity to voltage drop** — Current in a loop is identical at every point; unlike voltage, it is unaffected by line resistance.
> 3. **Twisted-pair noise rejection** — Current loops reject common-mode electrostatic noise.
> 4. **Intrinsic safety (IS)** — Limited loop current is safe in hazardous (explosive) atmospheres.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **ADC Resolution (LSB weight)** | $Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}$ | Quantization step in Volts; $V_{FS}$ = full-scale voltage |
| **Quantization Error (Max)** | $E_{max} = \pm \dfrac{Q}{2}$ | Theoretical maximum quantization error |
| **Signal-to-Quantization-Noise Ratio** | $SQNR = 6.02n + 1.76 \text{ dB}$ | For ideal $n$-bit ADC |
| **% Resolution** | $\%R = \dfrac{100}{2^{n}}$ | Percentage of full scale per LSB |
| **Binary Code to Voltage** | $V_{out} = \dfrac{D}{2^{n}} \times V_{FS}$ | $D$ = digital input code |
| **Voltage to Engineering Units** | $E.U. = \left(\dfrac{I - 4}{16}\right) \times Span$ | For 4–20 mA loop |
| **Shunt Voltage** | $V_{shunt} = I \times R_{shunt}$ | Typically $R_{shunt} = 250\,\Omega$ |
| **Opto-Isolator CTR** | $CTR = \dfrac{I_C}{I_F} \times 100\%$ | Current Transfer Ratio |
| **Anti-Aliasing Filter** | $f_c \geq \dfrac{f_s}{2}$ | Nyquist criterion (Shannon) |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of ADC Quantization Step Size

**Problem Statement:** An analog input module uses a **12-bit ADC** with a unipolar input range of **0 V to +10 V**. Compute the (a) quantization step size $Q$, (b) the maximum quantization error $E_{max}$, (c) the number of discrete codes, and (d) the percentage resolution.

### Given Data
- Number of bits: $n = 12$
- Full-scale voltage: $V_{FS} = 10\,\text{V}$
- Minimum voltage: $V_{min} = 0\,\text{V}$

### Step (a): Quantization Step Size $Q$

The quantization step size (also called the Least Significant Bit weight) is the smallest voltage change the ADC can resolve.

$$
Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}}
$$

Substituting the given values:

$$
Q = \dfrac{10\,\text{V}}{2^{12}}
$$

$$
Q = \dfrac{10}{4096}\,\text{V}
$$

$$
Q \approx 2.4414\,\text{mV per LSB}
$$

### Step (b): Maximum Quantization Error $E_{max}$

The quantization error is the difference between the true analog voltage and its nearest quantized digital value. By definition, this error can never exceed **half of one quantization step** in either direction.

$$
E_{max} = \pm \dfrac{Q}{2}
$$

$$
E_{max} = \pm \dfrac{2.4414\,\text{mV}}{2}
$$

$$
E_{max} = \pm 1.2207\,\text{mV}
$$

### Step (c): Total Number of Discrete Codes

For an $n$-bit unipolar ADC, the digital output ranges from **0** to **$2^n - 1$**, yielding $2^n$ distinct codes.

$$
N_{codes} = 2^{12} = 4096
$$

### Step (d): Percentage Resolution

The percentage resolution is the fraction of the full scale represented by one LSB, expressed as a percentage.

$$
\%R = \dfrac{100}{2^{n}}
$$

$$
\%R = \dfrac{100}{4096}
$$

$$
\%R \approx 0.0244\%
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: 4–20 mA to Engineering Units Conversion

**Problem Statement:** A level transmitter uses a 4–20 mA signal to measure tank level over a range of **0 to 5 meters**. The PLC receives **12 mA**. Calculate the actual tank level in meters.

### Given Data
- Lower range current: $I_{min} = 4\,\text{mA}$
- Upper range current: $I_{max} = 20\,\text{mA}$
- Measured current: $I = 12\,\text{mA}$
- Lower Engineering Unit (LRV): $E.U._{min} = 0\,\text{m}$
- Upper Engineering Unit (URV): $E.U._{max} = 5\,\text{m}$

### Step 1: Apply the Linear Transfer Equation

The relationship between the current signal and the engineering units is linear, taking the form:

$$
E.U. = \left[\dfrac{I - I_{min}}{I_{max} - I_{min}}\right] \times \left(E.U._{max} - E.U._{min}\right) + E.U._{min}
$$

### Step 2: Substitute Values

$$
E.U. = \left[\dfrac{12 - 4}{20 - 4}\right] \times (5 - 0) + 0
$$

$$
E.U. = \left[\dfrac{8}{16}\right] \times 5
$$

$$
E.U. = 0.5 \times 5
$$

$$
E.U. = 2.5\,\text{meters}
$$

The tank level is **2.5 meters**, which is exactly **50% of the full-scale range**, consistent with the current being at the midpoint of the 4–20 mA span.

## 3.3 Derivation: Opto-Isolator CTR Analysis

**Problem Statement:** An opto-isolator has a forward LED voltage $V_F = 1.2\,\text{V}$ at $I_F = 10\,\text{mA}$, and a Current Transfer Ratio (CTR) of **50%**. The supply voltage on the output side is $V_{CC} = 5\,\text{V}$ and a pull-up resistor $R_C = 1\,\text{k}\Omega$ is used. Find the output logic voltage when the LED is ON.

### Step 1: Compute the Output Collector Current

$$
I_C = CTR \times I_F
$$

$$
I_C = 0.50 \times 10\,\text{mA}
$$

$$
I_C = 5\,\text{mA}
$$

### Step 2: Compute the Voltage Drop Across the Pull-Up Resistor

$$
V_{R_C} = I_C \times R_C
$$

$$
V_{R_C} = 5\,\text{mA} \times 1\,\text{k}\Omega
$$

$$
V_{R_C} = 5\,\text{V}
$$

### Step 3: Compute the Output Voltage

Since the phototransistor saturates and pulls its collector to near-ground, the output logic LOW voltage is:

$$
V_{OUT} = V_{CC} - V_{R_C} = 5 - 5 = 0\,\text{V (Logic LOW)}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Analog Input Module — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fieldA[Field Sensor 4-20 mA] --> condA[Signal Conditioner]
    condA --> muxA[Analog Multiplexer 8 to 1]
    muxA --> filterA[Anti Aliasing Low Pass Filter]
    filterA --> ampA[Instrumentation Amplifier]
    ampA --> adcA[12 bit or 16 bit ADC]
    adcA --> isoA[Digital Isolator iCoupler]
    isoA --> bufA[Buffer Register]
    bufA --> cpuA[PLC RTU CPU via Backplane]
```

## 4.2 Analog Output Module — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpuB[PLC RTU CPU] --> bufB[Output Latch Register]
    bufB --> isoB[Digital Isolator]
    isoB --> dacB[12 bit or 16 bit DAC]
    dacB --> ampB[Power Amplifier]
    ampB --> v2iB[Voltage to Current Converter 4-20 mA]
    v2iB --> fieldB[Field Actuator Valve or VFD]
```

## 4.3 Discrete Input Module — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    sw1[Field Switch Limit Switch Push Button] --> div[Voltage Divider Resistor Network]
    div --> rect[Bridge Rectifier for AC DC]
    rect --> isoC[Optical Isolator LED and Phototransistor]
    isoC --> sch[Schmitt Trigger Threshold Detector]
    sch --> led[Status LED Indicator]
    sch --> bufC[Input Buffer Register]
    bufC --> cpuC[PLC RTU CPU]
```

## 4.4 Discrete Output Module — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cpuD[PLC RTU CPU] --> bufD[Output Latch Register]
    bufD --> isoD[Optical Isolator or Relay Coil]
    isoD --> drv[Driver Transistor Darlington]
    drv --> load[Field Load Contactor Solenoid Lamp]
```

## 4.5 SCADA Integration — End-to-End I/O Dataflow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FIELD[Physical Process Layer]
        PT[Pressure Transmitter 4-20 mA]
        LSH[Level Switch Discrete]
        PCV[Pneumatic Control Valve Analog Out]
    end
    subgraph IO[Input Output Module Rack]
        AIM[Analog Input Module]
        DIM[Discrete Input Module]
        AOM[Analog Output Module]
        DOM[Discrete Output Module]
    end
    subgraph CTRL[Controller Layer]
        PLC[PLC or RTU Processor]
    end
    subgraph SCADA[Supervisory Layer]
        HMI[HMI Workstation]
        HIS[Historical Database]
    end
    PT --> AIM
    LSH --> DIM
    PLC --> AOM
    PLC --> DOM
    AOM --> PCV
    DOM --> LSH
    AIM --> PLC
    DIM --> PLC
    PLC --> HMI
    HMI --> HIS
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024 Style]`
**Differentiate between Analog and Discrete I/O modules in a SCADA system with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**
Analog I/O modules interface with continuously varying process signals such as temperature (RTD), pressure (4–20 mA transmitter), or flow. They require an internal ADC (for inputs) or DAC (for outputs) to convert between the physical signal and digital representation. Resolution is typically 12-bit or 16-bit.

Discrete I/O modules interface with two-state signals such as a limit switch (open/closed), motor contactor (start/stop), or push button. They require only threshold detection (via a Schmitt trigger) and no conversion algorithm. Each channel represents one Boolean bit (0 or 1).

| Parameter | Analog I/O | Discrete I/O |
|---|---|---|
| Signal Nature | Continuous | Binary (2-state) |
| Conversion | ADC / DAC | Threshold Detection |
| Example | 4–20 mA pressure Tx | 24 V DC limit switch |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — Model Question, June 2024 Style]`
**Explain why the 4–20 mA current loop is preferred over 0–10 V in industrial analog signal transmission.**

**Model Answer:**
The 4–20 mA standard is preferred because:

1. **Live-zero fault detection:** A reading of 4 mA represents zero process variable; a reading of 0 mA unambiguously indicates a wire break or sensor failure, which is impossible to differentiate in a 0–10 V system where 0 V could mean either "zero process value" or "broken wire."
2. **Immunity to line resistance:** Current is identical at every point in a series loop, so voltage drops across long cable runs do not affect accuracy.
3. **Noise rejection:** Twisted-pair current loops reject common-mode electrostatic and electromagnetic noise better than single-ended voltage signals.
4. **Intrinsic Safety:** The bounded loop current (max 20 mA) prevents ignition in hazardous (explosive) environments.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]`
**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and working of a typical **Analog Input Module** used in a SCADA system. Describe the function of each block in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 12-bit ADC is used in an analog input module with an input range of **–10 V to +10 V**. Calculate:
- (i) The quantization step size $Q$
- (ii) The maximum quantization error
- (iii) The number of discrete codes
- (iv) The digital output code (in decimal and binary) when the input voltage is **+6.5 V**. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.A(a) — Analog Input Module Architecture (7 Marks)

An Analog Input Module performs the following functions sequentially. Each block plays a critical role in ensuring accurate, noise-free, and safe transfer of field signals to the PLC/RTU.

1. **Input Terminal Block & Protection:** Provides screw terminals for field wiring, with TVS (Transient Voltage Suppressor) diodes and PTC fuses for over-voltage and over-current protection.

2. **Signal Conditioner:** Amplifies weak signals (e.g., thermocouple $\mu$V-level output) and converts current-loop signals (4–20 mA) into proportional voltages using a precision **250 $\Omega$ shunt resistor**, yielding **1–5 V**.

3. **Analog Multiplexer (MUX):** A solid-state switch (e.g., ADG408) that selects one of 8 or 16 input channels sequentially to share a single ADC, reducing cost.

4. **Anti-Aliasing Low-Pass Filter:** A passive RC or active Butterworth filter with cutoff frequency $f_c \geq f_s / 2$ (Nyquist criterion) to remove high-frequency noise that could alias into the measurement band.

5. **Instrumentation Amplifier:** Provides high Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR > 100 dB) and programmable gain to scale the signal to the ADC input range.

6. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H) Circuit:** Holds the input voltage constant during the ADC conversion time (typically 1–20 $\mu$s) to avoid errors.

7. **ADC (Successive Approximation Type):** Converts the held voltage into a digital word of 12/16 bits.

8. **Digital Isolator:** Uses iCoupler® (transformer-based) or opto-isolation to provide **1500–2500 V RMS galvanic isolation** between field ground and logic ground.

9. **Buffer Register & Backplane Interface:** The digital word is latched into a memory-mapped register accessible to the CPU via the backplane (e.g., PCI, EtherCAT, PROFIBUS).

#### Solution to Q.A(b) — 12-bit Bipolar ADC Calculations (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- $n = 12$
- Range: $-10\,\text{V}$ to $+10\,\text{V}$ (Bipolar)
- Full-scale span: $V_{FS} = 20\,\text{V}$

**(i) Quantization Step Size:**

$$
Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^{n}} = \dfrac{20}{4096}
$$

$$
Q \approx 4.8828\,\text{mV per LSB}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Quantization Error:**

$$
E_{max} = \pm \dfrac{Q}{2} = \pm \dfrac{4.8828\,\text{mV}}{2}
$$

$$
E_{max} = \pm 2.4414\,\text{mV}
$$

**(iii) Total Number of Discrete Codes:**

For a bipolar ADC, codes range from **0 to 4095**, but interpretation uses **two's-complement**, giving signed values from **–2048 to +2047**, total **4096 codes**.

$$
N_{codes} = 2^{12} = 4096
$$

**(iv) Digital Code for +6.5 V:**

$$
D = \text{round}\left[\dfrac{V_{in} + 10}{Q}\right]
$$

$$
D = \text{round}\left[\dfrac{6.5 + 10}{0.0048828}\right]
$$

$$
D = \text{round}\left[\dfrac{16.5}{0.0048828}\right] = \text{round}[3379.2] = 3379
$$

**Binary equivalent (12-bit unsigned):** `110100101011`

In **two's-complement signed** (since MSB = 1), this represents **+3379** in the positive range.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style]`
**(a)** Explain the concept of **Galvanic Isolation** in Discrete I/O Modules. With a circuit diagram, describe the working of an **opto-isolator** and derive the relationship between LED forward current and output transistor collector current. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A temperature transmitter with a range of **0–200 °C** outputs a **4–20 mA** signal. The signal is terminated across a **250 $\Omega$** precision resistor to produce a voltage for a 12-bit unipolar ADC with a **0–10 V** range. Calculate:
- (i) The voltage across the shunt when the process temperature is **150 °C**
- (ii) The corresponding digital output code of the ADC
- (iii) The quantization step size of the ADC **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Solution to Q.B(a) — Galvanic Isolation & Opto-Isolator (7 Marks)

**Concept of Galvanic Isolation:**
Galvanic isolation is a design technique where there is **no direct electrical conductive path** between two sections of a circuit. Energy and information are transferred via optical, magnetic (transformer), or capacitive coupling. In a Discrete I/O module, isolation is mandatory because:

- The field side may carry high voltages (120/230 V AC) or be exposed to lightning-induced transients.
- The logic/CPU side operates at low voltages (5 V DC / 3.3 V DC).
- Ground potential differences between the field (earthed at the plant) and the control cabinet (earthed at the MCC room) can cause **ground loops**, introducing 50 Hz hum and measurement errors.

**Opto-Isolator Working Principle:**
An opto-isolator consists of a **Gallium Arsenide (GaAs) infrared LED** on the input side and a **silicon phototransistor** (or photodarlington, phototriac) on the output side, separated by a transparent insulating dielectric gap (typically 0.4 mm to 1 mm).

**Derivation of CTR:**

Let the LED forward current be $I_F$. The LED emits infrared photons at a flux $\Phi$ proportional to $I_F$:

$$
\Phi = k_1 \cdot I_F
$$

where $k_1$ is the LED's luminous efficacy.

A fraction of these photons strike the phototransistor's base-collector junction, generating a photocurrent $I_{ph}$:

$$
I_{ph} = k_2 \cdot \Phi = k_1 k_2 I_F
$$

The collector current, assuming transistor current gain $\beta$, is:

$$
I_C = (\beta + 1) I_{ph} \approx \beta \cdot k_1 k_2 \cdot I_F
$$

Defining the **Current Transfer Ratio**:

$$
CTR = \dfrac{I_C}{I_F} \times 100\% = \beta \cdot k_1 k_2 \times 100\%
$$

Typical CTR values range from **20% to 300%** depending on the device family.

#### Solution to Q.B(b) — Temperature Transmitter Calculations (7 Marks)

**Given:**
- Temperature range: $T_{min} = 0\,°\text{C}$, $T_{max} = 200\,°\text{C}$
- Loop signal: 4–20 mA
- $R_{shunt} = 250\,\Omega$
- ADC: 12-bit, unipolar, 0–10 V

**(i) Voltage across shunt at T = 150 °C:**

First, find the loop current:

$$
I = I_{min} + \left[\dfrac{T - T_{min}}{T_{max} - T_{min}}\right] \times (I_{max} - I_{min})
$$

$$
I = 4 + \left[\dfrac{150 - 0}{200 - 0}\right] \times (20 - 4)
$$

$$
I = 4 + 0.75 \times 16 = 4 + 12 = 16\,\text{mA}
$$

Voltage across shunt:

$$
V_{shunt} = I \times R_{shunt} = 16\,\text{mA} \times 250\,\Omega
$$

$$
V_{shunt} = 4.0\,\text{V}
$$

**(ii) Digital Output Code:**

$$
D = \text{round}\left[\dfrac{V_{shunt}}{Q}\right] = \text{round}\left[\dfrac{V_{shunt} \times 2^n}{V_{FS}}\right]
$$

$$
D = \text{round}\left[\dfrac{4.0 \times 4096}{10}\right] = \text{round}[1638.4] = 1638
$$

**Binary (12-bit):** `011001100110`

**(iii) Quantization Step Size:**

$$
Q = \dfrac{V_{FS}}{2^n} = \dfrac{10}{4096} \approx 2.4414\,\text{mV}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Bipolar vs. Unipolar ADC:** When given a range like **–10 V to +10 V**, many students forget that $V_{FS} = 20\,\text{V}$ (the *span*), not 10 V. Marks are deducted for using the wrong full-scale.
> 2. **Live Zero in 4–20 mA:** Students often calculate percentage as $(I - 0)/(20 - 0)$ instead of the correct $(I - 4)/(20 - 4)$. The "live zero" of 4 mA is not zero — it represents 0% of the process variable.
> 3. **Resolution Direction:** A *higher* number of bits = *smaller* quantization step = *higher* resolution. Do not state the inverse.
> 4. **CTR Definition:** CTR is defined as $I_C / I_F$, not $I_F / I_C$. Confusing this ratio is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Unit Consistency:** Always convert mA to A before multiplying with Ohms, or explicitly write mA × $\Omega$ = mV to avoid losing dimensional consistency marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **I/O Module Role:** Acts as the *signal gateway* between field instruments and the SCADA controller — provides **conditioning, isolation, conversion, and protection**.
- **Two Categories:** Analog (continuous, ADC/DAC required) vs. Discrete (binary, threshold detection only).
- **4–20 mA Dominance:** Live-zero fault detection + noise immunity + intrinsic safety + immunity to line resistance.
- **Key ADC Parameters:** Resolution ($n$ bits), Full-Scale Voltage ($V_{FS}$), Quantization Step ($Q = V_{FS}/2^n$), Quantization Error ($\pm Q/2$), SQNR ($6.02n + 1.76$ dB).
- **Galvanic Isolation:** Provided by **opto-isolators** (LED + phototransistor) or **transformer/capacitive isolators** (iCoupler®). Voltage rating: typically **1500–2500 V RMS**.
- **Sourcing vs. Sinking:** PNP = source current; NPN = sink current. Match to the load type.
- **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** Cutoff must satisfy Nyquist: $f_c \geq f_s / 2$ to prevent high-frequency noise from folding back into the measurement band.
- **Engineering Units Conversion:** $E.U. = \left[(I - 4)/(20 - 4)\right] \times Span + LRV$.
- **Module Diagnostics:** Status LEDs per channel, hot-swappable design, and field-side fuse protection are hallmarks of industrial-grade I/O modules.
- **KTU Exam Frequency:** Analog I/O conversions, 4–20 mA scaling, and ADC resolution problems appear in **>80% of past papers**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Power Supply of PLCs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT633 - SUPERVISORY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Power Supply of PLCs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

A **PLC Power Supply Unit (PSU)** is the dedicated internal module within a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) chassis that converts the available industrial AC mains supply (typically **230 V AC, 50 Hz** in India or **120 V AC, 60 Hz** in North America) into the regulated low-voltage DC levels required to energise the CPU, memory, input/output (I/O) modules, communication interfaces, and internal backplane electronics of the PLC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition:** The PLC power supply is *not* merely a transformer. It is a regulated, isolated, fault-tolerant power conversion block that guarantees ride-through during brief mains disturbances (typically **10 ms – 20 ms**) and supplies both the **logic-level power** (typically **5 V DC / 3.3 V DC** for the CPU) and the **field-level power** (typically **24 V DC** for sensors and actuators).

The power supply is the **first line of defence** for the PLC against industrial electrical noise, voltage sags, surges, harmonics, and transients present on the plant mains.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Heart of the PLC"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Human Circulatory System**
> Think of the PLC power supply as the **heart and circulatory system** of the human body. Just as the heart pumps oxygenated blood at a steady pressure to every organ (brain, limbs, lungs), the PSU pumps **regulated DC voltage at a stable current** to every subsystem of the PLC (CPU = brain, I/O = limbs, communication ports = nervous system). If the heart stops or pressure drops, the body collapses — similarly, if the PSU fails or fluctuates, the PLC executes erratic logic, resets, or suffers I/O corruption.

A more engineering-oriented analogy: the PSU is like the **voltage regulator in an automobile**. The alternator produces variable AC, but every electronic gadget (ECU, infotainment, sensors) needs rock-steady **5 V / 3.3 V / 12 V**. The regulator smooths everything out — exactly what a PLC PSU does for **5 V DC** and **24 V DC** rails.

---

### 1.3 Why a PLC Needs a Dedicated PSU (and not just an adapter)

| Factor | Industrial Reality | PSU Requirement |
|---|---|---|
| **Voltage sags** | Motor starts cause 15–30 % dips | Must ride through **10 ms – 20 ms** |
| **Electrical noise** | VFDs, contactors, relays | EMI/RFI filtering mandatory |
| **Isolation** | Field side at hundreds of volts | Galvanic isolation **≥ 1500 V RMS** |
| **Reliability** | PLC downtime costs ₹₹₹ | MTBF **≥ 100,000 hours** |
| **Temperature** | Panels hit **60 °C** | Operation **0 °C – 60 °C** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Constants / Standard Metrics (must memorise for KTU):**
> - AC Mains: **230 V AC, 50 Hz** (India) / **120 V AC, 60 Hz** (USA)
> - Standard PLC Logic Rail: **5 V DC** (legacy) or **3.3 V DC** (modern)
> - Standard Sensor/Actuator Rail: **24 V DC** (IEC 61131-2 standard)
> - Hold-up time: **≥ 10 ms** at full load
> - Isolation voltage: **1500 V AC** (input-to-output, input-to-PE)
> - Line regulation: **± 0.5 %** to **± 2 %**
> - Load regulation: **± 1 %** to **± 3 %**
> - Ripple & noise: **< 50 mV RMS**, **< 150 mV peak-to-peak**
> - Efficiency (SMPS): **> 85 %** at full load
> - Efficiency (linear): **< 50 %**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of PLC Power Supplies

PLC power supplies are classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

#### Axis A — By Topology / Conversion Technique

1. **Linear Power Supply (LPS)**
   - Uses a **50 Hz / 60 Hz mains transformer → rectifier → LC filter → linear regulator (78xx/79xx series)**
   - Pros: extremely clean output, low ripple, fast transient response, no EMI
   - Cons: heavy, bulky (large iron-core transformer), inefficient (**30–50 %**), dissipates heat as $P_{diss} = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_{load}$
   - Application: legacy Allen-Bradley SLC-500, Siemens S5

2. **Switched-Mode Power Supply (SMPS)**
   - Uses a **high-frequency (50 kHz – 500 kHz) ferrite transformer → high-frequency rectifier → LC filter → feedback regulation**
   - Pros: lightweight, compact, efficient (**80–95 %**), wide input range
   - Cons: generates EMI, requires EMI filtering, slower transient response
   - Application: modern Allen-Bradley CompactLogix, Siemens S7-1200/1500, Schneider M340

#### Axis B — By Isolation Strategy

1. **Isolated PSU** — output is galvanically isolated from mains using a transformer. **Mandatory** for PLCs.
2. **Non-isolated** (buck/boost converters) — direct connection, not used as a standalone PLC PSU.

#### Axis C — By Redundancy

1. **Single PSU** — standard, lowest cost
2. **Redundant PSU** — two PSUs in parallel with **OR-ing diodes** or **MOSFET ideal-diodes**, providing hot-swappable failover. Used in critical process control.

---

### 2.2 Functional Block Diagram — Stages Inside a PLC SMPS

```
Mains → EMI Filter → Bridge Rectifier → Bulk DC → HF Switching → HF Transformer → HF Rectifier → Filter → Feedback Loop → Output (5V/24V)
```

Each stage plays a specific role:

| Stage | Function | Key Component |
|---|---|---|
| **EMI Filter** | Blocks conducted noise from mains and prevents PLC noise reaching mains | X-capacitor, Y-capacitor, common-mode choke |
| **Bridge Rectifier** | Converts AC → pulsating DC | 4× diodes (or single bridge IC) |
| **Bulk Capacitor** | Smooths pulsating DC, stores energy for hold-up | Electrolytic capacitor (e.g., **470 µF / 400 V**) |
| **HF Switching Stage** | Chops DC at **50–500 kHz** for transformer coupling | MOSFET (e.g., **IRF840**) or PWM controller IC (e.g., **UC3842**) |
| **HF Transformer** | Steps down (or up) voltage, provides **galvanic isolation** | Ferrite-core transformer |
| **HF Rectifier** | Rectifies high-frequency AC output | Schottky diodes (lower $V_F$, faster switching) |
| **Output Filter** | Removes switching ripple | LC π-filter |
| **Feedback Loop** | Maintains constant output despite load/input variation | Optocoupler + TL431 reference |

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — PSU Sizing & Performance Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Numerical & Derivative Questions:**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{out} = V_{out} \cdot I_{out}$ | Output power delivered to PLC loads | **Watts (W)** |
| 2 | $\eta = \dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\,\%$ | Power supply efficiency | Linear: **30–50 %**, SMPS: **80–95 %** |
| 3 | $P_{diss} = P_{in} - P_{out}$ | Power lost as heat | **W** |
| 4 | $P_{diss,\,linear} = (V_{in} - V_{out}) \cdot I_{load}$ | Linear regulator dissipation | **W** |
| 5 | $t_{hold} = \dfrac{\dfrac{1}{2} C V_{dc}^2}{P_{out}}$ | Hold-up time from bulk capacitor (energy stored vs. consumed) | **seconds** |
| 6 | $\text{Line Reg} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{V_{nominal}} \times 100\,\%$ | Output change per input change | **±0.5 % – ±2 %** |
| 7 | $\text{Load Reg} = \dfrac{V_{NL} - V_{FL}}{V_{FL}} \times 100\,\%$ | Output change from no-load to full-load | **±1 % – ±3 %** |
| 8 | $I_{cap} = \dfrac{I_{out}}{\Delta V_{out}} \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot V_{ripple}$ | Required bulk capacitance sizing | **Farads (F)** |
| 9 | $\text{Ripple Factor} = \dfrac{V_{ac,rms}}{V_{dc}}$ | Ratio of AC ripple to DC level | Linear: **< 0.05**, SMPS: **0.01 – 0.05** |
| 10 | $\text{MTBF}_{sys}$ | $\dfrac{1}{\sum_i \dfrac{1}{MTBF_i}}$ — system MTBF from parts | **hours** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Table Symbols:** All absolute-value and conditional expressions in exam answers should be written using $\vert \cdot \vert$ or $\mid \cdot \mid$ — never use a single pipe character `|` inline within a table row, as it breaks markdown table parsing.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

PLC power supplies are not merely textbook topics — they are at the heart of every industrial control panel:

- **Plant Reliability:** The PSU is the **#1 failure component** in field studies of industrial control systems. Proper PSU selection prevents unplanned downtime.
- **Process Safety (SIL):** In Safety Instrumented Systems (per **IEC 61508/61511**), redundant PSUs are mandatory for **SIL-2** and above.
- **Energy Efficiency:** Replacing linear PSUs with SMPS reduces panel heat load, shrinking air-conditioning costs in control rooms by **10–15 %**.
- **EMC Compliance:** Well-designed PSUs make the PLC conform to **IEC 61131-2** and **EN 61131-2** EMC standards, which is a legal requirement for CE-marking in Europe and BIS in India.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Numerical problems on PSU sizing, hold-up time, and efficiency appear almost every year in Part B. Master the derivations below — they are high-weight, high-scoring items.

---

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hold-Up Time Calculation

**Problem Statement:** A PLC SMPS has a bulk capacitor of value $C = 470\ \mu F$ charged to a DC bus voltage of $V_{dc} = 380\ V$. The output load draws $P_{out} = 24\ W$. Find the hold-up time available to the PSU if the mains fails.

**Step 1 — Identify the energy stored in the bulk capacitor**

The energy stored in a capacitor charged to voltage $V$ is given by the universal capacitor energy formula:

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} \, C \, V_{dc}^{\,2}$$

Substituting the values:

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (470 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (380)^{2}$$

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot (470 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (144{,}400)$$

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot 67.868$$

$$E_{stored} = 33.934\ \text{Joules (J)}$$

This means **33.934 J** of energy is "trapped" inside the capacitor, available to feed the output load during a mains outage.

**Step 2 — Determine the discharge time given constant load power**

For a constant-power load (which is typical for digital electronics where power consumption is roughly independent of voltage up to a certain minimum), the relationship between energy, power, and time is:

$$E_{stored} = P_{out} \cdot t_{hold}$$

Rearranging for hold-up time:

$$t_{hold} = \frac{E_{stored}}{P_{out}}$$

Substituting:

$$t_{hold} = \frac{33.934}{24}$$

$$t_{hold} = 1.414\ \text{seconds (s)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Reality Check:** Hold-up time is *not* until full discharge — it's until the DC bus falls below the minimum input voltage of the HF switching stage (typically **~100 V**). Recalculating for a practical drop from **380 V → 100 V**:

$$t_{hold,\,practical} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} \, C \, (V_{dc}^{2} - V_{min}^{2})}{P_{out}} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} \cdot (470 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (380^{2} - 100^{2})}{24}$$

$$t_{hold,\,practical} = \frac{235 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (144{,}400 - 10{,}000)}{24}$$

$$t_{hold,\,practical} = \frac{235 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 134{,}400}{24} = \frac{31.584}{24} = 1.316\ \text{s}$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Power Supply Sizing for a PLC System

**Problem Statement:** A Siemens S7-1200 PLC system has the following modules:
- CPU 1214C: **5 V DC, 1100 mA**
- SM 1223 DI8/DQ8: **5 V DC, 145 mA** (×2 modules)
- SM 1231 AI4: **5 V DC, 80 mA**
- Output relay field supply: **24 V DC, 500 mA**

Find the **minimum PSU rating** required.

**Step 1 — Total internal (backplane) current**

$$I_{5V} = I_{CPU} + 2 \cdot I_{DI/DO} + I_{AI}$$

$$I_{5V} = 1100 + 2(145) + 80$$

$$I_{5V} = 1100 + 290 + 80$$

$$I_{5V} = 1470\ \text{mA} = 1.47\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Internal power dissipation at 5 V**

$$P_{5V} = V_{5V} \cdot I_{5V} = 5 \cdot 1.47 = 7.35\ \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Add field-side 24 V load**

$$P_{24V} = V_{24V} \cdot I_{24V} = 24 \cdot 0.5 = 12\ \text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Total output power**

$$P_{out} = P_{5V} + P_{24V} = 7.35 + 12 = 19.35\ \text{W}$$

**Step 5 — Apply safety derating factor (typically 20 %)**

$$P_{PSU} = 1.2 \cdot P_{out} = 1.2 \cdot 19.35 = 23.22\ \text{W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A **24 W / 5 V–5.5 A / 24 V–1 A** PSU would be the minimum. Practically, engineers choose the next standard size — **30 W** — for headroom and to prevent running the PSU at 100 % load continuously (which shortens life).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Efficiency Comparison: Linear vs SMPS

Given the same PSU requirement: **$V_{in} = 230\ V_{AC}$ → $V_{out} = 24\ V_{DC}$**, $I_{out} = 2\ A$.

**Linear PSU:**

$$P_{out} = 24 \cdot 2 = 48\ \text{W}$$

Assuming rectifier and linear regulator efficiency of **40 %**:

$$P_{in,linear} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta_{linear}} = \frac{48}{0.40} = 120\ \text{W}$$

$$P_{diss,linear} = 120 - 48 = 72\ \text{W} \text{ of heat}$$

**SMPS:**

Assuming efficiency **88 %**:

$$P_{in,SMPS} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta_{SMPS}} = \frac{48}{0.88} = 54.55\ \text{W}$$

$$P_{diss,SMPS} = 54.55 - 48 = 6.55\ \text{W} \text{ of heat}$$

**Heat-load saving:** $72 - 6.55 = 65.45\ \text{W}$ — a **90.9 %** reduction in panel waste heat. This is why modern PLCs exclusively use SMPS.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Choose Linear vs SMPS

| Criterion | Linear PSU | SMPS |
|---|---|---|
| Efficiency | ❌ Low (30–50 %) | ✅ High (80–95 %) |
| Size/Weight | ❌ Heavy transformer | ✅ Compact ferrite core |
| EMI Generated | ✅ Minimal | ❌ Requires filtering |
| Output Ripple | ✅ < 5 mV | ⚠️ 20–50 mV |
| Hold-up Time | ✅ Bulk cap easy to design | ⚠️ Smaller caps, faster discharge |
| Cost at low power | ✅ Cheaper | ❌ Slightly more expensive |
| Wide Input Range | ❌ Limited | ✅ 85–265 V AC universal |
| Modern PLC Use | ❌ Legacy only | ✅ Industry standard |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of a PLC Power Supply System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mainsAC["AC Mains 230V 50Hz"]:::input
    emiF["EMI Filter X-Cap Y-Cap Choke"]:::filter
    bridgeR["Bridge Rectifier"]:::rectify
    bulkC["Bulk Capacitor 470uF 400V"]:::storage
    hSwitch["HF Switch 100kHz MOSFET"]:::switch
    hfXfmr["HF Ferrite Transformer Isolation 1500V"]:::iso
    hfRect["HF Schottky Rectifier"]:::rectify
    outFilter["Output LC Filter"]:::filter
    fbLoop["Feedback Optocoupler TL431"]:::control
    rail5V["5V DC Logic Rail CPU Memory"]:::output
    rail24V["24V DC Sensor Actuator Rail"]:::output
    battBkp["Battery Backup UPS"]:::backup
    redundant["Redundant OR-ing Diode"]:::backup
    psuA["PSU A Primary"]:::psu
    psuB["PSU B Standby"]:::psu

    mainsAC --> emiF
    emiF --> bridgeR
    bridgeR --> bulkC
    bulkC --> hSwitch
    hSwitch --> hfXfmr
    hfXfmr --> hfRect
    hfRect --> outFilter
    outFilter --> fbLoop
    fbLoop --> hSwitch
    outFilter --> rail5V
    outFilter --> rail24V

    rail24V --> battBkp
    rail5V --> redundant
    rail24V --> redundant

    psuA --> redundant
    psuB --> redundant

    classDef input fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef filter fill:#fff5d6,stroke:#caa400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef rectify fill:#d6ecff,stroke:#1e6fa8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef storage fill:#e8d6ff,stroke:#6a1fa8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef switch fill:#ffd6e8,stroke:#a8136a,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef iso fill:#d6ffe8,stroke:#1a8c47,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#ffe8d6,stroke:#a85400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d6f5ff,stroke:#006a8c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef backup fill:#ffe2c5,stroke:#a84400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef psu fill:#e2e2ff,stroke:#3a3a8c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Power Distribution Flow Inside a Modular PLC

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    psuUnit["PSU Module Slot 0"]:::psu
    backplane["Backplane Power Bus 5V DC"]:::bus
    cpuSlot["CPU Slot 1"]:::module
    memSlot["Memory Slot 2"]:::module
    diSlot["DI Module Slot 3"]:::module
    doSlot["DO Module Slot 4"]:::module
    aiSlot["AI Module Slot 5"]:::module
    aoSlot["AO Module Slot 6"]:::module
    comSlot["Comms Module Slot 7"]:::module

    field24V["24V DC Field Supply"]:::field
    sensor1["Sensor Proximity"]:::field
    sensor2["Sensor Photoelectric"]:::field
    actuator1["Actuator Solenoid Valve"]:::field
    actuator2["Actuator Motor Contactor"]:::field

    psuUnit --> backplane
    backplane --> cpuSlot
    backplane --> memSlot
    backplane --> diSlot
    backplane --> doSlot
    backplane --> aiSlot
    backplane --> aoSlot
    backplane --> comSlot

    field24V --> sensor1
    field24V --> sensor2
    field24V --> actuator1
    field24V --> actuator2

    classDef psu fill:#e2e2ff,stroke:#3a3a8c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#fff5d6,stroke:#caa400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef module fill:#d6ecff,stroke:#1e6fa8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef field fill:#d6ffe8,stroke:#1a8c47,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Redundant PSU Configuration (Hot-Standby)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mains1["Mains Source 1"]:::input
    mains2["Mains Source 2 UPS"]:::input
    psuA2["PSU A Primary 24V"]:::psu
    psuB2["PSU B Standby 24V"]:::psu
    oringA["OR-ing Diode A"]:::control
    oringB["OR-ing Diode B"]:::control
    sharedBus["24V Shared DC Bus"]:::output
    plcLoad["PLC Backplane 5V"]:::output
    alarmGen["Alarm to CPU Power Good Signal"]:::control

    mains1 --> psuA2
    mains2 --> psuB2
    psuA2 --> oringA
    psuB2 --> oringB
    oringA --> sharedBus
    oringB --> sharedBus
    sharedBus --> plcLoad
    psuA2 --> alarmGen
    psuB2 --> alarmGen

    classDef input fill:#fde2e2,stroke:#c33,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef psu fill:#e2e2ff,stroke:#3a3a8c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef control fill:#ffe8d6,stroke:#a85400,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d6f5ff,stroke:#006a8c,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Diagram Adaptation Note:** Physical drawings of the PSU hardware (e.g., toroidal transformer, PCB layout) are not generated as Mermaid. Instead, the **functional block architecture** is rendered to convey the equivalent engineering information — this is the standard KTU-accepted approach for schematic-style topics.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**  
*List any three key specifications that must be considered while selecting a power supply for a PLC system. State typical numerical values for each.*

**Model Answer:**

A PLC power supply must be selected based on the following three key specifications:

1. **Output Voltage and Current Rating** — The PSU must supply both the **5 V DC** logic rail (for CPU/memory) and the **24 V DC** field rail (for sensors/actuators). Typical current ratings range from **2 A to 10 A** on the 24 V rail depending on the number of I/O modules.

2. **Hold-Up Time** — The PSU must continue to operate during brief mains interruptions. A minimum hold-up time of **10 ms** (one half-cycle of 50 Hz mains) is the industry baseline per IEC 61131-2.

3. **Isolation Voltage** — Galvanic isolation between mains and low-voltage DC output must withstand at least **1500 V AC** for 1 minute (per IEC 61131-2), to protect the CPU and operators from mains transients.

*(Auxiliary valid specs: Line/load regulation < ±2 %, ripple < 50 mV RMS, efficiency > 85 %, operating temperature 0 °C – 60 °C, MTBF > 100,000 hours.)*

---

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**  
*Differentiate between a linear power supply and a switched-mode power supply (SMPS) used in PLCs. Which one is preferred in modern PLCs and why?*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Linear PSU | SMPS |
|---|---|---|
| **Topology** | Mains transformer + linear regulator | HF switching at 50–500 kHz + HF transformer |
| **Efficiency** | 30 % – 50 % | 80 % – 95 % |
| **Weight / Size** | Heavy (iron-core, 50 Hz) | Light (ferrite-core, high frequency) |
| **Heat Dissipation** | High (up to 70 % of input as heat) | Low (< 15 % of input as heat) |
| **Output Ripple** | Very low (< 5 mV) | Moderate (20–50 mV) |
| **EMI** | Negligible | Requires EMI filter |
| **Cost at low wattage** | Lower | Slightly higher |
| **Input Range** | Narrow (e.g., 230 V ± 10 %) | Universal (85–265 V AC) |

**Modern PLCs prefer SMPS** because of its **high efficiency, smaller form factor, universal input range, and lower heat generation**, which directly translates to **longer component life and reduced panel air-conditioning load**.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A — **(14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]**

**(a)** *(7 Marks)*  
Explain, with a neat block diagram, the functional stages of a Switched-Mode Power Supply (SMPS) used in a modern PLC. Identify the role of each stage.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)*  
A PLC system requires the following modules powered from a common PSU:

| Module | Qty | 5 V Current (mA) | 24 V Current (mA) |
|---|---|---|---|
| CPU | 1 | 1200 | — |
| Digital Input | 3 | 100 each | 200 each |
| Digital Output | 2 | 150 each | 500 each |
| Analog Input | 1 | 80 | 100 |

Calculate the **minimum output power rating** of the PSU, applying a **20 % derating factor**. If the PSU operates at **88 % efficiency** from a 230 V AC mains, determine the **input current** drawn from the mains.

---

### Model Solution — Question A

**(a) SMPS Block Diagram and Stage-wise Function**

A modern PLC SMPS consists of **eight functional stages**:

| Stage | Function |
|---|---|
| **EMI Filter** | Suppresses conducted EMI/RFI in both directions — prevents mains noise reaching the PLC and prevents high-frequency switching noise from the SMPS from polluting the mains. |
| **Bridge Rectifier** | Converts the 230 V AC sine wave into full-wave rectified pulsating DC. |
| **Bulk Capacitor** | Smooths pulsating DC into a near-constant high-voltage DC bus (**~380 V DC** for 230 V AC mains) and stores energy for hold-up during mains dips. |
| **HF Switching Stage** | Chops the high-voltage DC at high frequency (**50 kHz – 500 kHz**) using a MOSFET driven by a PWM controller IC. High frequency allows a small, lightweight transformer. |
| **HF Ferrite Transformer** | Steps down (or up) the voltage and provides **galvanic isolation** of 1500 V AC between primary and secondary. |
| **HF Rectifier** | Rectifies the high-frequency AC output of the secondary winding using Schottky diodes (chosen for low forward voltage drop and fast switching). |
| **Output LC Filter** | Removes high-frequency switching ripple, producing clean DC at 5 V and 24 V. |
| **Feedback Loop** | Uses an optocoupler + TL431 shunt reference to sample the output voltage and modulate the PWM duty cycle, maintaining constant output regardless of load or input variations. |

*(Block diagram as drawn in SECTION 4.1 above should be reproduced in the exam booklet.)*

**(b) Numerical Sizing**

**Step 1 — Total 5 V current**

$$I_{5V} = 1 \cdot 1200 + 3 \cdot 100 + 2 \cdot 150 + 1 \cdot 80$$

$$I_{5V} = 1200 + 300 + 300 + 80 = 1880\ \text{mA} = 1.88\ \text{A}$$

**Step 2 — Power on 5 V rail**

$$P_{5V} = 5 \cdot 1.88 = 9.40\ \text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Total 24 V current**

$$I_{24V} = 3 \cdot 200 + 2 \cdot 500 + 1 \cdot 100$$

$$I_{24V} = 600 + 1000 + 100 = 1700\ \text{mA} = 1.70\ \text{A}$$

**Step 4 — Power on 24 V rail**

$$P_{24V} = 24 \cdot 1.70 = 40.80\ \text{W}$$

**Step 5 — Total output power**

$$P_{out} = P_{5V} + P_{24V} = 9.40 + 40.80 = 50.20\ \text{W}$$

**Step 6 — Apply 20 % derating**

$$P_{rated} = 1.20 \cdot 50.20 = 60.24\ \text{W}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Choose the **next standard size ≥ 60.24 W** — typically a **75 W** PLC PSU.

**Step 7 — Input power at 88 % efficiency**

$$P_{in} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \frac{50.20}{0.88} = 57.05\ \text{W}$$

**Step 8 — Input current from 230 V AC mains**

$$I_{in} = \frac{P_{in}}{V_{in}} = \frac{57.05}{230} = 0.248\ \text{A} \approx 248\ \text{mA}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Part B):**
> - **Skipping the derating factor** — Students lose 1–2 marks by using the bare $P_{out}$ without the 20 % safety margin.
> - **Using $V_{in}$ as RMS instead of peak** — Make sure you use the **RMS** value when computing input current: $V_{RMS} = 230\ \text{V}$.
> - **Forgetting units** — Always state mA vs A vs kW; KTU examiners deduct marks for ambiguous units.
> - **Not separating 5 V and 24 V power calculations** — Many students lump everything into one rail, which is methodologically wrong.

---

#### Question B — **(14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** — Alternative Choice

**(a)** *(7 Marks)*  
What is hold-up time in a PLC power supply? Derive the expression for hold-up time from the energy stored in the bulk capacitor. A PLC PSU has a bulk capacitor of **680 µF** charged to **400 V DC**. If the load draws **30 W**, calculate the practical hold-up time available if the input minimum operating voltage is **100 V DC**.

**(b)** *(7 Marks)*  
Discuss the role of redundancy in PLC power supplies for critical process control applications. With the help of a block diagram, explain the OR-ing diode method of paralleling two PSUs.

---

### Model Solution — Question B

**(a) Hold-Up Time — Concept and Derivation**

**Definition:** Hold-up time is the duration for which the PSU continues to deliver regulated output voltage **after the AC mains has failed**, before the output voltage collapses below acceptable limits. This protects the PLC from brief mains interruptions and sags.

**Derivation:**

Energy stored in a capacitor at voltage $V$:

$$E_{stored} = \frac{1}{2} \, C \, V^{2}$$

If the capacitor discharges from initial voltage $V_i$ down to a minimum operating voltage $V_{min}$, the **usable** energy is:

$$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} \, C \, (V_i^{2} - V_{min}^{2})$$

For a constant-power load $P_{out}$:

$$t_{hold} = \frac{\Delta E}{P_{out}} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} \, C \, (V_i^{2} - V_{min}^{2})}{P_{out}}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $C = 680\ \mu F = 680 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{F}$, $V_i = 400\ \text{V}$, $V_{min} = 100\ \text{V}$, $P_{out} = 30\ \text{W}$.

$$t_{hold} = \frac{\frac{1}{2} \cdot (680 \times 10^{-6}) \cdot (400^{2} - 100^{2})}{30}$$

$$t_{hold} = \frac{340 \times 10^{-6} \cdot (160{,}000 - 10{,}000)}{30}$$

$$t_{hold} = \frac{340 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 150{,}000}{30}$$

$$t_{hold} = \frac{51.0}{30} = 1.70\ \text{seconds}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The PLC has **1.7 seconds** of ride-through capability. If mains is restored within this window, the PLC continues running without any reset — invaluable for motor starters and conveyor systems where brief mains blips are common.

**(b) Redundancy in PLC PSUs**

In **critical process control applications** (refineries, power plants, pharmaceutical batch processes, safety interlocks), unscheduled PLC downtime can cost lakhs of rupees per hour and pose safety hazards. Hence, **two PSUs** are paralleled through **OR-ing diodes** so that if one fails, the other seamlessly takes over.

**OR-ing Diode Configuration:**

- Two PSUs (A — primary, B — standby) each feed a common DC bus through a **Schottky diode** (low $V_F$ for minimal loss).
- Under normal conditions, both PSUs share the load (with slight imbalance due to diode voltage drops).
- If PSU A fails, PSU B continues to feed the load with no interruption; an alarm is raised via the PSU's "Power Good" signal.
- Modern designs use **MOSFET ideal-diode ICs** (e.g., LTC4357) instead of diodes — these drop only **~30 mV** versus **~500 mV** for a Schottky, greatly reducing dissipation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Justification for Redundancy:**
> 1. Eliminates single-point-of-failure in PSU section.
> 2. Enables **hot-swap** maintenance — failed PSU can be replaced without shutting the PLC down.
> 3. Mandatory for **SIL-2 / SIL-3** safety instrumented systems as per IEC 61508.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes in Exam Answers:**
> - Students often confuse **hold-up time** (single PSU capability) with **redundancy** (two-PSU failover). Be clear on which concept the question asks.
> - In OR-ing diode diagrams, students forget to include the **Power Good / Fail alarm** signal — examiners specifically look for this in the diagram.
> - Forgetting to square $V_i$ and $V_{min}$ in the hold-up formula is a frequent computational error.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Power Supply of PLCs**

- **Definition:** A PLC PSU converts AC mains (230 V AC / 120 V AC) into regulated **5 V DC** (logic) and **24 V DC** (field) rails.
- **Two main topologies:** **Linear** (heavy, inefficient, low noise) and **SMPS** (light, efficient, modular — used in all modern PLCs).
- **Eight stages of SMPS:** EMI Filter → Bridge Rectifier → Bulk Capacitor → HF Switch → HF Transformer → HF Rectifier → Output Filter → Feedback Loop.
- **Key specifications (must memorise):**
  - Hold-up time: **≥ 10 ms** (typical **1 – 2 s** practical)
  - Isolation: **1500 V AC** (per IEC 61131-2)
  - Ripple: **< 50 mV RMS**
  - Efficiency: **> 85 %** (SMPS)
  - Line regulation: **± 0.5 % – ±2 %**
  - Load regulation: **± 1 % – ±3 %**
  - MTBF: **> 100,000 hours**
- **Core formulas:**
  - $P_{out} = V \cdot I$
  - $\eta = (P_{out}/P_{in}) \times 100\,\%$
  - $t_{hold} = \dfrac{\tfrac{1}{2} C (V_i^{2} - V_{min}^{2})}{P_{out}}$
  - $P_{rated} = 1.2 \cdot P_{out}$ (with 20 % derating)
- **Redundancy:** Two PSUs paralleled via **OR-ing diodes** or **MOSFET ideal-diodes** for critical processes.
- **Always apply a 20 % derating factor** when sizing the PSU to extend its operational life.
- **SMPS is the industry standard** today; linear PSUs are only of historical/academic significance.
- **KTU 2024 Scheme alignment:** This topic maps to **CO1** of PERAT633 — *Understand the architecture and subsystems of a PLC-based supervisory control system*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: PLC Programming Languages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT633 - SUPERVISORY CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** PLC Programming Languages ## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **PLC Programming Languages** are formally defined under the international standard **IEC 61131-3** (International Electrotechnical Commission), which standardizes the programming languages, data types, and execution semantics used in **Programmable Logic Controllers (PLCs)**. The standard defines **five (5) distinct programming languages** — two textual and three graphical — to ensure portability, readability, and vendor-neutral implementation across industrial automation systems. The five standard PLC programming languages per IEC 61131-3 are: | S.No. | Language | Type | Acronym | |:-----:|:---------|:----:|:-------:| | 1 | Ladder Diagram | Graphical | **LD** | | 2 | Function Block Diagram | Graphical | **FBD** | | 3 | Structured Text | Textual | **ST** | | 4 | Instruction List | Textual | **IL** | | 5 | Sequential Function Chart | Graphical | **SFC** | --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Five Languages of a Factory Manager"** Imagine you are a **factory floor manager** instructing workers through a manufacturing process. You could communicate in different ways: - **Ladder Diagram (LD)** → You draw a **wiring sketch** on a clipboard showing which switch connects to which light. Electricians instantly understand it because it looks just like the physical relay circuits they have wired for decades. - **Function Block Diagram (FBD)** → You give the workers a **flowchart of black boxes** (rectangles with labels) connected by wires — each box does a job, and arrows show data flowing from one to the next. - **Structured Text (ST)** → You write a **precise step-by-step instruction list in English-like code** (similar to Pascal or C), e.g., "IF temperature > 100 THEN start_cooling := TRUE". This is for engineers who prefer coding. - **Instruction List (IL)** → You write a **low-level assembly-style list** of short mnemonics like `LD`, `AND`, `OUT` — compact, but harder to read for beginners. - **Sequential Function Chart (SFC)** → You create a **state machine roadmap** — boxes representing process steps connected by transitions, perfectly suited to batch processes like "Mix → Heat → Cool → Discharge". All five languages ultimately achieve the **same control objective**; the choice depends on the **application complexity, programmer background, and industry convention**. --- ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **IEC 61131-3 Standard Origin Year:** **1993** (first edition), revised in 2013. - **Number of standardized languages:** **5** (enforced for vendor compliance). - **Typical execution cycle (scan time) of a PLC:** **5 to 20 milliseconds** for small to medium programs. -
