- Used for **total accumulated run-time** of a machine (e.g., total hours a pump has operated for maintenance scheduling).

## 2.2 Types of PLC Counters

### 2.2.1 Count Up Counter (CTU)
- Increments ACC by 1 for every **false-to-true (rising edge)** transition of the count input.
- Sets the **DN bit** to 1 when ACC ≥ PRE.
- The **OV (Overflow)** bit is set if ACC exceeds the **upper limit of 32,767** (for 16-bit counters).
- A **Reset (RES)** instruction clears the counter.

### 2.2.2 Count Down Counter (CTD)
- Decrements ACC by 1 for every rising edge of the count-down input.
- The ACC is preloaded with the PRE value.
- Sets the DN bit when ACC ≤ 0.
- The **UN (Underflow)** bit is set if ACC goes below **−32,768**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Unit | Boundary Condition | Notes |
|-----------|--------|------|-------------------|-------|
| Accumulated Value | $ACC$ | integer (counts) | $0 \leq ACC \leq 32{,}767$ (16-bit) | Updated each CPU scan |
| Preset Value | $PRE$ | integer (counts) | User-defined | Target threshold |
| Time Base | $TB$ | seconds | $0.01\,\text{s},\ 0.1\,\text{s},\ 1.0\,\text{s}$ | Hardware-specific |
| Total Time Delay | $T_d$ | seconds | $T_d = PRE \times TB$ | The *actual* time delay |
| Count Limit (CTU) | $ACC_{max}$ | integer | $32{,}767$ | Triggers OV flag |
| Count Limit (CTD) | $ACC_{min}$ | integer | $-32{,}768$ | Triggers UN flag |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Equation:**
> The relationship between Preset, Time Base, and Total Delay is:
> $$T_d = PRE \times TB$$
> where $T_d$ is the final time delay in seconds.

## 2.4 Why the 'Why' Matters: CPU Scan Dependency

PLC timers and counters are **soft** — they depend on the **CPU scan time**. If your scan time is $T_{scan}$ and $TB$ is smaller than $T_{scan}$, the timer will lose accuracy. For KTU problems, assume $TB \gg T_{scan}$ (the scan time is neglected), so the time delay is exactly $T_d = PRE \times TB$.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Total Time Delay ($T_d$)

We are asked: *"If a TON timer has PRE = 250 and Time Base = 0.1 s, what is the total time delay before the DN bit turns ON?"*

**Given:**
- Preset Value: $PRE = 250$ (integer)
- Time Base: $TB = 0.1\,\text{s}$

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation.**

The total time delay is the time required for the Accumulated Value (ACC) to reach the Preset Value (PRE). Since ACC increments by 1 every $TB$ seconds:

$$T_d = \text{(Number of increments)} \times \text{(Time per increment)}$$

**Step 2 — Express in variables.**

$$\text{Number of increments} = PRE - ACC_{initial} = PRE - 0 = PRE$$

$$T_d = PRE \times TB$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$T_d = 250 \times 0.1\,\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**

$$\boxed{T_d = 25\,\text{seconds}}$$

**Logic Explanation:** The PLC's internal clock ticks 10 times every second (since $TB = 0.1$ s). For the ACC to climb from 0 to 250, exactly 250 ticks are needed. 250 ticks × 0.1 s/tick = 25 s.

## 3.2 Counter Arithmetic: Net Count of CTU + CTD Cascade

Consider a parking lot: cars entering trigger a CTU, and cars leaving trigger a CTD. We need to find the net number of cars currently parked.

**Given:**
- Cars entered = 45 (CTU ACC = 45)
- Cars left = 30 (CTD ACC = 30)
- Counter is 16-bit, signed.

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**

$$N_{parked} = ACC_{CTU} - ACC_{CTD}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$N_{parked} = 45 - 30$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate.**

$$\boxed{N_{parked} = 15 \text{ cars}}$$

**Step 4 — Boundary check.**

Since the result is well within $[-32{,}768,\ 32{,}767]$, neither the OV nor the UN bit is activated. The system is operating nominally.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: TON vs TOF vs RTO

| Feature | TON (On-Delay) | TOF (Off-Delay) | RTO (Retentive On) |
|---------|----------------|------------------|---------------------|
| DN Bit sets when | $ACC \geq PRE$ while EN is TRUE | $ACC \geq PRE$ *after* EN goes FALSE | $ACC \geq PRE$ while EN is TRUE |
| Resets on EN = FALSE? | **Yes** (ACC → 0) | No (DN is already 1) | **No** (Retained) |
| Needs separate RES? | No | No | **Yes** (mandatory) |
| Timing Bit (TT) | 1 while counting | 1 while counting down | 1 while counting |
| KTU Exam Frequency | Very High | High | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Many students confuse TOF with TON. Remember: TOF **delays turning OFF**, TON **delays turning ON**. A simple mnemonic — *TOF = Timed-OFF*; *TON = Timed-ON*.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 TON Timer — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start]) --> inputCheckA{Input Rung TRUE?}
    inputCheckA -- No --> resetStateA[ACC=0, DN=0, TT=0]
    resetStateA --> waitA[/Wait for Input/]
    waitA --> inputCheckA
    inputCheckA -- Yes --> incrA[ACC increments by 1 every TB]
    incrA --> ttSetA[TT Bit = 1]
    ttSetA --> cmpA{ACC >= PRE?}
    cmpA -- No --> incrA
    cmpA -- Yes --> dnSetA[DN Bit = 1, TT Bit = 0]
    dnSetA --> doneA([Output Energized])
    doneA --> inputCheckA
```

## 4.2 TOF Timer — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([Start]) --> inputCheckB{Input Rung TRUE?}
    inputCheckB -- Yes --> dnInstantB[DN Bit = 1 Immediately, ACC=0]
    dnInstantB --> holdB[/Output stays ON/]
    holdB --> inputCheckB
    inputCheckB -- No --> incrB[ACC starts incrementing every TB]
    incrB --> ttSetB[TT Bit = 1]
    ttSetB --> cmpB{ACC >= PRE?}
    cmpB -- No --> incrB
    cmpB -- Yes --> dnResetB[DN Bit = 0, TT Bit = 0]
    dnResetB --> doneB([Output De-Energized])
    doneB --> inputCheckB
```

## 4.3 CTU Counter — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    cuPulse([CU Pulse Input]) --> edgeDetectA[Rising Edge Detector]
    edgeDetectA --> incrACC[Increment ACC by 1]
    incrACC --> ovChk{ACC > 32767?}
    ovChk -- Yes --> ovFlag[OV Bit = 1]
    ovChk -- No --> dnChk{ACC >= PRE?}
    dnFlag[DN Bit = 1] --> outCTU([Counter Done Output])
    dnChk -- No --> loopbackA[Wait for next pulse]
    loopbackA --> cuPulse
    resPulse([RES Input TRUE]) --> clearA[ACC = 0, DN = 0, OV = 0]
    clearA --> loopbackA
```

## 4.4 Timing Diagram — TON vs TOF (Visual Truth Table)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Timing Diagram: TON vs TOF Behaviour
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat %s
    section Input Signal
    Input ON (Rung TRUE)    :a1, 0, 5
    section TON Output
    TON: Delayed ON         :a2, 2, 3
    section TOF Output
    TOF: Holds then OFF     :a3, 0, 3
    section RTO Output
    RTO: Latched ON         :a4, 2, 6
```

**Reading the Gantt Chart:**
- **Input** is TRUE from $t = 0$ to $t = 5$.
- **TON** output becomes TRUE only at $t = 2$ (2 s delay).
- **TOF** output is TRUE from $t = 0$ and turns OFF at $t = 3$ (3 s after input went FALSE at $t = 5$ — wait, in this chart TOF is drawn for a different cycle; verify the input cycle).
- **RTO** stays latched even after the input goes FALSE.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** When drawing timing diagrams in the exam, use a graph with the X-axis as time and clearly mark: (1) the input pulse, (2) the TT bit, (3) the DN bit, and (4) the output coil. This fetches full marks.

## 4.5 Ladder Logic — TON for Motor Star-Delta Starter

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1([Line 1: Start PB]) --> OR[/OR: Stop PB NC + M_Contactor/]
    OR --> Mcont[Motor Contactor Coil]
    Mcont --> Tstart{T4:0 TON, PRE=100, TB=0.1s}
    Tstart -- DN=TRUE --> Mdelta[Delta Contactor Coil]
    Mcont --> Ttimer[/TON enabled while M is ON/]
```

In the actual ladder diagram (which the student should draw by hand in the exam):

**Rung 1:** `[Start PB] ---[ ]---[/]---( M_contactor )` (with parallel seal-in contact)
**Rung 2:** `[ M_contactor ]---[ TON, T4:0, PRE=100, TB=0.1s ]`
**Rung 3:** `[ T4:0.DN ]---( Delta_contactor )` (with NC contact of star contactor in series for interlocking)

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between a TON timer and a TOF timer with the help of a timing diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | TON (On-Delay) | TOF (Off-Delay) |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| **Output turns ON** | After PRE seconds of input being TRUE | Immediately when input becomes TRUE |
| **Output turns OFF** | Immediately when input becomes FALSE | After PRE seconds of input being FALSE |
| **DN bit sets** | When $ACC \geq PRE$ (input TRUE) | When $ACC \geq PRE$ (input FALSE) |
| **Application** | Delayed motor start | Cooling fan run-on after motor stop |

**Timing Diagram Sketch:**

```
Input:     ___|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|___
TON Out:       ___|‾‾‾‾‾|___      (delays ON, instant OFF)
TOF Out:   ___|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|___      (instant ON, delays OFF)
```

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for difference table, 1 mark for diagram)*

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is a retentive timer? Why is a separate RESET instruction required for it?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A **retentive timer (RTO)** measures the total time its input has been TRUE, but unlike a TON, it does **not reset the ACC value** when the input goes FALSE. The accumulated value is retained in memory. Therefore, to clear the ACC back to zero, an explicit **RES (Reset) instruction** must be used in a separate rung. It is commonly used for totalizing machine run-time for predictive maintenance. *(1 mark definition, 1 mark RESET reason, 1 mark application)*

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Reset (RES) for RTO** — The most common mistake. RTO *will never reset on its own*. Always draw the RES rung.
> 2. **Confusing CTU ACC direction** — Students often decrement in CTU or increment in CTD. Double-check the arrow direction.
> 3. **Skipping the timing diagram** — In any 7-mark sub-part, the timing diagram is worth at least **2 marks**. Never omit it.
> 4. **Wrong Time Base unit** — If the question says "10 seconds delay" but the TB is 0.1 s, the PRE = 100, not 10.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

#### (a) Explain the operation of TON and TOF timers with neat timing diagrams. State two industrial applications of each. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**TON Operation (3 Marks):**
- When the input rung (IN) is FALSE, the **EN, TT, and DN bits are all 0**, and ACC = 0.
- When IN becomes TRUE, **EN = 1**, and ACC starts incrementing by 1 every Time Base (TB) seconds.
- The **TT (Timer-Timing) bit = 1** while counting.
- When **ACC ≥ PRE**, the **DN bit = 1**, TT resets to 0, and the output is energized.
- If IN goes FALSE anytime, everything resets instantly.

**TOF Operation (2 Marks):**
- When IN is TRUE, DN immediately becomes 1 (instant ON), ACC = 0.
- When IN goes FALSE, ACC starts incrementing. The output stays ON.
- When ACC ≥ PRE, DN resets to 0 (delayed OFF), and the output de-energizes.

**Applications (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
- **TON:** Motor star-delta starter, sequential pump start, traffic light green-phase timing.
- **TOF:** Cooling fan run-on after furnace off, elevator door-hold-open timer, conveyor belt trailing-off.

**Timing Diagram:**

```
        ┌──── Input ─────────────────┐
IN  ____|                            |________
                                
        ┌──── TON Output ────────────┐
TON __________|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|_______
        ▲ t=0                   t=Td
                                
        ┌──── TOF Output ────────────────────┐
TOF ________________|‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾‾|__________
                                      ▲ t=Td after IN went low
```

#### (b) A conveyor belt must run for 30 seconds after a part is detected by a photoelectric sensor. Design a ladder logic program using an appropriate timer. Assume TB = 0.1 s. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Preset Value (1 Mark):**

Given $T_d = 30$ s, $TB = 0.1$ s.

$$PRE = \frac{T_d}{TB} = \frac{30}{0.1} = 300$$

**Step 2 — Identify Inputs and Outputs (1 Mark):**
- **Input:** `I:0/0` — Photo Sensor (NO contact)
- **Output:** `O:0/0` — Conveyor Motor (Contactor)

**Step 3 — Draw the Ladder Diagram (5 Marks):**

**Rung 1 — Start/Stop Latch with Timer-Trigger:**
```
|  I:0/0 (Sensor)  ] ----[ ]----[/]------------------( T4:0 TON )--|
|                       [ T4:0.DN ]                                 |
|   PRE = 300, TB = 0.1 s                                          |
```

**Rung 2 — Conveyor Output (sealed by Timer's TT bit):**
```
|  T4:0.TT (Timer Timing)  ] ----------------------------( O:0/0 Conveyor )--|
```

**Explanation:**
- The moment the photo sensor sees a part, `I:0/0` closes, the TON instruction is enabled.
- The **TT bit** stays TRUE for 30 seconds (while the timer is counting).
- The conveyor is wired to the **TT bit**, so it runs for exactly 30 s.
- When ACC = 300, DN = 1, TT = 0, and the conveyor stops.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

#### (a) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a CTU (Count-Up) counter. Mention the function of the CU, RES, DN, and OV bits. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (2 Marks):** *(Refer to Section 4.3 above for the Mermaid block diagram)*

**Operation (3 Marks):**
- The CTU counter has two control inputs: **CU (Count Up)** and **RES (Reset)**.
- Each time the CU input makes a **false-to-true transition** (rising edge), the **ACC register is incremented by 1**.
- When **ACC ≥ PRE**, the **DN bit is set to 1**.
- If ACC exceeds **32,767** (the 16-bit upper limit), the **OV (Overflow) bit is set to 1**.
- A TRUE on the **RES input** clears ACC to 0, DN to 0, and OV to 0.

**Bit Functions (2 Marks — 0.5 each):**
- **CU (Count Up):** Input that increments the counter on a rising edge.
- **RES (Reset):** Forces ACC = 0, DN = 0, OV = 0.
- **DN (Done):** Set when ACC ≥ PRE.
- **OV (Overflow):** Set when ACC > 32,767 (indicates counter has overflowed its 16-bit range).

#### (b) A bottling plant needs to count 500 bottles on a conveyor. Once 500 bottles are counted, a "Batch Complete" lamp should glow and a solenoid should stop the conveyor. Design the ladder program. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — I/O Assignment (1 Mark):**
- **Input:** `I:0/0` — Bottle Proximity Sensor (one pulse per bottle)
- **Output 1:** `O:0/0` — Conveyor Motor
- **Output 2:** `O:0/1` — Batch Complete Lamp
- **Counter:** `C5:0` CTU, PRE = 500

**Step 2 — Ladder Diagram (6 Marks):**

**Rung 1 — Counter Logic:**
```
|  I:0/0 (Sensor)  ] -----------------( C5:0 CTU )--|
|   PRE = 500                                     |
|   ACC starts at 0                               |
```

**Rung 2 — Conveyor Run with NC Contact of DN (stops at 500):**
```
|  Start_PB  ]---[/]---[ ]----------------( O:0/0 Conveyor )--|
|  Stop_PB   ]---[ ]                                      |
|  C5:0.DN   ]---[/]  (NC — breaks the seal at 500)       |
```

**Rung 3 — Batch Complete Lamp:**
```
|  C5:0.DN   ] --------------------------------( O:0/1 Lamp )--|
```

**Rung 4 — Manual Reset:**
```
|  Reset_PB  ] ---------------------------------( RES C5:0 )--|
```

**Explanation:**
- Each bottle passing the sensor gives one pulse → CTU counts up.
- When 500 is reached, DN = 1.
- The NC contact of DN in Rung 2 opens, breaking the latch and stopping the conveyor.
- The NO contact of DN in Rung 3 closes, turning ON the Batch Complete lamp.
- Pressing Reset_PB zeroes the counter and restarts the cycle.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU):**

- ✅ **TON** = On-Delay; output delayed turning **ON**; resets instantly when input goes FALSE.
- ✅ **TOF** = Off-Delay; output delayed turning **OFF**; DN is TRUE while input is TRUE.
- ✅ **RTO** = Retentive On-Delay; **never resets on its own**; requires explicit **RES** instruction.
- ✅ **CTU** counts **up**; **CTD** counts **down**; both increment/decrement only on **rising edges** (transitions), not on level.
- ✅ **Time Delay formula:** $T_d = PRE \times TB$. (e.g., $PRE = 100,\ TB = 0.1$ s $\Rightarrow T_d = 10$ s).
- ✅ **Preset Value (PRE)** is the target; **Accumulated Value (ACC)** is the current count/time.
- ✅ **DN bit** for a timer/counter = "Done" bit; it goes HIGH when the target is reached.
- ✅ **TT bit** = "Timer-Timing" — only for timers, only TRUE while counting.
- ✅ **OV bit** = Overflow, triggers if CTU ACC > **32,767**.
- ✅ **UN bit** = Underflow, triggers if CTD ACC < **−32,768**.
- ✅ For 16-bit counters, the safe operating range is $-32{,}768 \leq ACC \leq 32{,}767$.
- ✅ In ladder diagrams, always show the **RES rung** explicitly for RTO and counters.
- ✅ **Timing diagrams are mandatory** for 7+ mark questions in KTU exams.
- ✅ **Time Base (TB)** is manufacturer-specific: AB uses 0.01/0.1/1 s; Siemens uses 10 ms/100 ms/1 s.
- ✅ PLC timers are **soft-instructions** — they depend on the CPU scan; assume scan time $\ll$ TB for exam problems.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Programs in PLC
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT633 - SUPERVISORY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Simple Programs in PLC

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a ruggedized, industrial-grade digital computer designed for the real-time control of manufacturing processes and automation systems. A **Simple Program in a PLC** refers to a foundational ladder logic (or instruction list) routine that performs elementary control tasks using **logical operations (AND, OR, NOT)**, **latching mechanisms (Set/Reset)**, and **time-based or count-based sequencing** using **Timers (TON, TOF, TP)** and **Counters (CTU, CTD)** as defined by the **IEC 61131-3** standard.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to *develop, interpret, and debug simple PLC programs* using standard ladder logic conventions, identify normally open (NO) and normally closed (NC) contacts, and apply Boolean logic to physical I/O assignments.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of a PLC program as a **recipe written for an industrial chef (the CPU)**. The chef doesn't understand English, but understands a list of checks:

- **Inputs (Ingredients):** Are the buttons pressed? Are the sensors detecting parts? (`X1`, `X2`, `X3`...)
- **Logic (Recipe Steps):** "IF ingredient A is present AND ingredient B is mixed THEN turn ON the oven." (`AND` gate, `OR` gate)
- **Outputs (Final Dish):** Motors, solenoids, lights, heaters (`Y1`, `Y2`, `Y3`...)

> [!NOTE]
> **Simple Analogy — The Elevator Door:** A simple PLC program decides when the elevator door opens. It checks: "Is the cabin at the floor? (Input A) AND is the door button pressed? (Input B)" → If YES, energize the door motor (Output). Add a **timer** to keep it open for 5 seconds, and you have a *complete* simple PLC program.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology

| Term | Definition | Standard Unit |
|------|------------|---------------|
| **Scan Time** | Time taken to read inputs, execute logic, and update outputs | **milliseconds (ms)** |
| **I/O** | Input/Output physical points | Discrete (bits) |
| **Preset Value (PV)** | Target count/time for timer or counter | seconds / pulses |
| **Accumulated Value (ACC)** | Current value of timer or counter | seconds / pulses |
| **Cycle Time** | Time between two consecutive scans | **ms** |
| **Boolean Logic** | Binary logic using 0 (FALSE) and 1 (TRUE) | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition — Ladder Logic:** Ladder Logic (LD) is a graphical programming language that resembles electrical relay logic diagrams. It uses two vertical "rails" (power lines) and horizontal "rungs" (logic instructions) to represent the control sequence.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Simple PLC Program

A simple PLC program consists of **three fundamental blocks**:

1. **Input Scan (Reading Sensors):** All physical inputs (limit switches, push buttons, proximity sensors) are read into the **Input Image Table (IIT)**. This happens once per scan cycle.
2. **Logic Execution (Program Scan):** The CPU processes the user program **rung-by-rung, top-to-bottom** using the values stored in the IIT. Boolean algebra is evaluated to determine intermediate results.
3. **Output Scan (Writing to Actuators):** The final logical results are written from the **Output Image Table (OIT)** to the physical output terminals (motor contactors, indicator lamps, valves).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** In KTU exams, students often forget the *scan cycle order*. If the same address (e.g., `Q0.0`) is used in two rungs, only the **last assignment** during the program scan is latched into the OIT. The first one is overwritten.

## 2.2 Building Blocks of Simple Programs

### A. Basic Logic Gates in Ladder Logic

| Logic Gate | Boolean Expression | Ladder Implementation |
|------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **AND** | $Y = A \cdot B$ | Two NO contacts in **series** |
| **OR** | $Y = A + B$ | Two NO contacts in **parallel** |
| **NOT** | $Y = \overline{A}$ | One **NC** contact |
| **NAND** | $Y = \overline{A \cdot B}$ | Two NO contacts in series + NC at output |
| **NOR** | $Y = \overline{A + B}$ | Two NO contacts in parallel + NC at output |
| **XOR** | $Y = A \oplus B$ | $Y = (A \cdot \overline{B}) + (\overline{A} \cdot B)$ |

### B. Latch / Unlatch (Set / Reset) Circuits

A **latch** holds an output ON *even after the input condition becomes FALSE*. This is critical for **start-stop motor control** circuits.

- **Set (Latch):** Output remains ON once activated.
- **Reset (Unlatch):** Output turns OFF.

The Boolean relation is:
$$Q_{n} = (S + Q_{n-1}) \cdot \overline{R}$$

where $S$ is the Set signal, $R$ is the Reset signal, and $Q_{n-1}$ is the previous state.

### C. Timer Instructions

| Timer Type | Symbol | Behavior |
|------------|--------|----------|
| **ON-Delay (TON)** | Timer On | Output turns ON **after** a preset delay |
| **OFF-Delay (TOF)** | Timer Off | Output turns OFF **after** a preset delay |
| **Retentive (RTO)** | Retentive Timer | Retains accumulated value through power loss |

The accumulated value follows:
$$ACC_{n} = ACC_{n-1} + \Delta t$$

where $\Delta t$ is the elapsed time base resolution (e.g., **100 ms** per tick).

Output energization occurs when:
$$ACC \geq PV$$

### D. Counter Instructions

| Counter Type | Symbol | Behavior |
|-------------|--------|----------|
| **CTU (Count Up)** | Counter Up | Increments on each input pulse |
| **CTD (Count Down)** | Counter Down | Decrements on each input pulse |

The counting relation is:
$$C_{n} = C_{n-1} \pm 1$$

Counter output activates when:
$$C \geq PV \quad \text{(for CTU)} \quad \text{or} \quad C \leq 0 \quad \text{(for CTD)}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Description | Units |
|---|-------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $T_{scan} = T_I + T_P + T_O$ | Total scan time = Input scan + Program scan + Output scan | **ms** |
| 2 | $T_{delay} = PV \times T_{base}$ | Effective timer delay = Preset $\times$ Time base | **seconds** |
| 3 | $ACC_{n} = ACC_{n-1} + T_{base}$ | Accumulator increment per scan | **seconds** |
| 4 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{T_{scan}}$ | Maximum countable input frequency | **Hz** |
| 5 | $Q_n = (S + Q_{n-1}) \cdot \overline{R}$ | Latch (Set-Reset) Boolean equation | Boolean |
| 6 | $Y = A \cdot B \cdot C$ | Series contact = AND logic | Boolean |
| 7 | $Y = A + B + C$ | Parallel contact = OR logic | Boolean |
| 8 | $Y = A \oplus B = (A \cdot \overline{B}) + (\overline{A} \cdot B)$ | Exclusive-OR expression | Boolean |
| 9 | $N_{required} = \lceil \log_2(\text{states}) \rceil$ | Number of bits for state encoding | bits |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in real-world conveyor sorting systems, bottle-filling plants, traffic signal controllers, and elevator logic. Engineers compute $T_{scan}$ to ensure PLC responsiveness is faster than the fastest sensor event, typically keeping $T_{scan} < 20$ **ms** for high-speed applications.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Conveyor Sorting:** Count products using **CTU** and divert them via a pneumatic cylinder triggered by a comparator.
- **Traffic Light Controller:** Use **TON timers** to sequence Red → Green → Yellow with precise dwell times.
- **Bottle Filling:** Use a **CTU counter** to count filled bottles and stop the conveyor at a preset batch quantity.
- **Motor Star-Delta Starter:** Use **three TON timers** for sequential switching (Star-run → Pause → Delta-run).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Timer Delay from Preset Value

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Context:** A TON timer in a Siemens S7-1200 PLC has a time base $T_{base} = 100 \text{ ms}$ and preset $PV = 25$. Compute the effective ON-delay.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters.**

We are given:
$$T_{base} = 100 \text{ ms} = 0.1 \text{ s}$$
$$PV = 25 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the KTU standard timer formula.**

The effective delay is the product of the preset count and the time base:
$$T_{delay} = PV \times T_{base}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$T_{delay} = 25 \times 0.1 \text{ s}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the final result.**

$$T_{delay} = 2.5 \text{ seconds}$$

**Conclusion:** The output energizes exactly **2.5 seconds** after the timer enable input becomes TRUE.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Maximum Countable Frequency from Scan Time

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Context:** A PLC has a scan time $T_{scan} = 10 \text{ ms}$. Determine the maximum input pulse frequency that the CTU counter can reliably count without missing pulses.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — State the underlying physical principle.**

A counter increments **once per scan** if the input transition is detected. To count reliably, the input pulse must remain HIGH for at least **one complete scan cycle**.

**Step 2 — Define the Nyquist-like relation for counting.**

For reliable counting, the pulse period $T_{pulse}$ must be at least twice the scan time:
$$T_{pulse} \geq 2 \times T_{scan}$$

**Step 3 — Convert period to frequency.**

Frequency is the reciprocal of period:
$$f = \dfrac{1}{T_{pulse}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the maximum allowable period.**

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times T_{scan}} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times 0.010 \text{ s}}$$

**Step 5 — Compute.**

$$f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{0.020} = 50 \text{ Hz}$$

**Conclusion:** The counter can reliably count pulses up to **50 Hz**. Beyond this, the PLC will miss transitions and produce erroneous counts.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Start-Stop Latch Logic Simplification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Context:** Verify the Boolean equation of a motor start-stop circuit with overload protection.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the input variables.**

Let $S$ = Start push button (NO), $St$ = Stop push button (NC), $OL$ = Overload relay contact (NC), and $M$ = Motor output.

**Step 2 — Write the parallel branch equation.**

The motor runs if Start is pressed **OR** the motor is already latched (feedback contact), **AND** the stop and overload are not activated:
$$M = (S + M) \cdot \overline{St} \cdot \overline{OL}$$

**Step 3 — Expand using Boolean algebra.**

Apply the distributive law:
$$M = (S \cdot \overline{St} \cdot \overline{OL}) + (M \cdot \overline{St} \cdot \overline{OL})$$

**Step 4 — Apply the absorption identity.**

Since $M = M \cdot \overline{St} \cdot \overline{OL} + (S \cdot \overline{St} \cdot \overline{OL})$ and assuming the feedback $M$ already implies the safety conditions were met:
$$M_{n} = (S + M_{n-1}) \cdot \overline{St} \cdot \overline{OL}$$

**Conclusion:** This matches the canonical **Set-Reset latch** form, with combined reset condition $R = St + OL$.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Simple Program Classification

| Program Type | Primary Instructions | Typical Application | Marks Weightage (KTU) |
|--------------|----------------------|---------------------|------------------------|
| Logic Gates | NO, NC contacts | Signal conditioning | 3 marks (Part A) |
| Latch / Unlatch | Set, Reset, SR/RS flip-flop | Motor start-stop | 7 marks (Part B) |
| ON-Delay Timer | TON | Delayed start, debouncing | 7–14 marks |
| OFF-Delay Timer | TOF | Cooling fan, light-off delay | 7 marks |
| Up Counter | CTU | Batch counting, production tally | 7–14 marks |
| Down Counter | CTD | Inventory depletion tracking | 7 marks |
| Star-Delta Starter | 3 × TON + 3 outputs | 3-phase motor starting | 14 marks |
| Traffic Light | 4–6 × TON with sequencer | Road junction control | 14 marks |
| Conveyor Sorting | CTU + Comparator + Cylinder | Packaging line | 14 marks |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Traffic Light Sequencer

**Problem:** Design a ladder program for a 4-state traffic light using TON timers. State durations: Red = 30 s, Green = 25 s, Yellow = 5 s.

**Step 1 — Identify states and timer presets (with $T_{base} = 1 \text{ s}$).**

$$PV_{Red} = 30, \quad PV_{Green} = 25, \quad PV_{Yellow} = 5$$

**Step 2 — Establish state transition logic.**

The output of each timer becomes the enable input of the next state:

$$T_{Red}.Q \rightarrow \text{Enable } T_{Green}$$
$$T_{Green}.Q \rightarrow \text{Enable } T_{Yellow}$$
$$T_{Yellow}.Q \rightarrow \text{Reset all} \rightarrow \text{Enable } T_{Red}$$

**Step 3 — Total cycle time verification.**

$$T_{cycle} = 30 + 25 + 5 = 60 \text{ seconds}$$

This is consistent with a standard 1-minute traffic signal cycle.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 PLC Scan Cycle Flow

This diagram illustrates the **continuous scan cycle** of a simple PLC program, which is fundamental to understanding timing behavior.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power ON Start] --> B[Initialize Memory and I O Image Tables]
    B --> C[Read Physical Inputs to Input Image Table]
    C --> D[Execute Ladder Logic Rung 1 to N]
    D --> E[Update Output Image Table]
    E --> F[Write Outputs to Physical Terminals]
    F --> C
    F -.Periodic Watchdog.-> G[Diagnostics and Communication]
    G --> C
```

## 4.2 Start-Stop Latch Rung (Motor Control)

This rung implements the canonical **Set-Reset latch** pattern for a 3-phase motor with overload protection.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    L1[L+ Power Rail] --> N1[Stop PB NC - I0.1]
    N1 --> N2[Overload NC - I0.2]
    N2 --> N3{Start PB NO - I0.0}
    N3 -->|Press| O1[Motor Contactor - Q0.0]
    N2 --> N4[Motor Feedback NO - I0.3]
    N4 --> O1
    O1 --> L2[L- Return Rail]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Read this ladder as:** Power flows from L+ through Stop (NC) and Overload (NC). If Start (NO) is pressed OR the feedback contact from the motor (NO) is closed, then the motor output Q0.0 is energized. Pressing Stop breaks the circuit and de-energizes the coil.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture for a Conveyor Counter

This block diagram shows a typical simple PLC program architecture with input conditioning, counting logic, and output actuation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph InputModule[Input Module]
        S1[Proximity Sensor I0.0] --> F1[Input Debounce Filter]
        S2[Reset Pushbutton I0.1] --> F1
        S3[Enable Selector I0.2] --> F1
    end
    F1 --> C1[CTU Counter Block]
    C1 --> C2{Compare C with Preset PV}
    C2 -->|C greater equal PV| R1[Set Output Q0.0]
    C2 -->|C less than PV| R2[Reset Output Q0.0]
    R1 --> A1[Conveyor Motor Starter]
    R2 --> A1
    A1 --> O1[Indicator Lamp Q0.1]
```

## 4.4 Timer Sequence — ON-Delay Waveform

The diagram below shows the relationship between the **enable input (IN)**, **accumulator (ACC)**, **preset (PV)**, and **output (Q)** of a TON timer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TON[TON Timer Block]
        IN[Enable Input IN] --> T1[Internal Clock Tbase]
        T1 --> ACC[Accumulator ACC]
        ACC --> CMP{ACC greater equal PV}
        CMP -->|YES| Q[Output Q = 1]
        CMP -->|NO| Q2[Output Q = 0]
    end
    PV[Preset Value PV] --> CMP
```

**Timing Behavior:**

- When `IN = 1`, `ACC` begins incrementing at each time-base tick.
- When `ACC ≥ PV`, output `Q` becomes `1`.
- When `IN = 0`, `ACC` resets to `0` and `Q` returns to `0` immediately.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define a Programmable Logic Controller. List any four advantages of PLCs over conventional relay-based control systems.**

**Model Answer (Key Points):**

A PLC is a digital industrial computer that uses programmable memory to store instructions for implementing specific functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations to control industrial machines and processes.

**Four Advantages of PLCs over Relay Logic:**
1. **Flexibility:** Program can be modified without rewiring hardware.
2. **Reliability:** Solid-state components with no moving parts; MTBF > 100,000 hours.
3. **Compactness:** A single PLC replaces hundreds of relays and timers.
4. **Diagnostic Capability:** Built-in LEDs, fault indicators, and communication ports for remote monitoring.
5. **Lower Long-term Cost:** Reduced downtime and easier troubleshooting.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Explain the difference between a TON and a TOF timer with a suitable timing diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | TON (On-Delay) | TOF (Off-Delay) |
|---------|----------------|-----------------|
| Operation | Output turns ON after input has been TRUE for a preset time | Output turns OFF after input has been FALSE for a preset time |
| When IN = 1 | ACC starts incrementing | Output Q = 1 immediately, ACC = 0 |
| When IN = 0 | Q = 0 immediately, ACC = 0 | ACC starts incrementing; Q = 0 when ACC ≥ PV |
| Use Case | Delayed motor start | Fan continues running after machine stop |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Design a ladder logic program for a **3-phase induction motor Star-Delta starter** with the following sequence:
- Press Start → Motor runs in Star mode for 10 seconds.
- Transition to Delta mode (with 1 second pause between Star and Delta to avoid short circuit).
- A Stop button immediately stops the motor.
- Overload relay must trip the system if activated.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the operation of an **up-counter (CTU)** with a suitable ladder diagram. A counter is set with a preset value of 50. If the input pulse frequency is 2 Hz, calculate the time taken to reach the preset value.

---

### Model Answer for Question A(a) — Star-Delta Starter

**Solution Outline:**

**Rung 1 — Master Start-Stop Latch:**
- Series: `Stop_NC` → `Overload_NC` → (parallel: `Start_NO` || `RunFeedback_NO`) → `Motor_Run` coil.

**Rung 2 — Star Contactor (with Timer T1):**
- Branch: `Motor_Run` → `TON_T1` (PV = 10 s) → `Star_Contactor` coil.
- The Star contactor is energized only while the motor is running AND T1 has not yet timed out.

**Rung 3 — Delta Contactor (with Timer T2 for 1 s pause):**
- Branch: `Motor_Run` → `T1.Q` (NC, opens when T1 times out) → `TON_T2` (PV = 1 s) → `Delta_Contactor` coil.
- Note: T2's NC contact ensures the 1-second delay after T1 expires.

**Rung 4 — Electrical Interlock (Safety):**
- Place `Star_Contactor_NC` contact in series with `Delta_Contactor` coil and vice versa to prevent simultaneous energization.

**Boolean Logic Summary:**

$$Motor\_Run = (Start + Motor\_Run_{prev}) \cdot \overline{Stop} \cdot \overline{OL}$$

$$Star = Motor\_Run \cdot \overline{T1.Q}$$

$$Delta = Motor\_Run \cdot T1.Q \cdot T2.Q$$

**Marks Distribution (as per KTU):**
- 2 marks: Correct identification of start-stop latch requirement.
- 2 marks: Correct use of TON timers for sequencing.
- 2 marks: Interlock logic between Star and Delta.
- 1 mark: Overload handling and neatness.

---

### Model Answer for Question A(b) — CTU Counter and Time Calculation

**Operation of CTU Counter:**

A CTU (Count Up) counter increments its accumulator by 1 on every rising edge of its count input (CU). When the accumulator value $C$ reaches or exceeds the preset value $PV$, the output $Q$ is set to 1. A separate reset input (R) clears the accumulator to 0.

**Ladder Diagram Description:**
- Rung 1: `Pulse_Input` → CU input of `C1`.
- Rung 2: `Reset_PB` → R input of `C1`.
- Rung 3: `C1.Q` → `Output_Lamp`.

**Time Calculation:**

Given:
$$PV = 50 \text{ pulses}, \quad f = 2 \text{ Hz}$$

The time period of one pulse:
$$T_{pulse} = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{1}{2} = 0.5 \text{ seconds}$$

Total time to reach preset:
$$T_{total} = PV \times T_{pulse} = 50 \times 0.5$$

$$T_{total} = 25 \text{ seconds}$$

**Final Answer:** The output lamp will turn ON after **25 seconds**.

---

### Question B (Alternative Choice): [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Implement the following Boolean expression in ladder logic and explain each rung:
$$Y = (A \cdot B) + (\overline{C} \cdot D)$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** A conveyor belt carries bottles. A sensor counts the bottles. When 12 bottles are counted, the conveyor stops automatically and a pilot lamp glows. A reset button clears the counter. Develop the ladder program and calculate the time to count 12 bottles if each bottle takes 1.5 seconds to pass the sensor.

---

### Model Answer for Question B(a) — Boolean to Ladder

**Interpretation:** The expression has two parallel branches:
- Branch 1: $A \cdot B$ → Series connection of NO contacts A and B.
- Branch 2: $\overline{C} \cdot D$ → NC contact of C in series with NO contact of D.
- Both branches in parallel drive output Y.

**Ladder Description:**

```
    L+ ──[ A ]──[ B ]──+──( Y )── L-
                         |
    L+ ──[ /C ]──[ D ]──+
```

**Explanation of Each Rung:**
- **Branch 1 (Top):** Power flows when both A and B are TRUE simultaneously (AND logic).
- **Branch 2 (Bottom):** Power flows when C is FALSE (NC contact closed) AND D is TRUE (AND logic with inverted C).
- **Output Y:** Energized if either branch completes the circuit (OR logic).

**Marks Distribution (as per KTU):**
- 2 marks: Correct Boolean interpretation.
- 3 marks: Correct ladder translation.
- 2 marks: Clear explanation.

---

### Model Answer for Question B(b) — Bottle Counter with Time

**Ladder Program Description:**

- **Rung 1:** `Sensor_I0.0` → CU input of `C1` (CTU, PV = 12).
- **Rung 2:** `Reset_PB_I0.1` → R input of `C1`.
- **Rung 3:** `C1.Q` → `Conveyor_Motor_Q0.0` (NC contact, stops motor when C1.Q = 1).
- **Rung 4:** `C1.Q` → `Pilot_Lamp_Q0.1` (NO contact, turns lamp ON).

**Time Calculation:**

Given:
$$PV = 12 \text{ bottles}, \quad T_{bottle} = 1.5 \text{ seconds/bottle}$$

$$T_{total} = PV \times T_{bottle} = 12 \times 1.5$$

$$T_{total} = 18 \text{ seconds}$$

**Final Answer:** The conveyor stops and the pilot lamp glows after **18 seconds**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting Interlocks in Star-Delta:** Students lose 2–3 marks by not adding NC contacts of the Star contactor in the Delta rung (and vice versa). This is a **critical safety requirement** and examiners specifically check for it.
> 2. **Wrong Timer Type:** Using TOF instead of TON in traffic light or motor delay problems is a frequent error. Re-read the question — if it says "turn ON after 5 seconds," it is **TON**.
> 3. **Scan Time Ignorance:** Students often assume an input pulse is detected every time without considering $T_{scan}$. Always verify that the pulse width $> T_{scan}$.
> 4. **Latching Without Reset:** Writing a latch without a corresponding unlatch (reset) rung is incomplete. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks for unsafe latches that cannot be stopped.
> 5. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Mixing up ms and s in timer calculations is a common mistake. Always convert $T_{base}$ to match the desired output unit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **PLC** executes programs in a continuous **scan cycle**: Input scan → Program scan → Output scan.
- **Ladder Logic** uses two vertical power rails and horizontal rungs; power flows from left to right.
- **NO contact** = TRUE when input is ON; **NC contact** = TRUE when input is OFF.
- **Series contacts = AND logic**; **Parallel branches = OR logic**.
- **Latch (Set)** holds output ON; **Unlatch (Reset)** turns it OFF — always pair them for safety.
- **TON timer** delays the ON transition; **TOF timer** delays the OFF transition; **RTO** is retentive.
- **CTU counter** increments on input pulses; output activates when $C \geq PV$.
- Effective timer delay: $T_{delay} = PV \times T_{base}$.
- Maximum countable frequency: $f_{max} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times T_{scan}}$.
- Latch Boolean form: $Q_n = (S + Q_{n-1}) \cdot \overline{R}$.
- **Always add overload and interlock contacts** in motor control programs — KTU examiners reward safety.
- **Address conventions:** Inputs typically use `I` or `X`; outputs use `Q` or `Y`; memory bits use `M`.
- **Star-Delta starter** requires **three** TON timers: T1 (Star run time), T2 (transition pause), and T3 (optional full-run indicator).
- **Counter time** for fixed pulse interval: $T = PV \times T_{pulse}$.
- For high-speed counting, ensure pulse width $> T_{scan}$, otherwise use **high-speed counter (HSC)** modules.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Math Operations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT633 - SUPERVISORY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Math Operations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 1 – Math Operations in Supervisory Control

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA)** and **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** systems, **Math Operations** (also termed *Arithmetic and Logical Instructions* or *Data Manipulation Instructions*) refer to the standardized set of computational instructions executed by the controller's Central Processing Unit (CPU) to perform numerical calculations, logical evaluations, comparisons, and data transformations on variables stored in memory locations, Input/Output (I/O) registers, timers, and counters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**  
> Math Operations in Supervisory Control encompass all processor-level instructions that operate on binary, integer, floating-point, and BCD (Binary-Coded Decimal) data types to facilitate real-time process automation, data acquisition, and control logic execution.

These operations are fundamental building blocks of every PLC program, forming the **arithmetic logic unit (ALU)** functions within the controller's scan cycle. According to the **IEC 61131-3** international standard (which KTU 2024 follows), math operations are classified into four primary categories:

1. **Arithmetic Operations** — Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication, Division, Modulo
2. **Comparison Operations** — Equal, Not Equal, Greater Than, Greater Than or Equal, Less Than, Less Than or Equal
3. **Logical / Bitwise Operations** — AND, OR, XOR, NOT (Inversion)
4. **Mathematical Functions** — Square Root, Absolute Value, Trigonometric, Logarithmic, Exponential, Statistical

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Factory Floor Calculator":**  
> Imagine you are the supervisor of a bottling plant. You have 5 machines filling bottles at different rates. To know the **total output**, you need to **add** the outputs (Arithmetic). To know if **Machine 3 is running faster than Machine 1**, you need to **compare** their rates (Comparison). To check if **ALL safety sensors are active** (sensor 1 AND sensor 2 AND sensor 3 must be ON), you use **logical AND**. To calculate the **average fill volume**, you need to **sum and divide** (Mathematical Functions).  
>  
> Math operations in a PLC are exactly this — a high-speed, industrial-grade calculator that runs millions of such operations per second, making real-time decisions to keep the process running safely and efficiently.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value | Unit |
|-----------|---------------|------|
| PLC Scan Cycle Time | **10 – 50 ms** | milliseconds |
| Typical Integer Data Width | **16-bit** (Word) | bits |
| Floating Point Precision | **32-bit** (IEEE 754 Single Precision) | bits |
| Analog Input Resolution | **12-bit to 16-bit** | bits |
| BCD Digit Range | **0 – 9 per nibble** | decimal digits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:**  
> The data type selected for a math operation directly impacts **memory consumption, computation speed, and precision**. For example, using a 32-bit float for a simple counter (which only needs 16-bit) wastes memory and increases scan time.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Math Operations — Structured Logic Breakdown

### 2.1.1 Arithmetic Operations

Arithmetic operations perform mathematical computation on two source operands and store the result in a destination register. In ladder logic, the format is:

**`DEST = SOURCE_A [OPERATOR] SOURCE_B`**

| Operation | Symbol | Description | Result Range (16-bit) |
|-----------|--------|-------------|----------------------|
| Addition | `+` | Sum of two values | $-32{,}768$ to $+32{,}767$ |
| Subtraction | `-` | Difference of two values | $-32{,}768$ to $+32{,}767$ |
| Multiplication | `*` | Product of two values | $-32{,}768$ to $+32{,}767$ (truncated) |
| Division | `/` | Quotient (integer part only) | $-32{,}768$ to $+32{,}767$ |
| Modulo | `MOD` | Remainder after division | $0$ to $32{,}767$ |
| Negation | `NEG` | Two's complement sign reversal | $-32{,}768$ to $+32{,}767$ |
| Increment | `INC` | Add 1 to accumulator | — |
| Decrement | `DEC` | Subtract 1 from accumulator | — |

### 2.1.2 Comparison Operations

Comparison operations are **purely logical** — they produce a Boolean (TRUE/FALSE) output based on the relationship between two values. The result is NOT a number; it is a single bit (1 or 0).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Comparisons Matter in SCADA:**  
> In a SCADA system, comparison instructions drive **conditional logic**. For example: "IF tank_level > 90% THEN start_pump_2 = TRUE". This is the foundation of every alarm, interlock, and automated control decision.

### 2.1.3 Logical / Bitwise Operations

Bitwise operations perform logic on individual bits within a word:

- **AND**: Result bit is 1 only if BOTH input bits are 1
- **OR**: Result bit is 1 if EITHER input bit is 1
- **XOR (Exclusive OR)**: Result bit is 1 if input bits are DIFFERENT
- **NOT (Inversion / Complement)**: Flips every bit (1 → 0, 0 → 1)

### 2.1.4 Mathematical Functions

Advanced functions commonly found in modern PLCs (Allen-Bradley, Siemens S7, Schneider M340):

| Function | Mathematical Notation | Engineering Use Case |
|----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Square Root | $\sqrt{x}$ | Flow rate from differential pressure |
| Absolute Value | $\vert x \vert$ | Error magnitude calculation |
| Sine | $\sin(\theta)$ | Trigonometric positioning |
| Cosine | $\cos(\theta)$ | Robotic arm angle calculation |
| Natural Logarithm | $\ln(x)$ | Exponential decay processes |
| Exponential | $e^x$ | Growth model calculations |
| Power | $x^y$ | Engineering scaling |
| Scale / Linear Conversion | $y = mx + c$ | Raw ADC to engineering units |

## 2.2 The Core "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

**Step 1 — Data Fetch:** The CPU retrieves the values from the specified source addresses during the input scan or memory read phase.

**Step 2 — Operation Execution:** The ALU within the CPU performs the mathematical or logical operation using binary arithmetic at the register level.

**Step 3 — Result Storage:** The computed result is written to the destination address.

**Step 4 — Status Flag Update:** Special relay flags are updated:
- **Zero Flag (Z)**: Set to 1 if result = 0
- **Carry Flag (C)**: Set to 1 if result exceeds data type range
- **Overflow Flag (V)**: Set to 1 on signed arithmetic overflow
- **Sign Flag (S)**: Set to 1 if result is negative (MSB = 1)

**Step 5 — Output Update:** If the result drives an output coil, the physical output is energized/de-energized during the output scan phase.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MUST MEMORIZE FOR KTU EXAMS**

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|-------------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | Linear Scaling (ADC to Engineering Units) | $E = \left( \frac{A - A_{min}}{A_{max} - A_{min}} \right) \times (E_{max} - E_{min}) + E_{min}$ | $E$ = Engineering value, $A$ = ADC count |
| 2 | Percentage Calculation | $\% = \frac{\text{Actual}}{\text{Full Scale}} \times 100$ | Dimensionless |
| 3 | Moving Average (n samples) | $\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | $n$ = window size |
| 4 | PID Output (Proportional Term) | $P_{out} = K_p \times e(t)$ | $K_p$ = Proportional gain, $e$ = error |
| 5 | PID Output (Integral Term) | $I_{out} = K_i \int_0^t e(\tau) \, d\tau$ | $K_i$ = Integral gain |
| 6 | PID Output (Derivative Term) | $D_{out} = K_d \frac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $K_d$ = Derivative gain |
| 7 | Flow from Differential Pressure | $Q = C \sqrt{\Delta P}$ | $C$ = Orifice constant |
| 8 | Two's Complement (Negative Number) | $-x = \bar{x} + 1$ | Bitwise NOT + 1 |
| 9 | Modulo Operation | $a \, \text{MOD} \, b = a - b \times \lfloor a/b \rfloor$ | $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ = floor function |
| 10 | Bitwise AND Masking | $A \, \& \, M = \text{Extracted Bits}$ | $M$ = Mask pattern |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL EXAM TIP:** KTU 2024 examiners frequently test the **Linear Scaling formula** and **Two's Complement negation**. Master these two — they appear in nearly every Part B question involving analog I/O.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Math operations are the **backbone of every industrial process**:

- **Oil & Gas (Petroleum Refineries):** Flow computation using orifice plate equations, tank level percentage calculation, compressor surge detection via real-time comparison.
- **Power Plants:** Steam temperature averaging across multiple thermocouples, turbine speed calculation from encoder pulses, generator frequency monitoring.
- **Water Treatment:** pH neutralization control using PID math, chemical dosing rate calculation, filter differential pressure trending.
- **Manufacturing:** Robotic arm trajectory calculation using sine/cosine, conveyor speed synchronization, quality control statistical analysis.
- **Building Automation:** HVAC energy consumption calculation, occupancy-based lighting control using comparison logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Two's Complement Representation (Negative Number Formation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Asked Derivation — Practice this step-by-step for full marks!**

### Problem: Convert the decimal number $-47$ into its 16-bit Two's Complement binary representation.

**Step 1 — Represent the positive magnitude (47) in binary:**

$$
47_{10} = 32 + 8 + 4 + 2 + 1
$$

$$
47_{10} = 2^5 + 2^3 + 2^2 + 2^1 + 2^0
$$

**Conversion Logic:** We identify powers of 2 that sum to 47.

$$
47_{10} = 0010\,1111_2 \quad \text{(8-bit representation)}
$$

**Step 2 — Extend to 16-bit width (pad with leading zeros):**

$$
47_{10} = 0000\,0000\,0010\,1111_2
$$

**Step 3 — Invert all bits (One's Complement / Bitwise NOT):**

$$
\overline{0000\,0000\,0010\,1111} = 1111\,1111\,1101\,0000
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Every 0 becomes 1, and every 1 becomes 0.

**Step 4 — Add 1 to the inverted value (Two's Complement):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
1111\,1111\,1101\,0000 \\
+\, 0000\,0000\,0000\,0001 \\
\hline
1111\,1111\,1101\,0001
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Adding 1 produces the Two's Complement representation, which is how the PLC's ALU internally represents $-47$.

**Final Result:**

$$
-47_{10} = 1111\,1111\,1101\,0001_2
$$

### Verification (Adding 47 and -47 should give zero):

$$
\begin{aligned}
& \,0000\,0000\,0010\,1111 \quad (+47) \\
+& \,1111\,1111\,1101\,0001 \quad (-47) \\
\hline
& \,1\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000
\end{aligned}
$$

The carry bit (1) is discarded in 16-bit arithmetic, leaving all zeros — confirming the representation is correct.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Linear Scaling Formula for Analog Input

> [!NOTE]
> **This derivation is tested in nearly every KTU SCADA/Supervisory Control exam. Master it completely.**

### Problem: A PLC receives a raw analog input from a pressure transmitter.  
- Raw input range: **4000 to 20000 counts** (from a 16-bit ADC)  
- Engineering range: **0 to 10 bar**  
- Current raw reading: **12000 counts**  
- **Find:** Engineering pressure value in bar

### The General Linear Scaling Formula:

$$
E = \left( \frac{A - A_{min}}{A_{max} - A_{min}} \right) \times (E_{max} - E_{min}) + E_{min}
$$

Where:
- $E$ = Engineering (output) value
- $A$ = Current ADC count (input value)
- $A_{min}, A_{max}$ = Input range limits
- $E_{min}, E_{max}$ = Engineering range limits

### Step 1 — Substitute known values into the formula:

$$
E = \left( \frac{12000 - 4000}{20000 - 4000} \right) \times (10 - 0) + 0
$$

**Conversion Logic:** $A = 12000$, $A_{min} = 4000$, $A_{max} = 20000$, $E_{min} = 0$, $E_{max} = 10$.

### Step 2 — Compute the numerator of the fraction:

$$
12000 - 4000 = 8000
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Subtract the minimum raw count from the current raw count to find how far the current reading is above the zero point.

### Step 3 — Compute the denominator of the fraction:

$$
20000 - 4000 = 16000
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Subtract the minimum from the maximum raw count to find the total input span.

### Step 4 — Compute the fraction (normalized position):

$$
\frac{8000}{16000} = 0.5
$$

**Conversion Logic:** This tells us the current reading is at 50% of the full input range.

### Step 5 — Multiply by the engineering span:

$$
0.5 \times (10 - 0) = 0.5 \times 10 = 5.0
$$

**Conversion Logic:** 50% of the 10 bar span equals 5 bar.

### Step 6 — Add the engineering minimum offset:

$$
E = 5.0 + 0 = 5.0 \, \text{bar}
$$

### Final Answer:

$$
\boxed{E = 5.0 \, \text{bar}}
$$

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Moving Average Filter (Noise Reduction)

### Problem: Compute a 5-point moving average for the following temperature readings (in °C):  
$\{68, 72, 70, 74, 66, 71, 69, 73, 67, 72\}$

### The General Formula:

$$
\bar{x}_k = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=k-n+1}^{k} x_i
$$

Where $n = 5$ (window size), and $k$ is the current sample index.

### Step 1 — Compute the average for the first valid window (samples 1–5):

$$
\bar{x}_5 = \frac{68 + 72 + 70 + 74 + 66}{5} = \frac{350}{5} = 70.0\,°C
$$

**Conversion Logic:** Sum all 5 values and divide by window size.

### Step 2 — Compute the average for the second window (samples 2–6):

$$
\bar{x}_6 = \frac{72 + 70 + 74 + 66 + 71}{5} = \frac{353}{5} = 70.6\,°C
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the average for the third window (samples 3–7):

$$
\bar{x}_7 = \frac{70 + 74 + 66 + 71 + 69}{5} = \frac{350}{5} = 70.0\,°C
$$

### Step 4 — Tabulated Results:

| Window | Samples | Sum | Average (°C) |
|--------|---------|-----|--------------|
| 1 | 68, 72, 70, 74, 66 | 350 | 70.0 |
| 2 | 72, 70, 74, 66, 71 | 353 | 70.6 |
| 3 | 70, 74, 66, 71, 69 | 350 | 70.0 |
| 4 | 74, 66, 71, 69, 73 | 353 | 70.6 |
| 5 | 66, 71, 69, 73, 67 | 346 | 69.2 |
| 6 | 71, 69, 73, 67, 72 | 352 | 70.4 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The moving average smooths out the spike at sample 4 (74°C) and the dip at sample 5 (66°C), producing a stable trend suitable for SCADA trending displays.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Operation Selection Guide

| If the Control Objective Is... | Use This Operation | Typical PLC Instruction |
|-------------------------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Sum flow rates from 3 pipes | Arithmetic Addition | `ADD` (Allen-Bradley) / `+` (IEC 61131-3) |
| Compare tank level to setpoint | Comparison | `GRT` / `GEQ` / `LES` / `LEQ` |
| Check if 2 safety conditions are both met | Logical AND | `AND` |
| Convert raw ADC to engineering units | Linear Scaling | `SCP` (Scale with Parameters) |
| Calculate reactor temperature trend | Moving Average | User-defined function block |
| Reverse direction of conveyor | Bitwise XOR / NOT | `XOR` / `INV` |
| Detect overflow in tank | Comparison + Alarm | `GRT` with limit value |
| Compute pump efficiency | Division | `DIV` |
| PID Temperature Control | Addition + Scaling | `PID` instruction block |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Math Operations in a PLC Scan Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On / Scan Start] --> B[Input Scan]
    B --> C[Read All I/O Values into Input Image Table]
    C --> D[Program Scan Execution]
    D --> E{Math Operation Encountered?}
    E -->|No| F[Continue Program Scan]
    E -->|Yes| G[Fetch Source A from Memory]
    G --> H[Fetch Source B from Memory]
    H --> I[ALU Executes Operation]
    I --> J{Operation Type?}
    J -->|Arithmetic| K[Addition Subtraction Multiplication Division Modulo]
    J -->|Comparison| L[Equal NotEqual Greater GreaterEqual Less LessEqual]
    J -->|Logical| M[AND OR XOR NOT Inversion]
    J -->|Math Function| N[SQRT ABS SIN COS LN EXP]
    K --> O[Update Status Flags Z C V S]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Store Result in Destination]
    P --> Q[Set Output Coils if Applicable]
    Q --> F
    F --> R[Output Scan]
    R --> S[Write Output Image Table to Physical Outputs]
    S --> T[Housekeeping Communication Diagnostics]
    T --> U[Return to Step B Next Scan]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Flowchart:**  
> This diagram illustrates the complete scan cycle of a PLC when it encounters a math operation. The CPU fetches two source operands, routes them to the ALU, performs the operation based on its type (Arithmetic, Comparison, Logical, or Mathematical Function), updates the status flags, and stores the result — all within a single scan cycle typically lasting 10–50 ms.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Data Flow in a SCADA Math Operation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Field["FIELD LEVEL - Sensors and Actuators"]
        PT[Pressure Transmitter 4-20 mA]
        TT[Temperature Sensor RTD]
        FT[Flow Meter Pulse Output]
    end
    
    subgraph PLC["CONTROL LEVEL - PLC Processor"]
        AI[Analog Input Module 16-bit ADC]
        DI[Digital Input Module]
        CPU[CPU with ALU and Memory]
        AO[Analog Output Module]
        DO[Digital Output Module]
    end
    
    subgraph SCADA["SUPERVISORY LEVEL - SCADA HMI"]
        HMI[Operator HMI Screen]
        DB[Historical Database]
        ALM[Alarm Management System]
    end
    
    PT -->|4-20 mA Signal| AI
    TT -->|Resistance Signal| AI
    FT -->|Pulse Train| DI
    AI -->|Digital Counts| CPU
    DI -->|Boolean State| CPU
    CPU -->|Engineering Units bar Celsius L/min| HMI
    CPU -->|Comparison Result| ALM
    CPU -->|Control Signal 4-20 mA| AO
    CPU -->|Relay Coil| DO
    CPU -->|Trend Data| DB
    AO -->|Signal| VLV[Control Valve]
    DO -->|Signal| MTR[Motor Contactor]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Diagram Interpretation Skill:**  
> When asked to "draw and explain" in an exam, draw this three-level hierarchy (Field → Control → Supervisory) and label the type of math operation occurring at each level. Field level is analog (scaling needed); Control level is where math instructions execute; Supervisory level displays results.

## 4.3 Decision Flow: Comparison Operation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Comparison Instruction Triggered] --> Fetch[Fetch Value A and Value B from Memory]
    Fetch --> Check1{Is A equal to B?}
    Check1 -->|Yes| EqOut[EQ Output TRUE]
    Check1 -->|No| Check2{Is A greater than B?}
    Check2 -->|Yes| GtOut[GT Output TRUE]
    Check2 -->|No| Check3{Is A less than B?}
    Check3 -->|Yes| LtOut[LT Output TRUE]
    Check3 -->|No| End[No Comparison Output TRUE]
    EqOut --> End
    GtOut --> End
    LtOut --> End
    End --> Apply[Apply Result to Output Coil or Internal Relay]
    Apply --> NextScan[Proceed to Next Instruction in Scan]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define the term "Comparison Instruction" as used in PLC-based Supervisory Control. Give two examples.**

**Model Answer:**

A Comparison Instruction in PLC programming is a logical instruction that compares two numerical values stored in memory locations or registers and produces a Boolean (TRUE/FALSE) output based on their relationship. The result is a single bit (1 or 0) that can drive an output coil, internal relay, or function block.

**Two examples:**
1. **Greater Than (GRT):** Activates an output when Value A > Value B
2. **Less Than or Equal (LEQ):** Activates an output when Value A ≤ Value B

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**What is a "Modulo" operation? How is it used in supervisory control applications?**

**Model Answer:**

The Modulo operation returns the **remainder** after integer division of two numbers. It is mathematically expressed as:

$$
a \, \text{MOD} \, b = a - b \times \lfloor a/b \rfloor
$$

where $\lfloor \cdot \rfloor$ denotes the floor function.

**Application in Supervisory Control:**  
Modulo is used in **packaging and bottling industries** to track the remainder of products in a conveyor cycle. For example, if a machine packs 12 bottles per crate and 347 bottles have been produced, $347 \, \text{MOD} \, 12 = 11$ bottles are unpacked in the current cycle. It is also used in **circular buffer indexing** for communication protocols like Modbus.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of math operations in a PLC scan cycle. List any four categories of math operations with one example each. **(7 Marks)**

**b)** A 4–20 mA pressure transmitter is connected to a 16-bit analog input module of a PLC. The raw count range is 4000 to 20000 counts corresponding to 0–100 bar. If the current reading is 14800 counts, calculate the engineering pressure value. Use the linear scaling formula. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Block Diagram (3 Marks):**

```
[Input Scan] → [Read I/O Image Table] → [Program Scan]
       ↓
[Fetch Source A & B] → [ALU Executes] → [Update Status Flags]
       ↓
[Store Result in Destination] → [Update Output Coils] → [Output Scan]
```

**Four Categories of Math Operations (4 Marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Arithmetic Operations:** Addition (`ADD`) — e.g., Sum of flow rates from multiple pipes.
2. **Comparison Operations:** Greater Than (`GRT`) — e.g., Trigger alarm if temperature > 150°C.
3. **Logical/Bitwise Operations:** AND — e.g., Start pump only if Level_OK AND Pressure_OK.
4. **Mathematical Functions:** Square Root (`SQRT`) — e.g., Compute flow from differential pressure using $Q = C\sqrt{\Delta P}$.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given Data:**
- Raw count range: $A_{min} = 4000$, $A_{max} = 20000$
- Engineering range: $E_{min} = 0$ bar, $E_{max} = 100$ bar
- Current raw reading: $A = 14800$ counts

**Linear Scaling Formula:**

$$
E = \left( \frac{A - A_{min}}{A_{max} - A_{min}} \right) \times (E_{max} - E_{min}) + E_{min}
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute values (1 Mark):**

$$
E = \left( \frac{14800 - 4000}{20000 - 4000} \right) \times (100 - 0) + 0
$$

**Step 2 — Compute numerator (1 Mark):**

$$
14800 - 4000 = 10800
$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator (1 Mark):**

$$
20000 - 4000 = 16000
$$

**Step 4 — Compute fraction (1 Mark):**

$$
\frac{10800}{16000} = 0.675
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by engineering span (1 Mark):**

$$
0.675 \times 100 = 67.5
$$

**Step 6 — Add minimum offset (1 Mark):**

$$
E = 67.5 + 0 = 67.5 \, \text{bar}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{E = 67.5 \, \text{bar}}
$$

**Verification:** 67.5% of full scale → $(67.5/100) \times (20000 - 4000) + 4000 = 10800 + 4000 = 14800$ ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**a)** Explain the following math operations with one industrial application for each: **(7 Marks)**
   (i) Bitwise AND  
   (ii) Two's Complement Negation  
   (iii) Square Root function  
   (iv) Modulo Division

**b)** Convert the decimal number $-85$ into its 16-bit Two's Complement representation. Show all steps. Verify your answer. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**(i) Bitwise AND (1.75 Marks):**  
AND performs logical conjunction on corresponding bits of two words. The result bit is 1 only if BOTH input bits are 1, else 0.  
**Application:** Used in **safety interlock systems** — "Start Conveyor only if Guard_Door_Closed AND Emergency_Stop_Released AND Motor_Overload_OK."

**(ii) Two's Complement Negation (1.75 Marks):**  
To negate a signed integer, invert all bits and add 1. This is how PLC ALUs internally represent negative numbers.  
**Application:** Used in **signed arithmetic for bidirectional flow measurement** where flow can be positive (forward) or negative (reverse).

**(iii) Square Root Function (1.75 Marks):**  
Computes the square root of a non-negative number, $\sqrt{x}$.  
**Application:** Used in **orifice plate flow measurement** where flow rate $Q = C\sqrt{\Delta P}$ is derived from differential pressure.

**(iv) Modulo Division (1.75 Marks):**  
Returns the remainder after integer division.  
**Application:** Used in **bottle-filling lines** to determine the remainder of bottles in an incomplete crate cycle.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Convert $-85_{10}$ to 16-bit Two's Complement:**

**Step 1 — Represent $+85$ in binary (1 Mark):**

$$
85 = 64 + 16 + 4 + 1 = 2^6 + 2^4 + 2^2 + 2^0
$$

$$
85_{10} = 0000\,0000\,0101\,0101_2
$$

**Step 2 — Invert all bits (2 Marks):**

$$
\overline{0000\,0000\,0101\,0101} = 1111\,1111\,1010\,1010
$$

**Step 3 — Add 1 (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 1111\,1111\,1010\,1010 \\
+& 0000\,0000\,0000\,0001 \\
\hline
& 1111\,1111\,1010\,1011
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Verification (2 Marks):** Adding $+85$ and $-85$ should yield zero:

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 0000\,0000\,0101\,0101 \quad (+85) \\
+& 1111\,1111\,1010\,1011 \quad (-85) \\
\hline
& 1\,0000\,0000\,0000\,0000 = 0 \quad \text{(carry discarded)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{-85_{10} = 1111\,1111\,1010\,1011_2}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the +1 step in Two's Complement** — Inverting bits alone gives One's Complement, not Two's. KTU examiners specifically deduct **1–2 marks** for this omission.
> 
> 2. **Mixing up linear scaling formula** — Students often place the engineering range in the numerator and raw range in the denominator. **Memory aid:** Raw goes on the **"Road"** (denominator), Engineering goes on the **"Elevated"** (numerator multiplier).
> 
> 3. **Ignoring integer overflow in division** — In PLC, `7 / 3 = 2` (integer division, remainder lost). Using the wrong result in subsequent calculations causes cascading errors.
> 
> 4. **Not specifying data types in answers** — Always mention whether you used INT (16-bit), DINT (32-bit), or REAL (float) in your solution. KTU 2024 scheme rewards this specification.
> 
> 5. **Confusing comparison output with arithmetic output** — Comparisons produce Boolean (bit) outputs, not numerical values. Writing "GRT returns 1" is acceptable, but writing "GRT returns 1.0" is imprecise and loses a mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Math Operations in Supervisory Control**

- Math operations are classified into **four categories**: Arithmetic, Comparison, Logical/Bitwise, and Mathematical Functions.

- Arithmetic operations follow the format: **`DEST = SOURCE_A [operator] SOURCE_B`** and produce numerical results.

- Comparison operations produce **Boolean (TRUE/FALSE) outputs** — they do NOT return a number.

- The **status flags** (Zero, Carry, Overflow, Sign) are automatically updated after every math operation and are essential for error handling.

- **Two's Complement** = Invert all bits + Add 1. This is how PLCs represent negative numbers internally.

- The **linear scaling formula** is the most frequently tested topic — memorize: $E = \left(\frac{A - A_{min}}{A_{max} - A_{min}}\right) \times (E_{max} - E_{min}) + E_{min}$

- **Integer division truncates** the decimal portion — use the Modulo operation to recover the remainder.

- **Moving average filters** reduce sensor noise in SCADA trending by averaging the last *n* samples.

- The **PID control algorithm** is fundamentally a combination of math operations: multiplication (P-term), integration (I-term), and differentiation (D-term).

- Always specify the **data type** (INT, DINT, REAL) in your exam answers — KTU 2024 scheme awards marks for precision.

- The **scan cycle** executes math operations during the program scan phase, between the input scan and output scan phases.

- **Bitwise AND** is used for masking, **OR** is used for setting bits, **XOR** is used for toggling bits, and **NOT** is used for inverting bits.

- **Square root** function is critical in flow measurement applications involving orifice plates and venturi meters.

- For KTU Part B questions, always show **all calculation steps** with units — partial marks are awarded for correct intermediate steps.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sequencers
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT633 - SUPERVISORY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sequencers

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme Terminology):**
> A **Sequencer** is a specialized control function in a PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) or supervisory control system that automatically executes a predefined series of **output patterns (steps)** in a sequential manner, advancing from one step to the next based on either elapsed time or the occurrence of a specific transition event.

In KTU 2024 syllabus context, sequencers are categorized into two primary classes:

1. **Drum Sequencer (Time-Driven Sequencer)** – Transitions between steps occur based on a fixed time duration (preset time per step). Functionally analogous to an electromechanical drum cam timer.
2. **Event-Driven Sequencer** – Transitions occur based on the satisfaction of an external condition (e.g., a sensor reaching a threshold, a process variable crossing a setpoint).

Mathematically, a sequencer can be represented as a mapping function:

$$
f : S \rightarrow O
$$

where $S = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n\}$ is the set of sequential steps and $O = \{o_1, o_2, \dots, o_n\}$ is the corresponding set of output bit patterns stored in the sequencer table (also called the *sequencer file* or *mask file*).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — A Traffic Light Controller:**
> Imagine a four-way traffic signal. The controller has 4 fixed steps: **Green → Yellow → Red → Red+Yellow**. Each step energizes a specific combination of lamps. The system moves to the next step either after a fixed timer expires (time-driven) or when a vehicle sensor detects congestion (event-driven). This is exactly what a sequencer does in an industrial control system.

Another simpler analogy is a **washing machine cycle**: Wash → Rinse → Spin → Done. The washing machine controller advances through each phase, energizing different combinations of outputs (motor, water pump, drain valve, buzzer) in a fixed or conditional order. A sequencer in a PLC performs precisely this same logical orchestration for industrial machinery.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard)

| Term | Standard Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Step** | A single stage in the sequence where a specific output pattern is held active. |
| **Mask File / Sequencer Table** | A data file (often a word/bit array) that stores all output patterns, one row per step. |
| **Transition Condition** | The event (timer elapsed / input true) that causes advancement to the next step. |
| **Preset / Accumulator** | Timer-related registers that count elapsed time per step. |
| **Reset** | Returning the sequencer to Step 0 or the initial state. |
| **Word Length** | The number of output bits controlled per step (e.g., 16-bit word = 16 outputs). |
| **Position Pointer** | Internal register pointing to the currently active step. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU 2024 Scheme places strong emphasis on **drum sequencer operation** and **event-driven sequencer design** in the context of PLC-based supervisory control. Numerical problems on calculating step times, total cycle time, and output patterns for given input conditions are commonly tested.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Drum Sequencer

A **drum sequencer** operates like a rotating electromechanical drum fitted with cam-operated switches. In its modern PLC implementation:

1. **Initialization:** On power-up or reset, the *position pointer* is set to **Step 0** (or Step 1 depending on indexing).
2. **Output Activation:** The PLC reads the output word stored at the row corresponding to the current step from the **mask file** and writes it to the physical output module.
3. **Timer Start:** A preset timer (often called the *step timer* or *drum timer*) is started with the **preset time** $T_{step}$ corresponding to the current step.
4. **Counting:** The timer accumulator decrements (or increments) at the scan rate / time-base interval.
5. **Transition Check:** When the accumulator reaches zero (or the preset value is reached), the sequencer **advances** the position pointer to the next step.
6. **Loop Completion:** When the last step completes, the pointer either **wraps around** to Step 0 (continuous mode) or **stops** (single-cycle mode).

### 2.2 Operational Breakdown of an Event-Driven Sequencer

Unlike the drum sequencer, the event-driven sequencer does **not** use an internal timer. Instead:

1. Each step has an associated **transition condition** (a Boolean expression over input bits / process variables).
2. The sequencer remains in the current step, holding its output pattern, **until the transition condition becomes true**.
3. Upon satisfaction, the position pointer increments, and the new output pattern is loaded.
4. Event-driven sequencers are widely used in **batch process control** (e.g., reactor charging → heating → reaction → cooling → discharge).

### 2.3 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Sequencer Design

- **Why use a sequencer instead of standard ladder logic?** A sequencer replaces **hundreds of rungs of ladder code** with a single instruction and a data table. It is compact, easy to modify (change a word in the table instead of rewiring), and self-documenting.
- **How does it integrate with supervisory control?** In a SCADA hierarchy, the sequencer resides at the **PLC (control) level**. The SCADA HMI monitors the current step, allows operators to start/pause/reset the sequence, and logs historical step transitions.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Number of steps in a sequencer | $N = (S_{end} - S_{start}) + 1$ | Total steps from start to end index | dimensionless |
| Output word for step $i$ | $W_i = M[i]$ where $M$ is mask file | Bit pattern loaded at step $i$ | bit-vector |
| Total cycle time (drum) | $T_{cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{step,i}$ | Sum of all individual step times | seconds (s) |
| Step frequency | $f_{step} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cycle}}$ | Inverse of cycle time | Hertz (Hz) |
| Duty cycle of output $j$ | $D_j = \dfrac{\sum_{i \,:\, M[i]_j = 1} T_{step,i}}{T_{cycle}} \times 100\%$ | Percentage of time output $j$ is ON | percent (%) |
| Position pointer update | $P_{k+1} = (P_k + 1) \mod N$ | Modular increment (wrap-around) | step index |
| Event transition function | $T_i : \mathbb{B}^m \rightarrow \mathbb{B}$ | Boolean function of $m$ input conditions | Boolean |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Note:** In a supervisory control system, the sequencer's output word is often **multiplexed** through the PLC's output module to drive solenoid valves, motor contactors, and indicator lamps. The mask file may be configured with bits that are **always ON** (e.g., a master enable) by using a mask word applied via a logical AND before output.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Automotive assembly lines** – Sequencers coordinate robotic welding, painting, and inspection stations.
- **Water treatment plants** – Multi-stage filtration, chemical dosing, and backwash sequences.
- **Power plants** – Boiler startup/shutdown sequences (purge → ignition → ramp-up → sync).
- **Food & beverage** – Batch mixing, cooking, packaging, and CIP (Clean-In-Place) cycles.
- **Traffic and tunnel ventilation control** – Sequential damper/jet fan operation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Total Cycle Time of a Drum Sequencer

Let the sequencer have $N$ steps, and let the time spent in the $i$-th step be $T_{step,i}$ (in seconds). The total cycle time $T_{cycle}$ is the time required to complete one full pass through all $N$ steps.

**Step 1:** Define the time spent in each step.

$$
T_{step,i} = t_i \quad \text{where} \quad i \in \{1, 2, \dots, N\}
$$

**Step 2:** Since steps execute serially (one after another), the total cycle time is the algebraic sum of all individual step times.

$$
T_{cycle} = T_{step,1} + T_{step,2} + T_{step,3} + \dots + T_{step,N}
$$

**Step 3:** Express compactly using summation notation.

$$
T_{cycle} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} T_{step,i}
$$

**Conversion Logic:** The sequencer's position pointer advances from step 1 to step $N$. It then either wraps back to step 1 (continuous mode) or halts (single-cycle mode). The cycle time is therefore the total elapsed time from the start of step 1 to the end of step $N$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Duty Cycle of an Individual Output Bit

Consider an output bit $j$ (one of the bits in the output word). Bit $j$ is ON during those steps where the mask file entry $M[i]$ has the $j$-th bit set to 1.

**Step 1:** Identify all steps during which output bit $j$ is energized.

$$
\mathcal{S}_j = \{i \in \{1, 2, \dots, N\} \; \vert \; M[i]_j = 1\}
$$
