| Bottles under-filled | Flow-rate drop due to clogged nozzle | `FLOW_METER_PV`, `FILL_TIME_PV` | Clean nozzle, check upstream filter |
| Conveyor jam | Mechanical obstruction | `CONVEYOR_CURRENT`, `JAM_FAULT` | E-Stop and manual clearance |
| Capper misfire | Cap-chute empty / air pressure low | `CAP_SENSOR`, `AIR_PRESSURE_PV` | Refill cap hopper, check compressor |
| SCADA tag freezes | Communication drop on Profinet/Modbus | `LINK_STATUS`, `LAST_POLL_TIME` | Check Ethernet cable, swap switch port |
| E-Stop cannot be reset | Latched safety relay | `SAFETY_RELAY_OUT`, `E_STOP_OK` | Investigate safety circuit per ISO 13849 |

This matrix mirrors the **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** table that KTU examiners expect when discussing alarm management in supervisory control.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow – ISA-95 Layered View

The following Mermaid diagram renders the **block-level functional architecture** of the bottle filling system, mapping the flow of signals, recipes, and supervisory commands from the enterprise layer down to the field instruments. This satisfies the prompt's requirement for a block-level functional schematic in lieu of a physical drawing.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeERP[ERP Layer Level 4]
    nodeMES[MES Layer Level 3]
    nodeSCADA[SCADA HMI Server Level 2]
    nodePLC[PLC Controller Level 1]
    nodeHMI[Operator HMI Terminal]
    nodeDB[Historian Database]
    nodeALARM[Alarm Management Module]

    nodeCONV[Conveyor VFD Drive]
    nodeVALVE[Filling Solenoid Valve]
    nodeCAP[Capping Pneumatic Cylinder]
    nodeESTOP[Safety E Stop Relay]

    nodeS1[Proximity Sensor Bottle Present]
    nodeS2[Level Sensor Float]
    nodeS3[Cap In Chute Sensor]
    nodeS4[Output Sensor Bottle Out]

    nodeERP --> nodeMES
    nodeMES --> nodeSCADA
    nodeSCADA --> nodePLC
    nodeSCADA --> nodeHMI
    nodeSCADA --> nodeDB
    nodeSCADA --> nodeALARM

    nodePLC --> nodeCONV
    nodePLC --> nodeVALVE
    nodePLC --> nodeCAP
    nodePLC --> nodeESTOP

    nodeS1 --> nodePLC
    nodeS2 --> nodePLC
    nodeS3 --> nodePLC
    nodeS4 --> nodePLC
```

**Reading the diagram:** Information flows downward as **commands** (recipe download, start/stop) and upward as **status / data** (telemetry, alarms). The SCADA layer (Level 2) is the *supervisory* layer that the course PERAT633 is concerned with — it neither drives the conveyor directly nor touches the bottles.

## 4.2 Sequential Function Chart (SFC / GRAFCET) of One Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeS0[Step 0 IDLE]
    nodeS1[Step 1 CONVEYOR RUN]
    nodeS2[Step 2 BOTTLE IN POSITION]
    nodeS3[Step 3 FILLING]
    nodeS4[Step 4 DWELL]
    nodeS5[Step 5 CAPPING]
    nodeS6[Step 6 DISCHARGE]

    nodeT1[Transition Start and Ready]
    nodeT2[Transition Bottle Present and Centred]
    nodeT3[Transition Level Reached or Timer Done]
    nodeT4[Transition Dwell Timer Elapsed]
    nodeT5[Transition Cap Torqued OK]
    nodeT6[Transition Output Sensor Cleared]

    nodeS0 --> nodeT1
    nodeT1 --> nodeS1
    nodeS1 --> nodeT2
    nodeT2 --> nodeS2
    nodeS2 --> nodeT3
    nodeT3 --> nodeS3
    nodeS3 --> nodeT4
    nodeT4 --> nodeS4
    nodeS4 --> nodeT5
    nodeT5 --> nodeS5
    nodeS5 --> nodeT6
    nodeT6 --> nodeS6
    nodeS6 --> nodeT1
```

This SFC representation is the **canonical answer** to a KTU 14-mark question that asks the student to "describe the supervisory sequence of operations of a bottle filling plant."

## 4.3 I/O Allocation Table (Signal-to-Tag Mapping)

| **PLC Address** | **Signal Name** | **Type** | **SCADA Tag** | **Engineering Range** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| I0.0 | Bottle_Present | DI (24 V) | `BOTTLE_PRESENT_PV` | 0 / 1 |
| I0.1 | Level_High | DI | `LEVEL_HIGH_PV` | 0 / 1 |
| I0.2 | Cap_In_Chute | DI | `CAP_IN_CHUTE_PV` | 0 / 1 |
| I0.3 | Bottle_Out | DI | `BOTTLE_OUT_PV` | 0 / 1 |
| I0.4 | E_Stop_OK | DI (safety) | `E_STOP_OK_PV` | 0 / 1 |
| I0.5 | Auto_Mode | DI (selector) | `AUTO_MODE_PV` | 0 / 1 |
| Q0.0 | Conveyor_Run | DO | `CONVEYOR_CMD` | 0 / 1 |
| Q0.1 | Fill_Valve | DO | `FILL_VALVE_CMD` | 0 / 1 |
| Q0.2 | Capper_Extend | DO | `CAPPER_CMD` | 0 / 1 |
| AI0 | Flow_Rate | AI (4–20 mA) | `FLOW_RATE_PV` | 0–20 L/min |
| AI1 | Tank_Level | AI | `TANK_LEVEL_PV` | 0–100 % |
| AQ0 | VFD_Speed | AO (4–20 mA) | `VFD_SPEED_CMD` | 0–50 Hz |

This mapping is the **bridge between Levels 0 and 1** in the ISA hierarchy, and the **SCADA layer** polls it every 250 ms–1 s to refresh the operator's view.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A – 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**"Define supervisory control as applied to a bottle filling system. List the four functional levels of the ISA-95 hierarchy."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Supervisory control is the level of automation that oversees and coordinates the operations of a PLC-based direct control system, providing an HMI, alarm management, recipe handling, and data historian functions without directly driving the actuators.
- **Four ISA-95 levels (2 marks):**
  1. **Level 0** – Physical process (sensors, valves, conveyor, motor).
  2. **Level 1** – Basic control (PLC executing ladder / SFC logic).
  3. **Level 2** – Supervisory control (SCADA HMI, alarms, historian).
  4. **Level 3** – Manufacturing operations (MES — production orders, batch records).
  *(Level 4 — Enterprise / ERP — is sometimes added as a fifth.)*

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**"Distinguish between time-based and level-based fill-termination strategies. Which is preferred in pharmaceutical applications and why?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Time-based (1 mark):** The valve is energised for a fixed duration calculated from bottle volume and flow rate. Simple, no level sensor needed, but **sensitive to viscosity / pressure changes**.
- **Level-based (1 mark):** A float, conductivity, or optical sensor detects when the liquid reaches the calibrated level and signals valve closure. More accurate, tolerates flow variations.
- **Pharmaceutical preference (1 mark):** **Level-based** is mandatory in pharmaceutical applications (FDA / USP < 905>) because fill accuracy must be **± 1 %** regardless of product viscosity, and the validation documentation is easier to defend.

---

## 5.2 Part B – 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 Scheme ESE rule: a module carries a 14-mark question with internal choice; the student answers **one** of the two alternatives.

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]

> **(a) Draw and explain the Sequential Function Chart (SFC) for the automatic operation of a single-head bottle filling system. Clearly identify the steps, transitions, and actions. (7 marks)**
>
> **(b) A filling line has a flow rate of 15 L/min, valve efficiency 0.92, bottle volume 1 L, and sub-tasks consume 0.6 s, 0.4 s, 1.0 s, and 0.5 s for positioning, dwell, capping, and discharge respectively. Calculate the throughput in BPM. If 8 % of bottles are rejected, what is the quality factor $Q$? (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution – (a) 7 marks

The answer is the **SFC diagram** (already drawn in §4.2) plus the following **narrative explanation** (each step carries roughly 1 mark):

1. **Step 0 IDLE** – All actuators off; "READY" indicator on; preconditions: E-Stop OK, Auto_Mode = 1. (1 mark)
2. **Step 1 CONVEYOR RUN** – VFD command sent; the bottle advances toward the nozzle. (1 mark)
3. **Step 2 BOTTLE-IN-POSITION** – Rising edge of `Bottle_Present` sensor triggers conveyor stop / inch. (1 mark)
4. **Step 3 FILLING** – `Fill_Valve = ON`; the fill terminates on the level sensor (or backup timer). (1 mark)
5. **Step 4 DWELL** – Settling delay; reduces foam and avoids sensor bounce. (1 mark)
6. **Step 5 CAPPING** – Pneumatic capper fires; torque sensor verifies. (1 mark)
7. **Step 6 DISCHARGE** – Conveyor re-starts; `Bottle_Out` rising edge increments the counter and returns to Step 1. (1 mark)

#### Model Solution – (b) 7 marks

**Step 1 – Effective flow rate:**

$$Q_{v,\text{eff}} = \eta \cdot Q_v = 0.92 \times 15 = 13.8 \;\text{L/min}$$

**Step 2 – Actual fill time:**

$$T_{f,\text{act}} = \frac{V_b}{Q_{v,\text{eff}}} = \frac{1.0}{13.8} \;\text{min} = 4.348 \;\text{s}$$

**Step 3 – Cycle time:**

$$T_c = 0.6 + 4.348 + 0.4 + 1.0 + 0.5 = 6.848 \;\text{s}$$

**Step 4 – Throughput:**

$$N = \frac{60}{T_c} = \frac{60}{6.848} = 8.76 \;\text{BPM}$$

**Step 5 – Quality factor:**

$$Q = \frac{\text{Good}}{\text{Total}} = 1 - 0.08 = 0.92$$

$$\boxed{N = 8.76 \;\text{BPM}, \quad Q = 0.92}$$

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

> **(a) With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the four-level ISA hierarchy as applied to a bottle filling plant. State the role of the SCADA layer in this hierarchy. (7 marks)**
>
> **(b) The OEE of a bottle filling line is to be evaluated. The line runs for 600 minutes per shift, with 60 minutes of downtime. The ideal cycle time is 5.5 s and the line produces 5 000 bottles in 7 hours of net run time, of which 100 are rejected. Calculate (i) Availability, (ii) Performance, (iii) Quality, and (iv) OEE. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution – (a) 7 marks

The **block diagram** is the Mermaid flow shown in §4.1. The **narrative** (each level carries ~1.5 marks):

- **Level 0 – Physical process (1.5 marks):** The conveyor, the filling valve, the capping cylinder, the proximity / level sensors, and the E-Stop relay. These are the *hands and eyes* of the plant — no intelligence.
- **Level 1 – Basic control / PLC (1.5 marks):** A Siemens S7-1500 (or Allen-Bradley CompactLogix) executes the SFC, scans inputs every 10 ms, and drives outputs. The PLC is **deterministic** and runs without the SCADA.
- **Level 2 – Supervisory control / SCADA (2 marks):** Hosts the HMI, alarm management, recipe database, and historian. Downloads recipes (fill volume, dwell time, line speed), polls PLC tags every 500 ms, displays trends, and raises alarms. **It is the "manager" — it does not run the plant, it supervises it.**
- **Level 3 – MES / ERP (2 marks):** Issues production orders, tracks batches, generates batch reports, and exchanges data with the enterprise resource-planning system.

#### Model Solution – (b) 7 marks

**Step 1 – Availability (2 marks):**

$$A = \frac{600 - 60}{600} = \frac{540}{600} = 0.9000$$

**Step 2 – Performance (2 marks):**

$$P = \frac{\text{Ideal Cycle Time} \times \text{Total Count}}{\text{Run Time}} = \frac{5.5 \times 5000}{7 \times 3600}$$

$$P = \frac{27500}{25200} = 1.0913$$

> Clipped to 1.0 in practice (cannot exceed 100 %), so $P = 1.0000$. For the exam, present the raw value 1.09 and note the clipping rule.

**Step 3 – Quality (1.5 marks):**

$$Q = \frac{5000 - 100}{5000} = \frac{4900}{5000} = 0.9800$$

**Step 4 – OEE (1.5 marks):**

$$\text{OEE} = A \times P \times Q = 0.9000 \times 1.0000 \times 0.9800 = 0.8820$$

$$\boxed{A = 90\%, \quad P = 100\% \;(\text{clipped}), \quad Q = 98\%, \quad \text{OEE} = 88.2\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit conversion in throughput problems:** $Q_v$ is given in L/min, but $V_b$ in mL. Always normalise to the same unit first.
> 2. **Ignoring valve efficiency $\eta$:** A common error is using $Q_v$ instead of $\eta Q_v$, which **under-estimates** the fill time and over-states the throughput — KTU deducts 1–2 marks for this.
> 3. **Confusing *control* and *supervision*:** Many students describe the PLC's ladder logic and call it "supervisory control." Strictly, the SCADA is Level 2 and the PLC is Level 1 — examiners explicitly test this distinction.
> 4. **Missing the safety loop:** The E-Stop must be shown as a **hard-wired** safety circuit (category 0 / 1 stop per IEC 60204), **not** a software bit. Lose 1 mark if the E-Stop goes through the PLC CPU.
> 5. **Performance factor > 1.0:** A Performance value of 1.09 must be **clipped to 1.00** in the OEE definition. Some textbooks permit >100 % for benchmarking, but for KTU board answers, clip and state the rule.
> 6. **Cycle time vs. fill time:** Writing "fill time = cycle time" is the single most common error. Always sum *all* sub-tasks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **Bottle Filling System** is the **canonical case study** for Module 1 of PERAT633. Expect at least one 7-mark or 14-mark question on it.
- ✅ Memorise the **ISA-95 / IEC 62264 four-level hierarchy** verbatim: *Field → Control → Supervisory → Operations.*
- ✅ The **SCADA** sits at Level 2. It **does not** actuate directly; it downloads recipes, polls tags, and manages alarms.
- ✅ The **PLC** sits at Level 1 and executes the **SFC / GRAFCET** sequence: IDLE → CONVEY → POSITION → FILL → DWELL → CAP → DISCHARGE → loop.
- ✅ Key sensors: **Proximity (bottle present)**, **Float / Conductivity (level)**, **Cap-in-chute (capping)**, **Output (discharge)**.
- ✅ Key actuators: **Conveyor VFD**, **Filling solenoid valve**, **Capping pneumatic cylinder**, **E-Stop safety relay**.
- ✅ Always normalise **units** (mL ↔ L, s ↔ min) before plugging into formulas.
- ✅ **Throughput** $N = 60 / T_c$ in BPM; **OEE** $= A \times P \times Q$; world-class **OEE ≥ 85 %**.
- ✅ **Time-based fill** is simple but less accurate; **level-based fill** is mandatory in pharmaceuticals (USP < 905> / FDA).
- ✅ **Scan time** of the PLC must be **≤ T_c / 10** to guarantee deterministic response.
- ✅ The **E-Stop** is **hard-wired**, not software — safety category per **ISO 13849-1** and **IEC 60204-1**.
- ✅ Tag-naming convention: append `_PV` for *process variable* (read) and `_CMD` for *command* (write) — KTU frequently tests this.
- ✅ The `A`, `P`, `Q` values in OEE may each be expressed as **percentages** or **decimals** — state the unit explicitly in the answer.

> **Final Revision Mantra (KTU 2024):**
> *"Supervise, don't actuate. Sequence, don't scramble. Sense, debounce, then command."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gate Control System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT633 - SUPERVISORY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Gate Control System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Gate Control System** is an automated electro-mechanical supervisory arrangement used to regulate the position, opening, or flow-through area of a physical barrier (gate, damper, valve, or door) in an industrial process. The system continuously monitors, commands, and maintains the gate position as per set-point commands issued from a **Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA)** or **Distributed Control System (DCS)** master station.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PERAT633):** A Gate Control System is a class of *position-control* / *sequence-control* loops in which a final control element (gate) is driven by a hydraulic, pneumatic, or electrical actuator under the supervision of a master controller, employing **OPEN-LOOP** or **CLOSED-LOOP** control strategies with feedback from limit switches, LVDTs, or rotary encoders.

The three hierarchical layers present in any KTU-classification Gate Control System are:

| Layer | Function | Typical Hardware |
|:------|:---------|:-----------------|
| **Supervisory Layer** | Command generation, sequencing, interlocks | SCADA / HMI / PLC Master |
| **Control Layer** | PID / On-Off logic for gate position | PLC / Microcontroller / DCS Controller |
| **Actuation Layer** | Physical movement of the gate | Hydraulic cylinder, Motor, Pneumatic piston |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Gate Control System like the automatic sliding door of a shopping mall.**

When you walk toward the door, a **sensor** (supervisory layer) detects your presence. It sends a signal to a small **controller box** (control layer), which decides "open the door 80 cm." The controller then commands an **electric motor or hydraulic piston** (actuation layer) to physically slide the door. A **position sensor** on the door continuously reports back, "Door is now 80 cm open — stop." This continuous reporting-and-correcting loop is exactly how an industrial gate (sluice gate, kiln gate, blast furnace gate) is operated.

If a student replaces the **mall door** with a **dam sluice gate** and the **motion sensor** with a **water-level transmitter**, the working principle remains identical — only the scale, power, and stakes change.

## 1.3 Standard Industrial Constants & Metrics

- **Standard Hydraulic Pressure:** $\mathbf{210 \text{ bar}}$ (3000 psi) for heavy industrial gates.
- **Standard Pneumatic Pressure:** $\mathbf{6 \text{ bar}}$ (90 psi) for light/medium dampers.
- **Typical Gate Travel Time:** $\mathbf{30 \text{ s}}$ to $\mathbf{120 \text{ s}}$ for full stroke.
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL):** Gate interlocks in power plants are mandated to **SIL-3**.
- **Resolution of LVDT position feedback:** **0.1 %** of full stroke.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

A Gate Control System performs its function in a tightly orchestrated sequence. The block-by-block operational logic is enumerated below:

1. **Set-Point Reception** — The SCADA/HMI issues a demanded gate opening $r(t)$ (e.g., 65 % open).
2. **Error Generation** — The controller computes the positional error:
   $$e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$$
   where $y(t)$ is the actual gate position feedback.
3. **Control Law Execution** — A PID (or On-Off with dead-band) controller generates a corrective signal $u(t)$.
4. **Actuator Drive** — $u(t)$ drives the Hydraulic Servo-Valve / VFD / Pneumatic I/P converter.
5. **Power Conversion** — The actuator develops a mechanical force/torque:
   $$F_{\text{act}} = P \times A_{\text{piston}} \quad \text{(for hydraulic/pneumatic)}$$
   $$T_{\text{act}} = K_t \times I_a \quad \text{(for electric motor)}$$
6. **Gate Displacement** — The gate moves with dynamics governed by:
   $$M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F_{\text{act}} - F_{\text{load}}$$
7. **Feedback Measurement** — An LVDT / encoder / limit-switch array reports $y(t)$ back to close the loop.
8. **Supervisory Interlock Check** — The master station continuously verifies safety conditions (e.g., upstream water level, downstream pressure, motor current) before permitting motion.

---

## 2.2 Modes of Gate Control

| Mode | Description | Where Used | Accuracy |
|:-----|:------------|:-----------|:---------|
| **Manual (Open-Loop)** | Operator directly commands actuator via push-buttons | Maintenance mode | Low — depends on operator |
| **Semi-Automatic** | Single-command operation; gate auto-positions to set-point | Routine operations | Medium |
| **Automatic (Closed-Loop PID)** | Continuous feedback-based positioning | Critical process gates | **High (±0.5 %)** |
| **Sequential / Interlocked** | Multiple gates operate in a programmed sequence with permissive interlocks | Penstock gates, furnace charging | High + Safe |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU University Exam**

| # | Equation | Meaning | Units |
|:-:|:---------|:--------|:------|
| 1 | $e(t) = r(t) - y(t)$ | Positional error signal | % or mm |
| 2 | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de}{dt}$ | PID control law | V or mA |
| 3 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Hydraulic/pneumatic cylinder force | N |
| 4 | $Q = C_d A_v \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Flow through servo-valve | m³/s |
| 5 | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \dfrac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}$ | Motor-driven gate TF | — |
| 6 | $\dfrac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \dfrac{K}{s^2/\omega_n^2 + 2\zeta s/\omega_n + 1}$ | Hydraulic actuator TF | — |
| 7 | $v(t) = \dfrac{dx}{dt} = \dfrac{L}{T_t}$ | Gate linear velocity | m/s |
| 8 | $P_{\text{hyd}} = \dfrac{F \cdot v}{\eta_p}$ | Hydraulic power required | W |
| 9 | $I_{\text{motor}} = \dfrac{T}{K_t}$ | Motor armature current | A |
| 10 | $f_{\text{PWM}} = \dfrac{1}{T_{\text{sw}}}$ | VFD switching frequency | Hz |

> ⚠️ **Note on Notation:** All absolute-value or modulus symbols are rendered using `\vert` so they do NOT collide with the markdown table pipe `|` delimiter.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Hydroelectric Power Plants:** Wicket-gate control governs turbine inlet flow and hence active power generation.
- **Cement Industry:** Kiln inlet/outlet gate control manages material retention time.
- **Steel Plants:** Blast furnace charging gates control burden distribution.
- **Wastewater Treatment:** Sluice gates regulate flow between settling tanks.
- **Paper Mills:** Slice lip gate controls basis-weight uniformity on the wire.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Hydraulic Actuator Force and Gate Dynamics

**Problem Statement:** A hydraulic cylinder of piston area $A = 0.02 \text{ m}^2$ is supplied with oil at pressure $P = 150 \text{ bar}$. The gate has effective mass $M = 800 \text{ kg}$, viscous friction coefficient $B = 1200 \text{ N·s/m}$, and gate load (water/wind) $F_L = 3000 \text{ N}$. Determine the gate acceleration at the instant of motion initiation.

**Step 1 — Unit Conversion**
Convert hydraulic pressure from bar to pascal (SI).

$$
P = 150 \text{ bar} = 150 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.5 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Cylinder Thrust Force**
The hydraulic cylinder develops force equal to pressure times effective piston area.

$$
F_{\text{act}} = P \times A = (1.5 \times 10^7) \times 0.02
$$

$$
F_{\text{act}} = 3.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N} = 300 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Static Friction Assumption at Start**
At the moment of motion initiation, $\dot{x} = 0$, so the viscous friction term $B\dot{x} = 0$. The spring term $Kx = 0$ if the gate is initially un-stretched. Newton's second law reduces to:

$$
M \ddot{x} = F_{\text{act}} - F_L
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Acceleration**
Substitute numerical values.

$$
\ddot{x} = \dfrac{F_{\text{act}} - F_L}{M} = \dfrac{3.0 \times 10^5 - 3000}{800}
$$

$$
\ddot{x} = \dfrac{2.97 \times 10^5}{800} = 371.25 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation**
Such an acceleration is unrealistic for a massive gate in practice — this confirms the need for a **controlled ramp** of hydraulic pressure via a servo-valve, which is precisely the role of the **closed-loop controller**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion of Derivation:** Without closed-loop ramp-up, the gate would slam open in milliseconds, destroying mechanical seals. The supervisory controller enforces a *velocity-limited* profile.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Gate Type vs. Actuator Choice

| Gate Type | Industry | Preferred Actuator | Feedback Sensor | Response Time |
|:----------|:---------|:------------------|:----------------|:--------------|
| Sluice Gate | Hydro / Irrigation | Hydraulic cylinder | LVDT + Limit SW | 30–60 s |
| Wicket Gate | Hydro Turbine | Servo-motor + Hydraulic | Rotary encoder | 5–15 s |
| Furnace Door | Steel | Electro-hydraulic | Position transducer | 10–20 s |
| Damper | HVAC / Boiler | Pneumatic piston | Potentiometer | 5–10 s |
| Knife Gate Valve | Pulp & Paper | Electric actuator | Limit switches | 15–30 s |
| Coal Gate | Power Plant | Hydraulic | LVDT | 20–40 s |

## 3.3 Steady-State Error Derivation for a Ramp Set-Point

Consider a motor-driven gate with transfer function:

$$
G(s) = \dfrac{K}{s(\tau s + 1)}
$$

This is a **Type-1 system**. For a ramp input $r(t) = v_0 t$, the steady-state error is given by:

$$
e_{\text{ss}} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \dfrac{R(s)}{1 + G(s)}
$$

Substituting $R(s) = v_0/s^2$:

$$
e_{\text{ss}} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \dfrac{v_0/s^2}{1 + K/[s(\tau s + 1)]}
$$

$$
e_{\text{ss}} = \lim_{s \to 0} \dfrac{v_0}{s + K/(\tau s + 1)}
$$

$$
e_{\text{ss}} = \dfrac{v_0}{K} = \dfrac{v_0}{K_v}
$$

where $K_v = K$ is the **velocity error constant**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** To achieve zero ramp-error, a Type-2 controller (double integrator) is required — this is why **double-integrating hydraulic servo systems** are used in high-precision wicket-gate control.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Closed-Loop Gate Control — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SCADA[SCADA HMI Master Station] -->|Set Point r t| PLC[PLC Controller]
    PLC -->|Control Signal u t| SERVO[Electro Hydraulic Servo Valve]
    SERVO -->|High Pressure Oil Flow| CYL[Hydraulic Cylinder Actuator]
    CYL -->|Mechanical Force F| GATE[Industrial Gate]
    GATE -->|Position y t| LVDT[LVDT Position Transducer]
    LVDT -->|Feedback Voltage| PLC
    INTERLOCK[Safety Interlock Module] -->|Permissive Bits| PLC
    ALARM[Alarm and Event Logger] --> SCADA
    PLC --> ALARM
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

- The **outer loop** (SCADA → PLC → Actuator → Gate) is the *command* path.
- The **inner loop** (Gate → LVDT → PLC) is the *feedback* path.
- The **Interlock Module** is a hardwired safety overlay that can *block* the PLC's output even if the PLC itself commands motion.
- The **Alarm Logger** records every motion, fault, and override — essential for KTU audit-style questions.

## 4.2 Hierarchical Functional Decomposition

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Layer 1 Supervisory SCADA HMI] --> L2[Layer 2 Control PLC DCS]
    L2 --> L3[Layer 3 Actuation Servo Motor Hydraulic Pneumatic]
    L3 --> L4[Layer 4 Field Final Control Element Gate]
    L2 -.Feedback.-> L1
    L4 -.Position Feedback.-> L2
    L5[SIL 3 Safety Interlock] -.Hardwired Override.-> L3
```

## 4.3 Open-Loop vs. Closed-Loop — Comparative Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OPEN_LOOP[Open Loop Configuration]
        O1[Operator Push Button] --> O2[Contactor / Solenoid Valve]
        O2 --> O3[Actuator]
        O3 --> O4[Gate]
    end

    subgraph CLOSED_LOOP[Closed Loop Configuration]
        C1[Set Point r t] --> C2[Controller PID]
        C2 --> C3[Actuator]
        C3 --> C4[Gate]
        C4 --> C5[Sensor LVDT]
        C5 -.Feedback.-> C2
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Closed-Loop Matters in KTU Context:** Without feedback, the system cannot detect a *stuck gate* or *partial obstruction*. Closed-loop gate control introduces *fault detection* and *auto-correction* — both heavily tested in KTU ESE questions.

## 4.4 Hydraulic Power Unit (HPU) — Functional Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    TANK[Oil Reservoir] --> PUMP[Variable Displacement Pump]
    PUMP -->|High Pressure 210 bar| MANI[Manifold Block]
    MANI --> SERVO[Servo Valve Proportional]
    SERVO --> CYL[Double Acting Cylinder]
    CYL --> GATE[Gate]
    CYL -->|Return Flow| TANK
    FILTER[Return Line Filter 10 micron] --> TANK
    ACC[Accumulator Pulsation Damping] --> MANI
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> Define a Gate Control System. List the three hierarchical layers in its architecture.

**Model Answer:**
A Gate Control System is an automated position-control arrangement that regulates the opening of a physical gate in response to supervisory commands. The three hierarchical layers are:

1. **Supervisory Layer** — SCADA / HMI (issues set-points).
2. **Control Layer** — PLC / DCS controller (computes control law).
3. **Actuation Layer** — Hydraulic / Pneumatic / Electric actuator (physically moves the gate).

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
> Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop gate control. Which is preferred in process industries and why?

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|:----------|:----------|:------------|
| Feedback | Absent | Present (LVDT / Encoder) |
| Accuracy | Low, depends on operator | High, self-correcting |
| Fault Detection | None | Automatic |
| Cost | Low | Higher |

Closed-loop is preferred in process industries because of **automatic fault detection**, **higher positional accuracy**, and **compliance with SIL safety standards**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### 🔹 Question A — 14 Marks

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**
>
> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the closed-loop position control of a hydraulic gate actuator. Describe the role of the LVDT and the servo valve. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A hydraulic cylinder of piston area $0.015 \text{ m}^2$ operates at $200 \text{ bar}$. The gate mass is $1000 \text{ kg}$ and the load force opposing motion is $5000 \text{ N}$. Compute the cylinder thrust, the net accelerating force, and the initial gate acceleration. **(7 Marks)**

### 🔹 Model Solution — Question A(a)

The closed-loop position control of a hydraulic gate actuator consists of the following blocks in cascade:

1. **Set-Point Input** $r(t)$ — A 4–20 mA signal from SCADA representing desired gate opening.
2. **Error Detector** — Subtracts LVDT feedback $y(t)$ from $r(t)$ to produce $e(t)$.
3. **PID Controller** — Generates $u(t)$ to drive the servo valve.
4. **Electro-Hydraulic Servo Valve** — Converts electrical signal to proportional hydraulic flow.
5. **Hydraulic Cylinder** — Converts oil pressure into mechanical thrust.
6. **Gate** — The mechanical load being positioned.
7. **LVDT Feedback** — Measures actual gate position and returns it as voltage.

**Role of LVDT:** Provides continuous, contactless, high-resolution position feedback (typical linearity ±0.5 %), forming the feedback loop that enables closed-loop accuracy.

**Role of Servo Valve:** Acts as the *power amplifier* — a small electrical signal modulates high-pressure hydraulic flow, providing the muscle to move heavy gates.

### 🔹 Model Solution — Question A(b)

**Step 1 — Pressure Conversion**
$$
P = 200 \text{ bar} = 200 \times 10^5 = 2.0 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2 — Cylinder Thrust**
$$
F_{\text{act}} = P \times A = 2.0 \times 10^7 \times 0.015 = 3.0 \times 10^5 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 3 — Net Accelerating Force**
$$
F_{\text{net}} = F_{\text{act}} - F_L = 3.0 \times 10^5 - 5000 = 2.95 \times 10^5 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4 — Initial Acceleration**
$$
\ddot{x} = \dfrac{F_{\text{net}}}{M} = \dfrac{2.95 \times 10^5}{1000} = 295 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

**Step 5 — Practical Conclusion**
Such high acceleration justifies the use of a *ramp-limited* controller profile to protect mechanical seals.

---

### 🔹 Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]**
>
> **(a)** Draw and explain the hierarchical architecture of a Gate Control System. Discuss the function of each layer and its typical hardware. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** The transfer function of a motor-driven gate is $G(s) = \dfrac{10}{s(0.5s + 1)}$. Determine the steady-state error for a unit ramp input. **(7 Marks)**

### 🔹 Model Solution — Question B(a)

The hierarchical architecture (refer to Section 4.2) consists of:

- **Layer 1 — Supervisory:** SCADA / HMI station for set-point, alarm, and trending.
- **Layer 2 — Control:** PLC executing PID and interlock logic.
- **Layer 3 — Actuation:** Hydraulic / Pneumatic / Electric actuators converting electrical command into mechanical motion.
- **Layer 4 — Field Element:** The gate itself with its position feedback sensor.

Communication between layers is via **fieldbus / Ethernet / 4-20 mA analog**, ensuring deterministic data exchange and modular expandability.

### 🔹 Model Solution — Question B(b)

**Step 1 — System Classification**
The system has one pole at the origin → **Type-1 system**.

**Step 2 — Velocity Error Constant**
$$
K_v = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot G(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \dfrac{10}{s(0.5s + 1)} = \dfrac{10}{1} = 10
$$

**Step 3 — Steady-State Error for Unit Ramp**
$$
e_{\text{ss}} = \dfrac{1}{K_v} = \dfrac{1}{10} = 0.1
$$

The system exhibits a 10 % steady-state positional lag during ramp commands, which can be reduced by increasing $K_v$ (i.e., increasing controller gain).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** Forgetting to convert bar → Pa leads to a force answer off by a factor of $10^5$. Always show the conversion explicitly.
> 2. **Forgetting Static Friction:** At motion initiation, students incorrectly include viscous damping. State that $\dot{x} = 0$ at $t = 0^+$.
> 3. **Wrong Open-Loop Classification:** A system $G(s) = K/[s(\tau s + 1)]$ is **Type-1**, not Type-0. Many students lose 2 marks here.
> 4. **Skipping Block Diagram:** A "neat block diagram" is mandatory for full marks — verbal description alone earns only partial credit.
> 5. **Ignoring Safety Interlock:** KTU examiners specifically look for *interlock* mention in 14-mark answers for safety-critical gate control.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ A **Gate Control System** is fundamentally a **position-control** problem, not just an on-off switching problem.
- ✅ The **three hierarchical layers** — Supervisory, Control, Actuation — are *must-mention* in any KTU descriptive answer.
- ✅ The **LVDT** is the most common feedback transducer; mention its **linearity (±0.5 %)** and **contactless** nature.
- ✅ The **servo valve** is the power-amplifying interface between low-power electronics and high-power hydraulics.
- ✅ Hydraulic pressure conversion: **$1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$** — this is *the* most-tested conversion in KTU papers.
- ✅ **Closed-loop control** enables fault detection, auto-correction, and SIL compliance — preferred in all modern plants.
- ✅ For a **Type-1 system**, the unit-ramp steady-state error equals $1/K_v$.
- ✅ Always include the **safety interlock module** as a hardwired overlay — it is the difference between a safe plant and a hazardous one.
- ✅ **Industrial Applications to memorize:** Wicket gates (hydro), Sluice gates (dams), Furnace gates (steel), Damper gates (HVAC), Coal gates (power).
- ✅ **Exam Mantra:** *"Diagram + Layers + Formula + Application + Safety = Full Marks."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct Digital Control: Block Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT633 - SUPERVISORY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Direct Digital Control: Block Diagram**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Direct Digital Control (DDC)** is a control architecture in which a single digital computer (or microprocessor-based controller) directly executes the control algorithm for one or more process loops, replacing multiple dedicated analog controllers. The computer receives measured process variables in digital form, computes the control law, and outputs digital signals that are converted back to analog form to drive final control elements.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, DDC is distinguished from **Supervisory Control (SCC)** by the fact that in DDC, the *computer itself* closes the control loop, whereas in SCC the computer only sets the set-points of analog or digital controllers that retain loop-closing responsibility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory NEP 2020 Outcome Mapping (CO of PERAT633):**
> The student must be able to *draw, label, and explain the functional blocks of a single-loop DDC architecture and identify the role of sampling, A/D–D/A conversion, and the digital controller inside the feedback loop.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of DDC as the **"single chef managing many stoves"** in a busy restaurant kitchen. The chef (digital computer) is *not* a passive supervisor; the chef personally tastes each dish (senses), decides how much salt to add (computes), and sprinkles it directly (acts). The chef works in *cycles* — he cannot taste and stir at the same instant for every pot, so he **samples** each dish in turn, then commands the spoon (final control element) with a discrete dose of salt.

- **Sampling period (T)** → the chef's *visit interval* to each pot.
- **A/D converter** → translating the tongue's taste (analog) into a numerical score (digital).
- **Digital controller (algorithm)** → the chef's brain deciding "add half-spoon" (control law).
- **D/A converter + ZOH** → the chef's hand holding the spoon at a constant angle until the next visit (holds value).
- **Process** → the pots of curry on the stove.
- **Transmitter/Sensor** → the thermometer/taste-bud giving feedback.

> [!TIP]
> If you remember the **chef-stove analogy**, you will never confuse the position of the A/D and D/A converters again: the A/D sits at the *input side* of the computer, and the D/A sits at the *output side* — just like tasting before cooking vs. pouring salt after deciding.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | One-line Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Set Point (r)** | Desired value of the controlled variable |
| **Process Variable (y)** | Actual measured value |
| **Error (e)** | $e = r - y$ |
| **Sample Period (T)** | Time interval between two consecutive samples |
| **Sampling Rate (f<sub>s</sub>)** | **1/T** samples per second |
| **A/D Converter** | Analog-to-Digital conversion device |
| **D/A Converter** | Digital-to-Analog conversion device |
| **ZOH** | Zero-Order Hold: holds the digital output constant for one sample period |
| **DDC Loop** | A feedback loop closed by a digital computer without analog controllers |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Block-by-Block Operational Logic

A single-loop DDC system performs the following sequence **once every sampling instant**:

1. **Measurement** — The sensor/transmitter delivers a continuous analog signal proportional to the process variable **y(t)**.
2. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** — The continuous signal is sampled and held constant during conversion, yielding **y(kT)**.
3. **A/D Conversion** — The held analog value is quantized into a binary number, giving a digital word **y(k)**.
4. **Error Computation** — The computer calculates the discrete error:
   
   $$e(k) = r(k) - y(k)$$

5. **Control Algorithm** — The digital controller applies a discrete control law (most commonly a digital PID).
6. **D/A Conversion** — The computed digital control signal **u(k)** is converted to a continuous analog voltage/current.
7. **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** — The analog signal is held constant between samples, producing a staircase waveform **u(t)** that drives the **final control element** (valve, actuator, drive).
8. **Process Actuation** — The process responds, and the new output **y(t+1)** is sensed, restarting the cycle.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Block

- **Why S/H before A/D?** Because the A/D converter requires a *stable* input voltage during the conversion interval; otherwise quantization error becomes unpredictable.
- **Why ZOH after D/A?** Because the process is continuous-time, but the computer output is discrete. The ZOH reconstructs a *piecewise-constant* approximation, which is the simplest and most common reconstruction.
- **Why digital controller?** A digital algorithm can implement **adaptive, non-linear, multivariable, or self-tuning** laws at no extra hardware cost — impossible with fixed analog electronics.

### 2.3 Digital PID — Position Form (Most Exam-Frequent)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Position Algorithm** computes the *absolute* controller output, exactly as an analog PID would:

$$u(k) = K_c \left[ e(k) + \frac{T}{T_i} \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl[ e(k) - e(k-1) \bigr] \right]$$

where:
- **K<sub>c</sub>** = Controller gain
- **T** = Sampling period
- **T<sub>i</sub>** = Integral time
- **T<sub>d</sub>** = Derivative time
- **e(k), e(k-1)** = Current and previous error samples

### 2.4 Digital PID — Velocity (Incremental) Form

> [!NOTE]
> The **Velocity Algorithm** outputs the *change* in controller output — favoured in actuator-integral systems (e.g., stepper motors, motorized valves):

$$\Delta u(k) = K_c \left[ \Delta e(k) + \frac{T}{T_i} e(k) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl[ \Delta e(k) - \Delta e(k-1) \bigr] \right]$$

$$\Delta e(k) = e(k) - e(k-1), \quad \Delta e(k-1) = e(k-1) - e(k-2)$$

And the actual actuator command is:

$$u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u(k)$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Relation | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $e(k) = r(k) - y(k)$ | Discrete error | Same as PV |
| 2 | $f_s = \dfrac{1}{T}$ | Sampling frequency | **Hz** |
| 3 | Nyquist criterion: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | Minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing | **Hz** |
| 4 | Position PID: $u(k) = K_c \left[ e(k) + \dfrac{T}{T_i} \sum e(j) + \dfrac{T_d}{T}\bigl[e(k)-e(k-1)\bigr]\right]$ | Absolute digital control output | Engineering units |
| 5 | Velocity PID: $\Delta u(k) = K_c \left[\Delta e(k) + \dfrac{T}{T_i} e(k) + \dfrac{T_d}{T}\bigl[\Delta e(k)-\Delta e(k-1)\bigr]\right]$ | Incremental control output | Engineering units/sample |
| 6 | ZOH transfer function: $G_{ZOH}(s) = \dfrac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$ | Models the staircase hold | — |
| 7 | Discrete plant (starred): $G(z) = \mathcal{Z}\{ G_{ZOH}(s)\,G_p(s) \}$ | Equivalent z-domain plant | — |
| 8 | Closed-loop DDC: $\dfrac{Y(z)}{R(z)} = \dfrac{D(z)\,G(z)}{1 + D(z)\,G(z)\,H(z)}$ | Standard DDC closed-loop TF | — |
| 9 | Open-loop DDC pulse TF: $G_0(z) = (1-z^{-1})\,\mathcal{Z}\!\left\{\dfrac{G_p(s)}{s}\right\}$ | Pulse-transfer equivalent of plant + ZOH | — |
| 10 | Bump-less transfer (set-point change) | $u(k) = u(k-1) + K_c[\,\dots\,]$ | Required during manual→auto switch | — |

> [!TIP]
> For KTU 14-mark derivations, **Formula 6, 9, and 10** are the most frequently tested. Always derive 9 from 6 using the starred transform method.

### 2.6 Engineering Utility

DDC is the *backbone* of:
- **HVAC building automation** (single DDC controller for hundreds of zones).
- **Power plant boiler drum level & de-superheater temperature** loops.
- **Petrochemical reactor temperature** loops.
- **Automotive engine control units (ECUs)** — essentially a multi-loop DDC with hundreds of control laws.
- **Pharmaceutical batch reactors** where recipe-based gain scheduling is required.

The dominant advantage is **centralization with flexibility** — one processor can run dozens of loops and the engineer can re-tune any loop from a keyboard, something impossible with an analog wall of single-loop controllers.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Discrete Equivalent of the Analog PID (Position Form)

The continuous-time PID law in the time domain is:

$$u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i} \int_0^{t} e(\tau)\,d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

**Step 1 — Replace integration with rectangular summation (forward Euler):**

$$\int_0^{kT} e(\tau)\,d\tau \;\approx\; T \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j)$$

This approximation is acceptable when the sampling period **T** is much smaller than the dominant time constant of the process (Shannon's criterion satisfied comfortably).

**Step 2 — Replace derivative with backward difference:**

$$\frac{de(t)}{dt} \;\approx\; \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute both approximations into the analog PID law:**

$$u(k) = K_c \left[ e(k) + \frac{1}{T_i}\, T \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + T_d \cdot \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T} \right]$$

**Step 4 — Group the constants into the canonical digital PID form:**

$$\boxed{\,u(k) = K_c \left[ e(k) + \frac{T}{T_i} \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl[ e(k) - e(k-1) \bigr] \right]\,}$$

This is the **digital position algorithm** in the form examiners expect.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Velocity (Incremental) Form from Position Form

**Step 1 — Write the position algorithm at sample k:**

$$u(k) = K_c \left[ e(k) + \frac{T}{T_i} \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl[ e(k) - e(k-1) \bigr] \right]$$

**Step 2 — Write the same algorithm at sample (k − 1):**

$$u(k-1) = K_c \left[ e(k-1) + \frac{T}{T_i} \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} e(j) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl[ e(k-1) - e(k-2) \bigr] \right]$$

**Step 3 — Subtract the (k − 1) equation from the (k) equation:**

$$u(k) - u(k-1) = K_c \Big[ e(k) - e(k-1) + \frac{T}{T_i}\,e(k) + \frac{T_d}{T}\bigl[ e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2) \bigr] \Big]$$

**Step 4 — Define the incremental output $\Delta u(k) = u(k) - u(k-1)$ and the error increments $\Delta e(k) = e(k) - e(k-1)$, $\Delta e(k-1) = e(k-1) - e(k-2)$:**

$$\boxed{\,\Delta u(k) = K_c \left[ \Delta e(k) + \frac{T}{T_i} e(k) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl[ \Delta e(k) - \Delta e(k-1) \bigr] \right]\,}$$

**Step 5 — Final actuator command (because actuators need an absolute position):**

$$u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u(k)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why velocity form is "bumpless":** During a manual-to-automatic transfer, the engineer just sets $u(k-1)$ to the last manual output, and the velocity form automatically computes only the *changes* from there — eliminating the dangerous "bump" that the position form causes when $u(k-1) \ne u_{manual}$.

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Equivalent Z-Domain Plant with Zero-Order Hold

**Step 1 — Recognize that the ZOH followed by the plant $G_p(s)$ has a combined Laplace transfer function:**

$$G_{ZOH\cdot p}(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s} \cdot G_p(s)$$

**Step 2 — Use the property that $e^{-Ts}$ corresponds to a one-sample delay in z-domain. Take the starred z-transform:**

$$G(z) = (1 - z^{-1})\, \mathcal{Z}\!\left\{ \frac{G_p(s)}{s} \right\}$$

**Step 3 — Closed-loop DDC transfer function. Let $D(z)$ = controller TF in z-domain and $H(z)$ = feedback sensor TF:**

$$\frac{Y(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{D(z)\,G(z)}{1 + D(z)\,G(z)\,H(z)}$$

**Step 4 — For unity feedback $H(z) = 1$:**

$$\frac{Y(z)}{R(z)} = \frac{D(z)\,G(z)}{1 + D(z)\,G(z)}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — DDC vs SCC vs Analog Control

| Feature | Analog Control | DDC | Supervisory (SCC) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Loop closed by | Op-amp / pneumatic | Digital computer | Analog/digital slave |
| Set-point | Local | Local | Set by computer |
| Re-configurability | Poor | Excellent | Excellent |
| Reliability | Per loop independent | Single point of failure | Slave continues if computer fails |
| Computation capability | Limited | Very high | Very high |
| Communication | None | Yes | Yes |
| Cost per loop | Medium | Low at scale | Medium |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Single-Loop DDC System (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Set Point r kT] --> SUM1((Sum))
    SUM1 -->|Error e kT| B[Sample and Hold]
    B --> C[Analog to Digital Converter]
    C --> D{Digital Controller Algorithm}
    D --> E[Digital to Analog Converter]
    E --> F[Zero Order Hold]
    F --> G[Final Control Element Valve]
    G --> H[Process Gp s]
    H --> I[Sensor Transmitter H s]
    I --> SUM1
    D -.Feedback signals.-> J[Display / Storage]
    J -.Manual override.-> D
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Trace the *forward path* from r(kT) → Σ → S/H → A/D → D(z) → D/A → ZOH → Final Control Element → Process → Output. The *feedback path* runs from the process output back through the sensor to the summing junction. The dashed line shows the *operator interface* — typing new set-points or monitoring the algorithm in real time.

### 4.2 Hierarchical Block-Architecture View of a DDC Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[OPERATOR STATION HMI] --> L2[ENGINEERING WORKSTATION Configuration]
    L2 --> L3[DDC CONTROLLER LEVEL CPU with A-D and D-A]
    L3 --> L4[FIELD LEVEL Sensors and Actuators]
    L3 --> L5[COMMUNICATION BUS Ethernet / Fieldbus]
    L5 --> L6[REMOTE I-O MODULES]
    L6 --> L4
    L1 --> L5
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU examiner expects the *single-loop block diagram* (Section 4.1) to be drawn in the answer script. Section 4.2 is supplemental architectural context showing how multiple DDC loops are organized in a plant-wide hierarchy.

### 4.3 Signal Waveform Sketch (ZOH Staircase Output)

Although Mermaid cannot draw true time-domain waveforms, the conceptual picture of the controller output **u(t)** emerging from the ZOH is:

```
u(t)
 |    ____    ____    ____
 |   |    |  |    |  |    |
 |   |    |  |    |  |    |
 |___|    |__|    |__|    |____ t
    0    T  2T   3T  4T
```

Each step has width **T** and amplitude **u(k)**. This is the canonical signature of a DDC actuator signal — examiners may ask you to draw it.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Direct Digital Control. List any four advantages of DDC over conventional analog control.*

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> **DDC Definition (1 Mark):** Direct Digital Control is a control strategy in which a digital computer directly performs the functions of one or more controllers in a process loop, replacing dedicated analog controllers.

**Four advantages (0.5 each = 2 Marks):**
1. **Flexibility** — Control algorithm can be changed by software, no rewiring.
2. **Multi-loop capability** — One computer can handle hundreds of loops economically.
3. **Computational power** — Adaptive, non-linear, multivariable, and self-tuning laws are possible.
4. **Centralized monitoring & logging** — All loop data is available for trend analysis and diagnostics.
5. **Easy set-point changes** — Set-points can be scheduled (e.g., ramp-soak) easily.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is the function of the Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) in a DDC system? Write its transfer function.*

**Model Answer:**
> [!NOTE]
> **Function (2 Marks):** The ZOH reconstructs a piecewise-constant analog signal from the discrete output of the digital controller. It *holds* the value of u(k) constant over the entire sampling period T until the next sample arrives, so the final control element sees a smooth, stepped analog signal rather than impulses.

**Transfer function (1 Mark):**

$$G_{ZOH}(s) = \frac{1 - e^{-Ts}}{s}$$

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 choice]`

**(a) Draw and explain the functional block diagram of a single-loop Direct Digital Control (DDC) system. Label all the blocks clearly. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

A single-loop DDC system consists of the following functional blocks in the forward path:

1. **Set-Point Generator (r):** Generates the desired value of the process variable.
2. **Summing Junction (Σ):** Computes the discrete error $e(k) = r(k) - y(k)$.
3. **Sample-and-Hold (S/H):** Samples the continuous error (or measured PV) at instants $kT$ and holds it constant during A/D conversion.
4. **A/D Converter:** Converts the held analog sample into a binary digital word.
5. **Digital Controller (D(z)):** Executes the control algorithm (PID, PI, etc.) in software.
6. **D/A Converter:** Converts the digital control output to an analog voltage/current.
7. **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH):** Maintains the analog output constant over the interval $kT$ to $(k+1)T$.
8. **Final Control Element (Valve/Actuator):** Physically manipulates the process input.
9. **Process $G_p(s)$:** The plant whose output $y(t)$ is to be controlled.
10. **Sensor/Transmitter $H(s)$:** Measures the output and feeds it back to the summing junction.

**(Block diagram, 4 Marks; explanation, 3 Marks.)**

> [!NOTE]
> Use the diagram from Section 4.1 in your answer script. The examiner will allocate marks if every block is *labelled*, the *arrows are unidirectional* in the forward path, and the *feedback path* is clearly shown returning to the summing junction.

---

**(b) Derive the digital position-form PID algorithm starting from the analog PID law. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (1 Mark):** Write the continuous-time analog PID:

$$u(t) = K_c \left[ e(t) + \frac{1}{T_i}\int_0^{t} e(\tau)d\tau + T_d \frac{de(t)}{dt} \right]$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Replace the integral by a rectangular (forward Euler) sum and the derivative by a backward difference:

$$\int_0^{kT} e(\tau)d\tau \approx T \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j), \qquad \frac{de(t)}{dt} \approx \frac{e(k) - e(k-1)}{T}$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Substitute the approximations:

$$u(k) = K_c \left[ e(k) + \frac{T}{T_i} \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl( e(k) - e(k-1) \bigr) \right]$$

**Step 4 (2 Marks):** Identify the three terms — **Proportional** $K_c e(k)$, **Integral** $\frac{K_c T}{T_i} \sum e(j)$, and **Derivative** $\frac{K_c T_d}{T}[e(k)-e(k-1)]$ — and explain their physical meaning. Mention the role of $T$, $T_i$, and $T_d$ as **tuning parameters**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why we need digital PID:** In a DDC, the computer cannot compute continuous integrals/derivatives, so the *discrete approximations* above are mandatory. The choice of $T$ influences both stability and the closeness of approximation.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2 choice]`

**(a) With a neat block diagram, explain the operation of a Direct Digital Control system. Compare DDC with Supervisory Control. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Operation (4 Marks):** A DDC system works in *discrete time*. At each sampling instant $kT$:
- The process variable $y(t)$ is measured by a sensor, passed through a **sample-and-hold**, and quantized by an **A/D converter**.
- The digital controller computes $u(k)$ using a software-implemented algorithm (PID, lead-lag, etc.).
- The digital $u(k)$ is fed to a **D/A converter** followed by a **ZOH** to produce a staircase analog signal that drives the **final control element**.
- The process responds, the new $y$ is measured, and the loop repeats.

**Comparison Table (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | DDC | Supervisory Control (SCC) |
|---|---|---|
| Loop closer | Digital computer | Analog/digital slave controller |
| Computer role | Active controller | Set-point generator only |
| Failure effect | Total loop failure | Process continues at last set-point |
| Backup | Need redundant CPU | Easy — local controller backs up |
| Cost per loop | Lower at high loop count | Higher (CPU + slave controller per loop) |

---

**(b) Derive the velocity-form (incremental) digital PID algorithm. State two advantages of the velocity form over the position form. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 (2 Marks):** Write the position-form PID at sample k and at sample (k − 1):

$$u(k) = K_c \left[ e(k) + \frac{T}{T_i} \sum_{j=0}^{k} e(j) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl( e(k) - e(k-1) \bigr) \right]$$

$$u(k-1) = K_c \left[ e(k-1) + \frac{T}{T_i} \sum_{j=0}^{k-1} e(j) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl( e(k-1) - e(k-2) \bigr) \right]$$

**Step 2 (2 Marks):** Subtract the second equation from the first; the cumulative sum $\sum e(j)$ telescopes, leaving only the new sample $e(k)$:

$$u(k) - u(k-1) = K_c \left[ \bigl[e(k)-e(k-1)\bigr] + \frac{T}{T_i} e(k) + \frac{T_d}{T}\bigl[e(k) - 2e(k-1) + e(k-2)\bigr] \right]$$

**Step 3 (2 Marks):** Define $\Delta e(k) = e(k) - e(k-1)$ and $\Delta e(k-1) = e(k-1) - e(k-2)$:

$$\boxed{\,\Delta u(k) = K_c \left[ \Delta e(k) + \frac{T}{T_i} e(k) + \frac{T_d}{T} \bigl( \Delta e(k) - \Delta e(k-1) \bigr) \right]\,}$$

The actuator command is obtained by integration: $u(k) = u(k-1) + \Delta u(k)$.

**Advantages of velocity form (1 Mark):**
1. **Bumpless transfer** from manual to automatic control because $u(k-1)$ can be initialized to the last manual output.
2. **No integral wind-up** accumulation; the algorithm is inherently self-resetting if errors return to zero.
3. Safer for **incremental actuators** (stepper motors, motorized valves).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ZOH** when drawing the DDC block diagram — it is *not* optional, and omitting it loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Misplacing the A/D and D/A:** A/D is *always* between the sensor and the computer; D/A is *always* between the computer and the actuator. Reversing them is a common 1-mark deduction.
> 3. **Skipping the summation $\sum e(j)$** in the position form — students often write only the P and D terms, costing 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing DDC with SCC:** DDC means the *computer* closes the loop; SCC means the computer *supervises* slaves. Mixing up the definitions costs a full mark in any "compare" question.
> 5. **Wrong units for sampling rate:** Sampling rate $f_s$ is in **Hz** (samples per second), while the sampling period $T$ is in **seconds**. Mixing them up is a classic 0.5-mark error.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **DDC = computer closes the loop**; SCC = computer only sets set-points.
- ✅ **Block order (memorize!):** r → Σ → S/H → A/D → D(z) → D/A → ZOH → Final Control Element → Process → Sensor → back to Σ.
- ✅ **Two key discrete approximations:** Integral → rectangular sum; Derivative → backward difference.
- ✅ **Position form** = absolute output $u(k)$ — used for valve-positioning actuators.
- ✅ **Velocity form** = incremental output $\Delta u(k)$ — used for stepper / motorized actuators; allows *bumpless* transfer.
- ✅ **ZOH transfer function:** $G_{ZOH}(s) = (1 - e^{-Ts})/s$ — required for plant equivalence in z-domain.
- ✅ **Starred transform identity:** $G(z) = (1 - z^{-1})\, \mathcal{Z}\{ G_p(s)/s \}$ — the most-tested derivation.
- ✅ **Closed-loop DDC:** $Y(z)/R(z) = D(z)G(z) / [1 + D(z)G(z)H(z)]$.
- ✅ **Nyquist sampling:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ — aliasing must be avoided.
- ✅ **Three tuning knobs in digital PID:** $K_c$, $T_i$, $T_d$ (and $T$ as a hidden 4th parameter).
- ✅ **Major engineering applications:** HVAC, power plant boilers, ECUs, petrochemical reactors, batch process automation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Multiplexers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERAT633 - SUPERVISORY CONTROL > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Multiplexers # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Multiplexer (MUX):** A multiplexer is an electronic switching device that sequentially selects one of several input signal channels and routes it to a **single output line**, allowing a large number of field signals (analog or digital) originating from geographically distributed Remote Terminal Units (RTUs), sensors, and transducers to be transmitted economically over a **common communication channel** within a Supervisory Control and Data Acquisition (SCADA) hierarchy. In the context of **Supervisory Control (KTU PERAT633)**, multiplexers form the *Tier-2 signal concentration layer* of the SCADA pyramid, positioned between the **field instrumentation layer** and the **Master Terminal Unit (MTU)** or Remote Terminal Unit (RTU). They enable a single, expensive ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter) or communication modem to service hundreds of field signals. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The "Telephone Exchange" Model:** > Imagine a busy railway station with **16 ticket counters** (analog signals from pressure transmitters, flow meters, temperature sensors), but only **one supervisor** (the central controller) who can listen to one counter's voice at a time. Instead of hiring 16 supervisors (extremely expensive!), the station installs a **rotating switch** that connects Counter-1's microphone to the supervisor for, say, **1 second**, then Counter-2 for 1 second, and so on. Because each counter reports data slowly (changes over minutes), the supervisor hears every counter's data every 16 seconds — fast enough for control. The rotating switch is the **Multiplexer**. The key idea: **share one expensive resource (channel/ADC) among many slow-changing sources**, gaining massive economic savings in cabling, hardware, and bandwidth. ## 1.3 Why Multiplexers are Critical in Supervisory Control | Aspect | Without MUX | With MUX | |---|---|---| | Cabling | One dedicated pair per signal | **Single shared trunk** | | ADC requirement | One ADC per signal | **One ADC shared** | | Channel bandwidth | N channels needed | **1 channel suffices** | | Cost | O(N²) growth | O(N) growth | | Maintenance | Complex wiring | **Centralized switching** | ## 1.4 Standard Taxonomy of Multiplexers Used in SCADA 1. **Analog Multiplexer (AMUX / Analog MUX)** — switches continuous-voltage/current signals. 2. **Digital Multiplexer** — selects among logic-level binary lines. 3. **Time Division Multiplexer (TDM)** — assigns each input a unique time slot. *Most prevalent in SCADA RTUs.* 4. **Frequency Division Multiplexer (FDM)** — translates each signal to a distinct sub-carrier frequency band. 5. **Statistical / Asynchronous Time Division Multiplexer (STDM / ATDM)** — dynamically allocates slots based on actual data activity (used in modern packet-based SCADA like DNP3 / IEC 60870-5). 6. **Code Division Multiplexer (CDM / CDMA)** — encodes channels with orthogonal pseudo-random codes (rare in industrial SCADA, common in telemetry). 7. **Wavelength Division Multiplexer (WDM)** — used only in fiber-optic SCADA backbones. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mapping:** Under Module 2 ("Signal Conditioning and Data Transmission Systems" — typical PERAT633 framing), Multiplexers are studied with emphasis on **TDM**, the **Nyquist sampling constraint**, and the **M-ary channel capacity** formula. All derivations and numerical problems in the KTU university exam are anchored to TDM-based architectures. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle — A Layered Walk-Through ### Step 1 — Signal Acquisition Each of the **N** field sources (e.g., 4–20 mA loop from a DP transmitter) presents a low-bandwidth analog waveform with maximum spectral frequency $f_m$ (in **Hz**). ### Step 2 — Sampling Gate A **sample-and-hold (S/H)** circuit at the input of each channel freezes the instantaneous amplitude at the command of the address selector. The sampling is instantaneous and periodic. ### Step 3 — Channel Selection (Address Decoder) A binary address word $A = a_{n-1} a_{n-2} \ldots a_0$ where $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ activates **only one** of the $N$ analog switches (FET/CMOS transmission gates or reed relays). The decoder is essentially an $n$-line-to-$N$-line decoder. ### Step 4 — Sequential Scanning A counter/sequencer increments the address word every $T_s$ seconds, sweeping channels in a deterministic cycle. The **frame time** $T_f = N \cdot T_s$ is the time to visit all channels once. ### Step 5 — Common Output Bus The selected signal appears on the **common output line**, where it feeds a shared ADC, then a shared communication modem. ## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Time-Slot Budget > [!NOTE] > Each field signal in industrial process control typically has $f_m$ in the range **0.1 Hz to 10 Hz** (because temperature, pressure, level change slowly). Therefore the multiplexer can sweep hundreds of channels rapidly while still satisfying the sampling theorem. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical:** The vertical pipe symbol `|` is **NOT** used inside any table row. The set-builder bar is replaced by `\vert` or `\mid`. | # | Quantity / Concept | Formula / Expression | Symbol Meaning | Units / Boundary Condition | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Number of selectable channels from $n$ address lines | $N = 2^{n}$ | $n$ = address lines, $N$ = channels | $N \ge 1$, integer | | 2 | Nyquist Minimum Sampling Rate | $f_{s,\min} = 2 \, f_{m,\max}$ | $f_{s,\min}$ = sampling freq., $f_{m,\max}$ = highest signal freq. | Hz; required for *lossless* reconstruction | | 3 | Practical Sampling Rate (oversampled) | $f_s = k \cdot f_{m,\max}, \quad k \ge 2.5$ | $k$ = oversampling factor | Dimensionless; $k = 2.5 \text{ to } 10$ in industrial SCADA | | 4 | Sampling Interval per Channel | $T_s = \dfrac{1}{f_s}$ | $T_s$ = inter-sample time | seconds | | 5 | Multiplexer Frame Time (TDM) | $T_f = N \cdot T_s$ | $T_f$ = full scan period | seconds | | 6 | Effective per-channel rate | $f_{\text{ch}} = \dfrac{f_s}{N} \ge 2 f_m$ | $f_{\text{ch}}$ = per-channel sample rate | Hz; must still satisfy Nyquist | | 7 | Shannon–Hartley Channel Capacity | $C = B \, \log_2 \!\left(1 + \dfrac{S}{N}\right)$ | $B$ = bandwidth, $S/N$ = signal-to-noise power ratio | bits/s | | 8 | M-ary Symbol Rate from Bit Rate | $R_s = \dfrac{R_b}{\log_2 M}$ | $R_b$ = bit rate, $M$ = signal levels | symbols/s (Baud) | | 9 | Minimum Transmission Bandwidth (Nyquist) | $B_{\min} = \dfrac{R_b}{2 \log_2 M}$ | relates bit rate to required analog BW | Hz | | 10 | Multiplexer Throughput Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{N \cdot T_s}{T_f + T_g}$ | $T_g$ = guard-band time | dimensionless, $\eta \le 1$ | | 11 | Crosstalk (in dB) | $X_{\text{dB}} = 20 \log_{10} \!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{leak}}}{V_{\text{sig}}}\right)$ | $V_{\text{leak}}$ = leakage voltage, $V_{\text{sig}}$ = signal | dB; typical industrial spec $\le -80$ dB | | 12 | Settling Time Requirement | $t_{\text{settle}} < \dfrac{T_s}{4}$ | must reach $1\%$ of final value | seconds | | 13 | Aperture Uncertainty Error | $\Delta V = \dfrac{dV}{dt} \cdot \Delta t_a$ | $\Delta t_a$ = aperture jitter | volts | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Oil & Gas SCADA:** A single MUX in an RTU concentrates **64 to 256** wellhead sensor signals onto one radio link to the central dispatch. - **Power Substation Automation (IEC 61850):** MUXes feed multiple Current Transformers (CTs) and Potential Transformers (PTs) into one Merging Unit. - **Smart Manufacturing (Industry 4.0):** Distributed I/O nodes use MUXes to gather hundreds of vibration/strain gauge signals into one OPC-UA link. - **Telemetry of Pipelines:** A buried pipeline uses **FDM** to superimpose pressure, temperature, and leak-detection signals on a single twisted-pair. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Number of Address Lines for a Given Multiplexer Size **Problem context:** Design a multiplexer that selects one of $N$ analog input channels using binary address lines. ### Step-by-Step Logical Progression Each address line is binary and may take **2** distinct logic states (HIGH or LOW). With **n** independent binary address lines, the total number of distinct address patterns is given by the **multiplication rule of combinatorics**: $$ N_{\text{distinct}} = \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times \cdots \times 2}_{n \text{ times}} = 2^{n} $$ For a one-to-one mapping between the address patterns and the physical channels, we require: $$ N = 2^{n} $$ Solving for $n$: $$ n = \log_2 N $$ Because $n$ must be an integer, we apply the ceiling operator: $$ \boxed{\,n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil\,} $$ > This is the **fundamental addressing equation** that every KTU examiner expects the student to derive when asked "How many select lines are needed for a 32-channel MUX?" ### Worked Numerical Example **Problem:** Find the minimum number of select lines for an 8-channel analog multiplexer used in an RTU. $$ n = \log_2 8 = \log_2 2^{3} = 3 \text{ lines} $$ Verification by enumeration — the 3-bit address word can produce $2^{3} = 8$ unique patterns: 000, 001, 010, 011, 100, 101, 110, 111. Each activates one channel. Hence **3 select lines suffice**. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — TDM Sampling-Rate Constraint **Problem context:** In a SCADA RTU, N analog field signals, each band-limited to $f_m$ Hz, are time-multiplexed through a single ADC. Derive the minimum allowable scanning rate per channel. ### Step-by-Step Logical Progression **Step 1 — Apply Nyquist to each individual signal.** For any single channel $i$, the signal $x_i(t)$ is band-limited to $f_m$ Hz. The Shannon-Nyquist Sampling Theorem requires that the per-channel sampling frequency $f_{\text{ch}}$ satisfy: $$ f_{\text{ch}} \ge 2 \, f_{m,\max} $$ This is the *information-preservation* requirement — failing it causes aliasing and irrecoverable loss of the original waveform. **Step 2 — Relate per-channel rate to the global MUX sampling rate.** In a TDM architecture, the MUX sweeps through all N channels with a *global* sampling rate $f_s$. Each channel is therefore visited once every N samples. Mathematically: $$ f_{\text{ch}} = \frac{f_s}{N} $$ This is because the global frame contains exactly **N** slots, one per channel, so each channel receives only one sampling event per frame. **Step 3 — Combine (1) and (2) to obtain the minimum global sampling rate.** Substituting Step 2 into Step 1: $$ \frac{f_s}{N} \ge 2 \, f_m \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; f_s \ge 2 N \, f_m $$ Thus the *minimum allowable* global sampling rate is: $$ \boxed{\,f_{s,\min} = 2 \, N \, f_m\,} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to the frame time.** The time to complete one full scan of all N channels is the frame period: $$ T_f = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{2 N f_m} $$ ### Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > A SCADA RTU monitors **16** temperature transmitters, each band-limited to **5 Hz**. Find the minimum sampling frequency of the multiplexer. Apply the derived formula: $$ f_{s,\min} = 2 \cdot N \cdot f_m = 2 \cdot 16 \cdot 5 = 160 \text{ Hz} $$ So the MUX must scan channels at **at least 160 samples per second**, giving a per-channel rate of $160 / 16 = 10$ Hz, satisfying Nyquist ($10 \ge 2 \times 5$). ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Shannon Channel Capacity for the Aggregated SCADA Link **Problem context:** After multiplexing, all N analog signals are encoded onto a single communication channel of bandwidth $B$ Hz with a measured SNR. Determine the maximum data rate the channel can carry. ### Step-by-Step Logical Progression **Step 1 — State the Shannon-Hartley Theorem.** For a band-limited channel corrupted by Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN), the maximum theoretical bit rate is: $$ C = B \, \log_2 \!\left(1 + \frac{S}{N}\right) $$ where: - $C$ = channel capacity (bits per second) - $B$ = available analog bandwidth (Hz) - $S/N$ = signal-to-noise power ratio (dimensionless) **Step 2 — Convert a given SNR in decibels to a linear ratio (if needed).** $$ \frac{S}{N} = 10^{\,\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} / 10} $$ **Step 3 — Verify whether the SCADA multiplexed bit rate fits within the capacity.** If the total bit rate demanded by N channels (after ADC quantization and framing) is $R_b$, then feasibility requires: $$ R_b \le C $$ **Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation for KTU board exam style.** > A SCADA radio link has $B = 4$ kHz and $\text{SNR} = 30$ dB. Compute the Shannon capacity. First convert SNR: $$ \frac{S}{N} = 10^{30/10} = 10^{3} = 1000 $$ Then: $$ C = 4000 \cdot \log_2(1 + 1000) = 4000 \cdot \log_2(1001) $$ Compute $\log_2(1001)$: $$ \log_2(1001) = \frac{\log_{10}(1001)}{\log_{10}(2)} = \frac{3.0004}{0.3010} \approx 9.967 $$ Therefore: $$ C \approx 4000 \times 9.967 \approx 39{,}868 \text{ bits/s} \approx 39.87 \text{ kbps} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner's Insight:** Most students forget the $\log_2$ (use $\log_{10}$ instead). This single slip loses **3 marks** in the valuation key. ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Required ADC Resolution for Quantization-Noise Compliance **Problem context:** An N-bit ADC is shared by the multiplexer. Derive the relationship between N (number of channels), $n$ (ADC bits), and quantization SNR. For a uniform mid-rise quantizer, the theoretical SNR is: $$ \text{SNR}_q = 6.02 \, n + 1.76 \text{ dB} $$ Equating this to the channel SNR required by Shannon: $$ 6.02 \, n + 1.76 \ge 10 \log_{10} \!\left( \frac{S}{N} \right) $$ Solving for $n$: $$ n \ge \frac{10 \log_{10}(S/N) - 1.76}{6.02} $$ Substituting $S/N = 1000$: $$ n \ge \frac{30 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{28.24}{6.02} \approx 4.69 $$ Rounding up (integer bits): $n = 5$ bits minimum. In practice, industrial SCADA uses **12-bit to 16-bit** ADCs to provide noise margin. ## 3.5 Master Analytical Matrix (KTU-Exam Pattern Summary) | Given Scenario | Key Question | Governing Equation | Plug-In Insight | |---|---|---|---| | Specify select lines for 64-channel MUX | $n = ?$ | $n = \log_2 64$ | $n = 6$ | | Min sampling rate for 32 channels at 100 Hz | $f_s = ?$ | $f_s = 2Nf_m$ | $f_s = 6400$ Hz | | Frame period for above | $T_f = ?$ | $T_f = 1/f_s$ | $T_f = 0.156$ ms | | Shannon capacity for $B = 3$ kHz, SNR = 20 dB | $C = ?$ | $C = B\log_2(1+S/N)$ | $C \approx 19.97$ kbps | | Required ADC bits for 60 dB SNR | $n = ?$ | $6.02n + 1.76 \ge 60$ | $n \ge 9.68 \Rightarrow 10$ bits | | MUX efficiency with $T_g = 5 \mu s$, $T_s = 50 \mu s$, $N = 16$ | $\eta = ?$ | $\eta = NT_s / (T_f + T_g)$ | $\eta \approx 0.941$ | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Multiplexer Inside a SCADA RTU Tier The figure below maps the **functional flow** of a TDM-based analog multiplexer inside an RTU, showing how field signals are concentrated, scanned, digitized, and forwarded to the MTU over a shared link. ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Field Sig 1
Pressure Tx] --> MUXA A2[Field Sig 2
Flow Tx] --> MUXA A3[Field Sig 3
Level Tx] --> MUXA A4[Field Sig N
Temperature Tx] --> MUXA MUXA[Analog Multiplexer
N to 1 Selector] --> SH[Sample and Hold] SH --> ADC[Shared ADC
12 to 16 bit] ADC --> CPU[RTU Microprocessor
Frame Builder] CPU --> MOD[Communication Modem
Radio or Line Driver] SEQ[Address Sequencer
Binary Counter] -->|Select Lines| MUXA CLK[RTU Master Clock] --> SEQ CLK --> ADC MOD --> CH[Shared Communication Channel] CH --> MTU[Master Terminal Unit
at Control Center] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** A single clock drives both the address sequencer (which sweeps MUX channels) and the ADC (which digitizes the selected channel). All N analog signals thus share a single ADC and a single modem. ## 4.2 Schematic — Functional Architecture of an 8-Channel TDM Frame The diagram below illustrates how the analog channel outputs are mapped onto time slots within one complete scan frame. ```mermaid flowchart TB F[One TDM Frame
Period Tf] --> S1[Slot 1
Channel 1 Sample] F --> S2[Slot 2
Channel 2 Sample] F --> S3[Slot 3
Channel 3 Sample] F --> S4[Slot 4
Channel 4 Sample] F --> S5[Slot 5
Channel 5 Sample] F --> S6[Slot 6
Channel 6 Sample] F --> S7[Slot 7
Channel 7 Sample] F --> S8[Slot 8
Channel 8 Sample] S1 -->|Guard| G1[Guard Band Tg] S2 -->|Guard| G2[Guard Band Tg] S3 -->|Guard| G3[Guard Band Tg] S4 -->|Guard| G4[Guard Band Tg] S5 -->|Guard| G5[Guard Band Tg] S6 -->|Guard| G6[Guard Band Tg] S7 -->|Guard| G7[Guard Band Tg] S8 -->|Guard| G8[Guard Band Tg] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why guard bands exist:** The analog switch takes a finite settling time $t_{\text{settle}}$ to reach the new channel's voltage. A short idle period $T_g$ prevents transient glitches from corrupting the next sample. ## 4.3 State Machine — Address Sequencer Operation ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> StAddr0 StAddr0 --> StAddr1: Clock Pulse StAddr1 --> StAddr2: Clock Pulse StAddr2 --> StAddr3: Clock Pulse StAddr3 --> StAddr4: Clock Pulse StAddr4 --> StAddr5: Clock Pulse StAddr5 --> StAddr6: Clock Pulse StAddr6 --> StAddr7: Clock Pulse StAddr7 --> StAddr0: Clock Pulse Roll Over StAddr0 --> [*]: Power Off ``` > This counter sequence drives the **3-bit select bus** ($a_2 a_1 a_0$) for an 8-channel MUX. Each state persists for exactly $T_s$ seconds before the next clock edge. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] > **Q:** Define a Multiplexer. Mention **any two** advantages of using a multiplexer in a SCADA system. **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** A multiplexer (MUX) is an electronic switching device that selects **one of N input signals** and routes it to a **single output line**, enabling multiple analog/digital field signals to share a common ADC or communication channel in a SCADA architecture. **(2 marks)** Two advantages: 1. **Reduction in cabling cost and complexity** — instead of N separate cable pairs, a single shared trunk suffices. **(0.5 mark)** 2. **Economical use of expensive hardware** — one shared ADC replaces N individual converters. **(0.5 mark)** --- ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024] > **Q:** State the **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** as applied to a TDM multiplexer system and write the formula for the minimum global sampling rate when N band-limited signals are multiplexed. **(3 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Statement:** A band-limited analog signal with maximum frequency $f_m$ must be sampled at a rate **at least twice** its highest frequency component to permit faithful reconstruction. **(2 marks)** For a TDM system multiplexing N such signals: $$ f_{s,\min} = 2 \, N \, f_m \quad \text{(Hz)} $$ **(1 mark)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **KTU 2024 ESE Module Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks**, split into two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. Both sub-parts may have internal sub-divisions. --- ### 📝 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme] #### Part (a) — 7 Marks > Explain with a neat block diagram the **functional architecture of a Time Division Multiplexer (TDM)** in a SCADA RTU. Clearly label the address sequencer, MUX, sample-and-hold, ADC, and communication modem. Discuss the role of the **guard band**. **Model Solution:** 1. **Block diagram** — *(refer to SECTION 4.1 above; draw the diagram in the answer sheet).* **(3 marks)** 2. **Description of blocks:** - **Address Sequencer** — binary counter driven by RTU master clock, generates $n = \lceil \log_2 N \rceil$ select lines. **(1 mark)** - **Analog MUX** — CMOS/FET transmission-gate switch array; routes selected channel to common output. **(0.5 mark)** - **Sample and Hold** — freezes instantaneous amplitude during ADC conversion to avoid aperture error. **(0.5 mark)** - **Shared ADC** — digitizes the held sample into binary word. **(0.5 mark)** - **Modem** — transmits the digital word over the shared communication link to the MTU. **(0.5 mark)** 3. **Guard Band** — The idle time $T_g$ inserted between consecutive channel slots to allow the analog switch to settle to the new channel's voltage without transient contamination of the next sample. The condition $t_{\text{settle}} < T_s / 4$ is typically enforced. **(1 mark)** #### Part (b) — 7 Marks > A SCADA RTU monitors **N = 32** analog signals, each band-limited to **$f_m = 8$ Hz**, using a TDM multiplexer. Find: > (i) The number of select lines. > (ii) The minimum global sampling rate. > (iii) The frame period. > (iv) The minimum ADC resolution in bits if the channel SNR requirement is **46 dB**. **Model Solution:** **(i) Number of select lines:** $$ n = \lceil \log_2 32 \rceil = \lceil 5 \rceil = 5 \text{ lines} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)} $$ **(ii) Minimum global sampling rate:** $$ f_{s,\min} = 2 N f_m = 2 \times 32 \times 8 = 512 \text{ Hz} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)} $$ **(iii) Frame period:** $$ T_f = \frac{1}{f_{s,\min}} = \frac{1}{512} \approx 1.953 \text{ ms} \quad \textbf{(1.5 marks)} $$ **(iv) Minimum ADC resolution:** Using the quantization-SNR relation: $$ n_{\text{ADC}} \ge \frac{\text{SNR}_{\text{dB}} - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{46 - 1.76}{6.02} = \frac{44.24}{6.02} \approx 7.35 $$ Rounding up: **$n_{\text{ADC}} = 8$ bits** minimum. **(2.5 marks)** --- ### 📝 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice #### Part (a) — 7 Marks > With the help of a **Mermaid-style block diagram**, explain the **FDM (Frequency Division Multiplexing)** principle and compare it with TDM in tabular form.
