# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an RTU in a SCADA System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FieldInstruments["FIELD INSTRUMENTS LAYER"]
        sensorA[Pressure Transmitter]
        sensorB[Temperature RTD]
        sensorC[Breaker Status Contact]
        sensorD[Flow Meter with Pulse Output]
        actuatorA[Control Valve 4-20mA]
        actuatorB[Circuit Breaker Trip Coil]
    end

    subgraph RTUBlock["REMOTE TERMINAL UNIT RTU"]
        AIMod[Analog Input Module]
        DIMod[Digital Input Module]
        PIMod[Pulse Input Module]
        CPUBlock[CPU with RTOS Firmware]
        MemoryBlock[NVRAM Memory SOE Logs]
        AOMod[Analog Output Module]
        DOMod[Digital Output Module]
        CommMod[Communication Module DNP3 Modbus IEC61850]
        PowerMod[Redundant Power Supply AC plus DC Battery]
    end

    subgraph SCADAUpper["SCADA UPPER LAYER"]
        MasterMTU[SCADA Master MTU Control Center]
        HMIOperator[Operator HMI Workstation]
        Historian[Historian Database Server]
    end

    sensorA --> AIMod
    sensorB --> AIMod
    sensorC --> DIMod
    sensorD --> PIMod

    AIMod --> CPUBlock
    DIMod --> CPUBlock
    PIMod --> CPUBlock
    MemoryBlock --- CPUBlock
    PowerMod --- CPUBlock

    CPUBlock --> AOMod
    CPUBlock --> DOMod
    AOMod --> actuatorA
    DOMod --> actuatorB

    CommMod <--> MasterMTU
    MasterMTU --> HMIOperator
    MasterMTU --> Historian
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The arrows from the field instruments point **upward** (data acquisition), while the arrows to the actuators point **downward** (control commands). The **CPU** is at the center, with all I/O and communication modules connected to it. The **bidirectional** arrow between the RTU's communication module and the SCADA Master indicates **two-way traffic**: the RTU reports field data, and the master sends control commands.

---

## 4.2 Internal Subsystem Block Diagram of an RTU

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PowerSubsystem["POWER SUBSYSTEM"]
        PS_AC[AC Mains Input 110 220V]
        PS_DC[DC Battery Input 24 48V]
        PS_ISO[Isolation Transformer and Surge Protection]
        PS_REG[DC DC Regulators 5V 3.3V 1.8V]
        PS_AC --> PS_ISO
        PS_DC --> PS_ISO
        PS_ISO --> PS_REG
    end

    subgraph CoreSubsystem["CORE PROCESSING SUBSYSTEM"]
        CPUCore[Microprocessor ARM Cortex A8]
        RTOSKernel[RTOS Kernel VxWorks QNX or LinuxRT]
        FlashMem[Flash Memory for Firmware]
        NVRAM_Mem[NVRAM for SOE and Configuration]
        WD_Timer[Watchdog Timer Hardware]
        CPUCore --- RTOSKernel
        CPUCore --- FlashMem
        CPUCore --- NVRAM_Mem
        CPUCore --- WD_Timer
    end

    subgraph IOSubsystem["INPUT OUTPUT SUBSYSTEM"]
        AI_Ch[Analog Input Channels 4-20mA 16 bit ADC]
        DI_Ch[Digital Input Channels 24V Optoisolated]
        AO_Ch[Analog Output Channels 4-20mA 14 bit DAC]
        DO_Ch[Digital Output Channels Relay 5A]
        PI_Ch[Pulse Counter Inputs 10 kHz]
    end

    subgraph CommSubsystem["COMMUNICATION SUBSYSTEM"]
        SerialPorts[RS-232 RS-485 Ports]
        EthPort[Ethernet Port 10 100 Mbps]
        FiberPort[Fiber Optic Port for Long Distance]
        CellularModem[4G 5G Cellular Modem for Remote Sites]
        RadioModem[VHF UHF Radio Modem for Wireless Telemetry]
        ProtocolStack[DNP3 and Modbus and IEC 60870 Protocol Stack]
    end

    subgraph LocalInterface["LOCAL OPERATOR INTERFACE"]
        LCD_Display[LCD Display for Status]
        Keypad[Keypad for Local Configuration]
        LED_Status[LED Indicators for I/O Health]
        LR_Switch[Local Remote Selector Switch for Safety]
    end

    PS_REG --> CPUCore
    IOSubsystem --> CPUCore
    CPUCore --> CommSubsystem
    CPUCore --> LocalInterface
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** The **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** is a critical hardware feature unique to RTUs. If the firmware hangs (software crash), the WDT resets the CPU after a preset time-out (typically **1–5 seconds**). This is essential for **unattended remote sites** where no human can manually reboot the system.

---

## 4.3 RTU Communication Protocol Stack (DNP3 Example)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L5[Application Layer DNP3 Object Library - Binary Input - Analog Input - Counter - Event Objects]
    L4[Transport Layer DNP3 - Segment Assembly - Sequence Numbers]
    L3[Data Link Layer DNP3 - Frame Check Sequence CRC - Acknowledgment]
    L2[Physical Layer RS-485 or Ethernet or Fiber]
    L5 --> L4
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Matters in KTU:** Students are often asked *"List the layers of the DNP3 protocol used by RTUs."* The diagram above shows the **simplified 4-layer** model. DNP3 is a **poll-response** protocol: the SCADA master sends a read request, and the RTU responds with the requested data. Modern DNP3 over TCP/IP (DNP3-IP) uses the standard **5-layer OSI model**.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]

**Q: Define Remote Terminal Unit (RTU). List any four important functions performed by an RTU in a SCADA system. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (1 Mark):** A Remote Terminal Unit (RTU) is a microprocessor-based, ruggedized electronic device installed at remote field sites that interfaces physical field instruments (sensors and actuators) with the SCADA Master Terminal Unit. It performs data acquisition, local control, event logging, and telemetry communication.

**Four Important Functions (2 Marks, 0.5 each):**

1. **Data Acquisition (Telemetering):** Continuously scans analog inputs (e.g., 4–20 mA) and digital inputs (e.g., breaker status) from field instruments.
2. **Local Control Logic:** Executes pre-programmed control algorithms (PID, interlocks, auto-reclosing) independently of the SCADA master.
3. **Sequence of Events (SOE) Logging:** Time-stamps every change-of-state event with millisecond accuracy for post-fault analysis.
4. **Bidirectional Communication:** Reports process data to the SCADA master and receives control commands via protocols like DNP3, Modbus, or IEC 60870.

*(Alternative functions accepted: Alarm generation, data storage, self-diagnostics, watchdog timer reset.)*

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern]

**Q: Differentiate between RTU and PLC in a SCADA system. (3 Marks)**

**Model Answer (Tabular Form, 3 Marks):**

| Parameter | RTU | PLC |
|---|---|---|
| **Location** | Remote, unmanned, geographically distributed sites | Local factory floor, machine-level control |
| **Environment** | Harsh outdoor conditions (IP54, -40°C to +85°C) | Controlled indoor environment (IP20, 0°C to +55°C) |
| **Communication** | Wide-area telemetry (radio, cellular, satellite, leased line, DNP3) | Local industrial networks (Profibus, Ethernet/IP, Modbus TCP) |
| **Data Logging** | Extensive (SOE, trending, store-and-forward during outages) | Minimal |
| **Power Backup** | Built-in battery/solar with auto-switchover | External UPS required |
| **Cost** | Higher (ruggedized, wide-area comms) | Lower (commercial-grade) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall:** Many students write only 1–2 points (e.g., "RTU is for remote, PLC is for local"). This will fetch only **1.5 / 3 marks**. You must give at least **4 distinct points** in a proper table to secure full marks.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Actual Pattern]

**Q: (a)** Explain the functional block diagram of a Remote Terminal Unit (RTU) with neat sketches. Describe the functions of the CPU module, Communication module, and Power Supply module in detail. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A pressure transmitter operates with an input range of 0 to 25 bar and outputs a 4–20 mA current signal. The RTU's analog input module reads a current of 11.2 mA. Calculate the actual pressure being measured. If the RTU scans 320 I/O points, each requiring 8 μs of processing time, determine the total scan cycle time and the scan efficiency if the system operates at a scan rate of 100 Hz. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q (a) — 7 Marks:**

The functional block diagram of an RTU consists of the following major subsystems:

**1. CPU Module (2 Marks):**
- The CPU is the brain of the RTU, typically a **32-bit or 64-bit microprocessor** (ARM Cortex, PowerPC) running a **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)**.
- It executes the **firmware** that performs: I/O scanning, control logic, alarm processing, SOE timestamping, and protocol handling.
- The RTOS provides deterministic task scheduling, ensuring that critical operations (e.g., breaker tripping) execute within strict time deadlines.
- It interfaces with **NVRAM** (for non-volatile storage of logs) and **Flash memory** (for firmware).

**2. Communication Module (2.5 Marks):**
- Provides multiple physical interfaces: **RS-232, RS-485, Ethernet, Fiber-Optic, Cellular 4G/5G, and Radio**.
- Implements SCADA communication protocols: **DNP3 (IEEE 1815), Modbus RTU/TCP, IEC 60870-5-101/104, and IEC 61850** (for substation automation).
- Supports both **polled (master-slave)** and **report-by-exception (unsolicited reporting)** modes.
- Includes **store-and-forward** capability: during communication outages, the RTU buffers data in NVRAM and transmits it when the link is restored.
- Has built-in **cybersecurity features** per IEC 62351: authentication, encryption, and intrusion detection.

**3. Power Supply Module (2.5 Marks):**
- **Dual-source** input: AC mains (110/220 V) and DC battery (24/48 V) with **automatic failover** in < 10 ms.
- Includes **surge protection** (gas discharge tubes, TVS diodes) and **isolation transformers** to withstand lightning strikes.
- Provides regulated **DC outputs** (5 V, 3.3 V, 1.8 V) to internal circuitry via DC-DC converters.
- Often supports **solar charging** for off-grid remote sites.
- A **power-fail relay** alerts the SCADA master when primary power is lost.

> [!NOTE]
> For the **neat sketch**, refer to the **Section 4.2 diagram** of these notes. In the exam, a clear hand-drawn block diagram with labeled arrows and signal directions is expected for full marks.

---

**Model Solution for Q (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Part 1: Pressure Calculation (3.5 Marks)**

Given: $S_{min} = 0$ bar, $S_{max} = 25$ bar, $I_{loop} = 11.2$ mA.

Using the linear scaling formula:

$$E_{eng} = \left( \frac{I_{loop} - 4}{20 - 4} \right) \cdot (S_{max} - S_{min}) + S_{min}$$

Substituting:

$$E_{eng} = \left( \frac{11.2 - 4}{16} \right) \cdot (25 - 0) + 0$$

$$E_{eng} = \left( \frac{7.2}{16} \right) \cdot 25$$

$$E_{eng} = 0.45 \cdot 25$$

$$\boxed{E_{eng} = 11.25 \text{ bar}}$$

**Part 2: Scan Cycle Time (1.5 Marks)**

Given: $N_{I/O} = 320$, $t_{proc} = 8 \, \mu s = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ s.

$$t_{cycle} = N_{I/O} \times t_{proc} = 320 \times 8 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t_{cycle} = 2560 \times 10^{-6} = 2.56 \text{ ms}$$

**Part 3: Scan Efficiency (2 Marks)**

Given: $f_{scan} = 100$ Hz, so $t_{scan} = \frac{1}{100} = 10$ ms.

$$\eta_{scan} = \frac{t_{cycle}}{t_{scan}} \times 100\% = \frac{2.56 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-3}} \times 100\%$$

$$\eta_{scan} = \frac{2.56}{10} \times 100\%$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{scan} = 25.6\%}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — Q (b):** Students often confuse which formula to use first. The correct order is: **(1)** calculate pressure from current loop, **(2)** calculate scan cycle time, **(3)** calculate scan efficiency. Reversing the order or skipping unit conversions (e.g., writing $8 \, \mu s$ as $8 \times 10^{-3}$ s) will cost **1–2 marks**. Always convert μs to seconds (multiply by $10^{-6}$) and ms to seconds (multiply by $10^{-3}$).

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Expected Pattern — Alternative Choice]

**Q: (a)** Discuss in detail the various types of Input/Output (I/O) modules used in an RTU. Explain with examples how analog and digital signals from field instruments are conditioned and processed by the RTU. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An RTU has a Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF) of 250,000 hours. Calculate: (i) the failure rate, (ii) the reliability for a mission time of 2 years (17,520 hours), and (iii) the expected downtime per year. Comment on whether a redundant hot-standby configuration is justified for this RTU. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for Q (a) — 7 Marks:**

The RTU supports five main I/O module types, each serving a specific role:

**1. Analog Input (AI) Module (2 Marks):**
- Accepts continuous signals: **4–20 mA current loops, 0–10 V voltage, ±10 V bipolar, RTD (Pt100/Pt1000), and thermocouples (J, K, T types)**.
- Each channel is **optically isolated** to prevent ground loops and high-voltage transients from damaging the CPU.
- Uses a **16-bit Sigma-Delta ADC** for high-resolution conversion.
- **Signal conditioning chain:** Sensor → Low-pass filter (anti-aliasing) → Instrumentation amplifier → Multiplexer → ADC → Digital scaling in firmware.
- **Example:** A Pt100 RTD measures transformer winding temperature; the AI module linearizes the non-linear RTD curve and outputs the value in °C to the SCADA master.

**2. Digital Input (DI) Module (1.5 Marks):**
- Accepts **binary ON/OFF signals** from volt-free contacts (dry contacts) or volted contacts (24 V DC, 110 V AC).
- **Debounce filtering** (typically 5–50 ms) prevents false triggering from contact bounce.
- **Optocoupler isolation** provides 2.5 kV isolation between field and logic.
- **Example:** A circuit breaker's auxiliary contact (52a/52b) reports its open/closed status to the DI module.

**3. Analog Output (AO) Module (1.5 Marks):**
- Outputs **4–20 mA or 0–10 V** signals to control final control elements.
- Uses a **14-bit DAC** with current-loop driver.
- **Short-circuit and open-circuit detection** is built-in.
- **Example:** The RTU sends a 12 mA signal to an electro-pneumatic valve positioner to set it to 50% opening.

**4. Digital Output (DO) Module (1 Mark):**
- Uses **electromechanical relays (5 A / 230 V)** or **solid-state relays (triac/transistor)**.
- **Form-C (SPDT)** contacts are common for trip/close commands.
- **Example:** A DO contact energizes the trip coil of a 110 V DC circuit breaker.

**5. Pulse Input (PI) Module (1 Mark):**
- Counts high-frequency pulses (0–10 kHz) from energy meters, flow meters, and turbine flow sensors.
- Supports **totalization** (cumulative count) and **rate calculation** (pulses per second).
- **Example:** A water flow meter generates 100 pulses/litre; the PI module totalizes 1,000 pulses = 10 litres.

---

**Model Solution for Q (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $MTBF = 250{,}000$ hours, $t_{mission} = 17{,}520$ hours (2 years).

**Part (i): Failure Rate (1.5 Marks)**

$$\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF} = \frac{1}{250{,}000}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda = 4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ failures/hour}}$$

**Part (ii): Reliability for 2 Years (2.5 Marks)**

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-(4 \times 10^{-6}) \times 17{,}520}$$

$$R(t) = e^{-0.07008}$$

$$R(t) = 0.9323 \text{ or } 93.23\%$$

Unavailability:

$$A(t) = 1 - R(t) = 1 - 0.9323 = 0.0677 \text{ or } 6.77\%$$

**Part (iii): Expected Downtime per Year (2 Marks)**

$$T_{down} = A \times 8760 = 0.0677 \times 8760$$

$$T_{down} \approx 593 \text{ hours/year}$$

**Part (iv): Justification for Redundancy (1 Mark)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A downtime of **593 hours/year** is **unacceptable** for critical infrastructure (e.g., a 220 kV substation or a city water pumping station). A **redundant hot-standby** configuration is **strongly justified**, as it dramatically improves system reliability:
>
> $$R_{system} = 1 - (1 - R)^2 = 1 - (0.0677)^2 = 1 - 0.00458 \approx 99.54\%$$
>
> This reduces expected downtime to approximately **40 hours/year**, which is acceptable for most utility-grade applications.

> [!WARNING]
> **Valuation Pitfall — Q (b):** Students frequently forget to **comment on the redundancy** decision. KTU examiners explicitly allocate **1 mark** for the engineering judgment ("Justified / Not Justified with reasoning"). A bare numerical answer without the comment will lose that mark. Also, watch the **exponent sign** in $e^{-\lambda t}$ — a missing negative sign is a common careless error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the KTU Exam Hall:**

- **RTU Full Form:** Remote Terminal Unit (also called Remote Telemetry Unit).
- **Location:** Geographically remote, unmanned field sites (substations, pumping stations, wellheads, pipelines).
- **Core Function:** Bridge between physical field instruments and the SCADA master.
- **I/O Types (5):** AI, DI, AO, DO, PI — remember the 4–20 mA current loop is the **industry standard**.
- **4 mA = 0% of range, 20 mA = 100% of range, 0 mA = open circuit (fail-safe).**
- **Communication Protocols:** DNP3, Modbus, IEC 60870-5-101/104, IEC 61850.
- **SOE Resolution:** 1 ms (requires GPS / IRIG-B synchronization).
- **MTBF:** Typically **> 200,000 hours**; redundancy used for critical applications.
- **RTU vs PLC:** RTU = remote + wide-area telemetry + ruggedized; PLC = local + industrial network + clean environment.
- **Watchdog Timer:** Hardware reset on firmware hang — critical for unattended sites.
- **Power:** Dual-source (AC mains + DC battery) with surge protection for lightning.
- **Math Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $I_{loop} = 4 + 1.6 \cdot V_{meas}$ (for 0–10 bar example)
  - $E_{eng} = \left( \frac{I_{loop} - 4}{16} \right) \cdot (S_{max} - S_{min}) + S_{min}$
  - $t_{cycle} = N_{I/O} \times t_{proc}$
  - $\eta_{scan} = \frac{t_{cycle}}{t_{scan}} \times 100\%$
  - $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, where $\lambda = \frac{1}{MTBF}$
- **Reliability Improvement:** Redundant hot-standby gives $R_{system} = 1 - (1-R)^2$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Human-Machine Interface (HMI) Subsystem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT633 - SUPERVISORY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Human-Machine Interface (HMI) Subsystem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

The **Human-Machine Interface (HMI)** is the SCADA subsystem that enables bidirectional communication between the human operator and the underlying control/process layer. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the HMI is formally defined as the **Man-Machine Interface (MMI)** or **Operator Interface Unit (OIU)** — the consolidated hardware and software framework responsible for **process visualization, supervisory command dispatch, alarm management, and historical data trending** within an Industrial Automation and Supervisory Control hierarchy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> The HMI is treated as the *primary cognitive layer* of SCADA. The KTU examiner expects students to clearly distinguish HMI from the RTU/PLC level (which performs *control*) and from the MTU/SCADA server (which performs *data acquisition and storage*). The HMI's sole purpose is **perception → cognition → action** by the human operator.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Car Dashboard" View

Imagine you are driving a modern car:
- The **speedometer, fuel gauge, and tachometer** are *analog displays* — equivalent to a **mimic diagram** in SCADA.
- The **touchscreen infotainment system** is the *digital HMI* — equivalent to the **graphical operator console**.
- The **warning beeps and check-engine light** are the *alarm subsystem*.
- The **steering wheel and pedals** are your *input devices* (keyboard, mouse, touchscreen).
- The car's onboard computer (**ECU**) is the *PLC/RTU*, and the dashboard is what allows you, the driver, to *supervise* without directly interfacing with the engine.

Similarly, in a SCADA-controlled power plant or water treatment facility, the HMI is the operator's "dashboard" — providing visibility into thousands of field parameters and the means to issue supervisory commands without physical intervention.

## 1.3 Role of HMI in the SCADA Architecture

$$
\text{Field Layer} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{RTU/PLC (Control)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{MTU/SCADA Server (Data)} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{HMI (Operator)}
$$

The HMI sits at the **top of the automation pyramid** (often called Level 3 / Purdue Level 3.5) and performs the following four critical functions:

| Function | Description | KTU Board-Expected Term |
|---|---|---|
| **Visualization** | Graphical rendering of process state | Mimic Display |
| **Supervisory Control** | Operator-initiated setpoint changes | Soft Control / SBO |
| **Alarm Management** | Detection, prioritization, annunciation | Alarm Subsystem |
| **Historical Trending** | Storage and graphical retrieval of data | Historian View |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants / Standards referenced in KTU papers:**
> - **ISA-101.01-2015**: Human-Machine Interfaces for Process Automation Systems.
> - **IEC 62443**: Industrial network and system security (relevant for HMI access control).
> - **NEMA ICS 1 / ANSI**: Display color-coding conventions (Red = Danger, Amber = Caution, Green = Normal).
></mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Structure of the HMI

Modern HMI systems follow a **three-tier logical architecture**, as expected in KTU Module 2:

### Tier 1 — Display Layer (Presentation)
- Mimic diagrams, trends, alarm summaries, reports.
- Built using **HMI development software** (e.g., Wonderware InTouch, Siemens WinCC, GE iFIX, Ignition by Inductive Automation).
- Renders SCADA tags as graphical objects (tanks, motors, valves, pipes).

### Tier 2 — Logic Layer (Supervisory Functions)
- Alarm filtering and prioritization.
- Security and user authentication.
- Scripting for derived calculations (e.g., efficiency, totalizer).
- Communication drivers (OPC, Modbus TCP, DNP3, IEC 60870-5-104).

### Tier 3 — Data Layer (Connectivity)
- Bidirectional tag exchange with the SCADA server (MTU).
- Polling, subscription, and dead-band filtering.

## 2.2 Components of the HMI Subsystem

A typical KTU-style HMI consists of the following hardware and software building blocks:

**A. Hardware Components**
- **Operator Workstations (OWS)**: Industrial-grade PCs or HMI panels (e.g., Siemens SIMATIC HMI Panels).
- **Engineering Workstations (EWS)**: Used for configuring/developing HMI screens.
- **Display Devices**: LCD/LED monitors, touch screens, annunciator panels.
- **Input Devices**: Industrial keyboards, trackballs, touchscreens, function keys.
- **Printers**: For alarm/event logging (hardcopy logs).

**B. Software Components**
- **HMI Runtime Engine**: Renders mimic screens and exchanges tag values.
- **Graphics Library / Symbol Factory**: Pre-built industrial symbols (pumps, valves).
- **Alarm Subsystem Engine**: Handles limit-checking, priority sorting, annunciation.
- **Trend Subsystem**: Real-time and historical trending modules.
- **Security Module**: User authentication, role-based access control.

## 2.3 Design Principles for an Effective HMI (ISA-101 Highlights)

The KTU 2024 Scheme emphasizes the **High-Performance HMI (HP-HMI)** philosophy. The following principles are frequently asked:

1. **Consistency** — Similar processes must have similar screen layouts.
2. **Minimalism** — Avoid decorative elements (3-D pipes, gradients, animations) that distract the operator. Use ISA-101 "dark background, muted colors, semantic highlights."
3. **Exception-Based Design** — The screen should *highlight abnormal conditions*, not display normal conditions prominently.
4. **Hierarchy of Information** — Plant overview → Area overview → Unit overview → Detail view.
5. **Feedback** — Every operator action must produce a visible, audible, or tactile confirmation.
6. **Fitt's Law Compliance** — Frequently used controls must be larger and reachable.

## 2.4 Alarms in HMI — KTU High-Priority Topic

Alarms are abnormal process conditions requiring operator response. The KTU textbook classifies alarms into:

| Alarm Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Absolute Alarm** | Triggered when a tag crosses a fixed threshold | Temperature > 90 °C |
| **Deviation Alarm** | Triggered when a tag deviates from a setpoint by a tolerance | Level setpoint = 50%, deviation = ±5% |
| **Rate-of-Change Alarm** | Triggered by the slope of a signal exceeding a limit | dP/dt > 2 bar/min |
| **Digital Alarm** | Triggered by a discrete state change | Pump status = Fault |

### Alarm Priority (ISA-18.2 / KTU Convention)

- **Priority 1 (P1)** — **Critical / Life Safety**: Red, audible siren, unmissable.
- **Priority 2 (P2)** — **High / Equipment Safety**: Amber, audible buzzer.
- **Priority 3 (P3)** — **Medium / Operational**: Yellow, no audible.
- **Priority 4 (P4)** — **Low / Informational**: Cyan/Blue, log only.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table summarizes the key formulas and metrics used in HMI design and evaluation. Note that HMI is not a heavily mathematical topic, but KTU frequently tests **response time, alarm rate, and information density** calculations.

| # | Concept | Formula / Expression | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Screen Refresh Rate | $f_{refresh} = \dfrac{1}{T_{cycle}}$ | Hz | Typical: **1 Hz** for SCADA |
| 2 | Operator Response Time (Murphy's Model) | $T_{response} = T_{perceive} + T_{cognize} + T_{act}$ | seconds | Typical total: **2–5 s** |
| 3 | Alarm Rate (Bad Design Indicator) | $R_{alarm} = \dfrac{N_{alarms/day}}{T_{operation}}$ | alarms/hour | KTU target: **< 5 alarms/hour** to operator |
| 4 | Information Density | $D_{info} = \dfrac{N_{displayed\,tags}}{A_{screen\,area}}$ | tags/m² | Avoid exceeding **30 tags/m²** |
| 5 | Display Update Latency | $T_{latency} = T_{poll} + T_{process} + T_{render}$ | ms | Target: **< 1000 ms** |
| 6 | Mean Time to Acknowledge (MTTA) | $MTTA = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(t_{ack,i} - t_{raise,i})$ | seconds | Lower is better |
| 7 | Percentage Bad Alarms (PBA) | $PBA = \dfrac{N_{bad\,alarms}}{N_{total\,alarms}} \times 100\%$ | % | Target: **< 5%** (ISA-18.2) |
| 8 | Annunciator Flash Rate | $f_{flash} \in [0.5,\, 2.0]$ | Hz | Per ISA-18.1 / NEMA |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The single most cited cause of industrial disasters (Three Mile Island, Texas City Refinery, etc.) is **poorly designed HMI**, not malfunctioning control systems. The HMI is therefore often considered the **most safety-critical element** of the entire SCADA stack, despite containing no control logic of its own.

## 2.6 Real-World Utility

- **Power SCADA**: HMI displays one-line diagrams, breaker status, MW/MVAr flows.
- **Water Treatment**: HMI shows tank levels, pump status, chlorine residual trends.
- **Oil & Gas**: HMI provides pipeline cathodic protection, leak detection alarms.
- **Manufacturing**: HMI integrates with MES for batch tracking and OEE displays.
- **Building Management**: HMI aggregates HVAC, lighting, fire safety.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section presents analytical derivations commonly tested in KTU Module 2 numerical questions. Each derivation is expanded **step-by-step** with full algebraic justification.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Alarm Flood Rate & Operator Workload

The **alarm flood** condition is declared when an operator receives more alarms than can be processed. The KTU textbook (Popov & Bankov, adapted) defines the alarm flood threshold.

### Step 1 — Define the variables

Let:
- $N$ = Total number of alarms raised in a window.
- $\Delta t$ = Duration of the window (typically 10 minutes as per ISA-18.2).
- $R$ = Alarm rate (alarms per minute).

$$
R = \dfrac{N}{\Delta t}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply the ISA-18.2 flood criterion

An **alarm flood** is declared when:

$$
R \;>\; R_{threshold} = 10 \;\text{alarms per 10 minutes} \;=\; 1 \;\text{alarm/min}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute the operator workload

$$
W_{operator} = R \times T_{handle}
$$

where $T_{handle}$ is the average time to acknowledge and resolve one alarm (typically 60 s).

### Step 4 — Worked Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** During a plant upset, 48 alarms were raised in 8 minutes.

**Solution:**

$$
R = \dfrac{N}{\Delta t} = \dfrac{48}{8} = 6 \;\text{alarms/min}
$$

$$
R_{normalized} = 6 \times 10 = 60 \;\text{alarms/10-min window}
$$

Since $60 \gg 10$, an **alarm flood is confirmed**. The operator workload becomes:

$$
W_{operator} = 6 \times 60 = 360 \;\text{seconds/min} \equiv 6 \times T_{handle}
$$

This indicates the operator is **6× overloaded**, justifying the activation of the **Alarm Shelving / Rationalization** procedure.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Information Transfer Rate (Shannon-Inspired) for HMI

The HMI can be modeled as an **information channel** between the process (source) and the operator (destination).

### Step 1 — Define the channel

Each displayed tag carries a number of distinguishable states. For an analog tag, the number of distinguishable levels is:

$$
L = \dfrac{V_{max} - V_{min}}{\Delta V}
$$

where $\Delta V$ is the resolution (e.g., ADC quantization step).

### Step 2 — Information content per tag

By Hartley's formula:

$$
I_{tag} = \log_{2}(L) \quad \text{bits}
$$

### Step 3 — Total information on a screen

$$
I_{screen} = \sum_{j=1}^{M} I_{tag,j} \quad \text{bits}
$$

where $M$ = total tags on the screen.

### Step 4 — Information transfer rate

$$
\dot{I}_{screen} = \dfrac{I_{screen}}{T_{refresh}} \quad \text{bits/second}
$$

### Step 5 — Worked Example

**Problem:** A mimic displays 20 analog tags (12-bit ADC) and 10 digital tags (2 states). The screen refreshes every 2 s.

**Solution:**

$$
I_{analog} = 20 \times \log_{2}(2^{12}) = 20 \times 12 = 240 \;\text{bits}
$$

$$
I_{digital} = 10 \times \log_{2}(2) = 10 \times 1 = 10 \;\text{bits}
$$

$$
I_{screen} = 240 + 10 = 250 \;\text{bits}
$$

$$
\dot{I}_{screen} = \dfrac{250}{2} = 125 \;\text{bits/s}
$$

This is well within the operator's perceptual bandwidth (≈ **50 bits/s** for conscious processing per Miller's Law). Hence the screen is **cognitively manageable**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Display Update Latency Budget

The **end-to-end latency** from field change to operator perception is:

$$
T_{latency} = T_{RTU} + T_{uplink} + T_{MTU} + T_{HMI}
$$

Expanding each term (a typical KTU numerical question):

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{RTU} &= T_{scan} + T_{acquire} \\
T_{uplink} &= T_{propagation} + T_{tx} \\
T_{MTU} &= T_{poll} + T_{decode} + T_{store} \\
T_{HMI} &= T_{render} + T_{display}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Worked Example

Given:
- $T_{scan} = 100 \;\text{ms}$
- $T_{acquire} = 50 \;\text{ms}$
- $T_{propagation} = 20 \;\text{ms}$
- $T_{tx} = 80 \;\text{ms}$
- $T_{poll} = 500 \;\text{ms}$
- $T_{decode} = 30 \;\text{ms}$
- $T_{store} = 40 \;\text{ms}$
- $T_{render} = 100 \;\text{ms}$
- $T_{display} = 33 \;\text{ms}$

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{RTU} &= 100 + 50 = 150 \;\text{ms} \\
T_{uplink} &= 20 + 80 = 100 \;\text{ms} \\
T_{MTU} &= 500 + 30 + 40 = 570 \;\text{ms} \\
T_{HMI} &= 100 + 33 = 133 \;\text{ms} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
T_{latency} = 150 + 100 + 570 + 133 = 953 \;\text{ms} \;\approx\; 0.95 \;\text{s}
$$

Since the latency is **less than 1 s**, the design meets the standard SCADA acceptance criterion.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When asked about *bottleneck*, always identify the **largest single contributor** in the sum. Here, $T_{poll}$ (500 ms) dominates, suggesting the use of **report-by-exception** instead of polling to reduce latency.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Alarm Acknowledgment Model

The **average time-to-acknowledge** is the principal KPI evaluated by the KTU examiner:

$$
MTTA = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\bigl(t_{ack,i} - t_{raise,i}\bigr)
$$

Expanding with a priority-weighted variant (often tested in KTU as a 7-mark analytical question):

$$
MTTA_{weighted} = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i \bigl(t_{ack,i} - t_{raise,i}\bigr)}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} w_i}
$$

where $w_i$ is the priority weight (e.g., $w_{P1}=4,\; w_{P2}=3,\; w_{P3}=2,\; w_{P4}=1$).

This expression is commonly used to **evaluate operator performance** during post-incident reviews.

---

## 3.5 Quick-Reference Summary Table

| # | Quantity | Closed-Form Result (above) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Alarm flood check | $R > 1$ alarm/min ⇒ Flood |
| 2 | Info per screen | $I_{screen} = \sum \log_2 L_j$ |
| 3 | Latency budget | $T_{latency} = T_{RTU}+T_{uplink}+T_{MTU}+T_{HMI}$ |
| 4 | Weighted MTTA | Uses priority weights $w_i$ |
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->
