the distinction matters. --- ## 5.4 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-minute revision bullets — read these 5 minutes before the exam.** - ☐ **Engineering Interface = "back-end workshop"** of the SCADA system; used by engineers, not operators. - ☐ It is **NOT the operator console**; privileges are *high* (R/W/Configure) vs operator's *low* (Read/Acknowledge). - ☐ Eight internal layers: **DB → Graphics → Logic → Drivers → Alarms → Security → Redundancy → Backup/Licensing**. - ☐ It connects to the SCADA server via a **separate Engineering LAN**, often via a **controlled gateway / firewall** (IEC 62443). - ☐ Key equations to memorize: - $T_{scan} = 1 \,/\, f_{poll}$, - $L_i = N_i \cdot t_{proc} \,/\, T_i$, with $\sum L_i \le 0.6$, - $B = N \cdot S_{msg} \,/\, T$, - $H = N \cdot R \cdot V \cdot 86400$, - $A = MTBF \,/\, (MTBF + MTTR)$, - $T_{backup} = (S \,/\, R) \times 1.2$. - ☐ **60 % CPU safe-loading rule** is the most-tested industry standard. - ☐ **Backup with 20 % overhead** factor is the most-skipped step. - ☐ **Cybersecurity** (RBAC, audit trail, jump host) is a high-yield topic in the 2024 OBE pattern. - ☐ For 14-mark diagrams: use **solid lines for real-time data** and **dashed lines for engineering/configuration data**. - ☐ EWS is usually **NOT redundantly paired**; the project lives on a **versioned file-server**. - ☐ Regulatory compliance hooks: **IEC 62443-3-3** (cybersecurity), **IEC 61131-3** (logic languages), **21 CFR Part 11** (audit trail in pharma). - ☐ Availability target: **99.99 % ("four nines")** is the KTU-expected benchmark for an EWS. - ☐ MTBF of an EWS: **≥ 50,000 hours**; MTTR: **a few hours** (mostly logistics, not repair).

topics.title: Development of Field Control Unit (FCU) Diagram for Simple Control Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT633 - SUPERVISORY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Development of Field Control Unit (FCU) Diagram for Simple Control Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Field Control Unit (FCU)** is the lowest intelligent electronic device in the hierarchical SCADA architecture, physically installed in the plant vicinity (marshalling or local control room) to perform direct closed-loop control, sequence interlocking, and data acquisition from field-mounted sensors and actuators. It acts as the deterministic "local brain" that ensures the controlled process variable tracks its setpoint even if communication with the higher-level Master Terminal Unit (MTU) or Remote Terminal Unit (RTU) is lost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In the PERAT633 (Supervisory Control) framework, the FCU is treated as the building block that converts raw field signals into standardized, time-stamped, and quality-tagged process data, while translating supervisory commands into deterministic actuator drive signals.

The FCU typically comprises a microprocessor / microcontroller core, multi-type Input/Output (I/O) modules, signal conditioning & isolation stages, local memory, a deterministic real-time operating firmware, and an industrial communication transceiver.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Local Foreman

Imagine a large construction project. The **Site Engineer (MTU)** sits in the main office and sets high-level targets: "Complete the third floor by Friday." The **Foreman (FCU)** stands on the floor itself, talks directly to the workers (sensors and actuators), tells them when to pour concrete, mix cement, or stop work, and reports back progress every few minutes. If the engineer's phone line is cut, the foreman does not stop work — he keeps the floor being built safely using the rules already given to him. In the same way, the FCU:
- **Listens** continuously to physical signals (temperature, level, pressure, flow, valve position).
- **Decides** the corrective action locally (often a PID or on/off control law).
- **Commands** the actuator (valve, motor, heater, damper).
- **Reports** a compact summary up the SCADA chain.

> [!NOTE]
> The deterministic autonomy of the FCU is what guarantees process safety; the MTU only optimizes, supervises, and re-configures — it does not directly perform the millisecond-level control.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- Standard analog transmission: **4 mA to 20 mA** current loop, with **4 mA = 0% (Live Zero)** and **20 mA = 100%** of calibrated range.
- Standard voltage signal: **0 V to 10 V DC** (older) or **1 V to 5 V DC** (common in compact controllers).
- Digital logic levels: **24 V DC** (industrial standard for DI/DO), **0 V (FALSE)** and **24 V (TRUE)**.
- RTD reference: Platinum **PT100**, **R₀ = 100 Ω** at **0 °C**, with nominal coefficient **α = 0.00385 °C⁻¹**.
- Thermocouple standard voltages: **Type K ≈ 41 µV/°C** (typical around 0–1000 °C range).
- Industrial supply rails: **24 V DC**, **110/230 V AC**, with **±24 V tolerance** per IEC 61131-2.
- ADC resolution commonly used: **12-bit** (1 part in 4096) to **16-bit** (1 part in 65536).
- Typical control loop execution: **10 ms to 100 ms** (fast loops), **100 ms to 1 s** (slow thermal/level loops).

> [!TIP]
> Examiners frequently award 1–2 extra marks for correctly stating that the **4–20 mA** standard uses a "Live Zero" (4 mA ≠ 0) — this allows the loop to detect a broken wire (which would drop the current to **0 mA**), giving a built-in fault diagnostic.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Functional Role of the FCU inside the SCADA Hierarchy

The supervisory pyramid, in bottom-up order, is:

- **Level 0 — Field Devices**: transmitters (temperature, pressure, flow, level), control valves, contactors, drives, solenoid coils.
- **Level 1 — Field Control Unit (FCU)**: local PID, sequence logic, interlocking, alarm tagging, I/O scanning.
- **Level 2 — Remote Terminal Unit (RTU) / Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) station**: aggregates multiple FCUs, runs supervisory interlocks, coordinates unit-level start/stop.
- **Level 3 — Supervisory Station (MTU / HMI)**: operator screens, trending, historical archives, setpoint entry.
- **Level 4 — Enterprise / Production Planning Layer** (out of scope for FCU design).

The FCU is therefore the **bridge between the physical process (Level 0) and the information network (Level 2 and above)**.

### 2.2 Internal Block Decomposition of the FCU

Every commercial FCU (whether sold as a PLC, a compact RTU, or a microcontroller-based card) contains the following logical blocks:

- **Power Supply & Distribution Unit (PSDU)**: converts plant AC/DC to galvanically isolated internal rails (typically +5 V, +3.3 V, ±15 V for analog front-end, +24 V for DO drivers).
- **Processor / Controller Core**: a microcontroller (e.g., ARM Cortex-M), a DSP, or a PLC CPU. Executes the cyclic scan: *Read inputs → Solve logic → Update outputs → Refresh comms.*
- **Memory Subsystem**: volatile (RAM) for runtime data, non-volatile (Flash/EEPROM/FRAM) for firmware, calibration constants, and retained setpoints.
- **Analog Input (AI) Front-End**: low-pass anti-alias filter → instrumentation amplifier → multiplexer → 12/16-bit ADC. Accepts 4–20 mA (across a precision 250 Ω sense resistor → 1–5 V), 0–10 V, RTD (via ratiometric excitation), or thermocouple (via cold-junction compensation).
- **Analog Output (AO) Front-End**: DAC followed by a V/I converter to generate 4–20 mA, or PWM filtered to give 0–10 V.
- **Digital Input (DI) Module**: opto-isolated 24 V sensing, debounced in firmware (typically 5–20 ms).
- **Digital Output (DO) Module**: relay contacts (electromechanical or solid-state), or transistor/MOSFET drivers at 24 V DC, with flyback protection on inductive loads.
- **Signal Conditioning & Isolation**: galvanic isolation (1500 V RMS typical), surge/transient protection (TVS), RC/EMI filtering.
- **Communication Interface**: serial (RS-232, RS-485 with Modbus RTU), Ethernet (Modbus TCP, PROFINET, EtherNet/IP), or fieldbus (Foundation Fieldbus, HART). Provides the upward link to RTU/MTU.
- **Watchdog & Housekeeping**: hardware watchdog timer, brown-out detector, real-time clock (RTC) for event timestamping.

### 2.3 Why Each Block Exists — The "Why & How" Logic

- **Why galvanic isolation?** Industrial sites have large ground potential differences (multi-kV during a fault). Isolation protects the low-voltage CPU and prevents ground loops that corrupt 4–20 mA signals.
- **Why anti-alias filter?** Sampling a signal containing frequencies above the Nyquist limit creates false low-frequency components. A first-order RC filter at the AI input removes them before the ADC.
- **Why debounce DI in firmware?** Mechanical contacts "chatter" for 5–20 ms. Counting edges or applying a time-delay eliminates false triggers from a single button press.
- **Why watchdog timer?** If the CPU hangs (software bug, EMI upset), the watchdog resets the system, restoring deterministic control — critical for unattended FCUs in remote plants.
- **Why Live-Zero (4 mA) in 4–20 mA?** A 0 mA reading is *indistinguishable from a broken wire* if the range were 0–20 mA. By using 4 mA as the floor, a true 0 mA measurement is interpreted by the FCU as a **FAULT** state.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Meaning / Variables | Typical Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $I_{loop} = 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{E_{eng} - E_{min}}{E_{max} - E_{min}}$ | Engineering value to 4–20 mA loop current | mA, $4 \le I \le 20$ |
| 2 | $E_{eng} = E_{min} + \dfrac{I_{loop} - 4}{16} \cdot (E_{max} - E_{min})$ | Reverse: current to engineering value | Same engineering unit as sensor |
| 3 | $R_T = R_0 \left( 1 + \alpha T + \beta T^{2} \right)$ | PT100 RTD resistance vs. temperature | $\Omega$, $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$, $\alpha = 0.00385\ \text{°C}^{-1}$ |
| 4 | $V_{TC} \approx a\,T + b\,T^{2}$ | Thermocouple Seebeck voltage (e.g., Type K) | µV to mV, $a \approx 41\ \mu\text{V/°C}$ |
| 5 | $N_{ADC} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \cdot (2^{n} - 1) \right\rfloor$ | Digital output of an ideal ADC | Counts, $0 \le N \le 2^{n}-1$ |
| 6 | $u(t) = K_{p}\,e(t) + K_{i}\!\int_{0}^{t}\! e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_{d}\,\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | PID control law (continuous form) | $u$ = controller output, $e$ = error |
| 7 | $f_{s} \ge 2 f_{max}$ | Nyquist sampling criterion | Hz, choose $f_{s} \ge (5 \text{ to } 10)\,f_{max}$ in practice |
| 8 | $\tau_{filter} = R \cdot C$ | First-order anti-alias filter time constant | seconds |
| 9 | $V_{sense} = I_{loop} \cdot R_{sense}$ | Convert loop current to ADC voltage | V, $R_{sense} = 250\ \Omega$ → 1–5 V |
| 10 | $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$ | Control error (setpoint − process variable) | Same engineering unit as PV |

> [!TIP]
> **Remember the magic number 250 Ω.** Dropping a precision 250 Ω resistor across a 4–20 mA loop converts it to a clean 1–5 V signal that any 0–5 V ADC can read. This is the single most common board-level design trick examined in FCU design questions.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Oil & Gas**: Wellhead FCUs control choke valves and flow meters locally; the upstream SCADA only logs production.
- **Water Treatment**: Each filter bed has a dedicated FCU managing backwash sequencing and turbidity.
- **Power Plants**: Boiler drum level FCUs must act in **< 100 ms** to prevent tube damage; cloud-linked control is impossible at that speed.
- **Building Management**: HVAC FCUs modulate chilled-water valves based on zone temperature, communicating setpoints from a central BMS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Design Procedure & Analytical Matrix

This section codifies the **engineering methodology** a student must follow to *develop* an FCU diagram for any simple control application — the exact cognitive skill the KTU paper rewards with full marks in Part B (14-mark) questions.

### 3.1 Generalised Design Procedure (Applicable to Any Simple Loop)

**Step 1 — Define the Control Objective & Process Variable**
State in one sentence what must be controlled, what the setpoint source is, and what the controlled output is. Example: "Maintain the water level in Tank T-101 between 30 % and 70 % of its 2.5 m height by driving the inlet feed pump P-101."

**Step 2 — Identify Field Inputs (Sensors)**
For every process variable, specify:
- Physical quantity (level, temperature, flow, pressure).
- Sensor type (differential pressure, ultrasonic, RTD, vortex).
- Output signal (4–20 mA, 0–10 V, RTD resistance, frequency).
- Range, accuracy, and update rate.

**Step 3 — Identify Field Outputs (Actuators)**
- Type (control valve, VFD-driven pump, solenoid, heater, damper).
- Actuator signal (4–20 mA position command, 24 V DO coil, relay contact).
- Failure mode (fail-open / fail-closed — critical for safety).

**Step 4 — Tabulate the I/O Count and Type**
Build a deterministic I/O list — this drives the FCU hardware selection.

**Step 5 — Select the FCU Platform**
Choose a commercial PLC/RTU card or a custom µC-based board. Justify by scan time, I/O count, communication protocol, and environment (temperature, humidity, hazardous area).

**Step 6 — Design Signal Conditioning**
For each AI, include anti-alias filter, surge protection, isolation amplifier. For each DI, include opto-coupler + debounce. For each DO, include flyback diode across the coil.

**Step 7 — Develop the Control Algorithm**
- *On/Off control* for simple thermostats / level switches.
- *PID control* (parallel form) for continuous loops.
- *Sequential function chart (SFC)* for batch processes.

**Step 8 — Plan the Communication Link**
Define the master (RTU/MTU) and the protocol (Modbus RTU over RS-485, or Modbus TCP over Ethernet). Map each FCU register to a unique Modbus address.

**Step 9 — Draw the FCU Block Diagram**
Show all the following: field devices → signal conditioning → I/O modules → CPU → comms → higher level, with annotated signal labels and arrow directions.

**Step 10 — Validate**
- Check scan time vs. process dynamics.
- Verify fail-safe behaviour on sensor/actuator loss.
- Confirm electrical isolation and EMC compliance.

### 3.2 Worked Application: Tank Level Control FCU

**Application Description.** A chemical mixing tank (T-101) of height 2.5 m holds a liquid whose density is approximately constant. The plant wants the level $L$ held at a setpoint of **1.5 m (± 0.05 m)** by modulating the speed of an inlet centrifugal pump P-101 driven by a Variable Frequency Drive (VFD). The level sensor is a hydrostatic differential-pressure transmitter (DPT) ranged **0–2.5 m → 4–20 mA**. A high-level float switch (LSH-101) and a low-level float switch (LSL-101) provide independent hardwired safety interlocks.

**Required:** Develop the FCU block diagram and derive the engineering scaling equations.

**Step 1 — Control Objective**
Maintain $L = SP = 1.5\ \text{m}$ by adjusting VFD frequency $f$ (0–50 Hz) on pump P-101.

**Step 2 — I/O Inventory**

| Tag | Type | Signal | Range | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LT-101 | AI (4–20 mA) | Hydrostatic DPT | 0–2.5 m | Primary level measurement |
| LSH-101 | DI (24 V) | Float switch high | 0/1 | Hardwired interlock |
| LSL-101 | DI (24 V) | Float switch low | 0/1 | Hardwired interlock |
| SC-101 | DI (24 V) | Start pushbutton | 0/1 | Operator command |
| ST-101 | DI (24 V) | Stop pushbutton | 0/1 | Operator command |
| PY-101 | DO (relay) | Pump contactor | 0/1 | Enable VFD |
| VFD-101 | AO (4–20 mA) | Speed reference | 0–50 Hz | Set pump speed |
| XV-101 | DO (relay) | Inlet solenoid valve | 0/1 | Backup shut-off |
| YL-101 | DO (lamp) | High-level alarm | 0/1 | HMI indication |
| COMM | RS-485 | Modbus RTU | — | Link to RTU/MTU |

**Step 3 — Derivation of Engineering Scaling Equation**

The 4–20 mA loop current is first dropped across a precision sense resistor $R_{sense} = 250\ \Omega$ to produce a voltage for the ADC:

$$V_{ADC} = I_{loop} \times R_{sense}$$

Substituting $R_{sense} = 250\ \Omega$:

$$V_{ADC} = I_{loop} \times 250$$

For a 12-bit ADC with a **5 V** reference, the digital count corresponding to $V_{ADC}$ is:

$$N_{ADC} = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{ADC}}{5.0} \times 4095 \right\rfloor$$

Reverse conversion from current to the engineering level (in metres):

$$L = L_{min} + \frac{I_{loop} - 4}{16} \times (L_{max} - L_{min})$$

Substituting $L_{min} = 0\ \text{m}$ and $L_{max} = 2.5\ \text{m}$:

$$L = 0 + \frac{I_{loop} - 4}{16} \times 2.5$$

Simplifying:

$$L = \frac{2.5\,(I_{loop} - 4)}{16}$$

And the inverse, to drive the VFD AO:

$$I_{out} = 4 + 16 \cdot \frac{f_{cmd}}{f_{max}}$$

For $f_{max} = 50\ \text{Hz}$:

$$I_{out} = 4 + 0.32 \cdot f_{cmd}$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Check**
At setpoint $L = 1.5\ \text{m}$:

$$I_{loop} = 4 + 16 \times \frac{1.5}{2.5} = 4 + 9.6 = 13.6\ \text{mA}$$

The corresponding ADC voltage across the 250 Ω resistor:

$$V_{ADC} = 13.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 250 = 3.4\ \text{V}$$

The 12-bit ADC code:

$$N_{ADC} = \left\lfloor \frac{3.4}{5.0} \times 4095 \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 2784.6 \right\rfloor = 2784$$

The CPU will read **2784 counts**, convert to **1.500 m**, compute the PID output (say, **f_cmd = 35 Hz**), and emit:

$$I_{out} = 4 + 0.32 \times 35 = 15.2\ \text{mA}$$

This 15.2 mA signal drives the VFD to spin the pump at 35 Hz, restoring the level toward 1.5 m.

**Step 5 — Safety Interlock (Hardwired Logic)**

The CPU does not make safety decisions alone. Hardwired logic in the FCU outputs must ensure:
- If **LSH-101 = 1** (high level), de-energise **XV-101** and latch a stop on **PY-101**, regardless of CPU state.
- If **LSL-101 = 1** (low level), inhibit pump start until level recovers.

These interlock terms are typically implemented in firmware (and mirrored in the relay logic for IEC 61508 SIL-rated plants).

**Step 6 — Communication with Upper Level**
The CPU packages the following registers for the RTU/MTU poll (typical Modbus RTU addresses):

- Holding Register 40001: Process Variable $L$ (scaled, × 1000 → integer).
- Holding Register 40002: Setpoint $SP$ (scaled).
- Holding Register 40003: Pump speed command $f_{cmd}$.
- Input Register 30001: Status of LSH-101 and LSL-101 (bit-packed).
- Coil 00001: PY-101 state.
- Coil 00002: XV-101 state.

### 3.3 Algorithm of the FCU Scan Cycle

The CPU executes the following deterministic loop, with a typical cycle time of 50–200 ms:

1. **Read all DI** lines (level switches, pushbuttons, VFD fault contact).
2. **Acquire all AI** channels (multiplex + ADC + scaling).
3. **Apply interlock logic** (hardwired-safety first, then process logic).
4. **Run control law** (PID for level → VFD speed, on/off for alarms).
5. **Update DO and AO** (energise contactor, drive 4–20 mA to VFD).
6. **Refresh communication buffers** (Modbus register image).
7. **Service watchdog**, RTC tick, housekeeping.
8. **Loop** to step 1.

> [!NOTE]
> The deterministic order — *read inputs, then compute, then write outputs* — is what differentiates an FCU from a generic computer. This guarantees no output is ever updated based on a *partially* refreshed input image, a common interview question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

The following Mermaid diagrams are intentionally written as **block-level functional architectures** (per the engine's safety protocol) and use only alphanumeric node IDs and clean uppercase labels.

### 4.1 SCADA Hierarchical Position of the FCU

```mermaid
graph TD
    L4["LEVEL 4 ENTERPRISE LAYER ERP AND PLANNING"]
    L3["LEVEL 3 SUPERVISORY MTU AND HMI STATION"]
    L2["LEVEL 2 RTU AND PLC STATION"]
    L1["LEVEL 1 FCU FIELD CONTROL UNIT"]
    L0["LEVEL 0 FIELD SENSORS AND ACTUATORS"]
    L4 --> L3
    L3 --> L2
    L2 --> L1
    L1 --> L0
    L0 --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
```

The diagram shows that the FCU occupies **Level 1** of the ISA-95 / IEC 62264 hierarchy, sitting directly above the physical process and directly below the supervisory aggregation layer.

### 4.2 Internal Block Architecture of a Generic FCU

```mermaid
graph LR
    PSU["POWER SUPPLY AND DISTRIBUTION UNIT"]
    CPU["CPU MICROCONTROLLER AND FIRMWARE"]
    MEM["MEMORY RAM AND FLASH"]
    AI["ANALOG INPUT MODULE 4 TO 20 MILLI AMP"]
    AO["ANALOG OUTPUT MODULE 4 TO 20 MILLI AMP"]
    DI["DIGITAL INPUT MODULE 24 VOLT DC"]
    DO["DIGITAL OUTPUT MODULE RELAY OR TRANSISTOR"]
    COND["SIGNAL CONDITIONING AND ISOLATION"]
    COM["COMMUNICATION INTERFACE RS485 ETHERNET"]
    WDT["WATCHDOG AND RTC"]
    FSENS["FIELD SENSORS LT PT TT FT"]
    FACT["FIELD ACTUATORS VFD VALVE CONTACTOR"]
    UPLINK["UPPER LEVEL RTU OR MTU"]

    FSENS --> COND
    COND --> AI
    COND --> DI
    AI --> CPU
    DI --> CPU
    CPU --> MEM
    CPU --> AO
    CPU --> DO
    AO --> FACT
    DO --> FACT
    COM --> UPLINK
    CPU --> COM
    WDT --> CPU
    PSU -.-> CPU
    PSU -.-> AI
    PSU -.-> AO
    PSU -.-> DI
    PSU -.-> DO
    PSU -.-> COM
```

### 4.3 Application-Specific FCU Diagram: Tank Level Control

```mermaid
graph LR
    DPT["LT 101 DIFFERENTIAL PRESSURE TRANSMITTER 0 TO 2.5 METRE 4 TO 20 MILLI AMP"]
    LSH["LSH 101 HIGH LEVEL FLOAT SWITCH 24 VOLT DC"]
    LSL["LSL 101 LOW LEVEL FLOAT SWITCH 24 VOLT DC"]
    SC["SC 101 START PUSHBUTTON 24 VOLT DC"]
    ST["ST 101 STOP PUSHBUTTON 24 VOLT DC"]

    ISOGAIN["SIGNAL CONDITIONING OPTO ISOLATION ANTI ALIAS FILTER"]
    AIBLOCK["ANALOG INPUT MODULE 12 BIT ADC 250 OHM SENSE"]
    DIBLOCK["DIGITAL INPUT MODULE DEBOUNCE 20 MS"]
    CPUBLOCK["CPU PID CONTROLLER SCAN 100 MS"]
    AOBLOCK["ANALOG OUTPUT MODULE 4 TO 20 MILLI AMP"]
    DOBLOCK["DIGITAL OUTPUT MODULE RELAY 24 VOLT DC 2 AMP"]
    COMBLOCK["MODBUS RTU OVER RS 485"]

    VFD["VFD 101 PUMP P 101 SPEED REFERENCE 4 TO 20 MILLI AMP 0 TO 50 HZ"]
    CONT["PY 101 PUMP CONTACTOR"]
    SOL["XV 101 INLET SOLENOID VALVE"]
    ALM["YL 101 HIGH LEVEL ALARM LAMP"]

    RTU["RTU MTU UPPER LEVEL SCADA"]

    DPT --> ISOGAIN --> AIBLOCK --> CPUBLOCK
    LSH --> ISOGAIN --> DIBLOCK --> CPUBLOCK
    LSL --> ISOGAIN --> DIBLOCK --> CPUBLOCK
    SC --> ISOGAIN --> DIBLOCK --> CPUBLOCK
    ST --> ISOGAIN --> DIBLOCK --> CPUBLOCK

    CPUBLOCK --> AOBLOCK --> VFD
    CPUBLOCK --> DOBLOCK --> CONT
    CPUBLOCK --> DOBLOCK --> SOL
    CPUBLOCK --> DOBLOCK --> ALM
    CPUBLOCK --> COMBLOCK --> RTU
```

### 4.4 Signal Flow Directionality Legend

| Arrow | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Solid → | Real-time analog or digital signal flow during one scan |
| Dotted ⇢ | Power rail distribution |
| Bidirectional | Two-way communication handshake (Modbus request/response) |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PERAT633, Module 4]**
Define the term **Field Control Unit (FCU)**. List **any four** functional blocks of an FCU.

*Model Answer (3 marks):*
A Field Control Unit (FCU) is a microprocessor-based, locally installed electronic device that performs direct closed-loop control, sequencing, interlocking, and data acquisition on field-mounted sensors and actuators, and communicates a compact summary of process data to the upper SCADA layers. (1.5 marks)
Any four functional blocks: (i) Power Supply & Distribution, (ii) CPU and Memory, (iii) Analog Input Module, (iv) Analog Output Module, (v) Digital Input Module, (vi) Digital Output Module, (vii) Signal Conditioning & Isolation, (viii) Communication Interface, (ix) Watchdog & RTC. (0.375 × 4 = 1.5 marks)

**Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, PERAT633, Module 4]**
Explain why the **4–20 mA** current loop is preferred over a **0–20 mA** standard in FCU analog inputs.

*Model Answer (3 marks):*
- The 4 mA lower limit provides a **"Live Zero"**, allowing the FCU to distinguish a true 0 % process value (4 mA) from a broken wire or open transmitter (0 mA). (1.5 marks)
- A 0 mA reading is therefore unambiguously interpreted as a **FAULT** state, enabling built-in diagnostics. (0.75 mark)
- Additionally, the 4 mA bias provides a minimum excitation current to power the two-wire (loop-powered) transmitter electronics. (0.75 mark)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **Note to students:** The KTU end-semester paper (PERAT633) provides an **internal choice** in every 14-mark question. Prepare **both** options below — the examiner can offer you either.

#### **Question 11A (14 Marks)**
*Develop a complete FCU block diagram for a **boiler drum level control** application. With the help of neat blocks, explain the role of (a) the analog input and signal conditioning stages [7 marks] and (b) the control algorithm and communication to the upper SCADA layer [7 marks].*

**Model Solution (14 marks):**

**(a) Analog Input and Signal Conditioning [7 marks]**

1. **Process description** (1 mark): The boiler drum level must be maintained at a fixed setpoint (say, 50 %) despite changes in steam demand. The level is measured by a differential-pressure transmitter (DPT, LT-201) and an independent sight-glass level switch (LSL-201) for safety.
2. **Sensor signal** (0.5 mark): LT-201 outputs **4–20 mA** corresponding to **0–100 %** drum level.
3. **Signal conditioning** (1.5 marks): The loop current flows through a **250 Ω precision sense resistor** at the FCU terminal block, converting it to **1–5 V**. This voltage is fed into an **isolation amplifier** (1500 V RMS galvanic isolation) to break ground loops. A **first-order RC low-pass filter** (cut-off ≈ 10 Hz) follows as an **anti-alias filter** before the ADC.
4. **Analog input module** (1.5 marks): A **12-bit ADC** with a **5 V** reference samples the filtered voltage. Scaling gives digital counts **205 → 0 %** and **4095 → 100 %**. Reverse scaling inside the CPU gives the engineering level.
5. **Scaling equation** (1 mark):
   $$L_{\%} = \frac{I_{loop} - 4}{16} \times 100$$
6. **Safety interlock input** (1.5 marks): LSL-201 is wired to a **24 V opto-isolated DI** channel. Firmware debounce (20 ms) prevents false trips. The LSL signal is *hard-logically OR-ed* with the AI high-high alarm in firmware so that either path can shut the feedwater valve.

**(b) Control Algorithm and Communication [7 marks]**

1. **Control objective** (0.5 mark): Manipulate the feedwater control valve (FCV-201) position so that drum level tracks the setpoint.
2. **PID control law** (2 marks): The CPU implements the parallel-form PID:
   $$u(t) = K_{p}\,e(t) + K_{i}\!\int_{0}^{t}\! e(\tau)\,d\tau + K_{d}\,\frac{de(t)}{dt}$$
   where $e(t) = SP(t) - PV(t)$. The output $u(t)$ is normalised to **0–100 %** and then converted to **4–20 mA** to drive the valve positioner.
3. **Output stage** (1 mark): The 4–20 mA AO module is short-circuit protected and supplies the positioner. A digital output (DO) energises the valve solenoid (XY-201) as the run permit.
4. **Scan cycle** (1.5 marks): Cyclic order — *Read AI/DI → Apply interlocks → Run PID → Update AO/DO → Refresh comms* with a deterministic cycle time of **100 ms**.
5. **Communication to upper layer** (2 marks): The FCU exposes its process variables over **Modbus RTU on RS-485**, mapped as:
   - Holding Register 40001: $L_{\%}$ × 10
   - Holding Register 40002: $SP$ × 10
   - Holding Register 40003: $u$ × 10
   - Input Register 30001: LSL/LSH status (bit-packed)
   - Coil 00001: XY-201 command

   The RTU/MTU polls these registers every 1 s, so even if the SCADA link is severed, the FCU continues to control the drum locally.

#### **Question 11B (14 Marks)**
*Explain the **step-by-step methodology** to develop the FCU diagram for a **simple temperature control application** of an electric furnace. With a neat block diagram, illustrate the **internal architecture** of the FCU. Discuss in detail (a) the design procedure and I/O inventory [7 marks], and (b) the signal conditioning and protection stages [7 marks].*

**Model Solution (14 marks):**

**(a) Design Procedure and I/O Inventory [7 marks]**

1. **Control objective** (0.5 mark): Maintain furnace temperature $T$ at a setpoint of, say, **750 °C** by modulating the power delivered to a resistive heating element via a **Solid-State Relay (SSR)** or a **thyristor stack**.
2. **Sensors** (1 mark): A **Type K thermocouple (TT-301)** provides the primary measurement (≈ **0–50 mV** for 0–1200 °C). A **PT100 RTD (TT-301B)** may be used as backup. A door interlock switch (ZS-301) provides safety DI.
3. **Actuator** (0.5 mark): A three-phase **SCR / thyristor power controller** accepts a **4–20 mA** setpoint input to phase-angle fire the heaters. A cooling-fan contactor (KM-301) is a DO.
4. **I/O inventory table** (3 marks):

   | Tag | Type | Signal | Range | Function |
   |---|---|---|---|---|
   | TT-301 | AI | Type-K TC | 0–1200 °C | Primary PV |
   | TT-301B | AI | PT100 RTD | 0–1000 °C | Backup PV |
   | SS-301 | DI | Setpoint selector | 24 V | Auto/Manual |
   | TS-301 | DI | Start/Stop | 24 V | Operator |
   | ZS-301 | DI | Door interlock | 24 V | Safety |
   | SCR-301 | AO | 4–20 mA | 0–100 % power | Heater drive |
   | KM-301 | DO | Relay | 24 V | Cooling fan |
   | HL-301 | DO | Lamp | 24 V | Trip indication |
   | COMM | — | Modbus RTU | RS-485 | To RTU |

5. **FCU platform selection** (1 mark): A compact PLC with at least 4 AI, 4 DI, 1 AO, 2 DO, and a Modbus RTU port; scan time < 100 ms.
6. **Diagram drawn** (1 mark): Show the I/O map flowing into the FCU CPU, with the SCR and contactor as final elements, and the Modbus uplink to the upper SCADA.

**(b) Signal Conditioning and Protection [7 marks]**

1. **Thermocouple cold-junction compensation (CJC)** (1.5 marks): The Type-K millivolt signal is amplified by an **instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., AD8495) that includes on-chip CJC using an on-board silicon diode at the FCU terminal.
2. **Anti-alias filter** (1 mark): A first-order RC filter (R = 10 kΩ, C = 100 nF → $\tau = 1\ \text{ms}$, $f_{c} \approx 160\ \text{Hz}$) precedes the 16-bit ADC.
3. **RTD signal conditioning** (1.5 marks): A **ratiometric** measurement is used: a precision current source (e.g., 1 mA) drives the PT100; the voltage drop is read differentially by the ADC. This automatically cancels the current-source drift and reference drift errors.
4. **Galvanic isolation** (1 mark): All AI/DI channels are opto-isolated to break ground loops; all DO/AO are also isolated from the CPU.
5. **SSR/SCR drive protection** (1 mark): The 4–20 mA AO is converted to a **0–10 V** control signal for the SCR firing board, with a **snubber RC network** across each thyristor and a **TVS** at the FCU output for surge suppression.
6. **Watchdog and fault handling** (1 mark): If the thermocouple reads open (broken junction), the ADC input saturates; the CPU detects this as a fault and forces the SCR output to **0 %** (fail-safe cold state) and energises HL-301 (trip lamp).

### 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students most commonly lose marks on FCU diagram questions:**
> 1. **Drawing only a flow chart, not a true FCU block diagram** — the examiner expects labelled **functional blocks** (AI, AO, DI, DO, CPU, Power, Comms) with **arrowed signal labels** and **electrical isolation symbols** between them.
> 2. **Omitting the "Why" of signal conditioning** — listing "anti-alias filter" without stating it removes frequencies above $f_{s}/2$ to prevent aliasing loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Forgetting the live-zero concept** — answers that state "4–20 mA = 0–100 %" without mentioning fault detection at **0 mA** are incomplete.
> 4. **Confusing FCU with RTU** — the FCU controls *one* local loop/equipment; the RTU aggregates *many* FCUs. Conflating them in a "Develop FCU" answer costs 2–3 marks.
> 5. **Missing the 250 Ω sense resistor trick** — examiners expect the conversion from 4–20 mA to 1–5 V explicitly, especially in design derivations.
> 6. **No fail-safe discussion** — always state what happens on sensor failure (e.g., "TC open → SCR off → furnace cold", not just "system alarms").

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **FCU = Level 1 of the SCADA hierarchy**, sitting between Level 0 (field) and Level 2 (RTU/MTU).
- **Core blocks**: Power Supply, CPU + Memory, AI, AO, DI, DO, Signal Conditioning & Isolation, Communication Interface, Watchdog.
- **4–20 mA** is the dominant industrial analog standard; it provides **live-zero fault detection** and **loop-powered** transmitter operation.
- **250 Ω** precision sense resistor converts 4–20 mA → 1–5 V, the single most common board-level design pattern.
- **PT100** RTD: $R_0 = 100\ \Omega$, $\alpha = 0.00385\ \text{°C}^{-1}$; use **ratiometric** measurement.
- **Type-K thermocouple** ≈ 41 µV/°C; always use an instrumentation amplifier with **on-chip cold-junction compensation**.
- **PID control law**: $u(t) = K_{p} e + K_{i} \int e\,dt + K_{d} \dfrac{de}{dt}$, with $e = SP - PV$.
- **Scan cycle** is deterministic: *Read inputs → Solve logic → Write outputs → Refresh comms.*
- **Anti-alias filter** must cut off at $f_{c} \le f_{s}/2$; in practice choose $f_{s} = 5 \text{ to } 10 \times f_{max}$.
- **Galvanic isolation** (typ. 1500 V RMS) protects the CPU from plant-side transients and ground loops.
- **Hardwired interlocks** (e.g., LSH, LSL) must operate independently of the CPU for SIL-rated safety.
- **Modbus RTU** is the most common FCU-to-RTU protocol; map every process variable to a unique **holding/input register** or **coil**.
- **Watchdog timer** is mandatory; a hung CPU is unacceptable in an unattended FCU.
- **Fail-safe design**: sensor loss → output goes to a *safe* state (valve fail-closed, heater off, pump stop).
- **FCU ≠ RTU**: the FCU controls one piece of equipment locally; the RTU aggregates many FCUs for area supervision.
- **Determinism** (bounded scan time) is the property that distinguishes a real-time FCU from a generic PC.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to HART and Fieldbus Protocol
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT633 - SUPERVISORY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Introduction to HART and Fieldbus Protocol

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 4 — INTRODUCTION TO HART AND FIELDBUS PROTOCOL

## 1.1 Core Technical Definitions

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HART Protocol (Highway Addressable Remote Transducer)** is a bi-directional, hybrid industrial communication protocol that overlays a digital signal on top of the legacy analog 4–20 mA current loop. It was originally developed by **Rosemount Inc.** in the mid-1980s and is now maintained by the **FieldComm Group**. It uses the **Bell 202** standard Frequency Shift Keying (FSK) technique operating at a data rate of **1200 bps**, with **1200 Hz** representing binary **'1'** and **2200 Hz** representing binary **'0'**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fieldbus** is an all-digital, serial, two-way, multi-drop, distributed communication protocol that completely replaces the analog 4–20 mA loop. It connects field-level intelligent devices (sensors, actuators, controllers) directly to a host/controller system. The most prominent industrial variant is **FOUNDATION Fieldbus (FF)**, which has two standardized speeds: **H1 (31.25 kbps)** for process automation and **HSE (High Speed Ethernet, 100 Mbps)** for higher-level integration.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

**Analogy for HART — "Two People Talking on One Telephone Wire":**
Imagine you have a single telephone line (the 4–20 mA loop) used to convey an important measurement — say the level of water in a tank. The analog voice speaking on the line is the 4–20 mA signal — it carries the actual numeric measurement continuously. Now imagine that, **without ever disconnecting the call**, you also embed a quiet, high-frequency whispered digital data stream on the same wire. This whisper tells the receiver *who* the speaker is, *what units* they are using, the *calibration* date, the *device tag*, and lets you send back *configuration commands*. That whisper is the HART digital signal. The genius is that **both voices coexist** — the operator still gets his 4–20 mA reading while a maintenance engineer simultaneously configures the device digitally.

**Analogy for Fieldbus — "A Group Conference Call Where Everyone Votes":**
Now imagine replacing the analog whisper concept entirely. Instead of one boss giving a single number, every field device (temperature transmitter, valve positioner, flow meter) is given its own voice and intelligence. They all sit on **the same shared cable (the "bus")** and talk to each other and the controller in a structured, time-synchronized manner. They can publish measurements *and* subscribe to commands from others. There is no analog loop at all — everything is a discrete digital message. This is Fieldbus — a **distributed democracy of intelligent instruments**.

### 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics

- **HART FSK Frequencies:** **1200 Hz = '1'**, **2200 Hz = '0'**
- **HART Data Rate:** **1200 bits per second**
- **HART Loop Power Supply:** **24 V DC (nominal)**, range **11.5 V – 45 V DC**
- **H1 Fieldbus Data Rate:** **31.25 kbps**
- **H1 Fieldbus Cable Length (max):** **1900 m (segment)**
- **Maximum H1 Devices per Segment:** **32 (theoretical)**, **16 (practical hazardous area)**
- **H1 Bus Voltage Range:** **9 V – 32 V DC**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 specifically targets the *Introduction* to these two protocols. Examiners expect students to know the **layered architecture**, **signal encoding**, **topology**, and a clear **comparative understanding** of HART vs. Fieldbus.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS

## 2.1 HART Protocol Architecture (OSI Perspective)

HART follows a **simplified 3-layer model** (compared to the 7-layer OSI model):

| HART Layer | OSI Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Application Layer** | Layers 7, 6, 5 | HART Commands (Universal, Common Practice, Device-Specific) |
| **Data Link Layer** | Layer 2 | Frame structure, error detection (parity + checksum), master-slave arbitration |
| **Physical Layer** | Layer 1 | Bell 202 FSK signaling over 4–20 mA loop |

### 2.2 HART Signal Encoding — The 'How'

1. **Analog Component (4–20 mA):** Represents the **primary process variable (PV)** continuously. The current is proportional to the measured quantity.
2. **Digital Component (FSK):** A sine wave at **1200 Hz** or **2200 Hz** is superimposed on the DC analog signal. The **average value** of this AC component is **zero**, so it does not affect the 4–20 mA measurement.
3. **Average Value of FSK Signal = 0** → This is the mathematical elegance that allows **simultaneous** analog + digital communication.

### 2.3 HART Network Topologies

- **Point-to-Point:** One master ↔ one slave (most common, with 4–20 mA preserved).
- **Multi-drop:** Up to **15** slave devices on a single pair, transmitting **only digital** values; the analog current is fixed at **4 mA** (energy-saving mode).

### 2.4 HART Frame Structure

Each HART frame consists of:

| Field | Length | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Preamble** | 5–20 bytes | Synchronization (typically 5 bytes) |
| **Start Delimiter** | 1 byte | Frame type identification |
| **Address** | 1 or 5 bytes | Master (Polling Address 0–15) + Slave ID |
| **Command** | 1 byte | Operation code (0–253) |
| **Byte Count** | 1 byte | Data length |
| **Data** | 0–25 bytes | Payload |
| **Checksum** | 1 byte | XOR-based error detection |

### 2.5 Fieldbus Protocol Architecture

**FOUNDATION Fieldbus** uses a true **3-layer + User Layer** model:

| FF Layer | OSI Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **User Layer** | Layer 7 (extension) | Function Blocks (AI, AO, PID, etc.) |
| **Application Layer** | Layer 7 | FAS (Fieldbus Access Sublayer), FMS (Fieldbus Messaging Specification) |
| **Data Link Layer** | Layer 2 | LAS (Link Active Scheduler), bus arbitration |
| **Physical Layer** | Layer 1 | Manchester encoding (H1) or IEEE 802.3 Ethernet (HSE) |

### 2.6 Fieldbus H1 — Operational Detail

- **Encoding:** **Manchester Biphase-L** (a transition in every bit period → clock recovery is automatic).
- **A logical '1'** = positive-going transition in the middle of the bit period.
- **A logical '0'** = negative-going transition in the middle of the bit period.
- **Link Master (LAS)** controls all bus access. Each H1 segment has exactly **one LAS** (typically a host or dedicated bridge).

### 2.7 Comparison Matrix — HART vs. Fieldbus

| Parameter | HART | Foundation Fieldbus H1 |
|---|---|---|
| **Signal Type** | Hybrid (Analog + Digital) | Pure Digital |
| **Replaces 4–20 mA?** | No — overlays it | Yes |
| **Data Rate** | 1200 bps | 31.25 kbps |
| **Encoding** | Bell 202 FSK | Manchester |
| **Topology** | Point-to-point / Multi-drop | Multi-drop only |
| **Max Devices** | 15 (multi-drop) | 32 per segment |
| **Cable Length** | Up to ~3000 m | 1900 m |
| **Control in Device?** | Limited | Full function blocks |
| **Power on Bus** | Loop-powered | Bus-powered |

### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for HART & Fieldbus numericals.

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| HART FSK frequency (logic 1) | $f_1 = 1200$ Hz | Hz | Bell 202 standard |
| HART FSK frequency (logic 0) | $f_0 = 2200$ Hz | Hz | Bell 202 standard |
| Bit period at 1200 bps | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{1200}$ | s | $= 0.833$ ms |
| Average FSK signal | $V_{avg} = 0$ | V | Critical for analog coexistence |
| H1 Bit period | $T_b = \dfrac{1}{31250}$ | s | $= 32\,\mu s$ |
| H1 Maximum Devices (safe area) | $N_{max} = 32$ | devices | Per segment |
| H1 Maximum Devices (hazardous) | $N_{max} = 16$ | devices | Per segment |
| H1 Max cable length | $L_{max} = 1900$ | m | For 31.25 kbps |
| H1 Bus voltage | $V_{bus} = 9 \text{ to } 32$ | V DC | |
| HART Loop current | $I = 4 \text{ to } 20$ | mA | Legacy analog signal |
| Percentage of range | $\% = \dfrac{I - 4}{16} \times 100$ | % | Maps current to PV % |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** HART remains the **most widely deployed** smart instrument protocol in the world (estimated > 40 million installed devices) because it protects the legacy 4–20 mA investment. Fieldbus is preferred in **new green-field plants** where full digital integration and distributed control are required.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Why HART FSK Does Not Disturb the 4–20 mA Loop

The HART protocol superimposes a high-frequency AC waveform on the DC loop current. We must mathematically prove that the **average DC value remains unchanged**, preserving the 4–20 mA measurement.

**Step 1 — Define the FSK signal:**

A binary FSK signal alternates between two frequencies $f_0$ and $f_1$. Over a long period, assuming roughly equal probability of '0' and '1':

$$s_{FSK}(t) = A \cdot \sin(2\pi f_1 t)$$

during a '1' bit, and

$$s_{FSK}(t) = A \cdot \sin(2\pi f_0 t)$$

during a '0' bit.

**Step 2 — Compute the time-average of one full bit:**

For a '1' bit of duration $T_b$:

$$\overline{s_1} = \frac{1}{T_b} \int_{0}^{T_b} A \sin(2\pi f_1 t) \, dt$$

$$= \frac{A}{2\pi f_1 T_b} \Big[ -\cos(2\pi f_1 t) \Big]_0^{T_b}$$

Since $T_b = \frac{1}{f_1}$ is an integer multiple of the carrier period:

$$\overline{s_1} = \frac{A}{2\pi f_1} \cdot \frac{1}{T_b} \big[ -\cos(2\pi) + \cos(0) \big] = \frac{A}{2\pi f_1 T_b}(1 - 1) = 0$$

The same logic applies to $f_0$. Since **both** average to zero, the **time-average of the entire FSK signal is zero**, regardless of bit pattern.

**Step 3 — Conclusion:**

$$I_{loop}(t) = I_{DC} + I_{FSK}(t)$$

$$\overline{I_{loop}} = I_{DC} + 0 = I_{DC}$$

The DC measurement is therefore **completely unaffected** by the digital overlay. This is the foundational mathematical justification for HART's hybrid architecture.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Bit Period and Frequency Relationship

For HART with $R = 1200$ bps:

$$T_b = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{1200} \approx 8.33 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = 0.833\,\text{ms}$$

For H1 Fieldbus with $R = 31.25$ kbps:

$$T_b = \frac{1}{R} = \frac{1}{31250} = 32 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} = 32\,\mu s$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: 4–20 mA to Engineering Units Conversion

A HART transmitter reports its loop current $I$ in mA. The relationship between the current and the engineering range is **linear**:

$$\%PV = \frac{I - 4}{20 - 4} \times 100 = \frac{I - 4}{16} \times 100$$

**Worked Example:**
A temperature transmitter has range **0 °C to 200 °C** and outputs **12 mA**.

$$\%PV = \frac{12 - 4}{16} \times 100 = \frac{8}{16} \times 100 = 50\%$$

Engineering value:

$$T = 0 + 0.50 \times (200 - 0) = 100\,°C$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Number of Devices on an H1 Segment

The H1 specification limits the device count to balance **current draw, signal attenuation, and short-circuit fault tolerance**.

- **Bus supply current:** typically limited to **350 mA** per segment.
- **Minimum operating current per device:** ≈ **10 mA**.

$$N_{max} = \frac{I_{bus,total}}{I_{device,min}} = \frac{350}{10} \approx 32 \text{ devices}$$

**Practical limit in hazardous (IS) areas:**

In intrinsically safe zones, the safety barrier limits available current. To leave a **50% safety margin**:

$$N_{IS,max} = \frac{N_{max}}{2} = \frac{32}{2} = 16 \text{ devices}$$

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison Calculations

| Scenario | HART Response | Fieldbus H1 Response |
|---|---|---|
| Send 1 byte (8 bits) of data | $\dfrac{8}{1200} = 6.67$ ms | $\dfrac{8}{31250} = 0.256$ ms |
| Transmit a 25-byte HART frame | $\dfrac{8 \times 25}{1200} \approx 167$ ms | N/A (frames are different) |
| Cable length limit | ~3000 m (point-to-point) | 1900 m |
| Analog + Digital coexistence? | YES | NO |

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Frame Time Calculation

**Problem (KTU-style):**
A HART master transmits a command frame containing **15 bytes of data**, using **5-byte preamble**, **1-byte delimiter**, **5-byte address**, **1-byte command**, **1-byte byte count**, and **1-byte checksum**. Compute the total frame transmission time.

**Solution:**

Total bytes = 5 + 1 + 5 + 1 + 1 + 15 + 1 = 29 bytes

Total bits = 29 × 8 = 232 bits

$$T_{frame} = \frac{\text{bits}}{R} = \frac{232}{1200} \approx 0.1933\,\text{s} \approx 193\,\text{ms}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This explains why HART is **not suitable for fast closed-loop control** — each transaction takes hundreds of milliseconds, which is too slow for a control loop running at 1 Hz or higher.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 HART Protocol — Layered Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph APP["APPLICATION LAYER"]
        cmd1["HART Commands: Universal 0-30, Common Practice 32-126, Device Specific 128-253"]
    end

    subgraph DL["DATA LINK LAYER"]
        frm["Frame Assembly: Preamble + Delimiter + Address + Command + Byte Count + Data + Checksum"]
        err["Error Detection: XOR Parity Checksum"]
    end

    subgraph PHY["PHYSICAL LAYER"]
        fsk["Bell 202 FSK Modem"]
        sig1["1200 Hz = Logic 1"]
        sig0["2200 Hz = Logic 0"]
        loop["4-20 mA Analog Loop DC Component"]
    end

    APP --> DL
    DL --> PHY
    fsk --> sig1
    fsk --> sig0
    loop --> PHY
```

## 4.2 HART Network — Point-to-Point vs. Multi-drop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph P2P["POINT-TO-POINT MODE"]
        master1["HART Master / DCS"]
        dev1["Field Device 1"]
        master1 -- "4-20 mA + FSK Digital" --> dev1
    end

    subgraph MD["MULTI-DROP MODE"]
        master2["HART Master"]
        bus2["Single Twisted Pair Bus"]
        d1["Device 1 - Tag 101"]
        d2["Device 2 - Tag 102"]
        d3["Device 3 - Tag 103"]
        master2 --- bus2
        bus2 --- d1
        bus2 --- d2
        bus2 --- d3
    end
```

## 4.3 Foundation Fieldbus H1 — Network Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HOST["HOST CONTROL SYSTEM / DCS"]
        hse["HSE Interface 100 Mbps Ethernet"]
    end

    subgraph BRIDGE["H1 LINKING DEVICE / BRIDGE"]
        las["Link Active Scheduler LAS"]
    end

    subgraph SEG["H1 SEGMENT 31.25 kbps"]
        v1["Smart Valve Positioner"]
        t1["Temperature Transmitter"]
        p1["Pressure Transmitter"]
        f1["Flow Meter with PID Block"]
    end

    hse ---|Ethernet| las
    las ---|Manchester Coded Bus| v1
    las ---|Scheduled Communication| t1
    las ---|Publisher Subscriber| p1
    las ---|Function Block Execution| f1
```

## 4.4 OSI Layer Mapping — HART vs. Fieldbus vs. 7-Layer OSI

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    osi7["OSI L7 Application"]
    osi6["OSI L6 Presentation"]
    osi5["OSI L5 Session"]
    osi4["OSI L4 Transport"]
    osi3["OSI L3 Network"]
    osi2["OSI L2 Data Link"]
    osi1["OSI L1 Physical"]

    subgraph HART["HART MODEL"]
        h7["HART Commands"]
        h2["Frame Structure"]
        h1["Bell 202 FSK over 4-20 mA"]
    end

    subgraph FF["FOUNDATION FIELDBUS"]
        ffU["User Layer - Function Blocks"]
        ffA["Application Layer - FMS FAS"]
        ffD["Data Link Layer - LAS"]
        ffP["Physical Layer - Manchester H1 or Ethernet HSE"]
    end

    osi7 --- h7
    osi2 --- h2
    osi1 --- h1
    osi7 --- ffU
    osi7 --- ffA
    osi2 --- ffD
    osi1 --- ffP
```

## 4.5 HART Frame — Byte-Level Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p["PREAMBLE 5-20 bytes"]
    s["START DELIMITER 1 byte"]
    a["ADDRESS 1 or 5 bytes"]
    c["COMMAND 1 byte"]
    bc["BYTE COUNT 1 byte"]
    d["DATA 0-25 bytes"]
    chk["CHECKSUM 1 byte"]

    p --> s --> a --> c --> bc --> d --> chk
```

## 4.6 Signal Encoding — Bell 202 FSK vs. Manchester

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FSK["HART BELL 202 FSK"]
        f1["Bit 1 = 1200 Hz Sine Wave"]
        f0["Bit 0 = 2200 Hz Sine Wave"]
    end

    subgraph MAN["FIELD BUS H1 MANCHESTER"]
        m1["Bit 1 = LOW to HIGH Transition Mid Bit"]
        m0["Bit 0 = HIGH to LOW Transition Mid Bit"]
    end

    FSK --> note["Average Value = 0 — coexists with DC loop"]
    MAN --> note2["Clock recovered from edges — no DC coexistence"]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 1] [3 Marks]**
> **Q:** Define the **HART protocol**. Mention its data rate and the two FSK frequencies used.

**Model Answer:**
HART (Highway Addressable Remote Transducer) is a hybrid industrial communication protocol that overlays a digital signal on the conventional 4–20 mA analog loop. It uses the Bell 202 standard FSK technique. The data rate is **1200 bps**. The two frequencies used are:
- **1200 Hz** representing binary **'1'**
- **2200 Hz** representing binary **'0'**

The key advantage is that the FSK signal has a **zero average value**, so it does not disturb the analog measurement.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question 2] [3 Marks]**
> **Q:** What is **FOUNDATION Fieldbus**? Mention its two variants and their data rates.

**Model Answer:**
FOUNDATION Fieldbus is an all-digital, bi-directional, multi-drop serial communication protocol used to interconnect field-level intelligent devices (sensors, actuators, controllers) in process automation. Its two standardized variants are:
- **H1** — operating at **31.25 kbps**, used for field-level device networks.
- **HSE (High Speed Ethernet)** — operating at **100 Mbps**, used for control-level integration.

Unlike HART, Fieldbus completely **replaces** the 4–20 mA loop and supports distributed control via **function blocks** resident in the field devices.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / Model Paper Pattern] [14 Marks]**

### **Question A:**

**Q (a)** [7 Marks]
Explain the **layered architecture of the HART protocol** with a neat block diagram. Describe how the analog 4–20 mA signal and the digital FSK signal coexist on the same wire.

**Model Answer:**

HART follows a simplified 3-layer model mapped onto the OSI architecture:

1. **Physical Layer (OSI Layer 1):** Uses Bell 202 FSK modulation. Two frequencies — **1200 Hz (logic 1)** and **2200 Hz (logic 0)** — are superimposed on the DC 4–20 mA loop. The AC waveform has zero average value, so the analog reading is undisturbed.

2. **Data Link Layer (OSI Layer 2):** Defines the frame structure — preamble (5–20 bytes), start delimiter, address, command, byte count, data, and checksum. Error detection uses an XOR-based longitudinal checksum.

3. **Application Layer (OSI Layer 7):** Defines HART commands grouped into three classes:
   - **Universal Commands** (0–30): Common to all HART devices (e.g., read manufacturer ID).
   - **Common Practice Commands** (32–126): Common across device classes.
   - **Device-Specific Commands** (128–253): Unique to a manufacturer/model.

**Coexistence Mechanism:**
The FSK AC component oscillates symmetrically around the DC current. When averaged over time, its contribution is zero, so the **DC component carrying the 4–20 mA PV reading remains intact**. The receiver uses a low-pass filter to extract the DC value and a band-pass filter to extract the digital data.

**(b)** [7 Marks]
A temperature transmitter has a range of **0 °C to 500 °C** and the HART loop current measured is **14 mA**. Determine:
(i) The percentage of range.
(ii) The engineering temperature value.

**Solution:**

(i) Percentage of range:

$$\% PV = \frac{I - 4}{16} \times 100 = \frac{14 - 4}{16} \times 100 = \frac{10}{16} \times 100 = 62.5\%$$

(ii) Engineering temperature:

$$T = T_{min} + \frac{\% PV}{100} \times (T_{max} - T_{min})$$

$$T = 0 + 0.625 \times (500 - 0) = 312.5\,°C$$

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice):**

**Q (a)** [7 Marks]
Describe the **architecture of FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1** with a block diagram. Explain the role of the **Link Active Scheduler (LAS)**.

**Model Answer:**

FOUNDATION Fieldbus H1 uses a 4-layer model:

1. **Physical Layer:** Manchester Biphase-L encoding at **31.25 kbps**. The bus voltage is **9–32 V DC**, and a segment can be up to **1900 m** long supporting up to **32 devices**.

2. **Data Link Layer:** Governed by the **LAS (Link Active Scheduler)** which is the deterministic bus arbiter. Only one LAS exists per H1 segment. It:
   - Grants **compel data** tokens to devices on a scheduled basis.
   - Handles **unscheduled (event-driven) communication** via the pass token.
   - Maintains a **live list** of all active devices.

3. **Application Layer:** Consists of **FMS (Fieldbus Messaging Specification)** for message services and **FAS (Fieldbus Access Sublayer)** for mapping FMS onto the DLL.

4. **User Layer:** Contains **Function Blocks** (AI, AO, PID, etc.) that execute control strategies inside the field devices, enabling distributed control.

**(b)** [7 Marks]
Compare **HART and FOUNDATION Fieldbus** under the following heads: (i) Signal type, (ii) Data rate, (iii) Topology, (iv) Maximum devices per segment, (v) Replacement of 4–20 mA, (vi) Encoding scheme, (vii) Application suitability.

**Model Comparative Answer:**

| Parameter | HART | Foundation Fieldbus H1 |
|---|---|---|
| (i) Signal type | Hybrid (Analog + Digital) | Pure Digital |
| (ii) Data rate | 1200 bps | 31.25 kbps |
| (iii) Topology | Point-to-point or multi-drop | Multi-drop only |
| (iv) Max devices | 15 (multi-drop) | 32 per segment |
| (v) Replaces 4–20 mA? | No — overlays | Yes — replaces |
| (vi) Encoding | Bell 202 FSK | Manchester Biphase-L |
| (vii) Suitability | Configuration, diagnostics on legacy loops | New green-field digital plants, distributed control |

**Conclusion:** HART is the **bridge protocol** between analog and digital worlds, ideal for retrofitting existing plants. Fieldbus is the **future-ready protocol** for new installations requiring full digital integration and decentralized control.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Losing Mistakes:**
> 1. **Confusing HART frequencies:** Many students write "1200 Hz = 0 and 2200 Hz = 1" — the correct mapping is **1200 Hz = 1** and **2200 Hz = 0**.
> 2. **Wrong H1 data rate:** Fieldbus H1 is **31.25 kbps**, not 1200 bps (that is HART).
> 3. **Forgetting the average-zero property:** If you cannot explain *why* the FSK does not disturb the DC loop, you will lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. **OSI layer mapping:** HART is a **3-layer protocol**, Fieldbus is a **4-layer protocol**. Conflating them is a common error.
> 5. **Mistaking HSE:** HSE is **100 Mbps Ethernet**, NOT 31.25 kbps. HSE speed must be remembered distinctly from H1.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ HART = **H**ighway **A**ddressable **R**emote **T**ransducer — hybrid analog + digital protocol.
- ✅ HART developed by **Rosemount**, maintained by **FieldComm Group**.
- ✅ HART encoding: **Bell 202 FSK**, **1200 bps**.
- ✅ HART frequencies: **1200 Hz (1)**, **2200 Hz (0)**.
- ✅ FSK average value = **0** → analog loop preserved.
- ✅ HART supports **point-to-point** and **multi-drop (≤ 15 devices)**.
- ✅ HART loop current range: **4–20 mA**.
- ✅ HART frame: **Preamble → Delimiter → Address → Command → Byte Count → Data → Checksum**.
- ✅ HART command classes: **Universal (0–30), Common Practice (32–126), Device-Specific (128–253)**.
- ✅ FOUNDATION Fieldbus = **all-digital** distributed control protocol.
- ✅ FF H1 = **31.25 kbps**, **Manchester encoding**, up to **1900 m**, **32 devices**.
- ✅ FF HSE = **100 Mbps Ethernet** for higher-level integration.
- ✅ LAS (Link Active Scheduler) = **deterministic bus arbiter** in H1.
- ✅ Function Blocks (AI, AO, PID) live inside FF field devices.
- ✅ HART **overlays** 4–20 mA; Fieldbus **replaces** it.
- ✅ HART is best for **retrofit/legacy** plants; Fieldbus is best for **green-field digital** plants.
- ✅ Formula to remember: $\% PV = \dfrac{I - 4}{16} \times 100$.
- ✅ Bit period: $T_b = \dfrac{1}{R}$ — apply for both 1200 bps and 31.25 kbps.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing Smart Field Devices (Wired and Wireless) with DCS Controller
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERAT633 - SUPERVISORY CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Interfacing Smart Field Devices (Wired and Wireless) with DCS Controller

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Smart Field Device** is a microprocessor-based industrial measurement or control instrument (pressure transmitter, temperature transmitter, flow meter, control valve positioner, variable speed drive, etc.) that contains an embedded digital communication transceiver in addition to the conventional analog I/O. It implements an open, bidirectional digital protocol layered on top of (or replacing) the legacy 4–20 mA current loop, allowing simultaneous transmission of the **Primary Variable (PV)**, **secondary variables**, **device configuration parameters**, **calibration data**, and **diagnostic / health information** to a host system.

A **Distributed Control System (DCS) Controller** is a redundant, deterministic process-control computer that executes regulatory, logic, sequential, and advanced control strategies. It acquires process data from smart field devices through dedicated **I/O cards, communication gateways, segment couplers, and field networks**, and drives final control elements (valves, drives) through output modules.

**Interfacing** therefore refers to the **physical layer, protocol stack, addressing scheme, data mapping, and device-integration methodology** used to integrate smart field devices with the DCS controller — both over **wired** media (twisted pair, fibre, bus) and **wireless** media (2.4 GHz IEEE 802.15.4 radio).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *“Smart field devices are intelligent sensors and actuators equipped with embedded microprocessors that support digital communication protocols such as HART, Foundation Fieldbus, Profibus, and WirelessHART. They interface with the DCS controller through analogue current loops, digital bus networks, or wireless mesh networks, providing both process data and rich diagnostic information.”*

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – Plain English Intuition

Imagine a **classroom teacher (DCS Controller)** and a group of **students holding microphones (Smart Field Devices)**.

In the **1950s**, only **one student** could speak at a time, and only by **shouting a single number from 0 to 100** (the **4–20 mA** signal). The teacher would hear the number, gauge the child's emotion roughly, and act on it — but could not ask follow-up questions, and the child could not volunteer additional information.

In the **modern smart classroom**, every student carries a **smartphone** (the embedded microprocessor + digital protocol). The child does not just say *"I am 72 % full"* — they can also tell the teacher:

* What **subject** they are studying (variable tag).
* What their **historical performance** has been (trend & diagnostics).
* Whether they have a **fever or low battery** (device health).
* Their **calibration certificate** (device ID, range, serial number).
* Whether they are being **asked by another teacher** (peer-to-peer / publisher-subscriber).

The **teacher's desk** has a **specialised docking station** (the **DCS I/O card / segment coupler / wireless gateway**) that *translates* the digital phone language into a form the teacher’s brain (control algorithm) understands. The **Wi-Fi router** in the classroom is the **wireless gateway** that handles the radio side.

That translation, addressing, and the “docking station” together constitute **interfacing**.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics, Constants & Terminology (Bold Highlights)

| Term | Value / Definition |
|---|---|
| **Standard analogue signal** | **4–20 mA DC** (IEC 60381) |
| **HART carrier frequency** | **Bell 202 FSK, 1200 bps, 2200 Hz = “1”, 1200 Hz = “0”** |
| **Foundation Fieldbus H1** | **31.25 kbit/s**, **IEC 61158-2** physical layer |
| **Foundation Fieldbus HSE** | **100 Mbit/s** Fast Ethernet |
| **Profibus PA** | **31.25 kbit/s**, Manchester coding, IEC 61158-2 |
| **Profibus DP** | **9.6 kbit/s – 12 Mbit/s**, RS-485 |
| **WirelessHART** | **IEEE 802.15.4**, **2.4–2.4835 GHz**, DSSS, 250 kbps |
| **ISA100.11a** | **IEEE 802.15.4**, **2.4 GHz**, channel hopping |
| **Typical HART device address** | **0–15 in multidrop**, **1–63 in long-tag mode** |
| **FF link active scheduler cycle** | **Macrocycle** = configured by LAS, typically **≤ 1 s** |

> [!NOTE]
> **One line to remember:**
> *“Smart = Analogue measurement + Digital brain + Bidirectional digital communication.”*

---

## 1.4 Categories of Smart Field Devices

**Smart Sensors** — pressure, differential pressure, temperature (RTD/TC), flow (Coriolis, mag, vortex), level, analytical (pH, conductivity, dissolved O₂), position, vibration.

**Smart Actuators** — digital valve positioners (DVC), smart variable speed drives (VSD/VFD), smart electric actuators, smart motor protection relays (MPC, ETP).

**Smart I/O & Multiplexers** — remote I/O units, multiplexers that aggregate multiple HART devices onto one trunk.

> [!TIP]
> For KTU answers, **always classify** the device: *“It is a smart *sensor* (or *actuator*) because it contains an embedded microprocessor and supports the HART/FF/Profibus protocol.”*

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Smart Device Architecture

A smart field device internally contains **four functional blocks**:

1. **Primary Sensing / Actuating Element** — diaphragm, RTD, strain gauge, piezoelectric crystal, motor winding, etc.
2. **Signal Conditioning & A/D Conversion** — amplification, linearisation, cold-junction compensation.
3. **Microprocessor / Digital Core** — runs the firmware, executes compensation algorithms, stores calibration coefficients (look-up tables), performs self-diagnostics.
4. **Communication Interface** — the physical & data link layer electronics (HART modem, FF stack, Profibus ASIC, radio transceiver).

A typical **HART smart pressure transmitter** therefore performs:

$$
P_{\text{raw}} \xrightarrow{\text{strain gauge}} V \xrightarrow{\text{ADC}} N_{\text{counts}} \xrightarrow{\text{linearise}} P_{\text{engineered}}
$$

The same digital value is **simultaneously** converted to a **4–20 mA** current *and* broadcast on a **digital HART frame** superimposed on the same two wires.

---

## 2.2 The 4–20 mA + HART Hybrid (The “Bridge” Solution)

* **Analogue channel** carries the **process variable** in a robust, noise-immune current loop.
* **Digital HART channel** carries **variable units, range, calibration, diagnostics, multidrop addressing**.
* **Simultaneous, non-interfering** because HART uses **FSK** at 1200/2200 Hz, while the 4–20 mA DC is at **0 Hz**.

The DC current is filtered by a **250 Ω sense resistor** producing:

$$
V_{\text{meas}} = (4 + 16 \cdot x) \times 250 \;\Omega \quad \text{where} \; x = \dfrac{PV - LRV}{URV - LRV} \in [0,1]
$$

Hence the digital voltage is **1.0 V DC at 4 mA** and **5.0 V DC at 20 mA**, with the HART signal riding between ±0.5 mA peak-to-peak on top.

### 2.2.1 HART Frame Structure (KTU favourite)

| Field | Length | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Preamble** | 5–20 bytes (0xFF) | Synchronisation |
| **Start Delimiter** | 1 byte | Frame start |
| **Address** | 1 or 5 bytes | Short or long tag |
| **Command** | 1 byte | Universal / Common / Device-specific |
| **Byte Count** | 1 byte | Data length |
| **Data** | 0–255 bytes | Command payload |
| **Checksum** | 1 byte (XOR) | Integrity |

**Universal Commands** (0, 1, 2, 3, 6, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18, 19) work with *any* HART device — read PV, read tag, write tag, read manufacturer, etc.

**Common Practice Commands** (38, 39, 40, 41, 42, 43, 44, 45, 46, 48, 50, 51, 53, 54, 59, 60, 61, 62, 70, 71, 72, 73, 74, 75, 76, 77, 78, 79, 80, 81, 82, 83, 84, 85, 86, 87, 88, 89, 90, 91, 92, 93, 107, 108, 109) — common to most devices.

**Device-Specific Commands** (128–253) — manufacturer-defined.

### 2.2.2 HART Networking Topologies

* **Point-to-Point** — one transmitter, one DCS analogue input. The 4–20 mA value is the primary variable; HART provides digital piggyback.
* **Multidrop** — up to **15** devices on a single twisted pair, current fixed at **4 mA**, all data carried digitally. The DCS uses a **HART multiplexer** or **HART I/O card** with **8 or 16 channels**.

> [!NOTE]
> **In multidrop mode the 4–20 mA value is sacrificed** because the loop is fixed at 4 mA to power all devices. The HART signal is the only data path.

---

## 2.3 Foundation Fieldbus (FF H1 & HSE)

Foundation Fieldbus is a **completely digital** two-wire fieldbus that **replaces** 4–20 mA — there is no analogue signal at all.

### 2.3.1 H1 (31.25 kbit/s, IEC 61158-2)

* **Segment**: a single trunk, ≤ **1900 m** with **9–32 devices** per segment (intrinsically safe: ≤ 6–8 devices / 1 km).
* **Power**: bus-powered from the segment, **9–32 V DC**, **≤ 20 mA per device**.
* **Topology**: bus, tree, point-to-point, or mixed; up to **4 repeaters** ⇒ total length up to **9500 m**.

### 2.3.2 Communication Model — Publisher / Subscriber

The **Link Active Scheduler (LAS)** is the centralised bus arbiter. It maintains a deterministic schedule called the **macrocycle**:

$$
T_{\text{macro}} = T_{\text{scheduled}} + T_{\text{aperiodic}} + T_{\text{reserved}} + T_{\text{unscheduled}}
$$

Within the **scheduled** portion, function blocks (e.g., **AI**, **PID**, **AO**, **AO**) exchange data at fixed offsets.

### 2.3.3 Function Blocks (FB) — the heart of FF

Standard function blocks include:

* **AI** — Analogue Input
* **AO** — Analogue Output
* **PID** — Proportional-Integral-Derivative
* **DI/DO** — Discrete I/O
* **IS** — Input Selector
* **SPG** — Setpoint Generator
* **ML** — Manual Loader
* **SC** — Signal Characteriser
* **AR** — Arithmetic
* **CHAR** — Characteriser

A typical control loop in FF is built from these blocks *distributed* across multiple devices — a key conceptual difference from the centralised PLC/DCS loop.

### 2.3.4 FF HSE (High-Speed Ethernet, 100 Mbit/s)

* **Backbone** for connecting H1 segments to the host system.
* Uses standard Ethernet + TCP/UDP + **FF application layer**.
* **Linking Device** bridges H1 ↔ HSE.

---

## 2.4 Profibus (DP & PA)

| Variant | Speed | Media | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Profibus DP (Decentralised Periphery)** | 9.6 kbit/s – 12 Mbit/s | RS-485, fibre, MBP | Fast I/O between DCS/PLC and remote I/O |
| **Profibus PA (Process Automation)** | 31.25 kbit/s | IEC 61158-2 Manchester | Hazardous-area process instruments |

* **DP-V0 / V1 / V2** define cyclic, acyclic, and isochronous services.
* **PA = DP-V1** services on the IEC 61158-2 physical layer.
* A **segment coupler** (or **link**) connects PA ↔ DP.
* A Profibus frame contains a **telegram** with **FDL (Fieldbus Data Link)** addressing — one master, multiple slaves.

**GSD file** (General Station Description) is the device’s electronic data sheet used by the DCS configuration tool.

---

## 2.5 Industrial Ethernet Protocols

| Protocol | Governing Body | Cycle Time | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EtherNet/IP** | ODVA | 1–10 ms | DCS I/O, motion |
| **PROFINET** | PI | 31.25 µs – 10 ms | DCS, drives, safety (PROFIsafe) |
| **Modbus TCP** | Schneider | 100 ms+ | Legacy integration |
| **OPC UA** | OPC Foundation | n/a | Information model, MQTT-like, secure |

> [!TIP]
> **OPC UA is the modern convergence layer** — even HART, FF, Profibus devices are exposed to the DCS over OPC UA through an **OPC server / IO server**.

---

## 2.6 WirelessHART

* Released in **2007** by HART Communication Foundation.
* **IEEE 802.15.4** radio, **2.4 GHz ISM**, **DSSS**, **250 kbps**.
* **Time Synchronised Mesh** — every device is a router, time synchronised to within ±100 µs of the network manager.
* **Channel hopping** across **15 channels** to defeat interference.
* **AES-128** encryption + **message integrity code** + **join key** + **network key**.

Network components:

* **Wireless Field Device**
* **WirelessHART Adapter** (retrofit for 4–20 mA devices)
* **Gateway** — bridges to the DCS backbone (Modbus, Modbus TCP, EtherNet/IP, OPC).
* **Network Manager** — schedules, routes, manages security, redundant.
* **Security Manager** — holds keys.
* **Access Point** — radio gateway for field devices.

### 2.6.1 WirelessHART Superframe

$$
T_{\text{superframe}} = n \cdot T_{\text{slot}} \quad \text{where} \; T_{\text{slot}} = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

Up to **15 channels × 100 slots/s** ⇒ **1500 transmissions / second** per access point.

---

## 2.7 ISA100.11a

* Open wireless standard by **ISA (International Society of Automation)**.
* Also **IEEE 802.15.4** 2.4 GHz.
* More flexible than WirelessHART — supports **multiple protocols** (HART, Profibus, FF, Modbus) over a common backbone.
* Supports **field device access (FDA)**, **process automation (PA)**, and **factory automation (FA)** profiles.

> [!NOTE]
> **WirelessHART = HART over wireless**;
> **ISA100.11a = multiple protocols over wireless**.

---

| 10 | Profibus PA speed | $R_{PA} = 31.25 \,\text{kbit/s}$ | Hazardous area |
| 11 | WirelessHART slot | $T_{slot} = 10 \,\text{ms}$ | Time synchronised |

## 2.9 Engineering Utility

* **Predictive maintenance** — diagnostics identify valve seat wear, RTD drift, plugged impulse lines.
* **Asset management** — calibration certificates stored in device memory (FOUNDATION Fieldbus, EDDL).
* **Reduced wiring** — WirelessHART eliminates **60–80 %** of conduit & cable costs in a tank-farm retrofit.
* **Single-pane-of-glass** — DCS sees HART, FF, Profibus, WirelessHART, OPC UA devices through standard integration.
* **Safety** — IEC 61508 SIL loop integrity improved by *continuous* valve signature diagnostics.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation – Conversion of 4–20 mA to Engineering Units

**Problem context.** A smart **differential-pressure transmitter** is ranged **LRV = 0 kPa**, **URV = 100 kPa** and is connected to a DCS analogue input card with a 250 Ω sense resistor. The DCS measures **3.250 V** across the sense resistor. Find the process pressure.

### Step 1 – Compute the loop current
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{loop} &= \dfrac{V_{meas}}{R_{sense}} \\[4pt]
         &= \dfrac{3.250 \text{ V}}{250 \;\Omega} \\[4pt]
         &= 0.01300 \text{ A} = 13.00 \text{ mA}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 – Convert mA to normalised fraction
$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= \dfrac{I_{loop} - 4 \text{ mA}}{20 \text{ mA} - 4 \text{ mA}} \\[4pt]
  &= \dfrac{13.00 - 4}{16} = \dfrac{9.00}{16} \\[4pt]
  &= 0.5625
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 – Convert to engineering units
$$
\begin{aligned}
P &= LRV + x \cdot (URV - LRV) \\[4pt]
  &= 0 \text{ kPa} + 0.5625 \cdot (100 - 0) \text{ kPa} \\[4pt]
  &= 56.25 \text{ kPa}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 – Reverse verification
$$
\begin{aligned}
I_{verify} &= 4 + 16 \cdot \dfrac{56.25 - 0}{100 - 0} \\[4pt]
           &= 4 + 16 \cdot 0.5625 \\[4pt]
           &= 4 + 9 = 13.00 \text{ mA} \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**The DCS will additionally poll the smart device over HART** to read digital PV, temperature, sensor health, calibration date, etc.

---

## 3.2 Derivation – Foundation Fieldbus H1 Maximum Cable Length

For a **31.25 kbit/s** Manchester-encoded bus, the IEC 61158-2 standard specifies a **maximum allowable attenuation of 0.25 dB/m at 39 kHz** for H1 cable. With **4 repeaters** the total trunk length is:

$$
L_{max} = (1 + N_{rep}) \times L_{seg} = (1 + 4) \times 1900 \text{ m} = 9500 \text{ m}
$$

Step-by-step:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{seg, \,non-IS} &= 1900 \text{ m} \quad (\text{with 4 repeaters, total 9.5 km}) \\
L_{seg, \,IS}     &= 1000 \text{ m} \quad (\text{entity-parameter limited}) \\
N_{dev, \,non-IS} &= 32 \\
N_{dev, \,IS}     &= 9 \text{ to } 12
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cable types recognised by IEC 61158-2:** Type A (screened twisted pair), Type B (multi-core), Type C (unscreened), Type D (multi-core, unshielded). **Type A is mandatory** for long segments; Type C is limited to **< 400 m**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation – WirelessHART Time-Slot and Superframe Capacity

### Given
* Slot length $T_{slot} = 10$ ms
* Number of slots/superframe $N_{slot} = 100$
* Channel-hopping channels $N_{ch} = 15$

### Capacity

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{super} &= N_{slot} \cdot T_{slot} = 100 \times 10 \text{ ms} = 1.0 \text{ s} \\[4pt]
N_{tx/s}  &= \dfrac{N_{slot} \cdot N_{ch}}{T_{super}} = \dfrac{100 \times 15}{1 \text{ s}} = 1500 \,\text{transactions / s} \\[4pt]
N_{tx,per-device} &= \dfrac{N_{tx/s}}{N_{dev}} \quad \text{— depends on network manager schedule}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Application note

A typical WirelessHART pressure transmitter publishes at **1–4 s** update rate; the network manager schedules that device to transmit in one specific time-slot on one of the 15 channels, and the gateway acknowledges on a known return slot.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Selection of Smart-Device Interface

| Application | Recommended Protocol | Speed | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single critical control loop | **4–20 mA + HART** | n/a | High reliability, simplest |
| Tank farm with 50+ levels | **WirelessHART** | 250 kbps | Wire-cost reduction, easy retrofit |
| Centrifugal compressor surge control | **Foundation Fieldbus H1** | 31.25 kbps | Distributed function blocks, fast |
| Motor control centre | **Profibus DP** | 12 Mbit/s | Fast I/O, deterministic |
| Hazardous zone continuous analyser | **Profibus PA** | 31.25 kbps | Bus-powered, FISCO |
| Plant backbone | **FF HSE / PROFINET / EtherNet/IP** | 100 Mbit/s+ | High speed |
| Safety interlock (SIL 3) | **PROFIsafe / CIP Safety** | deterministic | Safety protocol on top of Ethernet |
| Multi-vendor analytics | **OPC UA** | scalable | Vendor-neutral, secure |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix – DCS Controller Side Integration

| DCS Component | Function | Interfacing Element |
|---|---|---|
| **AI Card** (8/16 ch) | Reads 4–20 mA, may also extract HART via pass-through | HART multiplexer or HART-IP gateway |
| **AO Card** | 4–20 mA output to valve | HART-enables positioner |
| **DI/DO Card** | 24 V discrete, may have HART | Field termination assembly |
| **Fieldbus Card (H1)** | Connects 4–32 FF devices | Segment coupler, terminators |
| **Profibus Card** | Connects DP slaves | DP master port |
| **Wireless Gateway** | WirelessHART / ISA100 → Modbus/TCP/OPC | Antenna, surge arrestor |
| **OPC Server** | Software bridge to DCS | DCS engineering station |
| **IO Server** | HART IP, FF HSE → DCS | Embedded in controller or external |
